- **References:** `<3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net> <9ermbf$cel$1@freenet9.carleton.ca> <a_eQ6.5003$pa4.614935@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```

Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:a_eQ6.5003$pa4.614935@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net...
> Luis Gomez-Almeida <ar656@freenet.carleton.ca> wrote in message
> news:9ermbf$cel$1@freenet9.carleton.ca...
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I think one of the criteria in that situation is the shop environment
itself.  In my shop, the team leaders (who report to me) are expected to be
able to do any job that any of their staff can do.  Along the same lines, I
need to have a good understanding, technically, of what my teams do.
Consequently, people brought in from the outside to do team leader duties
typically have a hard time because they don't have that background.  The
higher in the hierarchy you go, the less impact there is.  In other shops,
this may not be the concern that it is for us.

Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Paul Pigott" <paul.pigott@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-05T02:04:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<W6XS6.62611$t12.4964669@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net> <YWfQ6.6800$DW1.302259@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3B1309D0.EA06A7E@earthlink.net> <WbrQ6.6837$DW1.303968@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3B157286.D48BF741@earthlink.net>`

```

John Grznar <jgrznar@earthlink.net> wrote in message
news:3B157286.D48BF741@earthlink.net...
>
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

    << much more sniping >>

> There are two valid ways to verify an idea.  The best way is through
empirical
> experiment.  The other way is through no-holds-barred, in-your-face,
debate.  The
> first way is preferred, but it is by no means foolproof.  The second
method can be
> done using polite sweet talking terms (more on this later.)  But the
> no-holds-barred method is better between persons who yet gained mutual
respect of
> each other (more on this later, too.)

  << and more sniping >>

I'm sorry.  Debate as a means of verifying an idea?  Please.  All debate
comes down to is who can present an idea in a better fashion.  Nothing in a
debate requires validation of data.  Talk fast enough and have little
respect for facts and anyone can win a debate.

Once, Catherine the Great asked the mathematician Leonhard Euler to argue
with atheist/philosopher Denis Diderot about the existence of God.  Diderot,
prior to the debate, had been told that the mathematician had a mathematical
proof for the existence of God.  Euler approached Diderot and said, "Sir, a
+ b/n = x, hence God exists; reply!"  Diderot, who knew nothing about
mathematics, gave up immediately and returned from whence he came.

Debate may be a good way to get varying ideas on the table, but don't ask me
to accept it as a means of validating those ideas.

Paul
```

Of course, programmers usually spell as badly as doctors write.  I think it
has something to do with the acronyms we speak in all day long.  And if you
code 370 Assembler, you're really in a bad way.  Everything you write ends
up at 8 letters or less!

Paul  8-)

#### ↳ Re: Motivating Programmers

- **From:** "Paul Pigott" <paul.pigott@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-03T17:37:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jBuS6.2184$Ji.208044@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net>`

```
I came into this thread late, and if I have the time I'll read all the
messages that have already accumulated, but in the meantime, let me throw in
my two cents.

I'm a Systems Manager in a mainframe COBOL shop, over 3 teams, with one team
doing e-commerce as well.  Each team has a team leader, and consists of 3-5
additional programmers.  The team leaders report to me.

I would not characterize any of my people as uninterested in their work.  On
the contrary, they are all very interested.  Certainly, some are more
interested than others, but none could be said to be distinterested.

We have had good times and bad times, of course.  The bad times have been
characterized by a lack of assignments, and general uncertainty in the
office (we were being bought out, changing offices, a number of resignations
occurred).  That period of time is now past.  Things seem to have settled
down, there are plenty of assignments, and I'm trying to get some more
interesting development work for my people.  I think that's one of the key
things for people in the IS field: work that is challenging and holds your
attention.  When forced to do mundane, uninteresting things, it seems to
send the message that "you aren't good enough for the hard things, so go do
this."

In the book "The Soul of a New Machine" (by Tracy Kidder) it's recongnized
that people will work long hours, developing their own solutions to
problems, but the work has to be interesting and compelling.  Rote
maintenance, for most people, becomes mind numbing (though there are
programmers that prefer maintenance to development).

Hygiene factors (salary, time off, benefits) often make a difference if they
are not sufficiently present.  But there comes a point where if the work
isn't rewarding, then the hygiene factors don't matter.  It becomes the
project leader's job to guide his people in the right direction, fostering
the natural enthusiasm of creative people.

Paul

John Grznar <jgrznar@earthlink.net> wrote in message
news:3B12996C.72F0C12B@earthlink.net...
> Hi guys,
>   I'm in a discussion with a colleague (we're both currently academics)
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: convert cobol

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-05T22:41:48+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B1D521C.AAA3B84E@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<Xns90B2C45796F1CID89798userdfncisde@127.0.0.1> <62zR6.52692$W14.3644959@e420r-sjo2.usenetserver.com>`

```
> > With, say, AcuCobol, it is possible to write one application on say
> > Windows and then distribute it to Unix (many), Linux, DOS, mainframe (?)
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> software to do the job, then get the hardware the software requires."

Well actually that was only appropriate _in_ time immemorial, like
several decades ago when the customer had no existing computers or IT
structure.

Certainly in the 50s and 60s there was a tendancy for companies to get
enthused about a particular computer (usually mainframe) and to buy one
would thought of which software they would run.

In most cases nowadays the customer already has existing systems and
does not want to retrain his IT staff to deal with another type of
hardware or another operating system.  Besides, there are already
screens on the desks and it is expected to run on those, whatever they
are.
 
> This cross-platform business implies "I've got my hardware, gimme some
> software that'll run on it."

That is correct, they already have the hardware, the processors, the
screens, the training.

Your aphorism is completely outdated for most customers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: convert cobol

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-05T07:34:34-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B1CDFEA.540436F@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<Xns90B2C45796F1CID89798userdfncisde@127.0.0.1> <62zR6.52692$W14.3644959@e420r-sjo2.usenetserver.com> <3B1D521C.AAA3B84E@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:

> > The standard which has held since time immemorial is "Pick the
> > software to do the job, then get the hardware the software requires."
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> structure.

It works well when you are buying hardware to run that one piece of software.
But sometimes you already have the hardware.  Sometimes you can pay for the
hardware by using it to run more than one application - so you find a compromise
- the hardware which can run accounting's 2nd choice of software, and
purchasing's 3rd choice of software.    Sometimes you anticipate growth,
sometimes you cut costs, sometimes you need a good development environment.
```

> >

requires."
>
> Well actually that was only appropriate _in_ time immemorial, like
> several decades ago when the customer had no existing computers or
IT
> structure.
>
> Certainly in the 50s and 60s there was a tendancy for companies to
get
> enthused about a particular computer (usually mainframe) and to buy
one
> would thought of which software they would run.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> screens on the desks and it is expected to run on those, whatever
they
> are.
>
> > This cross-platform business implies "I've got my hardware, gimme
some
> > software that'll run on it."
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Your aphorism is completely outdated for most customers.

It may be outdated for the customers, but not for me. I am not going
to convert a 300,000 line package for someone who is love with their
Macintosh or Web-TV or PalmPilot or PDP-8 or garage-door opener.

We don't make a product for the masses; our software costs
significantly more than the hardware it runs on.

When we get an inquiry (fortunately rare) about whether our software
runs on a Mac, the prospect gets the same reaction as a car buyer
would get from a Corvette dealership when he says: "Hey, I already own
these truck tires. Can you make me a sports car that fits 'em? I want
sumthin' com-pat-a-bly with my pickup!"

##### ↳ ↳ Re: convert cobol

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-05T22:56:13+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B1D557D.A1AEA39D@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<Xns90B2C45796F1CID89798userdfncisde@127.0.0.1> <62zR6.52692$W14.3644959@e420r-sjo2.usenetserver.com>`

```
> Lots of good stuff <snipped>.  Keep it up Richard you are constantly offering us
> insights to the shenanigans of your favourite bunch in Redmond, Wa. <G>
> 
> I'm assuming you are mainly a Windows user (?) - 

No, not much at all really.  I have several systems here, RedHat,
Mandrake (on laptop), SCO Unixware, OS/2, System Manager and Real/32. 
Yes I do have Windows 98 too.

Most of my client sites use System Manager or similar which is a
Multiuser DOS system.  They do run some client machines with Windows
though.  The same software runs on Windows for others, or on SCO or
Linux.

> is there a swell in your part
> of the world for Linux as a viable alternative to Windows. (Not too much COBOL
> wise that fits with Linux as yet - and of course, at a price !).

I have clients moving towards Linux, but not to replace Windows.  In
warehousing, for example, they do not use Windows much at all.  The
warehousemen get dumb serial terminals or Radio Terminals.  Their
systems run on Unix or Pick (or Universe) and use barcode readers not
mice.  Windows is only used in the front office for trivial things like
word processing.

They don't give the fork lift driver BMWs to run around in either.
```

- **References:** `<Xns90B2C45796F1CID89798userdfncisde@127.0.0.1> <9f6uvc$q9u$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <CUDR6.85760$gc5.3985427@e420r-sjo3.usenetserver.com> <3B17B2A7.F1874237@Azonic.co.nz> <zdXR6.125$n4.11401@e420r-atl4.usenetserver.com> <3B18ECF0.FCF3871@Azonic.co.nz> <s00S6.403$x9.9628@e420r-atl3.usenetserver.com> <3B19DC54.8D4D9D2F@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3B19DC54.8D4D9D2F@Azonic.co.nz...
> Grinder wrote:
> >
> > > It would also cost fees if you developed applications in C++ or VB
using
> > > SQL Server, or Oracle, or ...
> >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> you need not pay any licence fees at all.

I find the benefit of relational databases to powerful to pass
up--especially for enterprise-class applications.

> > In the case where I don't need a back-end like this, I don't suffer any
> > scaling to the implementation cost from license fees.
>
> And if you used Fujitsu with its built in Cobol data file system then
> you need not pay any licence fees at all.

I can use ISAM for free from practically any development platform--this
is not an exclusive feature of Cobol.

This is key to my conflict.  It appears that the principal justification for
the runtime license fees that come with cobol compilers is their ability
to run on multiple operating systems.

If I'm developing applications for Windows, I don't want to have to
pay for this.  By selecting a different language, I not only get better
access to the os internals, but I get it at a lesser cost to my end users.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: convert cobol

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-03T09:01:50+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B19EEEE.397BA1B7@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<Xns90B2C45796F1CID89798userdfncisde@127.0.0.1> <9f6uvc$q9u$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <CUDR6.85760$gc5.3985427@e420r-sjo3.usenetserver.com> <3B17B2A7.F1874237@Azonic.co.nz> <zdXR6.125$n4.11401@e420r-atl4.usenetserver.com> <3B18ECF0.FCF3871@Azonic.co.nz> <s00S6.403$x9.9628@e420r-atl3.usenetserver.com> <3B19DC54.8D4D9D2F@Azonic.co.nz> <F%bS6.105504$gc5.4877104@e420r-sjo3.usenetserver.com>`

```
Grinder wrote:
> 
> This is key to my conflict.  It appears that the principal justification for
> the runtime license fees that come with cobol compilers is their ability
> to run on multiple operating systems.

And if you used Fujitsu system then you need not pay any licence fees at
all (though this is Windows only).

Generally it is true that the run-times with licence fee provides a
mechanism for ditributing applications to many platforms.  

With, say, AcuCobol, it is possible to write one application on say
Windows and then distribute it to Unix (many), Linux, DOS, mainframe (?)
or client-server without recompiling, without even needing to restest,
and thus without having to own any platform except the one used for
development.  And, as I understand it (I don't use AcuCobol) this
application will run as GUI or TUI as appropriate.

This is a valuable feature.
 
> If I'm developing applications for Windows, I don't want to have to
> pay for this.  By selecting a different language, I not only get better
> access to the os internals, but I get it at a lesser cost to my end users.

Then what are you doing here ?

If you want to write in VB and/or C++ for Windows only (and rewrite
annually when the latest compiler comes out) then please do so.  You may
find that you avoid run-time licence fees but the costs will appear in
other ways.  With VB every release has broken previous versions, with
Windows you are locking in to a single platform (though it is widely
used), but one with many variations.

95, 98, NT, SE, Me, CE, 2000, XP, .NET all have differences in their 'OS
internals', enough to keep you rewriting, retesting and redistributing.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: convert cobol

_(thread depth: 10)_

```
> > This is key to my conflict.  It appears that the principal justification
for
> > the runtime license fees that come with cobol compilers is their ability
> > to run on multiple operating systems.
>
> And if you used Fujitsu system then you need not pay any licence fees at
> all (though this is Windows only).

You keep saying this, but multiple sales reps at Fujitsu disagree.  If
you want the full-blown, Enterprise edition of their compiler, it comes
with runtime license fees.

> Generally it is true that the run-times with licence fee provides a
> mechanism for ditributing applications to many platforms.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> > access to the os internals, but I get it at a lesser cost to my end
users.
>
> Then what are you doing here ?

Do you consider me an interloper?

I have legacy apps written in Cobol, as does the originator of this thread.
I originally responed to someone's question: "Why not rewrite it in a later
version of COBOL?"  To which I responded, "to escape runtime license
fees."

It's my impression your responses have been aimed at disputing my
suggestion.

> If you want to write in VB and/or C++ for Windows only (and rewrite
> annually when the latest compiler comes out) then please do so.  You may
> find that you avoid run-time licence fees but the costs will appear in
> other ways.

> With VB every release has broken previous versions, ....

Do you use VB?  This is simply not the case.  The most significant
difficulties in stepping up to the next version of VB came in moving
from 16 to 32 bit applications.  Those problems, though, are rooted
in the use of 16-bit addins (VBXs) that did not have 32-bit analogs.
The language itself, as well as it's attachements to the operating
system are very stable.

.NET is substantially different than VB3-6, but for good reason.  At
any rate, antecedent versions of VB still operate and their output
is still in use.

> with Windows you are locking in to a single platform (though it is widely
> used), but one with many variations.

> 95, 98, NT, SE, Me, CE, 2000, XP, .NET all have differences in their 'OS
> internals', enough to keep you rewriting, retesting and redistributing.

I've written scores of Windows applications, without this being an issue.
It's odd that you included CE and .NET--CE supports a radically different
hardware architecture and .NET isn't an operating system.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: convert cobol

_(thread depth: 11)_

If you provided your (nospam) email ID (privately or publicly) I could put
you in touch with the appropriate people at Fujitsu to get this corrected.

Similarly, if you actually gave the name of ONE (much less multiple) Fujitsu
sales people who claimed this was still true, I could have that tracked
down.

Bottom-Line:
  Fujitsu (with V6) does *NOT* have run-time license fees - anyone who has
any "real" names to associate with claims OTHER than this, can post them to
this newsgroup - or send them to me via private email (and I will forward
them to the appropriate people).
```

applications could be developed without those components.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: convert cobol

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Grinder" <no.spam@no.spam.spam.spam.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-04T17:20:06-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<l4US6.19064$e%.264148@e420r-sjo2.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<Xns90B2C45796F1CID89798userdfncisde@127.0.0.1> <9f6uvc$q9u$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <CUDR6.85760$gc5.3985427@e420r-sjo3.usenetserver.com> <3B17B2A7.F1874237@Azonic.co.nz> <zdXR6.125$n4.11401@e420r-atl4.usenetserver.com> <3B18ECF0.FCF3871@Azonic.co.nz> <s00S6.403$x9.9628@e420r-atl3.usenetserver.com> <3B19DC54.8D4D9D2F@Azonic.co.nz> <F%bS6.105504$gc5.4877104@e420r-sjo3.usenetserver.com> <3B19EEEE.397BA1B7@Azonic.co.nz> <s4CS6.11206$%R3.313621@e420r-sjo3.usenetserver.com> <3B1BB81F.EB0A22E6@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3B1BB81F.EB0A22E6@Azonic.co.nz...
> Grinder wrote:
> > >
> > > And if you used Fujitsu system then you need not pay any licence fees
at
> > > all (though this is Windows only).
> >
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> other editions.

I spoke with Mickey Rosen today, and it's true that there are no longer any
runtime fees.  (There are runtime license fees for one particular Enterprise
component-PowerBSORT, but not for the remainder of the kit.)

> I dispute your suggestion that it is axiomatic that you would need to
> pay run-time fees.  I don't.

I didn't think this "fact" was axiomatic--I was running with what a sales
rep told me--or at least what I heard.  At any rate, I'm genuinely happy
to be wrong.

> > > With VB every release has broken previous versions, ....
> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> apparently break many existing programs.

It is an entirely different framework--the environmental difference in
which it runs differs from the existing system even more than Win16
varied from Win32.

The big reason VB6 does not easily port to VB.net (sometimes
erroneously called VB7) is that multiple languages are being brought
under a common architecture.  VB is an odd enough duck, that small,
but pervasive mechanisms used by VB, do not perfectly fit C++.  As C++
is canon for Microsoft, the VB syntax loses.

This paradigm shift is significantly less dramatic than that imposed by
the shift from procedural (DOS-style) to event-driven (Windows)
programming.

Additional facilities are available for the VB6 programmer to utilize
the Common Language Runtime features through a compatibility
layer if they don't want to port their code, or wizards if they do.  How
well all of this works, though, is yet to be seen.

Before I sound like a Microsoft drone, I want to affirm that I'm not--I
think that part of this move is a crass attempt to keep their competitors
one-step behind.  But, like all heretics, there is some quantity of truth
in what they say.
```

"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz
>
> With, say, AcuCobol, it is possible to write one application on say
> Windows and then distribute it to Unix (many), Linux, DOS, mainframe
(?)
> or client-server without recompiling, without even needing to
restest,
> and thus without having to own any platform except the one used for
> development.  And, as I understand it (I don't use AcuCobol) this
> application will run as GUI or TUI as appropriate.
>
> This is a valuable feature.

I'll politely disagree.

The standard which has held since time immemorial is "Pick the
software to do the job, then get the hardware the software requires."

This cross-platform business implies "I've got my hardware, gimme some
software that'll run on it."
```

#### ↳ Re: convert cobol

- **From:** "Editor" <Editor@aboutlegacycoding.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-01T10:38:08-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9f89cg$q2i$1@allday.news.cais.net>`
- **References:** `<Xns90B2C45796F1CID89798userdfncisde@127.0.0.1>`

```
Oliver,
I have to agree with the majority here that converting COBOL to anything
else is a poor way to go.  The question has to be asked why:
1. Does Compiled COBOL code run slower than Compiled C?
2. Is the COBOL source harder to maintain that C.
3. Is there a platform you need to move the code to on which there is not a
COBOL compiler available?
4. Do you need to interface the COBOL code to the web or a GUI interface and
you mistakenly think that isn't possible and even pretty easy now?

What's the point?

There have been several good articles that discuss this whole issue in past
issues of www.aboutlegacycoding.com - and you can read them in the Archive
there.
Also, there is a new article in this months issue about exposing COBOL as
Web services in the new Microsoft .NET infrastructure that you may want to
read.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: convert cobol

- **From:** "Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-06-01T20:19:38+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9f8m13$fsp$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<Xns90B2C45796F1CID89798userdfncisde@127.0.0.1> <9f89cg$q2i$1@allday.news.cais.net>`

```
> 1. Does Compiled COBOL code run slower than Compiled C?
In general yes. The problem is that Cobol distances the programmer
from the chip architecture. To combat this the Cobol programmer
should have a basic understanding of assembler and good working
knowledge of the binary code produced by the Cobol compiler.

> 2. Is the COBOL source harder to maintain that C.
It depends on the level of the programmer doing the maintenance. It is
harder
for a Cobol programmer to understand C code.

> 3. Is there a platform you need to move the code to on which there is not
a
> COBOL compiler available?
Lots of Cobol platforms but very poor support from the vendors on accessing
the hardware / operating system directly. This makes C a better choice.

> 4. Do you need to interface the COBOL code to the web or a GUI interface
and
> you mistakenly think that isn't possible and even pretty easy now?
It is easy to interface COBOL to the WEB and to GUI's but the end results
tend
to look like 'my first program'. PROVE ME WRONG ! (please)

"Editor" <Editor@aboutlegacycoding.com> wrote in message
news:9f89cg$q2i$1@allday.news.cais.net...
> Oliver,
> I have to agree with the majority here that converting COBOL to anything
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 3. Is there a platform you need to move the code to on which there is not
a
> COBOL compiler available?
> 4. Do you need to interface the COBOL code to the web or a GUI interface
and
> you mistakenly think that isn't possible and even pretty easy now?
>
> What's the point?
>
> There have been several good articles that discuss this whole issue in
past
> issues of www.aboutlegacycoding.com - and you can read them in the Archive

…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: convert cobol

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-01T12:32:37-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B17DFC5.FF195F4B@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<Xns90B2C45796F1CID89798userdfncisde@127.0.0.1> <9f89cg$q2i$1@allday.news.cais.net> <9f8m13$fsp$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```


Alister Munro wrote:

> > 1. Does Compiled COBOL code run slower than Compiled C?
> In general yes. The problem is that Cobol distances the programmer
> from the chip architecture. To combat this the Cobol programmer
> should have a basic understanding of assembler and good working
> knowledge of the binary code produced by the Cobol compiler.

Does anybody know which optimizing compilers compare with average C and C++
programs?

Does Compiled C++ run slower than compiled C++?


> > 2. Is the COBOL source harder to maintain that C.
> It depends on the level of the programmer doing the maintenance. It is
> harder
> for a Cobol programmer to understand C code.

And with object oriented Cobol, it is possible to make the source very hard to
find as well.


> > 3. Is there a platform you need to move the code to on which there is not
> a
> > COBOL compiler available?
> Lots of Cobol platforms but very poor support from the vendors on accessing
> the hardware / operating system directly. This makes C a better choice.

If accessing the hardware / operating system directly is a high priority, mark C
up when evaluating all of the other criteria.


> > 4. Do you need to interface the COBOL code to the web or a GUI interface and
>

On Fri, 01 Jun 2001 12:32:37 -0600, Alister Munro wrote:
>
>> It is easy to interface COBOL to the WEB and to GUI's but the end results
>> tend to look like 'my first program'. PROVE ME WRONG ! (please)
=============
That might be true of your first GUI, but that's true for your first
application in any UI environment, regardless of language.  Once you
understand the UI conventions for a given environment, it will look
great.

Until you have a good grasp of how the standard Windows interface
works, you will without a doubt develop apps that aren't "quite"
right.  Windows just provides a platform, it doesn't force you to do
things in the standard way.

It is, however, readily possible to develop true Windows applications
with GUI ScreenIO and COBOL that are indistinguishable from Windows
applications written in any other language.

>PROVE ME WRONG! (please)  

Gladly.  Grab an eval copy and see for yourself:

http://www.screenio.com

Questions happily answered.

Regards,
Kevin

Norcom - COBOL Programming Tools
GUI ScreenIO - A Windows UI for COBOL
http://www.screenio.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: convert cobol

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-06-01T23:10:39+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9f9032$1bo$1@news5.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<Xns90B2C45796F1CID89798userdfncisde@127.0.0.1> <9f89cg$q2i$1@allday.news.cais.net> <9f8m13$fsp$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <3B17DFC5.FF195F4B@brazee.net> <3b17e2ff.271016120@news>`

```
The annoying thing is that under such operating systems as DOS / UNIX my
previous compiler vendor (MF) provided enough tools to allow me to write
programs in Cobol to enhance the character interface. This allowed me to
write
my own ACCEPT/DISPLAY/SOUND/WINDOWING routines which then
provide the interface to the client. At first It was just a case of reducing
the
amount of code written to just a CALL. I continued modifying my routines
with the hope that when developing for other operating systems I would only
have to modify my interface code.

Now the time has come to move these character applications to windows
without losing any functionality. So now I have obtained evals for MF net-
express 3.1 & Fujitsu 6.1. Net express gets dropped immediately due to
run-time licensing so that leaves Fujitsu.

Fujitsu provides 2 compiler models; Cobol & PowerCobol. Well I need
to drop PowerCobol because it is developed around the GUI rather than
supplying an abstraction layer to the GUI. It does provide a structure for
creating controls such as ActiveX but does not contain the constructs
necessary to manipulate all of the windows API's. I believe Fujitsu have
taken a lazy approach to this by addressing this issue with there support
for
VB  communication. So I have to drop PowerCobol as they are not
seriously developing the product (just my opinion. no death wish intended).

The Cobol product would have been perfect had it contained products such
as SP2 or GUIscreenIO (or whatever) written using only there supplied
compiler. But to call it a windows compiler simply because it runs under
windows is CRAP !. I should be able to create applictions such as SP2 using
just the Cobol compiler but the compiler does not support the callback
features
required by the win32api's (maybe wrong here, if I am, hope I am, then post
some example code that proves it).

An example of my ongoing requirements for modifying user interface code :)

I recently found a barcode printing program (foobar.c) on the web which I
converted to Cobol and then imbedded it into my print line system. Since I
could now print barcodes It made sense to be able to read barcodes. My
basic document reference which would be printed as a barcode is made up
of the ref# / type / company-id (i.e.. 12345678/WO/GL where WO means
Works Order file & GL means Glasgow data set). The field available to
the user is only the ref# which is defined as r-ordr-key PIC 9(09) BINARY.

The following code is the accept command which is contained within a
loop so that internal colour and positioning take place automatically.

* Address of data item to receive data
    SET zz-cio-ptr TO ADDRESS OF r-ordr-key
* Description of data. Binary-No sign-supp Leading 0's-Blank when zero
    SET zz-cio-B-nLB TO TRUE
* x"80" = 8 digits before the point, 0 after
    MOVE x"80" TO zz-cio-cols
* Additional code to handle the barcode
    SET zz-cio-addin-barcode TO TRUE
    MOVE "WO" TO zz-cio-barcode-id
* This jumps to a copy book which in turn makes the call to the
* accept / display commands (zzconio).
    PERFORM ZZconio-Accept

'zzconio' which is the basic console-io routine I use instead of ACCEPT/
DISPLAY can then accept a number typed by the operator or if a start
of barcode character is received can go on to test and validate that the
reference is indeed valid for the current file/data set.

Please forgive my ramblings as I have so many gripes this Fujitsu
compiler but unless I move to C I feel Fujitsu is the closest match to my
needs. Any comments/criticisms appreciated :)

"Kevin J. Hansen" <Support@ScreenIO.com> wrote in message
news:3b17e2ff.271016120@news...
> On Fri, 01 Jun 2001 12:32:37 -0600, Alister Munro wrote:
> >
> >> It is easy to interface COBOL to the WEB and to GUI's but the end
results
> >> tend to look like 'my first program'. PROVE ME WRONG ! (please)
> =============
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.screenio.com

- **Date:** 2001-06-01T23:57:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b182424.287693281@news>`
- **References:** `<Xns90B2C45796F1CID89798userdfncisde@127.0.0.1> <9f89cg$q2i$1@allday.news.cais.net> <9f8m13$fsp$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <3B17DFC5.FF195F4B@brazee.net> <3b17e2ff.271016120@news> <9f9032$1bo$1@news5.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
On Fri, 1 Jun 2001 23:10:39 +0100, "Alister Munro"
<alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk> wrote:

>The annoying thing is that under such operating systems as DOS / UNIX my
>previous compiler vendor (MF) provided enough tools to allow me to write
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>have to modify my interface code.

(snipped long - and logical - rant)

Alister,

Yup, you're right, the compiler vendor should, in principle at least,
provide the UI tools you need to develop an application.  It was true
15 years ago when we developed our first DOS UI tool, and it's still
true to a large extent.

We originally developed the UI we began marketing in 1985 because we
needed one ourselves, and we couldn't find a decent one so we wrote
our own.  There was so much demand for it that we ended up in the UI
business.  

The same thing happened with our Windows product.  We found that
vendors either didn't provide UI tools at all (CA-Realia, for
instance) or that they were too difficult to use or too non-COBOL in
how they were implemented to suit our way of thinking.  So, we
developed GUI ScreenIO.

Because we've been developing COBOL applications since 1974, we tried
to bring a COBOL developer's approach to the problem of a Windows UI
tool.

You shouldn't need to worry about Windows APIs, message loops, window
procedures, and all of the other complicated stuff that comes along
with low-level Windows programming.  The UI should make your job
simpler, not more complicated.

Sure, you can't do all of the low-level stuff that you can do if you
want to get at the low-level functions in the Windows API, but that's
the whole point of a COBOL UI - you shouldn't HAVE to.  The
application developer shouldn't be bothered by all (or, for that
matter, any) of the details of the UI.  IMHO, a good UI hides them,
and frees you to concentrate on your application.  

The entire story, incidentally, will be published on COBOLReport.com
in a couple of weeks.

Philosophically speaking, I still can't understand why compiler
vendors, with all the resources they (presumably) have available to
them, have not been able to come up with a great UI tool.  The fact
that we make a pretty good living selling our third-party UI tools
speaks for itself, however.

I guess I shouldn't complain.   ;-)

Kevin
Norcom - COBOL Programming Tools
GUI ScreenIO - A Windows UI for COBOL
http://www.screenio.com
```

So, call the Windows API's directly from within the COBOL program,
viz:

   CALL "GetWindow" WITH STDCALL USING
      BY VALUE this-window-handle
      BY VALUE hwnd-type
      RETURNING child-window-handle

If you run into a need for an API that, for whatever reason, can't be
invoked as above, there are worlds of free Active-X controls, one of
which will surely do the required task.


>
> I recently found a barcode printing program (foobar.c) on the web
which I
> converted to Cobol and then imbedded it into my print line system.
Since I
> could now print barcodes It made sense to be able to read barcodes.
My
> basic document reference which would be printed as a barcode is made
up
> of the ref# / type / company-id (i.e.. 12345678/WO/GL where WO means
> Works Order file & GL means Glasgow data set). The field available
to
> the user is only the ref# which is defined as r-ordr-key PIC 9(09)
BINARY.

PowerFORM - an adjuct to PowerCOBOL - prints barcodes. We use it to
print barcoded labels on sheet-fed devices, but it's really
printer-independent.

Barcode reading programs are unnecessary since (many/most) barcode
readers have resident keyboard encoders. Scanning a barcode is, to a
program, indistinguishable from the user typing the value.


>
> Please forgive my ramblings as I have so many gripes this Fujitsu
> compiler but unless I move to C I feel Fujitsu is the closest match
to my
> needs. Any comments/criticisms appreciated :)

No problem; frustration entitles. We try to console ourselves with:
1. Maybe we just don't know how, or
2. Maybe there's some other way, or
3. How can we circumvent the restriction.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: convert cobol

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-05T01:18:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B1C33C5.C3C43DE6@home.com>`
- **References:** `<Xns90B2C45796F1CID89798userdfncisde@127.0.0.1> <9f89cg$q2i$1@allday.news.cais.net> <9f8m13$fsp$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <3B17DFC5.FF195F4B@brazee.net> <3B186492.28E65DAE@home.com> <3B1B8CD2.D9744480@brazee.net>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:

>
> You're absolutely correct.  I should have said that if OO CoBOL is like any of the other
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> about finding where a business rule exists so that we can modify it).

Well I know I stand about as much chance as the Pope of converting Martin Luther to
catholicism - but here goes anyway <G>

Square one - there's no mystique to 'libraring' whether you are shuffling paper or shuffling
data/source. They both depend upon a logical, well thought out, and hierarchical approach.
(That's only telling you something you already know).

I can't address mainframes because I haven't programmed for them but I'm sure you will be
able to draw parallels to what I describe for Net Express below. And each time I write, I
think of your mantra 'Maintainability'. What I describe is probably applicable to other PC
compilers as well.

Take N/E - OO or non-OO. You establish a project. The IDE gives you a full screen 'picture'
of source. Down the left a hierarchical tree - unfortunately it isn't as yet alphabetic. It
lists each source and clicking on a particular source will expand the tree to show copy
members. I don't use this 'expand' feature - but it is there if you MUST have it. The right
side of the screen is an ALPHABETICAL listing of source and I include both programs and
copyfiles - but you can sub-divide if necessary. Most times I'm clicking on that right-side
alphabetical listing to bring up the source.
(Alternatively you can bring up the Windows 'Open Files' dialog).

Now to your difficulty of finding where something is 'tucked' away. If I've got a reasonably
well defined class called ImperialMetric - it's a fair bet you know what I'm talking about.
Further that could be sub-classed to cover Lengths, Volumes and US measurements (US gallons)
as opposed to Imperial. Let me re-stress, a sensible hierarchy easily identified by
meaningful (program) names.

Switch subject matter to address your 'where to find it'. I know zilch about payroll - but
you have a Calculate Payroll class which includes a method "claculate-grosspay".
Out of the blue management decides upon a scheme that allows employees to buy corporate
shares monthly, and for each one purchased they give you a freebie. The rules are 'It has
got to be x % of your monthly gross'.

Only a percentage of employees may take this option so somewhere in a Personnel Record you
have a flag indicating 'Likes buying shares'. You still use the traditional method
"calculate-grosspay". But your Business Class logic is :-

    If EmployeeDoesntByShares
        invoke CalcularePayroll "calculate-grosspay"
                    using...... returning

    Else invoke CalculatePayroll "calculate-grosspay-shares"
                    using x percentage etc.... returning
    End-if

Again not telling you anything you don't already know. The only difference being
that "calculate-grosspay-shares" does an :-

        invoke self "calculate-grosspay"

and with the percentage passed from the 'caller' returns a gross LESS percentage, to the
'caller'.

So your problem is, when making such a change, where is the code, what are the
ramifications. If you are au fait with the project then you can zero in on the IDE as I
described above. But you are a new 'maintainer' and don't know where the 'stuff' is. The N/E
IDE lets me do the following :-

    - bring up a particular source and 'search'
    - search a WHOLE project -
        (a) search for "method-id. 'calculate-grosspay' " or even wild cards
        (b) probably what really would appeal to you :-
             search for "invoke CalculatePayroll 'calculate-grosspay' " OR
                            "invoke CalculatePayroll 'calculate-grosspay-shares',
                plus again, wildcards

and for (a) or (b) each source affected is listed in an output window - click on the
reference and you go straight to the source.

Note it's the same approach whether OO or not. Given this flexibility I just don't see that
it is a nightmare to keep tabs on an application and the implications of changes or new
methods..

When do you want to be re-baptized <G>

Jimmy
```

"James J. Gavan" wrote:

> Howard Brazee wrote:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> catholicism - but here goes anyway <G>

First - I was just discussing one cost of using libraries.  We all know that any choice has some
costs and advantages.  If the advantages are worth the costs, we make a choice.  I DO use copy
libraries, but recognize that it is harder to find the source than if I didn't.


> Square one - there's no mystique to 'libraring' whether you are shuffling paper or shuffling
> data/source. They both depend upon a logical, well thought out, and hierarchical approach.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> (Alternatively you can bring up the Windows 'Open Files' dialog).

I haven't seen a mainframe editor which worked this way.  The closest thing I have seen in IBM
environments is the command SHOWPROC which I can use in JCL to look at a proc - provided the
proc is in the standard search order, and not in a JCLLIB order card.   And in my environment,
sometimes the code is in a data dictionary instead of a copylib.   I have had occasions where I
pull down a called program, compile it into a dummy library so that I can see ALL the source,
and then discover it calls another program, so I do the same thing.  I am just trying to find
out what the program does!


> Now to your difficulty of finding where something is 'tucked' away. If I've got a reasonably
> well defined class called ImperialMetric - it's a fair bet you know what I'm talking about.
> Further that could be sub-classed to cover Lengths, Volumes and US measurements (US gallons)
> as opposed to Imperial. Let me re-stress, a sensible hierarchy easily identified by
> meaningful (program) names.

I understand.
You require good discipline.   In shops where I have been, it isn't hard to have such discipline
in initial projects.  But I haven't ever seen it with old maintained projects (if we can find
documentation for instance, it is obsolete!)


> Switch subject matter to address your 'where to find it'. I know zilch about payroll - but
> you have a Calculate Payroll class which includes a method "claculate-grosspay".
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> methods..

I didn't say it was a nightmare - I said it is harder to find source.  You are showing that,
with proper discipline it can be 10 times as hard, instead of 1000 times as hard.   That is
useful, because with such costs reduced and the benefits remaining, that option becomes more
attractive.


> When do you want to be re-baptized <G>

When
1.   Mainframes have good OO tools.
2.  I have seen how well OO works in a changing enterprise business environment over time.

But even then, there will be some aspects of hidden source which will remain a pain.  (and
others which will be a delight).   Everything is a trade off.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: convert cobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-02T14:36:48+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B18EBF0.E9428844@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<Xns90B2C45796F1CID89798userdfncisde@127.0.0.1> <9f89cg$q2i$1@allday.news.cais.net> <9f8m13$fsp$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <3B17DFC5.FF195F4B@brazee.net>`

```
Alister Munro wrote:

> > 4. Do you need to interface the COBOL code to the web or a GUI interface and
>
> > you mistakenly think that isn't possible and even pretty easy now?

> It is easy to interface COBOL to the WEB and to GUI's but the end results
> tend to look like 'my first program'. PROVE ME WRONG ! (please)

How does interfacing to the web using Cobol look any different from
interfacing to the web using any other system ?

I have written several CGI programs, mostly in Cobol, and what they do
is accept text strings and emit HTML (with perhaps Javascript). The
appearance is entirely embodied within the HTML. In fact I mostly use
template files and texturally substitute to data items into the tags so
the appearance is completely independant of the program code in whatever
language is used.

As for GUIs there are several on the market that do an entirely
professional job.  I have also written a full blown graphical
applications in Cobol over the last decade (since Windows 3.0) that,
like just like every other Windows program, at its lowest level ran down
the API.

To write a full GUI in C or C++ you call the API, or use a library
provided by some vendor or other.  Just because in some cases this
library (eg MFC) is in the same box as the compiler does not mean it is
part of the language (C or C++), nor that it cannot be used from other
languages.

The appearance is untimately the result of the programmer.  If you think
that end result look like 'my first program' then it may well be that it
is.  OTOH most people use Cobol to get a job done with people who know
what they are trying to achieve, rather than make it look 'kewl'.
```

Well so far, I've avoided callbacks like the plague using OO, but Ken  Foskey,
(doesn't contribute here currently), assures me I'm using the technique all the
time. I'll dig out something from Will Price (objectz.com), plus see what Net
Express has in demos.

Now if you are specifically talking about returning events from Dialogs - that's
a different kettle of fish. Pete Dashwood could wise you up on using Components
with F/J POWERCOBOL .(His pet baby and 'he is right' !). Alternatively I can
send you what I do with dialogs using a generic MyDialog.cbl.

Write privately, and spell it out precisely what you are after. Then I can put
some stuff together.

One other thought where you were talking about the ability of using both Windows
and Linux. It's a guess, but although you can create common COBOL routines to do
things with both for GUIs - inevitably you have to acknowledge the nuances of
the O/S, and GUI wise - there has to be a distinct difference between Windows
GUIs and Linux GUIs - which means you would be passing a different set of
parameters per O/S, regardless of how your COBOL program wants the GUIs to
function.

Simple example, what is the Linux equivalent of the Windows "hide" or "setEmpty"
- may well be the same words. What syntax do they respectively use for
"setColor" for an entryfield etc..... - but you get the idea. .

Merant has an OO compiler for Linux (even more pricy !), but it doesn't do
anything about GUIs - just points you at ?????? (can't remember the module name
within Linux).

Jimmy

- **References:** `<Xns90B2C45796F1CID89798userdfncisde@127.0.0.1> <9f89cg$q2i$1@allday.news.cais.net> <9f8m13$fsp$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <3B17DFC5.FF195F4B@brazee.net> <3B18EBF0.E9428844@Azonic.co.nz> <9fa0kn$a0k$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk> <3B191634.B5A2B8C8@home.com> <9fbarc$pkh$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <3B193984.B46EC5AE@home.com>`

```
Concerning "callbacks"

I believe (but won't swear to it) that this is CLOSELY tied into the concept
of "recursive" programs in COBOL.

IBM (mainframe, PC, and Unix) compilers support it already
MERANT (Micro Focus - all platforms) compilers support it already
CA-REALIA (I believe) already supports it
It is included in the draft of the next COBOL Standard

Fujitsu does *NOT* currently provide "COBOL language support" for it.  (I
believe that PowerCOBOL does it - sort-of - under the covers, but it is NOT
part of the "basic" COBOL language).

As I recall, the earlier parts of this thread, the request was for a
"Fujitsu" source program doing "callbacks" - this may NOT be possible with
their current compiler, but is with other COBOL compilers in that
environment.  (FYI - I believe that IBM VisualAge COBOL has *no* run-time
fees, but also has somewhat less "robust" PC interfaces.)
```

Alister Munro wrote:
> 
> Fully agree that the end result (look and feel) of an application should be
> down
> to the programmer so how do we go about this using COBOL on windows ?

There are secral GUIs for Cobol that can give a nice appearance.  SP2,
PowerCobol, ACU, Dialog.  Do some research and find out what +can+ be
done instead of just moaning that it can't.

I mainly use SP2 for text mode windowing (TUI) because my clients want
this, but the system can work identically on Windows as a Windows
application, or over the net as a thin client with just a bit of
tweaking of the panel file.  

> I thought people liked using nice looking applications and as far as getting
> the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> GUI in COBOL you call for help to a third party ?

To write a full GUI in Cobol I just call the API.  Microfocus has
catered for this since version 2.4 a decade ago.

The main application that I wrote down to the API was a full graphical
Container Ship planning application that graphically displays the ship
and the bays, allows drag and drop of containers from the dockside
stacks onto the bays, has windows with graphs of bending, shearing and
torsion, even shows a view from helm.  The dialogs are actual Windows
dialogs and also use Farpoint controls for additional functionality and
prettiness.

This originally ran on Windows 3.0 with MF Cobol 2.5 a decade ago, and
has grown somewhat as it has met new requirements.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: convert cobol

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** gary drummond <gdrumm@ont.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-03T02:26:55-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B1A02DF.CF145AF8@ont.com>`
- **References:** `<Xns90B2C45796F1CID89798userdfncisde@127.0.0.1> <9f89cg$q2i$1@allday.news.cais.net> <9f8m13$fsp$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <3B17DFC5.FF195F4B@brazee.net> <3B18EBF0.E9428844@Azonic.co.nz> <9fa0kn$a0k$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Just food for thought... 
(I'm not picking on you Alister, it's just a good
 place to add my comments and rants)

If you can be sure that:
1-Your application will never outgrow a Windows platform.
2-It will never become multi-user.
3-Microsoft will never change the APIs.
4-There will never be run-time licenses on Windows.
5-Security will never be an issue.
6-The COBOL standard defines a relational database access method.
  (In case #2 is wrong, we wouldn't want a third-party RDBS <G>)

If you can answer true for all the above, develop to the Windows APIs.

The main course-compatibility/cost/risk.
Use of a third-party database is required if the number of users
exceeds the capabilities of the standard file formats, or you simply
need the relational features. Pick one that supports ALL of the 
platforms you may need, or maintain multiple sets of source code.

Use a third-party GUI add-on and/or a third-party Web interface
for any of the following reasons. (They should support multiple 
platforms)

We have seen posts where switching to Fujitsu is being done to avoid
large run-time fees, so it follows that using a single vendor's 
extensions is not a good idea-they might start charging for them, 
and some extensions are not usable on all platforms supported by 
the compilers. Keeping to the standards is a GOOD thing, it allows 
you to move to a less expensive compiler, when you want to.

If you want to develop on a Windows platform, and install on a another
platform, or if you must support multiple platforms with your 
applications.

If you can't spend the time and money changing and testing 
your low-level API interface code every time an API changes. 

If you think .net will have some growing-pains.

An extra side dish-at no extra charge.
Event driven programming is simply lots of messages, with very 
little warning-and processing them in addition to the normal
workload. We do have to keep priorities straight, and manage queues,
to insure nothing gets lost.

I think we missed a bet when we didn't just extend the 
message processing standard (MCS and Communications) to handle the 
new message sources. A different MCS could interface with each OS
platform, or window manager, translating the event codes to
a standard. We're dropping them instead, and supporting APIs-which
*could* mean we have to duplicate the MCS functions in our code.


Time to order dessert.
Will we have to learn all 700 Windows APIs? How many for X? Motif?
Will M$ be nice and package the APIs into application groups, so we 
only have to pay extra for the ones we need?
Does .net scare anyone else?

My run-time license expired,
pick up the check please <G>.

Gary

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: convert cobol

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Support@ScreenIO.com (Kevin J. Hansen)
- **Date:** 2001-06-03T18:53:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b1a7bdf.441224146@news>`
- **References:** `<Xns90B2C45796F1CID89798userdfncisde@127.0.0.1> <9f89cg$q2i$1@allday.news.cais.net> <9f8m13$fsp$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <3B17DFC5.FF195F4B@brazee.net> <3B18EBF0.E9428844@Azonic.co.nz> <9fa0kn$a0k$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
On Sat, 2 Jun 2001 08:22:13 +0100, "Alister Munro"
<alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk> wrote:

>Fully agree that the end result (look and feel) of an application should be
>down
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>GUI in COBOL you call for help to a third party ?
======================

Well, you certainly CAN write to the Windows API in COBOL; that's what
we did.  All you're doing is calling Windows functions from COBOL
using "C" calling conventions.  All of the mainstream compilers will
let you do this.

As for writing windows procedures, you can do that in COBOL, too,
provided that your compiler supports recursion.  I know that CA-Realia
and Merant do, and I believe the high-end Fujitsu compiler also does.
I'm not so sure about AcuCOBOL.  However, while it's easy to write a
window procedure (in COBOL or whatever) that doesn't abend, it's
another thing to actually get it to do the stuff you want it to do.
Lots of the Windows stuff is pretty subtle and finicky.  

IMHO, the subtleties of Windows API programming are just as obscure in
C as they are in COBOL.  There's also the little matter of figuring
out how to communicate between your window procedures and your
mainline application.  You can do it in C using global variables, but
you need to work out other strategies in COBOL.  It requires a solid
background in how software actually works.  If you don't understand
how arguments are passed and  what it means to obtain addressability
to an area of memory, you won't get very far, again, whether you use C
or COBOL.

The REAL key to low-level Windows programming is having a thorough
conceptual grasp of how it actually works - and that takes a lot of
time.  Sure, we got our first (crude) Windows apps up-and-running in
COBOL within 6 months of opening the Windows SDK documentation, but
they were, well, not too well done, and  I didn't really feel that I
had an intuitive grasp of Windows development until I'd been at it for
two or three years.  Today, after more than five years of 100% Windows
API programming - in COBOL - I can generally add new features or use
new Windows facilities within an hour or two of reading about them...
but I still feel I'm  learning new stuff about Windows every day...

Use a third party tool for your Windows UI to avoid this (painful)
learning process and so that you can concentrate on the application
instead of wasting all your time on the interface.  

As a rough indication of the difficulty of the Windows API, we rolled
out our first character-mode UI in 1985 after about 6 months/1,000
hours of development.  It changed little over its 15 year life.  It
was quite complete and solid.

Our GUI screen manager represents an investment of over 4 years/12,000
hours to date.  Sure, it does tremendously more than the old DOS
character mode product, but much of that time was spent in designing a
rational programming interface and actually implementing the Windows
stuff.  It was a complex and extremely challenging software project.
I wouldn't want to go through it again.

We did it in COBOL, by the way, and I'm convinced it would have taken
just as long (or longer) to develop it in any other language.  The
tricky stuff was grokking Windows, not actually implementing the
product.  Windows programming is hard.

So, don't underestimate the difficulty of implementing your UI at the
API level.  The whole point of a UI tool like ours is to hide all that
complexity from you and let you get on with your application.

If you need to call the API directly to step beyond what the UI tool
does, there's generally no reason why you can't do it.  It's
conceptually the same as calling an assembler routine to, say, perform
bitwise operations instead of doing 'em in COBOL.

Kevin
Norcom - COBOL Programming Tools
GUI ScreenIO - A Windows UI for COBOL
http://www.screenio.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: convert cobol

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-03T20:13:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XTwS6.5727$a3.618390@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<Xns90B2C45796F1CID89798userdfncisde@127.0.0.1> <9f89cg$q2i$1@allday.news.cais.net> <9f8m13$fsp$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <3B17DFC5.FF195F4B@brazee.net> <3B18EBF0.E9428844@Azonic.co.nz> <9fa0kn$a0k$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk> <3b1a7bdf.441224146@news>`

```
Kevin J. Hansen <Support@ScreenIO.com> wrote in message
news:3b1a7bdf.441224146@news...
>
> Well, you certainly CAN write to the Windows API in COBOL; that's what
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> and Merant do, and I believe the high-end Fujitsu compiler also does.

Unless I am mistaken, It's not recursive ability you need, it is
re-entrancy; which, by the way, also requires some thought on the part of
the programmer.

In a Window proc you would never call WindowProc (recursion) ; rather, you
would call DefWindowProc or SendMessage, which might call WindowProc while
WindowProc is already executing - re-entrancy.

But inasmuch as reentrancy requires pretty much the same thing as
recursion - separate stack variables at entry - I would not be amazed if
you get one automatically with the other in most compilers.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: convert cobol

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Support@ScreenIO.com (Kevin J. Hansen)
- **Date:** 2001-06-03T21:47:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b1aaf1b.454340276@news>`
- **References:** `<Xns90B2C45796F1CID89798userdfncisde@127.0.0.1> <9f89cg$q2i$1@allday.news.cais.net> <9f8m13$fsp$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <3B17DFC5.FF195F4B@brazee.net> <3B18EBF0.E9428844@Azonic.co.nz> <9fa0kn$a0k$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk> <3b1a7bdf.441224146@news> <XTwS6.5727$a3.618390@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
On Sun, 03 Jun 2001 20:13:43 GMT, "Michael Mattias"
<michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:

>Unless I am mistaken, It's not recursive ability you need, it is
>re-entrancy; which, by the way, also requires some thought on the part of
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>you get one automatically with the other in most compilers.
=============
Michael,

No, I don't think you're mistaken.  You've described very well what
happens.  I think you're also correct in saying that re-entrancy is
effectively the same as recursive ability, as far as the software is
concerned.  In fact, in the very early days of our project we really
DID write some truly recursive (as well as re-entrant) programs to
verify that we could do what needed to be done.

The nice thing is, other than being aware that each level of the
program gets its own set of local storage (in CA-Realia, at least),
you don't need to worry about stacks and such; all you need to do is
specify "program-name IS RECURSIVE" and the compiler deals with the
basic housekeeping stuff.  

But you are correct, you gotta understand what's going on in order to
have things come out the way you want.  It's not at all like straight
procedural code we're used to, and it can be a bear to debug!

Kevin

Norcom - COBOL Programming Tools

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: convert cobol

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Support@ScreenIO.com (Kevin J. Hansen)
- **Date:** 2001-06-03T22:48:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b1ab13a.454883497@news>`
- **References:** `<Xns90B2C45796F1CID89798userdfncisde@127.0.0.1> <9f89cg$q2i$1@allday.news.cais.net> <9f8m13$fsp$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <3B17DFC5.FF195F4B@brazee.net> <3B18EBF0.E9428844@Azonic.co.nz> <9fa0kn$a0k$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk> <3b1a7bdf.441224146@news> <XTwS6.5727$a3.618390@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <9fe89r$ns$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
On Sun, 3 Jun 2001 23:02:17 +0100, "Alister Munro"
<alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk> wrote:

>Re-entrancy is what were after to handle all the possible quirks of the
>winapi. So does Fujitsu Cobol compile code that will allow this to
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>the keyboard buffer.
===============
Alister,

If all you're after is picking up keystrokes, you could probably do it
at the Windows message-loop level of your program and not worry about
any but the most simple window procedure.

If you're going to do much more, though, such as create and use
standard Windows controls like edit controls, radiobuttons,
checkboxes, and such, you are going to find your life will rapidly
become complicated.  Anything but the simplest and most rudimentary
Windows application is going to require handling stuff in a window
procedure, and the more functionality you want, the more code you'll
have.  If you DON'T use the standard Windows controls, you're going to
need to deal with Windows WM_PAINT messages to redraw your window
contents on demand.  Either way, it takes some work on your part.


Now that I've told you how difficult it is <G>, I'll give you some
pointers:

If you haven't read it yet, a good place to start would be Charles
Petzold's "Programming Windows 95".  Don't take it as gospel, because
it's not really geared towards developing a general-purpose UI like
you want; it just touches the basics.  If you're like me, you'll be
completely befuddled the first time you wade through the first couple
of chapters, but after reading it a couple of times it begins to make
some sort of sense.  

Believe me, Windows programming is like nothing you've ever
encountered before!

Once you get the gist of what Petzold has to say, you'll want to
subscribe to the Microsoft Developer's Network.  That will get you all
of the documentation and libraries that you need.  (And a lot of stuff
you don't.)

Again, if you're like me, you'll probably spend a month or three
getting your arms around THAT mess, and then you can start actually
developing some Windows code.  

As for using Fujitsu COBOL, my understanding is that you'd need the
Enterprise Edition to support re-entrancy/recursion; the "basic" model
doesn't support it.  They talked to us about a year ago about the
possibility of implementing GUI ScreenIO using their product instead
of CA-Realia.  At that time, because we need to distribute the runtime
with our product, and because the Enterprise Edition charged royalties
for the runtime, we didn't go that route (CA-Realia's runtime is
royalty-free).  But, I have little doubt that we could convert to
Fujitsu in short order if we wished.  I expect that we'll look into it
again when we find the time.

We also did a lot of work with the Micro Focus compiler around 5 years
ago, and it worked reasonably well for low-level Windows stuff.  We
had fewer problems and better performance with CA-Realia, though, and
since we implemented the runtime so it will work with any 32-bit
COBOL, we're fairly content with where we are today.

I certainly wish you luck in your undertaking.

Kevin
Norcom - COBOL Programming Tools
GUI ScreenIO - A Windows UI for COBOL
http://www.screenio.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: convert cobol

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "James. J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-03T23:48:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B1ACD59.111D8039@home.com>`
- **References:** `<Xns90B2C45796F1CID89798userdfncisde@127.0.0.1> <9f89cg$q2i$1@allday.news.cais.net> <9f8m13$fsp$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <3B17DFC5.FF195F4B@brazee.net> <3B18EBF0.E9428844@Azonic.co.nz> <9fa0kn$a0k$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk> <3b1a7bdf.441224146@news> <XTwS6.5727$a3.618390@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <9fe89r$ns$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```


Alister Munro wrote:

> Re-entrancy is what were after to handle all the possible quirks of the
> winapi. So does Fujitsu Cobol compile code that will allow this to
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> the keyboard buffer.

Now you've been more concise in your question. You have several possible
options, which if you like, emulate that DOS keypressing you refer to.

- SP2 and Screen IO - you work totally in COBOL calling these packages which
return you Windows events

- Fujitsu - I don't know about the 'lower end' but it most certainly has to be
the case for POWERCOBOL. You Fujitsu users - please fill in details on this one.

- Net Express - ignoring direct API calls - two options (a) Dialog System
returning events, and the D/S module also allows you to build in checking
features, or (b) GUI classes. If you are familiar with the Microsoft Dialog
Editor, then you are close to what happens with the Merant (version) of Dialog
Editor used with the GUI classes.

Note that neither the Fujitsu or Merant approach necessitates third party tools,
although of course you can use SP2 or Screen IO with these products.

Whichever way you go, GUIs will NEVER be a part of Standard COBOL. (Briefly
COBOL missed the boat, and even if introduced at some indeterminate date, who is
going to switch their existing code to fit 'new' COBOL GUIs).

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: convert cobol

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Support@ScreenIO.com (Kevin J. Hansen)
- **Date:** 2001-06-04T16:42:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b1bb3a0.521032795@news>`
- **References:** `<Xns90B2C45796F1CID89798userdfncisde@127.0.0.1> <9f89cg$q2i$1@allday.news.cais.net> <9f8m13$fsp$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <3B17DFC5.FF195F4B@brazee.net> <3B18EBF0.E9428844@Azonic.co.nz> <9fa0kn$a0k$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk> <3b1a7bdf.441224146@news> <XTwS6.5727$a3.618390@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <9fe89r$ns$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <3B1ACD59.111D8039@home.com>`

```
On Sun, 03 Jun 2001 23:48:23 GMT, "James. J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
wrote:

>- Screen IO - you work totally in COBOL calling these packages which
>return you Windows events
=========
Hi, Jimmy,

A minor, but significant, correction - GUI ScreenIO doesn't bother you
with Windows events, it returns "application" events -  stuff that's
of interest to you as the developer.  

What's an "application" event?  Events that you define when you design
your panel; things like menu selections, button presses, whether a
field should cause a return to your program when the user enters or
leaves it, that sort of thing.  You define these up front when you
create your panel in the editor.  You, therefore, define all of the
possible reasons that GUI ScreenIO will return to your program in
advance.  If you think about it, this is a logical way of addressing
the issue of user interactoins with a given screen.

Then, when you display your panel at runtime, the runtime won't return
to your program until one of your predefined events occurs; the user
selects a menu item, presses a button, or whatever.  (You can have it
return in other situations, of course, but you usually wouldn't need
to bother.)  

This is conceptually the same as any other transaction processing
system; where you send a screen to a terminal and wait for a user
action.

You literally are not aware that you're running under Windows because
we do ALL of the low-level stuff.  That, in my opinion, is the whole
point of a UI tool.  Developers can then concentrate on their
application instead of getting all tangled up in the details of the
UI.

Regards,
Kevin
Norcom - COBOL Programming Tools
GUI ScreenIO - A Windows UI for COBOL
http://www.screenio.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: convert cobol

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-03T12:15:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CTpS6.4277$a3.478286@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<Xns90B2C45796F1CID89798userdfncisde@127.0.0.1> <lpXR6.11$m4.57@e420r-atl4.usenetserver.com> <9fa0kn$a0k$2@news8.svr.pol.co.uk> <D20S6.79$h7.4673@e420r-atl2.usenetserver.com> <3B19DD36.B1B00E83@Azonic.co.nz> <FecS6.11631$tm5.399636@e420r-sjo2.usenetserver.com> <3B19EFCC.5FED31FB@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
If you haven't attended one of the Microsoft "Windows .NET Developer
Summits" (free, but you have to register), you should. The .NET architecture
has nothing to do with "toll gates" or even using Microsoft products. The
entire run-time system is quite 'open'.

When I attended the seminar here, the principal presenter, Mr. Jeffrey
Richter, told the 700 or so of us (semi-facetiously)  .NET  is "similar to
the Java byte code concept, but since Microsoft is sponsoring this event, we
don't say 'Java'."  (By the way, I'm sure many of you have read one or more
of Mr. Richter's books. Be assured he is also an excellent public
presenter).

The official Microsoft speaker at the seminar summed up Microsoft's raison
d'etre for .NET: "We believe software will be a service, not a product; we
intend to be in the 'software as a service' market; .NET is our enabling
technology to make software as a service possible."

Yes, if Microsoft is right about this, they have a HUGE market lead for any
.NET applications which happen to run on the Windows O/S (where Microsoft
has already developed the run-time support), but .NET is anything but a
closed system.

MCM
(quotes paraphrased, as the memory is the second thing to go; but they're
close).

news:3B19EFCC.5FED31FB@Azonic.co.nz...

> 
> When I attended the seminar here, the principal presenter, Mr. Jeffrey
> Richter, told the 700 or so of us (semi-facetiously)  .NET  is "similar to
> the Java byte code concept,

As a way of running programs .NET includes a VM that runs byte codes and
MS has extended its compilers to produce this byte code in a uniform
way.  MC-C/C++ has had a byte-code (pcode) mechanism for nearly a decade
and VB has always produced byte-codes.  In fact their first Cobol back
in 1979 also did byte-code.

In theory this VM could be implemented on any platform, in practice it
is most likely that it will only be available for Windows in every
variety including CE, embedded, 'Windows for Phones' and for whatever
processors these run on.  MS needs to work on more than just Intel chips
as the desktop PC market becomes saturated and the handhelds and
embedded markets do not run on Pentiums.
 
> The official Microsoft speaker at the seminar summed up Microsoft's raison
> d'etre for .NET: "We believe software will be a service, not a product; we
> intend to be in the 'software as a service' market; .NET is our enabling
> technology to make software as a service possible."

Linux companies have been doing 'software as a service not a product'
for quite some years now.  But this is not quite the same as MS wants it
to be.  This can be seen in how MS licences will be phrased in the near
future.  Currently all my MS licences are perpetual.  I can install it
on a machine and run it forever.  ie I bought the 'licence to use' as a
product.  MS is changing the 'licence to use' to be an expiring licence
so that after a time the product stops working and I have to buy it
again, or keep updating it annually.

Thus MS changing from 'product based' to 'service based' is to ensure
that all its users are paying them money every year in order to run the
'service' of having an operating system.

The need for .NET in this particular case is to keep track of the
registrations (starting in Office XP and Windows XP), to provide the
updates and to ensure that they expire correctly if the service is not
paid for.

Of course the .NET 'technology' is hyped to give users a reason to move
to XP and its successors in the first place, otherwise they would stick
with Win98 and WinNT/2000 that they could run forever without sending
another cent to MS.
 
> Yes, if Microsoft is right about this, they have a HUGE market lead for any
> .NET applications which happen to run on the Windows O/S (where Microsoft
> has already developed the run-time support), but .NET is anything but a
> closed system.

While the byte-code support is important for running applications on the
various new machines of the future - PDAs, Phones, embedded systems, it
is not particularly important for desktop and server applications.  And
it is only important _if_ MS actually gets above the noise level on
these environments, or builds the byte code VM for other systems.

The actually important bits of .NET haven't been described in any detail
yet, just market hype.  The main part would be the linking of
application components across the net. Instead of writing all of your
application you could chosse a component somewhere on the net (.NET)
that could do part of the processing for you, send it the data and
collect the results.  This would save you developing the code yourself
or buying in a product to run on your own machine, or build in to your
application.

Now, of course, the machine offering that computation service would
charge a very small fee per transaction that you get computed by them. 
The way .NET works is that these transaction fees would be recorded at
some central point, say MSN in the Passport system.  Naturally as MSN
are providing this commerce system as a service they would take a small
percentage (cf tax).

All of what .NET has been talked about has already been available from
others in some form or another, nothing is particually new or innovative
(except, as usual, the hype).
```

But you know, I have absolutely no problem with this, because Microsoft is
being 'up front' with their belief that 'subscriptions' and 'per-transaction
fees'  will supplant 'perpetual license purchases.'

You can already see a little of this: Oracle offers a 'developer license'
program which is just what I was looking for. You can purchase a two-year or
four year single-user license (sounds like a 'subscription,' doesn't it?) at
an attractive (read, "low") price. This allows the developer to write and
test most of the code at his office before doing the 'on-site tuning' we all
know will be required.

MCM

- **Message-ID:** `<9fgit9$r9s$1@allthetime.news.cais.net>`
- **References:** `<Xns90B2C45796F1CID89798userdfncisde@127.0.0.1> <lpXR6.11$m4.57@e420r-atl4.usenetserver.com> <9fa0kn$a0k$2@news8.svr.pol.co.uk> <D20S6.79$h7.4673@e420r-atl2.usenetserver.com> <3B19DD36.B1B00E83@Azonic.co.nz> <FecS6.11631$tm5.399636@e420r-sjo2.usenetserver.com> <3B19EFCC.5FED31FB@Azonic.co.nz> <CTpS6.4277$a3.478286@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <3B1B416E.3144E9FB@Azonic.co.nz> <%KLS6.157$Lx.63668@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
The interesting thing is - the .NET platform is also designed to help other
tools and software developers charge the same way for their products and
services.
Microsoft expects everything to go to this license model.  And although they
wouldn't be the primary recipients of license fees for things like the
Fujitsu compliler, they are setting themselves up as the "bankers" so that
they will get a cut off of everything.
Personally, I don't think they have a chance in a million of making this
truly work the way they expect.  It's not that they can't pull it off
technically, its that Anti Trust laws and/or consumers at large will revolt.
It won't work for the same reason carpooling won't ever work.  Humans can't
stand to have someone else in control.
```

Editor wrote:
> 
> The interesting thing is - the .NET platform is also designed to help other
> tools and software developers charge the same way for their products and
> services.

Yes, just as have done previously with DOS and Windows.  Developers of
many software type could expect their products to sell to the millions
of DOS and Windows users.  Word Processors, Spreadsheets, disk
compression utilities, backup, web browsers, video players, ...

> Microsoft expects everything to go to this license model.  And although they
> wouldn't be the primary recipients of license fees for things like the
> Fujitsu compliler, they are setting themselves up as the "bankers" so that
> they will get a cut off of everything.

And they will also be the brokers.  By having in their Passport service
all the users and everything that they are enabled to do they can choose
which would be the most profitable to pick off next.  Are you providing
a nice little earner in a 'tidal prediction service' ?  Well just wait
until 'MS Tides' makes it easy for your existing users to move to theirs
because MS put their product on top of the list, and the browser can't
find yours anymore.

> Personally, I don't think they have a chance in a million of making this
> truly work the way they expect.  It's not that they can't pull it off
> technically, its that Anti Trust laws 

Which appear to be useless at this time.

> and/or consumers at large will revolt.
> It won't work for the same reason carpooling won't ever work.  Humans can't
> stand to have someone else in control.

Humans mostly don't notice.  When they install Windows it tells them
they need IE so they install it, it tells them they need to talk to MSN,
so they connect to MSN.

(I installed a new copy Win98 last week and the 'Connect to the
Internet' _only_ gave the option of MSN or Cancel.  It did not give any
options such as: use existing account, or select ISP.  This had to be
done entirely manually digging down levels under Control Panel).

Richard Plinston wrote:
>

…[40 more quoted lines elided]…

Do you remember the SR2 or maybe it was the OEM-second release of
Win 95? I paid for that thing, and it didn't give a choice, just 
installed everything M$, broke most of my third-party software 
(the ones that compete with M$), and broke my ATI-TV's live recording.
After the install it took 45 minutes for me to go around and uninstall
the crap I didn't want, and then-because their uninstalls didn't
work right, deleting the files and directories for the crap I
uninstalled. It did make a good coaster, except for the occasional
memory leak in the middle!

I just retired my last '95 system, got rid of the last app
that wouldn't run under 98 or 98SE, but I still have a Win 3.1 system
for
a few dos things that won't run under '98. If they had just eliminated
the dos/3.1 apps from '95, and went to pure Win apps, we would have
been better off (or it would have been cheaper than replacing 
our apps now!).

Maybe that's what they have in mind with XP and .net? I'm all for
it, because it would cost so much, I wouldn't do it, and then I
wouldn't have to upgrade any more <G>. .net must be free for the users,
because they could NEVER sell the consumers on a yearly license.
(Could we hope they will go the way of DRI's DOS?)

Gary
