gary drummond wrote:
> 
>  it didn't give a choice, just 
> installed everything M$, broke most of my third-party software 
> (the ones that compete with M$),

The ones that _used_ to compete with MS no doubt.

> If they had just eliminated
> the dos/3.1 apps from '95, and went to pure Win apps, we would have
> been better off (or it would have been cheaper than replacing
> our apps now!).

If they had eliminated the DOS and Win/3.1 apps in Win95 then yes we
would all be better off now, Win95 would have stagnated like WinNT 3.x
did and we would all be running OS/2 which _does_ run all DOS and Win3.1
apps quite nicely.

Windows 98 betas did eliminate DOS and Win3.1 programs and only ran
Win32 apps, but the beta testers simply stated that their companies
would stick with what did the job they wanted, not what MS wanted.  MS
then spent 6 months putting this support back in (with some lame excuse
about how much better tey were making it).

- **Date:** 2001-06-05T07:47:17+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B1C8075.24BDFF21@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<Xns90B2C45796F1CID89798userdfncisde@127.0.0.1> <lpXR6.11$m4.57@e420r-atl4.usenetserver.com> <9fa0kn$a0k$2@news8.svr.pol.co.uk> <D20S6.79$h7.4673@e420r-atl2.usenetserver.com> <3B19DD36.B1B00E83@Azonic.co.nz> <FecS6.11631$tm5.399636@e420r-sjo2.usenetserver.com> <3B19EFCC.5FED31FB@Azonic.co.nz> <CTpS6.4277$a3.478286@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <3B1B416E.3144E9FB@Azonic.co.nz> <%KLS6.157$Lx.63668@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <9fgit9$r9s$1@allthetime.news.cais.net>`

```
"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote 
> Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote 
> >  [snipped]
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> being 'up front' with their belief that 'subscriptions' and
'per-transaction
> fees'  will supplant 'perpetual license purchases.'

But will they offer a choice ?

I don't think so.

There is also another perception problem that affects MicroSoft.  When
the usual punter walks into a store and buys a computer he thinks that
he gets Windows 'for free' because if he has it removed the machine is
still the same price.  With increasing MS charges (XP is based on
NT/2000 pricing not 98) and reducing computer prices the proportion that
the hardware supplier gets has been reducing, making hardware
non=profitable.

Soon the big manufacturers will be offering Linux boxes that actually
don't pay the MS 'box tax' and undercut the price of Windows machines. 
This would make MS have to compete on price.  They won't do this so they
will have a 'free media only' Windows version installed that costs
nothing of the purchase price (and thus competes against Linux etc) but
there will be a monthly charge for the software included with the MSN
monthly charge.

As MS is buying up the cable companies around California and is
'negotiating' with AOL and others then it will more difficult to avoid
connecting via an MS service and so the monthly chatge may apply
regardless of which operating system is actually being run. (MS just
'invested' in our local ISP which is part of the local telecom)

They are attempting to return to 'per box' pricing where a fee was paid
to MS whether you bought Windows or not.  Soon you will pay an 'OS
service fee' to MS via your internet connection whether you use Windows
or not (unless you can arrange a non-MS connection by some means).

A further effect of the 'Windows for free' perception is that (I am
reliably informed) it gets passed around and installed on several
machines (ie small beer piracy).  MS won't care if each machine gets to
pay the monthly service fee anyway.

MS are not changing this for the benefit of the consumer, or to offer
choice in the market place.  Quite the reverse, "'subscriptions' and
'per-transaction fees'  will supplant 'perpetual license purchases.'"
because this will give MS a better cash flow, and will strangle whatever
competition has had the affrontery to survive MS's previous strategies.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: convert cobol

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Editor" <Editor@aboutlegacycoding.com>

I have attended a microsoft TechNet briefing on .NET and I about had a heart
attack before I even got in the door :-).
The registration staff people were all wearing T-Shirts.  On the front they
said "I'm a .NET enabled employee."  On the back it said "Only the strong
survive - COBOL"
I honestly didn't think the day would ever come again (after MS dropped
their COBOL compiler years ago), that I would ever see the words Microsoft
and COBOL on the same t-shirt.
```

- **Date:** 2001-06-01T00:47:11-04:00

- **Message-ID:** `<3B171E4F.927D515C@arvotek.net>`

```
Volker Englisch wrote:
> 
> David Essex <dessex@arvotek.net> wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> make[1]: Leaving directory `/usr/src/tinycobol-0.53/cobpp' [...]

I think the problem her is that yacc is just a link to bison.

To confirm this just use the following command.

ls -l /usr/bin/*yacc*

To compile TC requires Berkeley's Yacc (also called byacc). 

Bison will not work.

Byacc usually is available with most distributions or can be found on
the net.



David Essex
```

## Thread: Problem with writing to file. — 6 msgs

### Problem with writing to file.

- **From:** "eRRaTiK" <regulate@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-01T05:22:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dEFR6.93671$ff.709036@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`

```
Here's the code:


        ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
        CONFIGURATION SECTION.
        SOURCE-COMPUTER. IBM-PC.

      *//----------------------------------------------------------->.*

       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.

      *//----------------------------------------------------------->.*

       FILE-CONTROL.
      */ define input-output file names.*

      */ output errors file.*
           SELECT ERROR-FILE ASSIGN TO  "ERRORS.RPT".

      *//----------------------------------------------------------->.*

       DATA DIVISION.

      *//----------------------------------------------------------->.*

       FILE SECTION.
       FD  ERROR-FILE
           DATA RECORD IS PRINT-LINE.
       01  PRINT-LINE           PIC X(80).

      *//----------------------------------------------------------->.*

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

      *//----------------------------------------------------------->.*

      * Writes the record and error message associated
      * with it.*

       01  DETAIL-LINE.
           03 RECORD-INFO       PIC X(45).
           03 REPORT-MSG        PIC X(35).

      *//----------------------------------------------------------->.*

        PROCEDURE DIVISION.

      *//----------------------------------------------------------->.*


      * if invalid type, writes error in Error file.*

       Y20-TYPE-ERROR.
           MOVE "Invalid Transaction Code" TO REPORT-MSG.
           DISPLAY DETAIL-LINE.
           PERFORM Z30-WRITE-ERROR-MESSAGE.
           ADD 1 TO ERROR-TOTAL.

      * writes the message into the error file.*

       Z30-WRITE-ERROR-MESSAGE.
           DISPLAY DETAIL-LINE.
           WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM DETAIL-LINE AFTER 1.

      */--------------------- END PROGRAM ---------------------------.*

....

problem is - went it prints the error report the head and tail comes out
fine, but the error message from the detail line is blank. I know that
particular section of code is being performed because my counter displays
the correct number of errors generated by the "ADD 1 TO ERROR-TOTAL."

what could be the problem?

Note: I've written the code in a windows text editor (eliminated all tabs),
and I'm running the program on a unix machine. Program compiles fine.
Everything (so far) works except the printing of that single error line.

Any suggestions?

eRRaTiK
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem with writing to file.

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-01T18:15:30+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B17CDB2.7BB912A@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<dEFR6.93671$ff.709036@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`

```
eRRaTiK wrote:
> 
>        FILE-CONTROL.
>            SELECT ERROR-FILE ASSIGN TO  "ERRORS.RPT".
>        [...]
>            WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM DETAIL-LINE AFTER 1.

> 
> problem is - went it prints the error report the head and tail comes out
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> what could be the problem?

Just a guess, but the default for ORGANIZATION is SEQUENTIAL.  There may
be no line terminators after each write so the 'file' is just a long
string of characters with no line feeds or carriage returns (the AFTER 1
is ignored).  When you look at the file with a text editor it looks like
there is only one line, try scrolling right.  When it is sent to the
printer the line overflows and is lost.

Or try ORGANISATION LINE SEQUENTIAL, or whatever it is for your system.
```

```

eRRaTiK

Just a guess, but the default for ORGANIZATION is SEQUENTIAL.  There may
be no line terminators after each write so the 'file' is just a long
string of characters with no line feeds or carriage returns (the AFTER 1
is ignored).  When you look at the file with a text editor it looks like
there is only one line, try scrolling right.  When it is sent to the
printer the line overflows and is lost.

Or try ORGANISATION LINE SEQUENTIAL, or whatever it is for your system.
```

<a id="t-3af121a993"></a>
## Thread: RePost of the code for imbedded blanks problem. — 2 this month / 13 total

_Thread started [2001-05](2001-05.md#t-3af121a993)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RePost of the code for imbedded blanks problem.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-01T07:35:06+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B17379A.1BC900FB@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<lH7R6.5283$rn5.262199@www.newsranger.com> <20010530112257.11788.00001667@ng-ft1.aol.com> <3B15F4E5.B5520B2@lea.mpsc.mot.com> <IBqR6.6799$rn5.318130@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> , Nestor Aragon says...
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> style is foreign to me.  

> This is not the case with my original
> code which is straightforward and utterly simple.

What is 'straightfoeward and simple' is entirely the same as 'what one
is used to'.  You have written spagetti code with the same single letter
(or few anyway) paragraph names and the same idiom for several decades
so it is easy for you to recognise at a glance.

Most others don't see 'straightforward and utterly simple' when they
look at your code, they don't 'recognise' it (unless they have been
forced to work on programs written in the 60s). They see intertwined
paragraphs and twisty little passages.  They see that the program is
scattered all over from top to bottom (it starts at the top and the STOP
RUN is the last statement).

They don't 'rewrite it just to eliminate GO TO', they rewrite it to
convert what they see as a twisted jumble of code into something they
can understand.

While you may have used W-Field-1 and -2, X1 and X2, in specific ways
for decades and so <<know>> what these are, noone else has this brain
pattern hard wired in and so they need to refer back to determine what
these are.  They may even have to notate these mentally to translate
them into names that they use regularly.

The way that they try to understand your code is to rearrange it into
something that they do recognise and with it arranged into parts that
define the overall logic and parts that do the detail work instead of
how you wrote it with these intermixed.

Of course this means that you no longer see it the same way and thus you
think that it is more complicated.  As you say, it is utterly foreign to
you.

 
> In the trivial example here, there is nothing wrong with GO TO.

Most programmers don't do much work with trivial examples so they tend
to use the same idiom for examples and for real programs.  This is
because they recognise the code better if it follows some structure and
also uses meaningful names.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RePost of the code for imbedded blanks problem.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-01T17:11:02+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B17BE96.613B1C02@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<lH7R6.5283$rn5.262199@www.newsranger.com> <20010530112257.11788.00001667@ng-ft1.aol.com> <3B15F4E5.B5520B2@lea.mpsc.mot.com> <IBqR6.6799$rn5.318130@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
> 
> In the trivial example here, there is nothing wrong with GO TO.

There is never anything wrong with GO TO.  Where the 'wrongness' lies is
at the label where the GO TO goes.

That is, if one is examining a program written elsewhere it is necessary
to start by understanding it in small pieces.  As each piece is
understood these can be assembled into a whole so that eventually the
whole process can be understood.  It can then be changed as required.

If in examining one piece you come across a GO TO then this can be
readily understood.  It is very clear why it is being actioned and how
the program logic flows around it.

However, when one is examining a piece of code of a program that has GO
TOs and PERFORMs and there is a _label_ then it is impossible to know
how the logic flows around that piece of code without examining the
_whole_ program.

That label may be the target of a PERFORM, or a PERFORM may end at that
point, or it may be the target of a GO TO.  The flow may stop at that
label, or it may start at the label or it may flow through the label. 
It cannot be known without understanding the _whole_ program.

Thus the presence of GO TOs means that the program _cannot_ be
understood in pieces (without having prior knowledge of it as you do).

Now the original programmer (you for example) will already know the
whole program, especially if it is the same type of program they have
been writing for several decades.  But using structured programming is
_not_ about writing for the benefit of the writer, it is about the
benefit of the 'next guy'.

By restricting the types of coding used, such as 'no GO TO, no THRU, no
SECTIONs', and ensuring this by checking source code with, say, grep
then it is possible to understand code much more readily.  For example
it is only necessary to check that the first paragraph contains a
reachable STOP RUN to _know_ that the logic flow around each label can
only be that the paragraph above ends at the label (returning to the
PERFORM) and the logic flow starts here for each reference to this
label.

This type of restriction means that every paragraph can be examined
completely in isolation without having to know the complete program.

Other types of restriction can also work, such as 'only PERFORM SECTION
and GO TO Exit', but this does require more complex checking such as
making sure the GO TOs are to the correct exit point and that SECTION
keywords are correctly placed.

You don't seem to notice why others find this important. I can only
assume that you don't have to work on code written by others.  When you
do examine some other code you probably get quite angry and yell 'why
can't this programmer write programs like mine so that I can understand
it'.

While trivial examples can always be understood easily because the
'whole thing' can be grasped at one time, the style does not scale at
all well.
```

<a id="t-d379f3a775"></a>
## Thread: International Vote on COBOL Final Committee Draft — 5 this month / 9 total

_Thread started [2001-05](2001-05.md#t-d379f3a775)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: International Vote on COBOL Final Committee Draft

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-01T11:36:33-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9f8g9r$fur$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<4145699b.0105311326.ed32907@posting.google.com> <3B16D8D4.16B71799@home.com> <4145699b.0105312046.30d8473c@posting.google.com>`

```
1) J4 has been waiting almost a year for the FIRST draft of the Technical
Report on Finalizer - and it still isn't available.  (There was a "proposal"
when it was thought that it MIGHT still be added to the FCD, but the format
of that and a TR are significantly different.  The letter ballot on that
proposal failed.) It is expected that there will be one OR TWO public
reviews of it - once J4 finishes with it.  Therefore, the FASTEST that it
could be out is about 3 years.

2) Work has not really STARTED on the "Standard Class Library" Technical
report.  There was an "old" one that was pulled in '97 - but during
discussion at J4 it was decided that both CONTENT and PRESENTATION needed to
be reviewed.  (There isn't even a "work assignment" from ISO yet to
officially START work on it.  Although J4 can work on it before this exists,
that does put it SEVERAL years out for completion).

3) Technical Reports and Amendments are COMPLETELY different in that
Technical Reports are explicitly *not* Standards and no "upward
compatibility" is expected and it isn't even clear (at the time they are
developed) whether or not they will EVER be included in an official
Standard.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: International Vote on COBOL Final Committee Draft

- **From:** "James. J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-02T03:26:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B185D88.A24CEFEE@home.com>`
- **References:** `<4145699b.0105311326.ed32907@posting.google.com> <3B16D8D4.16B71799@home.com> <4145699b.0105312046.30d8473c@posting.google.com>`

```


Stephen J Spiro wrote:

> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote
> >
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://groups.google.com/groups?hl=en&lr=&safe=off&th=1601ec8d3174541b,12&ic=1&seekd=1165806355#p

Stephen,

OK, I know you wrote what you did with sincerity, same as I respect the enthusiasm of Chuck and Thane
as newcomers to the J4 table but.....

Agreed the introduction of the Intrinsic Functions (COBOL 97 ?) were SIGNIFICANT - wish I'd had
square root about 20 years ago <G>. This 'ex-Brit' doesn't think the 'Brits' were unfair and their
criticism wasn't in harsh words. It's the same ISO Brits who commented, 'we need standard
classes/collections (portability) otherwise vendors will do their own thing.....'.

Yes you are almost there on Finalizer - but that wasn't what I mentioned.

As you have said, you are the new boy on the block regarding OO. I'm no genius, just happen to be
somebody who is using it and a LONGgggggg....  way to go before I could ever claim to be an expert.

Yes you did have a document on OO classes, in 1993 - and now it is basically useless - I don't know
about Visual Age or Fujitsu but it bears no relationship to the currently WORKING classes available
in Net Express, (and I exclude their GUI classes which are specific to their product). Last I read
about J4 was 'a huntin' we will go' to see what is available in other languages. I would suggest to
you this could turn out to be a task as big as all the other enhancements you have already made to
the basic language. Don't believe me - ask Don. S.  for a copy of the Net Express help file which
lists all the classes and methods. Excluding the GUI classes, there are literally HUNDREDS of
possible methods. Then get a copy of the Fujitsu classes. Take your original list ('93), Net Express
and Fujitsu - and you will have a clearer idea of what I'm driving at.

Couple of months back Anne suggested I submit my ideas about collections - this is a HUGE task for a
loner. However, for Chuck's info I am more than prepared to contribute comments on classes, ONCE J4
comes up with a semblance of a list. I should also point out that as a result of Bill 'promoting'
here in CLC,  Ken Foskey and I 'offered' our services to Mr. J4, over TWO YEARS AGO  - polite
acknowledgment and Sweet FA since - not even a copy of that '93 document as background to what had
been going on. (I only became aware of the '93 document as a result of Bill resurrecting it a couple
of months ago).

Believe me 'the standard class library is not nearly finished', it hasn't yet even been started.

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: International Vote on COBOL Final Committee Draft

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-01T11:15:11-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9f8m3i$eh5$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<4145699b.0105311326.ed32907@posting.google.com>`

```
Stephen J Spiro <stephenjspiro@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:4145699b.0105311326.ed32907@posting.google.com...

> ... It  would appear that Australia did not send in a comment. ...

I'm pretty sure it would have been inappropriate for them to do so.

According to information available from http://www.iso.ch , there are twenty
national standards bodies that are participating members in the activities
of ISO/IEC JTC1/SC22, and twenty-five at the JTC1 level.

SAI (Australia) is one of several national standards bodies who are
participating members in JTC1, but apparently do not choose to be
participating members of (or even observers at) the SC22 subcommittee of
that organization.

Formal submission of ballots with comments regarding the transition from FCD
status to DIS status is invited from those national standards bodies who
have chosen to participate in the process; apparently, SAI (Australia) did
not choose to be a participating member of SC22 and thus would have had no
reason to submit a ballot on an issue being decided by that subcommittee.


For those who are interested in such matters, here's an expanded list of who
is and who is not on which committees:

SAI (Australia) has already been discussed; the other national standards
bodies who are full members of JTC1 but do not participate (either as
members or as observers) in SC22 are CSK (Democratic People's Republic of
Korea), NSAI (Ireland), SABS (South Africa) and SNV (Switzerland).

ASRO (Romania), DSTU (Ukraine),  EOS (Egypt), GOST R (Russian Federation)
and ON (Austria) are observers at the JTC1 level but full participating
members of SC22.

IPQ (Portugal), KATS (Republic of Korea), SIS (Sweden), SNZ (New Zealand)
and UNI (Italy) are listed as participating member organizations in JTC1 but
observers on SC22.

ABNT (Brazil), AFNOR (France), ANSI (United States), BSI (United Kingdom),
CSBTS (China), CSNI (Czech Republic), DIN (Germany), IBM (Belgium), JISC
(Japan), NEN (Netherlands), NSF (Norway), SCC (Canada), SFGS (Finland) and
SMIS (Slovenia) are all full participating members of both bodies.

  -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: From the dustbin, Cobol rises (or C doesn't float?)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Editor" <Editor@aboutlegacycoding.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-01T10:12:04-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9f87rl$md5$1@allthetime.news.cais.net>`
- **References:** `<3B1520D2.651DEE90@brazee.net> <WxiR6.17807$v4.250564@e420r-atl4.usenetserver.com> <3B16A9FB.85A762F1@ont.com> <4145699b.0105311335.5a25d89f@posting.google.com>`

```
I also think there is a different "Culture" here.
COBOL programmers come from an era where there was some loyalty between
company and employee.  My father worked for the same company for 47 years.
I've never worked for the same company for more than 3.

My point is, I think the "churn" or turnover rate is MUCH higher among C++,
Java, Internet Technology type employees than Legacy people.  During the
Internet BOOM when they were in such short supply, they learned to listen
each time a headhunter called and change jobs if they were offered even a
few thousand more per year.  Companies learned that there was no loyalty
anymore and that they had to pay dearly and expect high churn.  Where
companies had both types of technology in a singe IT shop there was frequent
culture shock and anger.  I know one local company where COBOL contractors
with years of business and technical experience get $65 per hour and C++
kids straight out of College get $125.

It's going to take a while for the market to "normalize", and salaries come
back in line, but I honestly think that COBOL programmers may be making MORE
than C++ programmers in about 5 years.  Legacy Technologist just need to
learn the value of their skills and demand equal compensation with other
technologies.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: From the dustbin, Cobol rises (or C doesn't float?)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James. J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-02T04:54:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B18722F.B631AD1E@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3B1520D2.651DEE90@brazee.net> <WxiR6.17807$v4.250564@e420r-atl4.usenetserver.com> <3B16A9FB.85A762F1@ont.com> <4145699b.0105311335.5a25d89f@posting.google.com> <9f87rl$md5$1@allthetime.news.cais.net>`

```


Editor wrote:

Makes sense what you wrote Buddy - and yes I can see your dad working for the
same outfit. But you zeroed in on the 'young employees' loyalty. There is
another side to the coin - "corporate loyalty to their employees'.

Certainly through the last century both in N. America and Europe, as a result of
the 'Dirty Thirties' employees were thankful to have a job, ANY job - and stuck
to it. OK so even since the mid 1800's there have been a few ruthless business
magnets, but generally speaking business tended to be conducted in a genteel
manner. Even with the upheaval of WWII that persisted through I would suggest to
the 1960s, 1970s.

Then commerce took on an aggressive streak. As an example of something which
made me swallow hard on first introduction, not necessarily the product, but an
example - A body comes up the TV screen with three shaving lathers; he's
promoting Gillette. He arrogantly brushes aside products X and Y - message, 'Use
Gillette, it's the best'.

That same thinking has permeated business - coupled with "It is the shareholders
who count - keep them happy and to hell with the employees".

Back in the '80s when it got rough in the oil game, 10,000 lost their jobs here
in Cowtown - Chief Execs to the lowliest. Nothing unusual for a VP to come in on
the Monday morning, somebody waiting at his desk to ensure he doesn't take any
confidential papers, and 'Yes please. Hand over the keys to your company car. We
have to let you go (fired)".

Given the aggressive take-over/merger game, certainly in N. America - the same
mindset persists. You can probably illustrate with parallels in the States but
let's take a scenario here in Calgary. Two to four smart engineers start up a
new oil company - make sure the Article of Association ensure their get a HUGE
golden handshake if they ever get the heave-ho. Eventually they do, game has
changed, their outfit has become ripe for a takeover. They leave, smiling all
over their chops and do another startup. Meanwhile those lowly
employees..........- faced with the aggravation of a merger and playing to a new
piper, or perhaps they get dumped as being no longer required.

Seeing what happened to their elders, small wonder the young don't have
'corporate loyalty' - and I don't think it is something that is going to turn
around anytime soon.
I think an 'elder' would be dishonest if he didn't advise, "Move on kid, try
something new and expand your horizons - or eventually get screwed if you stay
put".

Jimmy

> I also think there is a different "Culture" here.
> COBOL programmers come from an era where there was some loyalty between
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
> > the ads do not run as long.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: From the dustbin, Cobol rises (or C doesn't float?)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2001-06-02T10:04:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010602060450.01421.00002413@ng-fb1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3B18722F.B631AD1E@home.com>`

```
>From: "James. J. Gavan" jjgavan@home.com 
>Date: 2001-06-02 00:54 Eastern Daylight Time
[snip lots of gloomy but true stuff]
>I think an 'elder' would be dishonest if he didn't advise, "Move on 
>kid, try something new and expand your horizons - or eventually 
>get screwed if you stay put".

Having personally and recently experienced some of this 
dislocation--my intended "life-time" employer suddenly 
disappeared in a puff of smoke after over a hundred years in 
business, and my next was acquired (or was it pillaged)--I
don't think either employee or employer has much choice.
Loyalty presupposes stability.  An employee trying to be
loyal to a company, or a company trying to be loyal to an
employee, is like someone trying to be loyal to a robin.  No
matter how much you like the robin, you know that when the 
weather turns cold, the robin has to leave town or die.

The higher churning rate in OO programmers has created
a slight problem in business.  There are much fewer staff
programmers who understand the business of their
employer.  A language like "C" is so difficult to code that all 
an employer can hope for is to find someone that knows
the language.  No one would presume to look for a
C programmer who knows accounts receivable aging, or
a Java programmer who understands standard variable
cost.  


Ross
http://www.geocities.com/ross_klatte/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: From the dustbin, Cobol rises (or C doesn't float?)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-02T11:30:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<P74S6.95$zG.42286@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<3B18722F.B631AD1E@home.com> <20010602060450.01421.00002413@ng-fb1.aol.com>`

```
Ross Klatte <klatteross@aol.commmm> wrote in message
news:20010602060450.01421.00002413@ng-fb1.aol.com...
>
> The higher churning rate in OO programmers has created
> a slight problem in business.

Higher than the churning rate than what? Other types of programmers? I think
not. Programmers and other IT types have for a long time had a higher
turnover rate than, say, machinists or drivers; but not so much higher (if
at all) than than "entry-level, non-IT" posisitons. (This last is anecdotal,
not empirical).

> There are much fewer staff programmers who understand the business of
their
> employer.  A language like "C" is so difficult to code that all
> an employer can hope for is to find someone that knows
> the language.  No one would presume to look for a
> C programmer who knows accounts receivable aging, or
> a Java programmer who understands standard variable cost.

Again, there has always been a shortage of P/As who are skilled in both a
specific language and business acumen. This is why I regularly respond to
queries here from those just starting an IT career that they try to spend
some time actually doing the non-IT jobs for their employers or somewhere in
their chosen industry. Those jobs represent the real users  of the software
to be developed: how better to create a useful product than actually
suffering the frustration of poor software AS A USER?

What they don't seem to teach in the CompSci disciplines is the fundamental
fact that Information Technology does not exist in a vacuum: it exists to
support businesses.
```

_(thread depth: 8)_

I'll tell you one thing......as far as I'm concerned, if I could find a company
that was loyal to me, treated me well, and provided me with challenge and growth
opportunities for the rest of my career, I'd be just as loyal to them!   Loyalty
is never a one way street.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: From the dustbin, Cobol rises (or C doesn't float?)

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Editor" <Editor@aboutlegacycoding.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-06T09:41:13-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9flbt6$29mo$1@allgood.news.cais.net>`
- **References:** `<3B18722F.B631AD1E@home.com> <20010602060450.01421.00002413@ng-fb1.aol.com> <P74S6.95$zG.42286@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3B1BE41B.C4EB764B@mescoma.com>`

```
David,
I was lucky enough to find 3 companies to whom I could be loyal in my 30+
year career so far.
And all three went out of business - probably because they weren't as brutal
as their competitors.
It's a no-win situation any more.
Find a good company, with a good product that is loyal to their employees
even in hard times, and they go under.
Find a company with management that knows how to survive in today's economy
and they make the hard decisions and cut you the minute things get tight.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: From the dustbin, Cobol rises (or C doesn't float?)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Editor" <Editor@aboutlegacycoding.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-04T15:23:18-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9fgn7b$uh2$1@allthetime.news.cais.net>`
- **References:** `<3B18722F.B631AD1E@home.com> <20010602060450.01421.00002413@ng-fb1.aol.com>`

```
I love the "robin" analogy - it fits, but how you came to apply that to
programmers is beyond me :-).

In my post about this I think I pointed out that loyalty is mutual.  And
yes, the business world is changing.  I've been in .com businesses the last
7 years and I've shut three of them down now.  They were businesses that
probably shouldn't have existed anyway - they never had viable buisiness
models.
But know what - that company my Dad worked for for so long - went bankrupt
and died several years after his death.  No . . it didn't die because he
wasn't there anymore (although that's a nice thought for a son to have :-),
it died because it couldn't adjust to the changes in the retail clothing
business.

But I still think my analogy stands.  The BIG OLD IT departments of major
corporations were always bastions.  We used to talk of the Priesthood of
DataProcessing.  The idea that we professioals intentionally kept the ritual
secret from managment.  In the "old days" most large companies would lay off
1/2 the rest of the workforce before they would touch anybody in IT.
Nowdays everybody is more technically savy - or at least thinks they are.
So Upper Management is more comfortable cutting IT, even mainframe IT, when
times are hard.  And I know there are always exceptions both ways.  Average
length of employment at my current company is over 8 years and we have a lot
of people who have been here 20 years.

So I still think there is a legacy of expectaions here - COBOL programmers
"expect" to stay at a job longer.  And Large Shop IT managers "expect" to
keep them longer.  I'm sure that a lot of that is just a holdover and
reality is changing rapidly, but for now the overall churn is still less.
```

---

<a id="t-55d3c0338e"></a>
## Thread: Validation Question — 12 msgs

### Validation Question

- **From:** "eRRaTiK" <regulate@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-01T14:09:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SmNR6.94963$ff.719687@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`

```
Objective:

W-COURSE must be 1 numerical character followed by 3 alphabetic characters.
I want to validate each entry to ensure it follows the requirement for a
course code.

The source code:

----------------------------------------------------------------

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

       01  W-COURSE.
           03 W-C1              PIC X(1).
           03 W-C2              PIC X(3).

       01  VALID-COURSE         PIC X(2).

      * .
      * . more code here...
      * .

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.

       F16-CHECK-COURSE.
           MOVE "N" TO VALID-COURSE.
           IF (W-C1 IS NUMERIC) AND (W-C2 IS ALPHABETIC)
              MOVE "Y" TO VALID-COURSE
           ELSE
              DISPLAY "COURSE CODE INVALID!"
           END-IF.


----------------------------------------------------------------

Problem:

When I run the above check it doesn't work. All the courses, even incorrect
ones, are determined to be valid.

What's wrong, and how can I fix it?
```

#### ↳ Re: Validation Question

- **From:** Robaire <rmiller@linux.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-06-01T12:01:07-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c1ffhtolfkv1e1khpbiq81dmgrc7dou2em@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<SmNR6.94963$ff.719687@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`

```
Le Fri, 01 Jun 2001 14:09:54 GMT, "eRRaTiK" <regulate@hotmail.com> a
ï¿½crit:

>Objective:
>
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
>What's wrong, and how can I fix it?

1. Could you post the PROCEDURE DIVISION instructions that you use to
move the "course code" into W-COURSE, and the instructions that test
the value of VALID-COURSE.

2. For what reason is VALID-COURSE defined as PIC X(2) ?
In the code segment you supplied, VALID-COURSE is assigned only
1-character long literals ( "Y" and "N" ).

Robaire
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Validation Question

- **From:** "eRRaTiK" <regulate@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-01T20:36:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<K1TR6.95160$ff.722396@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`
- **References:** `<SmNR6.94963$ff.719687@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <c1ffhtolfkv1e1khpbiq81dmgrc7dou2em@4ax.com>`

```
As requested...

============================================================================
====

      *//----------------------------------------------------------->.*

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

      *//----------------------------------------------------------->.*

      * switches.*
       01  WSTU-STAT            PIC X(2).
       01  EOFSW                PIC X(2).
       01  EXISTSW              PIC X(2).
       01  EXEMPT-POINTS        PIC 9(1).
       01  VALID-COURSE         PIC X(1).


      * Writes the record and error message associated
      * with it.*

       01  DETAIL-LINE.
           03 RECORD-INFO       PIC X(45).
           03 REPORT-MSG        PIC X(35).

      * transaction record.*

       01  TRANSACTION-RECORD.
           03 T-ID              PIC X(8).
           03 T-TYPE            PIC X(1).
           03 T-THIRD           PIC X(20).
           03 T-FOURTH.
              05 T-AMT          PIC 9(6)V99.
              05 T-AMTX REDEFINES T-AMT PIC X(8).
              05 FILLER         PIC X(6).
           03 T-FIFTH           PIC X(20).

       01  W-COURSE.
           03 W-C1              PIC X(1).
           03 W-C2              PIC X(3).


      *//----------------------------------------------------------->.*

        PROCEDURE DIVISION.

      *//----------------------------------------------------------->.*

       A10-BEGIN-MAIN.
           PERFORM A20-OPENING-PROCEDURE.
           PERFORM A30-MAIN-PROCESS.
           PERFORM A40-CLOSING-PROCEDURE.

       A20-OPENING-PROCEDURE.
      * OPEN-FILES.*
           OPEN INPUT CHANGES-FILE.
           OPEN OUTPUT ERROR-FILE.

      * BATCH UPDATE PROCEDURE.*

       A30-MAIN-PROCESS.
           MOVE SPACES TO DETAIL-LINE.
           ACCEPT RUNDATE FROM DATE.
           PERFORM X10-PRINT-HEADING.
           READ CHANGES-FILE AT END DISPLAY "**END OF FILE**" STOP RUN.
           PERFORM B10-PROCESS-TRANSACTIONS UNTIL EOFSW = "Y".
           PERFORM X20-PRINT-TAIL.

       A40-CLOSING-PROCEDURE.
           CLOSE CHANGES-FILE ERROR-FILE.

       A50-EXIT-PROGRAM.
           STOP RUN.

       B10-PROCESS-TRANSACTIONS.
      *                                               .. handle this.*
           MOVE CHANGE-RECORD TO RECORD-INFO.
           PERFORM C10-UNSTRING-INPUT.
           MOVE T-ID TO STUD-ID.
           DISPLAY "CURRENT ACTION: " T-TYPE.
           PERFORM E10-EVALUATE-ACTION.
      *                                               .. get next.*
           READ CHANGES-FILE AT END MOVE "Y" TO EOFSW.

      * Unstrings the input records.*

       C10-UNSTRING-INPUT.
           MOVE SPACES TO TRANSACTION-RECORD.
           UNSTRING CHANGE-RECORD DELIMITED BY ","
               INTO T-ID T-TYPE T-THIRD T-FOURTH T-FIFTH.

      * VALIDATE student id, then perform insertion or update.*

       D10-CHECK-EXISTS.
           READ STUDENT-FILE
              INVALID KEY
                  PERFORM F15-CONTINUE-ADDING
              NOT INVALID KEY
                  PERFORM Y80-ALREADY-THERE
           END-READ.

      * Perform action based on code.*

       E10-EVALUATE-ACTION.
           EVALUATE T-TYPE
              WHEN "A"     PERFORM F10-NEW-RECORD
      *        WHEN "F"     PERFORM F20-CHANGE-FAMILY-NAME
      *        WHEN "G"     PERFORM F30-CHANGE-GIVEN-NAME
      *        WHEN "L"     PERFORM F40-MODIFY-LOAN
      *        WHEN "S"     PERFORM F50-CHANGE-STATUS
      *        WHEN "V"     PERFORM F60-VOID-RECORD
      *        WHEN "E"     PERFORM G10-ADD-STUD-PTS
      *.*
              WHEN OTHER   PERFORM Y20-TYPE-ERROR
           END-EVALUATE.

      * Depending on the next code, determines what to do.*

       E20-CHECK-CHANGES.
           EVALUATE TRUE
              WHEN T-TYPE = "A" PERFORM F10-NEW-RECORD
              WHEN T-TYPE = "F" OR "G" OR "L" OR "S" OR "V"
                PERFORM Y30-ERROR-NO-STUDENT
              WHEN OTHER   PERFORM Y20-TYPE-ERROR
           END-EVALUATE.

      * inserts a record into the system.*

       F10-NEW-RECORD.
           OPEN I-O STUDENT-FILE.
           PERFORM D10-CHECK-EXISTS.

       F15-CONTINUE-ADDING.
           MOVE T-FIFTH TO W-COURSE.
           DISPLAY W-COURSE WITH NO ADVANCING.
      *     PERFORM F16-CHECK-COURSE.*
           MOVE "Y" TO VALID-COURSE.
           DISPLAY VALID-COURSE.
           IF VALID-COURSE = "Y"
              DISPLAY "VALID COURSE.. ADDING RECORD.."
              MOVE T-ID TO STUD-ID
              MOVE T-THIRD  TO STUD-FAMNAME
              MOVE T-FOURTH TO STUD-GIVNAME
              MOVE T-FIFTH TO STUD-COURSE
              MOVE "C" TO STUD-STATUS
      *                                             ... write record.*
              WRITE STUDENT-DETAILS
                 INVALID KEY
                    PERFORM Y90-ADD-ERROR-MESSAGE
                 NOT INVALID KEY
                    DISPLAY "Student " STUD-ID " Details Added "
              END-WRITE
           ELSE
              PERFORM Y50-COURSE-ERROR
           END-IF.
           CLOSE STUDENT-FILE.


       F16-CHECK-COURSE.
           MOVE "N" TO VALID-COURSE.
           IF (W-C1 NUMERIC) AND (W-C2 ALPHABETIC)
              MOVE "Y" TO VALID-COURSE.


============================================================================
====


"Robaire" <rmiller@linux.ca> wrote in message
news:c1ffhtolfkv1e1khpbiq81dmgrc7dou2em@4ax.com...

�crit:

VALID-COURSE (or at least I thought they were).

In the code segment you supplied, VALID-COURSE is assigned only

Can you please also verify if  E20-CHECK-CHANGES will work as intended?

Regards,
eRRaTiK

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Validation Question

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-01T15:00:54-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B180286.2BAB3A07@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<SmNR6.94963$ff.719687@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <c1ffhtolfkv1e1khpbiq81dmgrc7dou2em@4ax.com> <K1TR6.95160$ff.722396@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`

```


eRRaTiK wrote:

>        F16-CHECK-COURSE.
>            MOVE "N" TO VALID-COURSE.
>            IF (W-C1 NUMERIC) AND (W-C2 ALPHABETIC)
>               MOVE "Y" TO VALID-COURSE.

But anyway, if you don't have an on-line debugger, add the following lines after
the above code:

DISPLAY 'DEBUG F16-Check found "' VALID-COURSE '"'
DISPLAY '       W-C1 = "' W-C1 '", W-C2 = "' W-C2 '"'.

Put in all of the quotes to make sure you can count spaces.

If you don't get displays, then you don't perform the test.  (as when the
perform is commented out).  If you get the displays, show us what you got.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Validation Question

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-01T18:05:50-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9f9743$9qi$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<SmNR6.94963$ff.719687@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <c1ffhtolfkv1e1khpbiq81dmgrc7dou2em@4ax.com> <K1TR6.95160$ff.722396@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`

```
I have *not* tested (or run) this but a quick view seems to show TWO
problems:

1) The only time that you PERFORM
     F16-CHECK-COURSE
is in the middle of
    F15-CONTINUE-ADDING.
*but* you have COMMENTED out that perform statement - so it will NEVER get
executed

2) The paragraph
     F10-NEW-RECORD.
seems "odd" to me - in that you OPEN this file for EVERY write statement
(and do NOT close it after each record).  You are not (yet) checking the
file status codes after all of your I/O statements so the system MIGHT be
trying to "tell" you something that you aren't listening to.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Validation Question

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-06-01T18:34:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0106011734.32fe806a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<SmNR6.94963$ff.719687@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <c1ffhtolfkv1e1khpbiq81dmgrc7dou2em@4ax.com> <K1TR6.95160$ff.722396@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`

```
"eRRaTiK" <regulate@hotmail.com> wrote 
> As requested...
> 
<snip>
> 
>        F15-CONTINUE-ADDING.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>            MOVE "Y" TO VALID-COURSE.

Please note that the line 
       *     PERFORM F16-CHECK-COURSE.*
is COMMENTED OUT!  You never perform the check! And even if it
weren't, you would be moving "Y" to VALID-COURSE no matter what
happened in F16-CHECK-COURSE!


> 
>        F16-CHECK-COURSE.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
It's SOOOOOO easy to miss these things. That's why structured
walkthru's are so valuable.

Stephen J Spiro
```

<a id="t-db0f582c7c"></a>
## Thread: AcuCOBOL data conversion — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### AcuCOBOL data conversion

- **From:** "Milton Bradley" <nospam@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-01T10:45:49-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9f8dh8$o4$1@news.chorus.net>`

```
Hello,

I am looking into the possibility of converting some legacy data from an
AIX/RS6000 system written in AcuCOBOL circa early 90's. Is there a way to
convert this data without source code or data definitions? A solution that
produces raw ASCII where I have to determine the columns would be
acceptable.

Another strategy I have been tossing around is to run a report from the
application to a file and then strip out the needed data. Is it
possible/easy to redirect a report to a file instead of a printer in AIX?
Thanks in advance...

Linda G
```

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-01T08:59:40-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B17BBEC.6148@paralynx.com>`
- **References:** `<9f8dh8$o4$1@news.chorus.net>`

```
Milton Bradley wrote:
> 
> Hello,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> acceptable.

Take a look at ParseRat (http://www.guysoftware.com/parserat.htm).
```

#### ↳ Re: AcuCOBOL data conversion

- **From:** "Ray Smith" <Ray.Smith@fujitsu.com.au>
- **Date:** 2001-06-02T16:58:35+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9f9uut$jq5@newshost.fujitsu.com.au>`
- **References:** `<9f8dh8$o4$1@news.chorus.net>`

```
Hi,

I don't know what version of data files you have but we use the newer
versions of AcuCOBOL
and it comes with a utility called "vutil" which can (amoung other things)
unload a data file to a flat
text file.

You might also consider contacting AcuCorp for advise?

Ray Smith.

#### ↳ Re: AcuCOBOL data conversion

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik-nws@siber.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-09T00:49:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B217302.117B5CD7@siber.com>`

```
We have a converter from AcuCobol data files to 
flat files (CSV) or DDBF files.

Copybooks for the files are desirable
but they are not required.

More here: http://www.siber.com/sct/datafile/

Vadim Maslov

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Portability

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Gene Merriman" <genem@progeni.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-01T12:02:56-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9f8efn$2cqe$1@allbad.news.cais.net>`
- **References:** `<9f3pll$3lb$1@allbad.news.cais.net> <20010530195116.21890.00001206@nso-cq.aol.com> <3b159baa_3@Usenet.com>`

```
Peter, I agree with your comments relative to the benefits to be derived
from a 4GL tool. As far as code structure goes, I feel that the COBOL code
produced by Progeni's 1Spec product is relatively clean and readable code
and produced in the classic structured format to enhance readability, if you
are inclined to do so at that level. The true benefits of a 4GL are realized
in the time required to develop applications and, the subsequent maintenance
requirements using a 4GL. There is significantly less code required to write
and maintain those apps making it much easier to get "one's mind around and
into" the real issue(s) being addressed.


Gene Merriman
The Progeni Corporation
"Envision the Possibilities"
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3b159baa_3@Usenet.com...

to

> COBOL
> > >program or you can write an entire application in it.  If you write in
> 1Spec
> > >(it's very much a COBOL like syntax) you get almost a 30 to 1
expansion -

a

languages.

…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> generated by our COBOL compiler (unless performance is suffering). Isn't
it

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Portability

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Gene Merriman" <genem@progeni.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-01T11:50:53-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9f8dp7$taj$1@allday.news.cais.net>`
- **References:** `<4145699b.0105201411.6975898b@posting.google.com> <3B0887BE.3FEC177A@soltec.net> <4145699b.0105211713.249bc948@posting.google.com> <9ecp67$ece$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3B0DC404.A7E7FC61@optonline.net> <9f3pll$3lb$1@allbad.news.cais.net>`

Thanks, Buddy for the kind remarks relative to our 1Spec product. It indeed
does provide for platform portability. As a member of the marketing team
here at Progeni, I find the comments of "Sff5ky" disturbing from several
perspectives. The first observation that I have is that the 1Spec product is
only 3 years old! Using it for the past 8 years would have not been
possible. Maybe the user was using a very early iteration of the the
products' development and even then, it produced pretty clean code! The
second observation being that while we are a small firm, we maintain
excellent relations with our client  base and have never experienced
negative feedback to the level suggested by "Sff5ky" . I would be most
interested in learning who those firms are where he/she experienced the
1Spec product. I understand that there might be a product in the market with
a similiar name ( Prodigy ) but personally, I have never run across them.

Gene Merriman
The Progeni Corporation
"Envision the Possibilities"

"Editor" <Editor@aboutlegacycoding.com> wrote in message
news:9f3pll$3lb$1@allbad.news.cais.net...
> If you are seriously interested in COBOL portablility, you have to check
out
> 1Spec by The Progeni Corporation www.progeni.com .  I guess you would call
> this product a "pre processor" but they call it a "natural extention" to
> COBOL.  In any event, 1Spec "verbs" can be added inline to an existing
COBOL
> program or you can write an entire application in it.  If you write in
1Spec
> (it's very much a COBOL like syntax) you get almost a 30 to 1 expansion -
> ie. 100 lines of 1Spec will generate 300 lines of COBOL.  It generates
nice
> clean, formated COBOL for a very wide range of compilers, platforms and
> screens.  For example, from the same "Spec" you can generate COBOL for a
> Unisys A series Mainframe with a green screen interface, or an IBM 390
with
> a Windows GUI interface or Web Interface, or you can generate for
MicroFocus
> or other Micro Compilers for NT, Unix, etc. and they will all run
> identically.
>
> It not only makes portability easy, it drastically increases efficiency in
> maintaining and writing COBOL code.  And because Web enablement is built
in,
> it also makes taking COBOL to the web pretty easy.
>
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

### Re: Australian comments

```

word of feedback on my comments. 

Tim Josling

PS cc. to comp.lang.cobol hope that's OK.

> Tim,

```

#### ↳ Re: Australian comments

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-01T16:02:33-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9f96u7$1ue$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<MABBIHGFPKHEHJLAPIPPMEFPCJAA.wmklein@ix.netcom.com> <3B17FB5B.159777B2@melbpc.org.au>`

```
Tim Josling quotes Bill Klein:

<<It looks (to me) as if Australia is not (currently) a "P" member of SC22.
However, it should have (???) been possible for "O" countries to submit
comments, but not vote.>>

According to the ISO web page, NSAI (Ireland) was neither a member nor an
observer on SC22, yet their vote was considered, so the reverse of this
assertion may well be true -- it may be possible to submit a *vote* as a
non-member, and the convener may elect to include that standards body's
input in the determination of consensus.

Whether it "should have been possible for "O" countries to submit comments,
but not vote" is a red herring.  SAI (Australia) is NOT an "O" member of
SC22.  For that matter, neither is NSAI (Ireland).

Moreover, what's being established is whether to move from FCD to DIS, the
issue is *consensus* on that subject, and comments are an integral part of
*votes* (which themselves are guides to the convener, "straw votes", to
judge whether consensus has been reached).   This is a fundamentally YES/NO
point in the process.

There are comment stages before and after it, and comments may accompany
those YES/NO indications, but comments *without* a vote aren't allowed for
*here*, nor should they be procedurally, whether those "no-vote" comments
come from "P"-members, from "O"-members, or from sources outside the
participatory membership altogether.

Tim also opined:

<<I suspected as much. I am starting to come around to your view that the
process is broken. Standards Australia never sent one word of feedback on my
comments. >>

That's a shame; I am personally convinced that what you have to say on these
issues is worth careful consideration.  But I must point out that, so far as
I know, neither ISO, IEC, JTC1, SC22, WG4, ANSI, NCITS, J4 nor J4-TAG have
anything at all to say about what procedures SAI (or any other national
standards body) uses to decide a national position -- or in fact whether SAI
(or any other national standards body) chooses to participate in the
development of that position at all.   That's between you and SAI, and maybe
the best way to address those procedures -- or the lack of response from
SAI -- is to get off the sidelines and work actively to help the process
along.

Bill wrote:

<<*NONE* of your comments are included in the international comments -
> viewable at:
>
>  http://people.ne.mediaone.net/doncobol/01-0373.pdf

I think that's understandable given SAI's non-membership in SC22, and that
comments are only one part of a ballot in this context.  SAI doesn't seem to
have chosen to be eligible to submit a response to this request that would
have included such comments.

Tim, Don't get me wrong, I'm not saying that your comments shouldn't have
been responded to, or included in an SAI response submitted as part of their
activities as members of JTC1 and/or SC22.  But I'm not at all convinced the
problem has its origins anywhere beyond the borders of Australia in this
particular case, or that the solution to that problem needs to be addressed
at the international level.    Neither ISO nor IEC nor ANSI nor NCITS has
any say over whether SAI chooses to participate in this process, or to what
degree.

I am convinced that there's a body of people in Australia who are truly
concerned about the future of COBOL (and of "Programming languages, their
environments and system software interfaces" in general), but if there's
nobody willing to participate with time and resources, that's not a problem
with the *processes*.  Your comments might as well have been thrown as paper
airplanes onto the field during a football game for all the effectiveness
they would have in influencing SAI, if SAI isn't participating in SC22 in
the first place!

Have you investigated what would be involved in having *formal* input into
SAI's decisions as to what committees of JTC1 they wish to be involved in
(specifically, their participation in SC22 and WG4), and what positions they
should take on behalf of the Australian programming community as part of
their participation in those committees?   Have you thought about "suiting
up and showing up" yourself, and encouraging others to do the same, as a way
of improving the responsiveness of the national standards body of your
country to these issues?

Also, I think there's at least one more formal opportunity for *public*
input coming up, so the fact that your comments didn't get included in the
process of moving from FCD to DIS does not mean they can't be considered in
the DIS-to-FDIS or even the FDIS-to-IS steps.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Australian comments

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 2001-06-03T07:42:49+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,tej
- **Message-ID:** `<3B195DD9.45FF3008@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<MABBIHGFPKHEHJLAPIPPMEFPCJAA.wmklein@ix.netcom.com> <3B17FB5B.159777B2@melbpc.org.au> <9f96u7$1ue$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck,

You are quite right the problem may well be in Australia. 

The representative of SAI did tell me that I could submit
comments, that they would be reviewed by an expert before being
forwarded. People should honour their commitments IMO. I am
taking this up with SAI, because as anyone who read my comments
will know I spent a lot of time on them. 

More broadly, though, there needs to be a mechanism for *people*
to submit comments, regardless of where they are located. I was
not asking for a vote, just for a way to get comments to the
committee, which was not allowed in the end. The nationalistic
structure of the process is a bug in my opinion, and if a local
organisation does not have their act together there should be a
way to get by them.

I think next time I will ask someone who lives in the USA to
submit them on behalf of the project. 

Tim Josling

Chuck Stevens wrote:

> Tim, Don't get me wrong, I'm not saying that your comments shouldn't have
> been responded to, or included in an SAI response submitted as part of their
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>     -Chuck Stevens
```

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>

Tim,

I agree 100% with your comments.

Doesn't it seem ridiculous that in this day and age, when we have
instantaneous, Worldwide communication, It is not possible to submit
comments from ANYWHERE, direct to whatever Standards body is responsible?

I've been saying this for over a year now.

There is no will on the part of the bodies concerned to set up such a
mechanism. They are hopelessly out of touch and they LIKE it like that...

A rigid hierarchical structure so any "annoying feedback" can be filtered
out...

With enough levels in the structure to ensure that responsibility is diluted
and nothing can be pinned  on anyone.

Then tell everybody what a fantastic job we did...

It makes me sick.

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Australian comments

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-06-02T20:34:52-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,tej
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0106021934.1cd9b146@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<MABBIHGFPKHEHJLAPIPPMEFPCJAA.wmklein@ix.netcom.com> <3B17FB5B.159777B2@melbpc.org.au> <9f96u7$1ue$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3B195DD9.45FF3008@melbpc.org.au>`

```
Anyone can send comments to any national body in these public
comments.  ANSI received quite a few from non-USA people.  Of course,
it ultimately has more significance if we get the same information
from a national body than an individual.  All comments received from
any individual anywhere are considered  equally in J4, but national
body comments will get more attention in WG4, which provides
direction.  In fact, the "US J4 Technical Advisory Group" uses the
comments received by ANSI to determine the US position (but foreign
comments ARE accepted with equal weight).
Tim Josling's comments to Standards Austraia HAVE been forwarded (by
Bill Klein) to J4, and WILL be considered somewhere in the process. (i
am not sure of all the procedures at this point.)

Still looking for people to join J4 and make a difference!

Stephen J Spiro
Member, J4 COBOL Standards Committee
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Australian comments

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James. J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-03T23:06:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B1AC37B.D9F4F26C@home.com>`
- **References:** `<MABBIHGFPKHEHJLAPIPPMEFPCJAA.wmklein@ix.netcom.com> <3B17FB5B.159777B2@melbpc.org.au> <9f96u7$1ue$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3B195DD9.45FF3008@melbpc.org.au> <4145699b.0106021934.1cd9b146@posting.google.com> <3B19E330.B00E3E36@melbpc.org.au> <4145699b.0106030838.2c91a8f0@posting.google.com>`

```


Stephen J Spiro wrote:

> Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au> wrote in message news:<3B19E330.B00E3E36@melbpc.org.au>...
> > Stephen,
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>

That's the whole point Stephen, and you are on the right tack. I believe you asked at one
stage would Huib Klink (the COBOL frog) participate. His answer as I recall,
"My employer's reaction would be 'What's in it for me?'".

Even in your own situation operating as an independent you can't always afford the time (or
money !) because of your contractual commitments. (As a retiree, Bill is in the same
position). Now I don't want to rub it in, but Chuck doesn't think the expense angle is a big
deal - his tab is picked up by Unisys. Have a conversation with him so that he 'sees the
light' from the perspective of individual contributors.

The other idea that was 'squished' when suggested by John Piggott, instead of all these
physical get togethers - let's do it by e-mail or tele-conferencing etc. That didn't seem to
glean much enthusiasm from others on J4.

Frankly, you don't need hordes of people on J4 - that's an immediate recipe for disaster. What
should be discussed is how do we, (J4),  encourage those with SPECIFIC interests in
categorized areas of COBOL to contribute and comment, working on Ad Hoc or sub-committees.

Tim's case is a bit 'exceptional' because as a compiler writer, he has to have an overall
picture. Me - I'll leave objectives and syntax on numeric handling to others with 'numbering'
expertise - BUT - as somebody actually 'trying' OO - I'll willingly contribute my two cents -
but not at the cost of air fare/hotel accommodation.

It wont happen - but here's a clue as to what the post mortem, (after COBOL 2002 has been put
to bed), should include - Why the ##@$$! should a group of UNPAID VOLUNTEERS be saddled with
this Fred Karno setup called ANSI/ISO - ignore all the bureaucratic BS in place to make you
jump hurdles. Give the whole thing a fresh look  - cut the Gordian knot and finish up with ONE
COBOL committee! (Instead of listing all the bureaucratic reasons why it can't be done,
rather, think on how it could be achieved).

Jimmy
```

V6 of Fujitsu offers an intrinsic function for returning the length of
a string, less trailing spaces.  However, there's no "SquishPete" or
similar function in the documentation.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help! remove embedded spaces from a string

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-04T17:33:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B1BC70C.541597CB@home.com>`
- **References:** `<D2WQ6.54$ex3.122975@nnrp3.sbc.net> <9f1fgs$eu4$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <pHXQ6.48$g03.9578@nnrp1.sbc.net> <3b146cf7.5915704@news1.attglobal.net> <3b14daf0_5@Usenet.com> <3B15B8CE.4A5EE696@home.com> <3b1b24d1.1697106@news1.attglobal.net>`

```


Thane Hubbell wrote:

> V6 of Fujitsu offers an intrinsic function for returning the length of
> a string, less trailing spaces.  However, there's no "SquishPete" or
> similar function in the documentation.
>

I assume the above is F/J specific - hence it is an 'extension' ? More later when
discussing Standard Classes with you., but I dabbled with (N/E) EXISTING class
methods to achieve the same, and following on Mr. Hancock's later message, catered
for removing excess spaces, leaving the 'true' spaces he wanted  - which would give
him his 'squished up' Legal Description. (It would be of interest to you using OO but
just academic to him).

Similar to what you mention above, there is a CharacterArray method :-

    invoke InObject "asArrayofSubstrings"

which returns you just the 'words' as separate items in a collection.

    invoke InObject "size" returning mysize and you also have a 'word' count.

OK having got there now either string them back together without any spaces or with a
space between each 'word'.

Now here's the stumbling block - which of umpteen methods do I use to achieve the
last. Sure if I persist, I'll get there - but - I shouldn't have to scratch around
like a farmyard chicken.

Message - when you J4 folks are looking at Standard Classes and Methods - those
method names have got to be real meaningful - AND - you can't just describe them in
isolation; the usage of several different methods used together has to be shown in
examples, to make the whole thing jive. Otherwise - not understanding what 'standard'
methods can do, developers will finish up writing their own routines - hence
re-invent the wheel.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help! remove embedded spaces from a string

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "DBuck" <dbuck@prairieinet.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-01T18:15:58-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9f97da$2nu1r$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de>`
- **References:** `<D2WQ6.54$ex3.122975@nnrp3.sbc.net> <9f1fgs$eu4$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <pHXQ6.48$g03.9578@nnrp1.sbc.net> <9f1t0n$1p2gi$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de> <3b14d75d_1@Usenet.com>`

```

Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3b14d75d_1@Usenet.com...
>
> "DBuck" <dbuck@prairieinet.net> wrote in message
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> 1. What field is FIELD-LENGTH the length of? If it is FIELD-A, then why
the
> reference modified test for trailing spaces? The length will tell you when
> you've finished.
>

Yes, FIELD-LENGTH is obviously the length of FIELD-A.  As this was a quick
example, I didn't think it was necessary to point that out.

The reference modified test for spaces is for speed.  If your average data
is actually less than half of your maximum length, it is well worth it.

> 2. Have you any idea of the code that will be generated to test for
trailing
> spaces on every iteration? .(..FIELD-A (SUB-1:) = SPACES)  As an example,
> consider that FIELD-A is 256 bytes and has 60 bytes before trailing
spaces.
> A fairly reasonable sample...The byte compares required will be 16,175.
Does
> that not strike you as a little excessive for a field that is 256 bytes
> long?
>

That depends upon the paricular compiler.  I would guess that, in most
cases, it is either a modified TR or a modified CLC.  If you would like, I
can give you an ASM example (or several).  If your average data is less than
half, the check for spaces is well worth it.  If the compare really offends
you, you can either let the code go through extra iterations of the routine
or throw some more code at it (INSPECT for the actual length, etc).  For the
most part, I would rather risk the 4 extra ASM instructions per character
used (not spaces) than the 4 extra ASM instructions per character not used
(trailing spaces).  This, of course, depends ultimately on the particular
application.

> 3. As there is no test to see if SUB-2 has overflowed after it is
> incremented, what happens if the output field is shorter than the input
one?
> (The user said that they could be different length fields.)
>

The resulting data within the field could end up shorter, but do you really
believe that the output field could be shorter?  Let's say the input field
is 256 - all used, but the output is 100 bytes.  What happened to the other
156 bytes?  Ooops, sorry sir, lost that account number when we removed the
spaces!?!  Doesn't make much sense, does it?  Should you *really* desire the
possibility of truncation, the code is easy to modify.

> Sometimes it is better to add a little more code and get it right...
please
> check further back up the thread to a previous post where I did this. (It
> uses 256 byte compares, for a field which is 256 bytes long.)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Sometimes, it is better to consider the code given and what it actually does
before responding.  I read your code.  It is harder to read (and, thus, to
maintain), uses more working storage, uses the same literal pool, and, in a
vast majority of real-world situations, slower.  Not exactly what I would be
touting as "getting it right".  For all that, you give the ability to have a
smaller output field than input field.  You can get the same thing from my
code by adding "OR SUB2 > FIELD-B-LENGTH" to the perform.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help! remove embedded spaces from a string

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-02T12:30:27+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b183321_3@Usenet.com>`

```
Well, your response was such blatant nonsense I had promised myself not to
respond but I'm having a quiet day so...

"DBuck" <dbuck@prairieinet.net> wrote in message
news:9f97da$2nu1r$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de...
>
> Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> > >                                             OR FIELD-A (SUB1:) =
SPACES
> > >    IF FIELD-A (SUB1:1) NOT = SPACES
> > >       MOVE FIELD-A (SUB1:1) TO FIELD-B (SUB2:1)
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > It isn't much code, and you probably aren't going to get any easier
than
> > > this...
> > >
> > While I agree it is not much code, I HAVE to ask the following
questions:
> >
> > 1. What field is FIELD-LENGTH the length of? If it is FIELD-A, then why
> the
> > reference modified test for trailing spaces? The length will tell you
when
> > you've finished.
> >
>

>>>
> Yes, FIELD-LENGTH is obviously the length of FIELD-A.  As this was a quick
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
<<<
Unbelievable! You have a length that would terminate the loop but you prefer
to do a byte compare on (length - current position) bytes!  And this is "for
speed"?!



> > 2. Have you any idea of the code that will be generated to test for
> trailing
> > spaces on every iteration? .(..FIELD-A (SUB-1:) = SPACES)  As an
example,
> > consider that FIELD-A is 256 bytes and has 60 bytes before trailing
> spaces.
> > A fairly reasonable sample...The byte compares required will be 16,175.

> Does
> > that not strike you as a little excessive for a field that is 256 bytes
> > long?
> >
>

>>>
> That depends upon the paricular compiler.  I would guess that, in most
> cases, it is either a modified TR or a modified CLC.
<<<
No it doesn't! And don't threaten me with mainframe assembler, I was writing
it before you were born...<G>

Yes, on the mainframe it IS a modified CLC, (and I'm NOT guessing) but it
still takes many times longer than an arithmetic compare (even CP and BC)
which is what you'd get by testing Length.
And, in the example stated 16,175 bytes will be compared (whether it is a
basic CLC which does them in 256 byte lumps, or an extended CLC which does
them according to the lengths in the attached registers, EVERY byte has to
be run through the ALU). In  the example I posted, 256 bytes get run through
the ALU.

>>>
>If you would like, I
> can give you an ASM example (or several).  If your average data is less
than
> half, the check for spaces is well worth it.
<<<
NO it isn't! Never. Not under any circumstances on ANY machine, or ANY
compiler! You are bullshitting because you are embarrassed. Don't be. It's
OK. It's only computer programming...<G>

How can I be so certain?  Because the byte compares are storage based (even
if you only compared ONE Byte) and the arithmetic compare is register based.
Arithmetic is therefore many times faster.

>>>
> If the compare really offends
> you, you can either let the code go through extra iterations of the
routine
> or throw some more code at it (INSPECT for the actual length, etc).  For
the
> most part, I would rather risk the 4 extra ASM instructions per character
> used (not spaces) than the 4 extra ASM instructions per character not used
> (trailing spaces).  This, of course, depends ultimately on the particular
> application.
>
<<<
Code NEVER offends me. Attitude sometimes does...(but rarely). It is only
code, not Life and Death...


> > 3. As there is no test to see if SUB-2 has overflowed after it is
> > incremented, what happens if the output field is shorter than the input
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

>>>
> The resulting data within the field could end up shorter, but do you
really
> believe that the output field could be shorter?  Let's say the input field
> is 256 - all used, but the output is 100 bytes.  What happened to the
other
> 156 bytes?  Ooops, sorry sir, lost that account number when we removed the
> spaces!?!  Doesn't make much sense, does it?  Should you *really* desire
the
> possibility of truncation, the code is easy to modify.
>
<<<
Yes, I really believe the output field could be shorter. This is based on
the fact that we are REMOVING bytes from the input field. In the case you
quote where the 256 input bytes are all used, the output field won't be
shorter, BECAUSE NOTHING WAS REMOVED.  In any case where the input contains
spaces (which is the long forgotten purpose of the exercise) the output
COULD be shorter.

Do you normally design your code around the most unlikely case?  A curious
approach...


>>>
> Sometimes, it is better to consider the code given and what it actually
does
> before responding.  I read your code.  It is harder to read (and, thus, to
> maintain), uses more working storage, uses the same literal pool, and, in
a
> vast majority of real-world situations, slower.
>>>
Hahaha! Wanna bet?!  Try executing mine 100,000 times and executing yours
100,000 times... I'll cream you...<G> And I'll do it on ANY machine or
compiler, so, please no more smoke screens...

As for being harder to read, that is totally a matter of opinion. Foodman
agrees with you...so you are in good company...<G>

>>>
> Not exactly what I would be
> touting as "getting it right".  For all that, you give the ability to have
a
> smaller output field than input field.  You can get the same thing from my
> code by adding "OR SUB2 > FIELD-B-LENGTH" to the perform.
>
<<<
Oh Dear! then you'd be occupying  "more Working-Storage" just like my
deplorable example...<G>

It was never my intention to give you a hard time (and I VERY rarely post
code to CLC) but you waved a red rag and now I have...

It never ceases to amaze me how defensive some people become about their
code. It's as if COBOL code is an extension of their self esteem. For my own
part, I can (and have) had code dissected by people whose opinion I respect,
and it has never done me any harm. I reckon it is an essential part of
learning.

(I didn't get to be the lean mean perfect coding machine you see today
without a little criticism...<LOL>)

At the end of the day it doesn't matter WHO's right (unless it's Foodman...I
couldn't stand that <G>) it's simply about WHAT's right.

Pete.


Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------

----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```
