```
No, it doesn't what?  Depend on the compiler?  How much would you like to
bet on that?

> Yes, on the mainframe it IS a modified CLC, (and I'm NOT guessing) but it
> still takes many times longer than an arithmetic compare (even CP and BC)
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> be run through the ALU). In  the example I posted, 256 bytes get run
through
> the ALU.
>

Since you *know* what it is doing, then try working through the code in you
mind....

> >>>
> >If you would like, I
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> How can I be so certain?  Because the byte compares are storage based
(even
> if you only compared ONE Byte) and the arithmetic compare is register
based.
> Arithmetic is therefore many times faster.
>

Really?  Never?  Why don't you post the assembler code for each routine, or
at least work it out for yourself?  Which routine is faster if the input
field is all spaces?  How about if the actual data is 5 bytes long (5
letters, 251 spaces)?  How about 50, or 100, or even 120?  Don't forget to
throw in 256 checks of your level 88, 256 checks of your length, 256 checks
of your input field character against spaces, and the number of checks
(depending on the number of non-spaces) of you sub2 against it's length (and
all of the addressing for each of those fields).

It might just be you that is embarrassed....

> >>>
> > If the compare really offends
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > most part, I would rather risk the 4 extra ASM instructions per
character
> > used (not spaces) than the 4 extra ASM instructions per character not
used
> > (trailing spaces).  This, of course, depends ultimately on the
particular
> > application.
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > incremented, what happens if the output field is shorter than the
input
> > one?
> > > (The user said that they could be different length fields.)
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> > believe that the output field could be shorter?  Let's say the input
field
> > is 256 - all used, but the output is 100 bytes.  What happened to the
> other
> > 156 bytes?  Ooops, sorry sir, lost that account number when we removed
the
> > spaces!?!  Doesn't make much sense, does it?  Should you *really* desire
> the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> shorter, BECAUSE NOTHING WAS REMOVED.  In any case where the input
contains
> spaces (which is the long forgotten purpose of the exercise) the output
> COULD be shorter.
>

The output field, or the output data?  Unless you intend to use variable
length fields (which you didn't), the output field has to be at least the
same length as the input field, in case there are no blanks.  Otherwise, you
will truncate (or, in this case, overflow you field and overwrite storage
immediately following it).  Don't believe me?  Change you output field to
occurs 100 times (and the corresponding output length), then put 256
non-spaces into the input field.  Bet you your next paycheck that it
truncates....

Since the output field has be at least as big as the input field, there is
no reason for the extra check on sub2.  It can't overflow unless sub1 does.

> Do you normally design your code around the most unlikely case?  A curious
> approach...
>

Do you normally design your programs so that your output fields are shorter
than the information that may go into them?  A very curious approach...

>
> >>>
> > Sometimes, it is better to consider the code given and what it actually
> does
> > before responding.  I read your code.  It is harder to read (and, thus,
to
> > maintain), uses more working storage, uses the same literal pool, and,
in
> a
> > vast majority of real-world situations, slower.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yeah, I'll take that bet.  OS390, MVS/ESA.  I'll try to find time to enter
them both in, come up with some sample data, and run them head-to-head.  I
hope that you are ready to put your money where your mouth is.  The only
time your code is faster is when the average data is greater than 128 bytes,
and even then, it will be close.  You'll know that when you work out the
instruction list...

> As for being harder to read, that is totally a matter of opinion. Foodman
> agrees with you...so you are in good company...<G>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > touting as "getting it right".  For all that, you give the ability to
have
> a
> > smaller output field than input field.  You can get the same thing from
my
> > code by adding "OR SUB2 > FIELD-B-LENGTH" to the perform.
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

First, I would still be using less working storage than your example.
Second, I never referred to you example as "deplorable", or anything like
that.  If you remember correctly, you are the one that started this with my
example...

> It was never my intention to give you a hard time (and I VERY rarely post
> code to CLC) but you waved a red rag and now I have...
>
> It never ceases to amaze me how defensive some people become about their
> code. It's as if COBOL code is an extension of their self esteem. For my
own
> part, I can (and have) had code dissected by people whose opinion I
respect,
> and it has never done me any harm. I reckon it is an essential part of
> learning.
>

Actually, I didn't think I was being all that defensive.  You seem to be of
the opinion that, since you've been programming since before I was born, you
can't learn anything.  I think that, if you step back and look at the ASM
instructions supporting the code, you will find that the example I posted is
both easier to read (most important) and faster (unless you average data is
over 128 bytes)....  Both examples will at least give the newer programmers
here something to work with...

> (I didn't get to be the lean mean perfect coding machine you see today

Foodman...I
> couldn't stand that <G>) it's simply about WHAT's right.
```

```
I've never used Xilerator, but I heard it was excellent.

The MicroFocus Animator is one of the most impressive software tools I've
ever seen. The first time I saw it (many years ago) it just blew me away.

Fujitsu Debugger offers similar functionality (you can step through source
COBOL, set breakpoints, monitor data fields etc.) but I have never seen a
debugger which actually shows the generated code as Assembler on the PC
(apart from an Assembler Debugger).

If Xilerator does that it would indeed be interesting.

more below...


"Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk> wrote in message
news:9fbbh5$hsb$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk...
> Using my old MF WB 2.4 I am able to animate with a tool called Xilerator.
> This displays source code in one window and assembly in another. Is there
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> well do the current mainframes hold up against the P4 (price /
performance)
> ?

I really don't want to get into a PC versus Mainframe war (I've upset D.
Buck enough already...) because they both have their place. I haven't seen
recent performance (Million Instructions Per Second (MIPS) type data - which
is often misleading anyway) for the current mainframes.

It's important to realise that (despite what your local PC salesman tells
you) processor power is NOT the be all and end all...especially when it
comes to "data processing" (as opposed to "computing").

Mainframes excel at data processing because the channel architectures (like
Block MUX) are light years ahead of the the PC integrated bus architecture
(both platforms can utilise Direct Memory Access (DMA) buses, but actual
data transfer from peripherals on the mainframe is carried out by a
subsystem which the PC is yet to emulate.)

Irrespective of processor power, this gives the mainframe a decided edge
when it comes to shovelling data around.

However, the picture is different when it comes to actual Arithmetic Logic
Unit (ALU - the essential heart of the CPU) cycle time and instruction
execution. Both platforms are much closer  in this department.

30 years ago we thought a mainframe capable of around .3 MIPS (IBM 360-30...
it took 1.5 microseconds to access 1 byte in hand threaded memory, and you
needed at least two bytes before you could really do anything) was pretty
good stuff. I remember watching the CDC Cyber 70 when I was first introduced
to it, with awe. It was designed by the legendary Seymour Cray and he
eschewed the then fashionable integrated circuitry for good old fashioned
(Transistor-Transistor Logic (TTL)). Result: a machine that was capable of
nearly 4 MIPS, but ran so hot you could heat the building with it and it
required water cooling...
(part of this huge increase in performance was obtained by utilising a
different software architecture as well as hardware: the Cyber used a 64 bit
"word" with 6 bit characters; I can't remember what the spare four bits
did...<G>)

Today we have memory chips that can provide a byte in under 100 nanoseconds
(and have saved the eyesight of thousands of Koreans who used to "knit"
little ferrite doughnuts onto wire lattices with a microscope) so the actual
cycle time for the ALU has decreased dramatically.

I have seen claims for P4 of around 20 MIPS. I don't know whether this is
true or not, or who made the calculations, or on what basis. As I said
above, I have seen no recent claims for mainframe MIPS (maybe someone
reading this who is on a mainframe site could ask the engineers and post
it?)

While these kind of figures are interesting as a measure of the dramatic
increase in power in hardware over time, it would be very wrong to place
credence in them for determining throughput or comparing performance.

The bottom line is that modern processors (both PC and mainframe) are
unimaginably FAST ...!!!

(Of course, we manage to slow them down by writing crap code...<G>)

Pete.


>
> "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
```

- **Message-ID:** `<3B1B36DE.E29FFA9C@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<D2WQ6.54$ex3.122975@nnrp3.sbc.net> <9f1fgs$eu4$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <pHXQ6.48$g03.9578@nnrp1.sbc.net> <9f1t0n$1p2gi$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de> <3b14d75d_1@Usenet.com> <9f97da$2nu1r$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de> <3b183321_3@Usenet.com> <9f9pkv$2pp45$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de> <9fa13p$f09$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk> <3b18eb26_1@news2.newsfeeds.com> <9fbbh5$hsb$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <3b199889_1@news2.newsfeeds.com> <9fcn9c$mbo$1@news5.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Alister Munro wrote:
> 
> Yup.. Xilerator did all the good stuff but it has been dropped from the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> my latest code on a 286 just to make sure it performs well.

That implies that you are generating your code so that it will run on a
286.  If you specify that you want 386 or better then the code will most
likely run faster on those machines (though not at all on a 286).  This
makes it rather counter-productive to test it on a 286 unless there is a
real need for this.

It is also rather pointless to use the 12 year old workbench to optimise
your code if you generate the production code from a later compiler as
they may have completely changed the code generator in critical places.

I don't believe that there is any significant benefit from optimising
code in applications that are most appropriate to Cobol.  I have run
Cobol applications compiled as .int on multiuser machines as low as 4MHz
8085 (MP/M 3 user) and 5MHz 8088 (Concurrent-CP/M 4 user) and everytime
I checked there was always spare CPU time - ie it was waiting for disk
accesses.

Of course everything seems sluggish when run on a Windows machine ;-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help! remove embedded spaces from a string

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2001-06-03T22:03:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010603180350.26589.00003655@nso-mb.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3B1B36DE.E29FFA9C@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
In article <3B1B36DE.E29FFA9C@Azonic.co.nz>, Richard Plinston
<riplin@Azonic.co.nz> writes:

>
>I don't believe that there is any significant benefit from optimising
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Again , optimization and performance is viewed as trivial for the PC platform.
(I assume you are referring to the PC as you were using the 8085/8088
processors.)  I still hold to the belief that optimization of code and logic
flows
should be considered no matter the platform or program function.
Many of the Q&D one time fix programs that I have seen written end up as 
long term production programs, because there is no imperative to go back
and correct the situation that created the need for the 'fix' program.

Any program that has to potential for processing a high volume of activity
should be carefully scrutinized for performanceissues.  Even low volume 
programs should be reviewed periodically.
```

```
I store screen panels in a run length encoded format which is parsed
at run-time character by character. Each display character can then
be converted to another depending on the terminal capabilities. Colour
attributes come next and again these are converted from a specific type
(i.e. title, fkey label, etc.)  to the desired colour held in another file
which
is selected by that user for that terminal. Some people like blue some
cyan and others green as there background colour. Some like shading
characters others don't. Some terminals get confused with $,#, & � as
do the printers. All this takes a reasonable amount of processing to get
through and so its nice to see it running well on an older machine. I don't
use the standard Cobol ACCEPT / DISPLAY commands as I prefer to
use my own code which once again could melt a few heat-sinks if not
done properly.

Although I use my 12 year old compiler set for 386-16 its still nice to
know that the code performs well on older slower machine. I still have
clients who use old 286 machines in there factories to allow time-sheet
data to be gathered real-time.

I have yet to see a peace of software run well on a 286 yet badly on a
386 or above so I suppose its a nice quick guide-line as to wither or
not I have cocked up the code.

About 18 months ago a client and I went for a demo of Citrix which we
were told not to expect to much from as most DOS programs ran Idle
at between 25-40% processor utilisation. I admitted I would probably
need to do some work on the code but when we ran the application it
didn't show on the performance graph. I still bear the stretch marks from
the grin that spread form ear to ear :)

<riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3B1B36DE.E29FFA9C@Azonic.co.nz...
```

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3b18eb26_1@news2.newsfeeds.com...
> "Blessed are the Peacemakers", Alister. <tee hee>

…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

Ooops.... here are you results.  So much for that whole "even on his chosen
platform"....

I wrote the programs at work (took me about 15 minutes) and tested them
throughout the day (a total of 24 tests).  Twenty-three of the 24, I had a
better clock time.  All 24, I had a better CPU time.  Just as a note, I ran
the large tests this morning (before our system got really buried).  The
smaller tests were submitted throughout the day.

I'm sure that Mr. Dashwood will now either be too embarrassed to respond, or
come up with some backpedaling bullsh*t about how he was only *really*
talking about the PC or I have somehow "rigged" the test.  It doesn't
matter.  I didn't run these test for him.  People like him are somehow
convinced that, since they were "programming before I was born", they no
longer have anything to learn.  Apparently, they do.  As do I, and everyone
else here.  Once again, with the results below, I state that the PERFORM is
much too expensive to allow to continue any longer than necessary.  Write
the code.  Use the LIST option on the compile.  You'll see what I am talking
about.  As long as your average for actual data is half or less (or even
more in this case), end the perform as soon as possible.

Mr. Dashwood assumed that I was new to the group, new to programming, or
simply didn't know my job.  For your reference, I have been posting to this
group for years now, under renegade@dwx.com, and now this address.  If you
look in the archive, you will find hundreds, maybe thousands of lines of
code posted.

I can't speak for what will happen on the PC.  I can guess, but that is not
my expertise.  I invite you all to test it.  Try it out and see how it works
for you.  Learn how it really works....

Mr. Dashwood, I hope that you will be man enough to admit that you were
wrong... even if it is only to yourself.  Oh, and as someone once said:

"(Oh, and never be too keen to post code to CLC...<G> especially if you are
easily wounded....)"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help! remove embedded spaces from a string

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-05T14:43:59+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b1c4a34_6@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<D2WQ6.54$ex3.122975@nnrp3.sbc.net> <9f1fgs$eu4$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <pHXQ6.48$g03.9578@nnrp1.sbc.net> <9f1t0n$1p2gi$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de> <3b14d75d_1@Usenet.com> <9f97da$2nu1r$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de> <3b183321_3@Usenet.com> <9f9pkv$2pp45$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de> <9fa13p$f09$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk> <3b18eb26_1@news2.newsfeeds.com> <9fh389$4080m$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de> <9fh3jh$445vg$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de>`

```
I just had a look at the code you posted and note that you ARE doing a test
for Length on the input field.
(and, although you optimised your own length field for the platform, you
didn't do me the same courtesy...but then, why would you help my case? This
is about personalities now, not computer code, right <G>?)

My whole objection was based on using a reference modified length check for
spaces INSTEAD of an arithmetic length check.

You may recall that my argument was predicated on the fact that storage
instructions take longer to execute than arithmetic ones...

I also can't seem to find any test data that has random embedded spaces in
it; the purpose of my code was to remove these.

On a more niggling level...

Here is the code you posted originally:

"MOVE SPACES TO FIELD-B.
MOVE 1 TO SUB2.
PERFORM VARYING SUB1 FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL SUB1 > FIELD-LENGTH
                                            OR FIELD-A (SUB1:) = SPACES
   IF FIELD-A (SUB1:1) NOT = SPACES
      MOVE FIELD-A (SUB1:1) TO FIELD-B (SUB2:1)
      ADD 1 TO SUB2
   END-IF
END-PERFORM"

Sadly this is NOT the code you tested (although it DOES have the length test
in it)

All arithmetic fields have been optimized, and we find that a new field has
magically sprung into being..."S1" with a value of '1' but defined the same
as the other optimized arithmetic fields so that no arithmetic conversion is
required... Good practice and I would condone it, but hardly "fair" in the
context we are discussing...

Also the code you posted does NOT meet the original requirement which was
for strings of unequal length. (NOT doing this also gives your code an
edge...)

I am therefore not satisfied that the tests are a fair representation of the
points being disputed and offer no (full) apology at this time....<G>

However, I DID find your results interesting and got to thinking about it
some more.

Your point about the PERFORM being an overhead best avoided, and exited as
quickly as possible is a very valid one and I share your concern. (My issue
is, and has always been, with the WAY in which you choose to detect this...a
reference modified compare for spaces).

I believed I had it covered with the test for input length, but I had
assumed (in my own mind when I posted the code...I agree I didn't express
it) that this would be preset to the position of the last non-blank in the
string (this would be established by a reversed INSPECT or a decrementing
PERFORM...VARYING, immediately BEFORE entering the main loop).

Given that this field is pre-set to the last non-blank character in the
string, the perform will exit as soon as it can,  and there is therefore
really no need for the reference modified test for remaining spaces.

That was what prompted my comment to you, asking why you used both tests...
Of course you NEED both tests if the length is set to the physical length of
the field.

OK, I accept I slipped up in not expressing something that I expected to be
taken as read... (The whole issue was not really that important to me; had
it been I would've taken greater care.)

That's all you're getting...<G>

(BTW, I am NOT easily wounded...even so, I rarely post code here...)

Pete.

PS You were totally wrong in your assumptions about what I assumed about
you. But I'm glad you are now prepared to stand by your code and put your
name on it rather than a "handle"...that shows growth.




Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help! remove embedded spaces from a string

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "DBuck" <dbuck@prairieinet.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-05T20:55:56-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9fk296$4f14h$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de>`
- **References:** `<D2WQ6.54$ex3.122975@nnrp3.sbc.net> <9f1fgs$eu4$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <pHXQ6.48$g03.9578@nnrp1.sbc.net> <9f1t0n$1p2gi$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de> <3b14d75d_1@Usenet.com> <9f97da$2nu1r$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de> <3b183321_3@Usenet.com> <9f9pkv$2pp45$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de> <9fa13p$f09$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk> <3b18eb26_1@news2.newsfeeds.com> <9fh389$4080m$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de> <9fh3jh$445vg$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de> <3b1c4a34_6@Usenet.com>`

```

Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3b1c4a34_6@Usenet.com...
> I just had a look at the code you posted and note that you ARE doing a
test
> for Length on the input field.
> (and, although you optimised your own length field for the platform, you
> didn't do me the same courtesy...but then, why would you help my case?
This
> is about personalities now, not computer code, right <G>?)
>

I actually optimized your code at first, but then changed it because I
didn't want anyone accusing me of changing your code....  And yes, *maybe* I
overreacted a bit...

> My whole objection was based on using a reference modified length check
for
> spaces INSTEAD of an arithmetic length check.
>
> You may recall that my argument was predicated on the fact that storage
> instructions take longer to execute than arithmetic ones...
>

I never debated that.  My debate was that letting the code go through all
iterations of the PERFORM was more costly than using the chekc for spaces...

> I also can't seem to find any test data that has random embedded spaces in
> it; the purpose of my code was to remove these.
>

None had randomly inbedded spaces, but did have information (non-spaces)
following spaces.  We both know that randomly imbedded spaces won't change
the result.  Last I knew, nobody was debating the fact that the code works -
just the speed of it...

> On a more niggling level...
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Sadly this is NOT the code you tested (although it DOES have the length
test
> in it)
>
> All arithmetic fields have been optimized, and we find that a new field
has
> magically sprung into being..."S1" with a value of '1' but defined the
same
> as the other optimized arithmetic fields so that no arithmetic conversion
is
> required... Good practice and I would condone it, but hardly "fair" in the
> context we are discussing...
>

I pretty much automatically use optimized fields.  I can change yours, if
you desire, and see what real difference it makes.  It will speed up the
routine, but not enough to overcome going through all iterations of it...

> Also the code you posted does NOT meet the original requirement which was
> for strings of unequal length. (NOT doing this also gives your code an
> edge...)
>

It is still my stance that, technically, you cannot code for an output field
length less than the input and expect all data to be carried forward.  You
can get less data if there are spaces in the field, but it is bad practice
to randomly remove the last part of the string because it really was the
full input length.

> I am therefore not satisfied that the tests are a fair representation of
the
> points being disputed and offer no (full) apology at this time....<G>
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> quickly as possible is a very valid one and I share your concern. (My
issue
> is, and has always been, with the WAY in which you choose to detect
this...a
> reference modified compare for spaces).
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I had assumed that "IF the input string was VERY long (more than 16K) you
MIGHT need to amend
the above code to detect the last non-blank character in it and set the
position of this character into the input string length." (from your post of
the code) indicated that you did NOT intend for there to be a prior check
for actual length of data.  With a prior check for length, then yes, the
code you posted would be quicker (so long as the length of the inout field
could support the extra INSPECT FUNCTION REVERSE).

> Given that this field is pre-set to the last non-blank character in the
> string, the perform will exit as soon as it can,  and there is therefore
> really no need for the reference modified test for remaining spaces.
>

You are entirely correct here.  IF the length of actual data is known, than
you do NOT want the check for spaces...

> That was what prompted my comment to you, asking why you used both
tests...
> Of course you NEED both tests if the length is set to the physical length
of
> the field.
>
> OK, I accept I slipped up in not expressing something that I expected to
be
> taken as read... (The whole issue was not really that important to me; had
> it been I would've taken greater care.)
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
Excellent!

Please mail me the tests privately, Source code, comple lists, run logs, and
Especially, test data.
(we need to see that your prediction of less than half the length is borne
out)

No bullshit, no backpedal, if I am persuaded that your tests are fair I WILL
apologise publicly (and sincerely).

I am concerned that you said:

"> I wrote the programs at work (took me about 15 minutes) and tested them"

The whole point was that you were supposed to run my code, not your
interpretation of it.

I await your post.

Pete.

"DBuck" <dbuck@prairieinet.net> wrote in message

will
```

```
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ooops.... here are you results.  So much for that whole "even on his
chosen
> platform"....
>>>
I was shocked at this result....<G>

So I had another look at it and realised that I had forgotten to post the
essential line that makes all the difference...
(The setting of "input-length" to be the position of the last non-blank
character in the string).

> I wrote the programs at work (took me about 15 minutes) and tested them
> throughout the day (a total of 24 tests).  Twenty-three of the 24, I had a
> better clock time.  All 24, I had a better CPU time.  Just as a note, I
ran
> the large tests this morning (before our system got really buried).  The
> smaller tests were submitted throughout the day.
>
Well you certainly did a thorough job and (with a few minor reservations
which I have expressed elsewhere in the thread) your sample was "fair".

However, the code that I was referring in in my later discussions was never
tested because I never got to post it. (I thought I had, but I certainly got
that wrong...)

>>>
> I'm sure that Mr. Dashwood will now either be too embarrassed to respond,
or
> come up with some backpedaling bullsh*t about how he was only *really*
> talking about the PC or I have somehow "rigged" the test.  It doesn't
> matter.  I didn't run these test for him.
<<<
Well, you got that wrong. I'm ALWAYS interested in something which produces
an unexpected result; whether it makes me look  like an idiot or not. I keep
my ego far away from my computer keyboard...

>>>
 People like him are somehow
> convinced that, since they were "programming before I was born", they no
> longer have anything to learn.
<<<
Always dangerous to generalise... there ARE no people like me...I'm a one
off <G> If you were born more than 35 years ago, I will humbly retract my
statement.

>>>
 Apparently, they do.  As do I, and everyone
> else here.
<<<
I do not consider myself immune from the learning process. And this has been
a good learning exercise...I learned "don't post incomplete code samples to
CLC, check them VERY carefully before you do, and preferably, run the code
before posting it, to ensure it REALLY does what you THINK it does.." I
slipped up on this occasion, but then I underestimated the ferocity of your
response <G>

>>>
 Once again, with the results below, I state that the PERFORM is
> much too expensive to allow to continue any longer than necessary.
<<<
I have NO issue with this and hold the same position.

The issue was in HOW you terminate the PERFORM loop. I contend, and this is
NOT a "back pedal" I stated it previously, that a storage based solution to
this problem ( e.g.a reference modified test for trailing spaces) can NEVER
be as fast as a simple arithmetic compare on a field which contains the
"logical" (rather than the "physical") string length. I say again that it
will run faster on any platform.
(Unfortunately I didn't include the line to set this up in the sample I
posted...an omission which certainly caused me some embarrassment...)

>>>
> Write
> the code.  Use the LIST option on the compile.  You'll see what I am
talking
> about.  As long as your average for actual data is half or less (or even
> more in this case), end the perform as soon as possible.
> >>
Actually you should ALWAYS end the perform as soon as possible, and that's
what I thought my code did.

>>>
> Mr. Dashwood assumed that I was new to the group, new to programming, or
> simply didn't know my job.  For your reference, I have been posting to
this
> group for years now, under renegade@dwx.com, and now this address.  If you
> look in the archive, you will find hundreds, maybe thousands of lines of
> code posted.
<<<
I assumed no such thing. I simply responded to your agressive and defensive
tone.

And if you posted thousands of lines which were all crap, it wouldn't say
much would it...<G>

>>>
> I can't speak for what will happen on the PC.  I can guess, but that is
not
> my expertise.  I invite you all to test it.  Try it out and see how it
works
> for you.  Learn how it really works....
> >>
I endorse the empirical approach on ANY platform...

>>>
> Mr. Dashwood, I hope that you will be man enough to admit that you were
> wrong... even if it is only to yourself.
<<<
Well, that's a real big ask...however, I have gone as far as I'm prepared
to...<G>

>>>
> Oh, and as someone once said:
>
> "(Oh, and never be too keen to post code to CLC...<G> especially if you
are
> easily wounded....)"
>
<<<
Sounds like a very wise man...<G>

Pete.
```

```
perform.  I posted the code example I did for 2 reason:  1)  it is just
about the least amount of code to get the job done, 2) I didn't know how big
the field in question was - if it is small (50 bytes or less), the INSPECT
or decrementing perform is not worth the extra code (the inspect would
probably be a little faster, but the maintenence would be easier with less
code).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help! remove embedded spaces from a string

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-05T07:43:23-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B1CE1FB.CF4D0011@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<D2WQ6.54$ex3.122975@nnrp3.sbc.net> <9f1fgs$eu4$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <pHXQ6.48$g03.9578@nnrp1.sbc.net> <9f1t0n$1p2gi$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de> <3b14d75d_1@Usenet.com> <9f97da$2nu1r$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de> <3b183321_3@Usenet.com> <9f9pkv$2pp45$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de> <9fa13p$f09$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk> <3b18eb26_1@news2.newsfeeds.com> <9fh389$4080m$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de>`

```
DBuck wrote:

> I'm sure that Mr. Dashwood will now either be too embarrassed to respond, or
> come up with some backpedaling bullsh*t about how he was only *really*
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> more in this case), end the perform as soon as possible.

It depends on how you measure the cost of the PERFORM.   In the case of your
examples, your code was at least as clear and maintainable as the alternative,
but if it weren't, then the PERFORM might be cheaper - even with faster times.
Replace your code with some fast spaghetti code, and the maintenance costs could
quickly eat up any gain in speed.

Costs:
Cost of doing something wrong (bugs)
Cost of analyzing what something is doing.
Cost of not being efficient in the computer.
Opportunity cost when an enhancement takes long to do.
Programmer cost.


Which of these will drop by itself over time?  Which will increase over time?

Efficient code is desirable, and sometimes mandatory.  But it isn't
automatically the end-all of programming.
```

```
don't be stingy with displays, you can use them to let the code "tell on
itself".
they also make de-bugging much easier.
```

<a id="t-46341e4805"></a>
## Thread: Validation Question — 5 msgs

### Re: Validation Question

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-02T07:17:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B189289.7629F2D3@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<SmNR6.94963$ff.719687@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <9f8snh$dle$1@news.netcologne.de> <ufTR6.95164$ff.722726@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`

```
eRRaTiK wrote:
> 
> Well I was wondering if the conditional test was valid. ie. is this a valid
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> Are you sure the Routine is reached ??

One way to be sure that the edit routine is being executed is to put a
unique display in that paragraph.  Your previous example would never
reach F16-CHECK-COURSE because the PERFORM statement was commented
out, and the VALID-COURSE flag was unconditionally set to "Y" after
the commented-out PERFORM of F16-CHECK-COURSE.

Now when you do finally execute your class-test code, you may find
there is a problem with ALPHABETIC.  In every COBOL I have used,
spaces are valid ALPHABETIC class characters, but probably not what
you want for this program.  My guess is that you will somehow need to
exclude spaces in any position in field W-C2.

I would probably define a class in SPECIAL-NAMES containing only
alphabetic characters but excluding spaces, and then use the special
alphabetic class in the IF statement.

> 
> It's definitely reached because if you look at the output file (it's a
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Please refer to test1.log.

Try something like this:

F16-CHECK-COURSE.
    DISPLAY "NOW EXECUTING PARAGRAPH F16-CHECK-COURSE"	
    MOVE "N" TO VALID-COURSE.
    IF (W-C1 NUMERIC) AND (W-C2 ALPHABETIC)
        DISPLAY "W-C1 CONTAINS (" W-C1 ") AND IT IS NUMERIC"
        DISPLAY "W-C2 CONTAINS (" W-C2 ") AND IT IS ALPHABETIC"
        MOVE "Y" TO VALID-COURSE.

This kind of test instrumentation will tell you if F16-CHECK-COURSE is
being executed, and will also tell you if blank characters are allowed
in W-C2 by the ALPHABETIC class test.
```

#### ↳ Re: Validation Question

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-02T16:14:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Just a comment about debugging displays. I always prefix these displays
w/"debug~". When I'm finished I enter the following command string:

x all;f all debug~;del all nx

It greatly simplifies the cleanup process.

To make the process even simpler I use an ISPF macro that inserts displays of
each pgraph name immediately behind the pgraph
headers. 

Jack

```

```
> I always felt these debug lines cluttered up production code and distracted the

remove my 'D' and if the standards mandate a MODID in columns 73-80, I put them
there.  It makes it easy to see the new code.
```

<a id="t-1204f86fe2"></a>
## Thread: web database via COBOL — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases) · [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### web database via COBOL

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2001-06-02T09:38:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010602053819.01421.00002411@ng-fb1.aol.com>`

```
Richard Plinston said, in a thread abou GUI's: 
>How does interfacing to the web using Cobol look any different from
>interfacing to the web using any other system ?

>I have written several CGI programs, mostly in Cobol, and what they do
>is accept text strings and emit HTML (with perhaps Javascript). The
>appearance is entirely embodied within the HTML. 
etc. 

How do you handle Client access to a Server database?  
Is that done all in COBOL?  
Or does the COBOL code make calls on non-COBOL routines? 

Ross
http://www.geocities.com/ross_klatte/
```

#### ↳ Re: web database via COBOL

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-03T08:35:11+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B19E8AF.F79432B9@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<20010602053819.01421.00002411@ng-fb1.aol.com>`

```
Ross Klatte wrote:
> 
> Richard Plinston said, in a thread abou GUI's:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> How do you handle Client access to a Server database?

You will need to ask a clearer question.  What exactly do you mean by
'client access to server database' in the context of CGI programs ?

In this case the 'client' is an HTML web browser and the 'server' is a
web server that activates the Cobol program that gets the data using
ACCEPTs and sends back the results using DISPLAYs.  The Cobol program
access whatever data files are necessary for the reply.

> Is that done all in COBOL?
> Or does the COBOL code make calls on non-COBOL routines?

That depends on the environment, on Windows I use Xitami server as it
does real CGI and it is just ACCEPT and DISPLAY.  On Linux under Apache
the code is the same, under OS/2 with Xitami I do a CALL "DosRead" for
posted data.  For some Windows Web servers I would probably need to use
WinCGI in which the data is passed in files, so it is only necessary to
open and read a text file.  This is sorted out in one of the Cobol
modules.

For 'Long running' or persistant CGI I would need to call the
appropriate library (exactly as any C, C++, Pascal or other program
would).

For thin client where the Windows panels are exported to the client so
that it runs as if it were a local application, but the code and data
access are on the server, then I can use SP2 thin client with no changes
to the normal SP2 program.
```

## Thread: Uncommented & Tested: Please look here people! — 1 msgs

### Re: Uncommented & Tested: Please look here people!

- **From:** "eRRaTiK" <regulate@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-02T09:48:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DD2S6.96407$ff.732952@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`
- **References:** `<SmNR6.94963$ff.719687@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <9f8snh$dle$1@news.netcologne.de> <ufTR6.95164$ff.722726@news-server.bigpond.net.au> <3B189289.7629F2D3@worldnet.att.net> <cy2S6.96391$ff.732533@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`

```
oh and the preceding paragraph as well in case anyone was wondering if I had
fixed up that one..

=========================================================

       F15-CONTINUE-ADDING.
           MOVE T-FIFTH TO W-COURSE.
           DISPLAY W-COURSE.
           PERFORM F16-CHECK-COURSE.
           IF VALID-COURSE = "Y"
              DISPLAY "VALID COURSE.. ADDING RECORD.."
              MOVE T-ID TO STUD-ID
              MOVE T-THIRD  TO STUD-FAMNAME
              MOVE T-FOURTH TO STUD-GIVNAME
              MOVE T-FIFTH TO STUD-COURSE
              MOVE "C" TO STUD-STATUS
      *                                             ... write record.*
              WRITE STUDENT-DETAILS
                 INVALID KEY
                    PERFORM Y90-ADD-ERROR-MESSAGE
                 NOT INVALID KEY
                    DISPLAY "Student " STUD-ID " Details Added "
              END-WRITE
           ELSE
              PERFORM Y50-COURSE-ERROR
           END-IF.

       F16-CHECK-COURSE.
           DISPLAY "NOW EXECUTING PARAGRAPH F16-CHECK-COURSE".
           MOVE "N" TO VALID-COURSE.
           DISPLAY "VALUE OF W-C1: " W-C1.
           DISPLAY "VALUE OF W-C2: " W-C2.
           DISPLAY "NOW ENTERING CONDITIONAL STATEMENT...".
           IF (W-C1 NUMERIC) AND (W-C2 ALPHABETIC)
              DISPLAY "W-C1 CONTAINS (" W-C1 ") AND IT IS NUMERIC"
              DISPLAY "W-C2 CONTAINS (" W-C2 ") AND IT IS ALPHABETIC"
              MOVE "Y" TO VALID-COURSE
           END-IF.

=========================================================

eRRaTiK
```

#### ↳ Re: Using DL/1 databases i subroutines

- **From:** mc4420@mclink.it (GiDeSa)
- **Date:** 2001-06-07T15:11:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b1f97e8.33263310@10.186.10.6>`
- **References:** `<3B14EC9E.DD06D25E@mac.com>`

```
I have made some subprograms using DL/1, in a VSE ambient.
The subprogram have the normal 'ENTRY DLITCBL'
as any batch program, as the first instruction, and 
all the EXEC DLI instructions.
Then you pre-compile the subprogram with DL/1 command precompiler.
The output, with all EXEC DLI expanded to Cobol CALL, must be then
compiled as a relocatable module, and at last linked with the main.
In this mode the subprogram automatically see the database through
the PSB loaded from main.

Giandomenico De Sanctis
```

<a id="t-bc6b8bc16b"></a>
## Thread: Great Text on Quantum Computation — 2 msgs

### Great Text on Quantum Computation

- **From:** Herwig Huener & Josella Simone Playton <webmaster@Herwig-Huener.de>
- **Date:** 2001-06-03T01:33:53+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B1977E1.4DF307D6@Herwig-Huener.de>`

```
2001-06-03 01:35:00 MEST

-> news:comp.lang.cobol, a few BCCs

A few month ago, I told about a WebSite where a
free implementation of a Quantum Computing Language
(QCL) could be found, which could serve as a
prototype for quantum computing language
elements within any language, such as COBOL2020.

Today, I tell about another text which is quite
worthwhile to read - a book this time:

"Quantum Computation and Quantum Information" by
Michael Nielsen and Ike Chuang.

The problem in understanding Quantum Computing
is a full understanding of Quantum Theory first
- but there are a few permissible shortcuts which
these authors take. They introduce in exceptionally
clear language QuBits and Quantum-gates, introducing
lighthandedly the Dirac-Notation as they go
along, and spare the reader the Schrï¿½dinger
equation and dull exercises in solving it. No
wavepackets, no hydrogen-atom, no harmonic oscillator
- for a basic understanding, these are not
(yet) needed.

As early as page 27 an understanding of quantum
teleportation is reached, quantum cryptography
and all other issues come somewhat later. After
the easy introductory parts there is, beginning
at page 61, a more detailed course in quantum
physics. The book has some 676 pages - the
greater part of which I have not yet read.

As far as I can see, the book places more
emphasis on Quantum Networks than on Quantum
Programming Languages. Also, philosophical issues
are not addressed - no "MWI or not MWI" discussion
which other authors (D. Deutsch) see as essential
in the subject. Only one Page is dedicated to the
poor Schrï¿½dinger's cat. Finally, the physical
construction of Quantum Computers is not addressed
- for that, you must look elsewhere.

I do not regret having invested the money
(DM 104.21, US$ 47.95).

Available, for instance, in

USA:

http://www.amazon.com/exec/obidos/ASIN/0521635039/o/qid=991522138/sr=8-1/ref=aps_sr_b_1_1/102-9192563-9376151

Germany:

http://www.amazon.de/exec/obidos/ASIN/0521635039/o/qid=991522113/sr=8-1/028-6420888-1569343

Maybe these URLs will be wrapped around,
but you will find your way through Amazon
websites easily.

Herwig
```

#### ↳ Re: Structured Programming

- **From:** "Paul Pigott" <paul.pigott@worldnet.att.net>

- **References:** `<w8%L6.10374$K5.863735@news1.rdc1.il.home.com>`

```
The classical definition that I learned of structured programming (at least
in the COBOL world) is programming that does not make use of the GOTO
statement.  Relying on logical data structures (PERFORM UNTIL, IF...END-IF,
EVALUATE...END-EVALUATE) to process data, rather than branching all over the
place to lots of different paragraph names with lots of different GOTO
statements.  It was developed in order to avoid spaghetti logic.

Paul

are

object
> is to have specific ways of doing things.
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> even more terms to understand.  I just stick to the terms that refer to
the
> language they were designed for.  For instance in C++ you have functions
and
> in Java you have methods.  COBOL isnt that mystical.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> modular, reusable code."  (Programming in RPG IV by Judy Yaeger).
According
> to the book peices of code that are compiled separately and called as
needed
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structured Programming

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-05T21:38:47-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9fk121$6ioi$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<w8%L6.10374$K5.863735@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <5HpS6.62501$4f7.4749984@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <9fg66f$a4r$3@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
Here, here!

If anyone really understood how "structured programming" with a read of the
same file in forty-eleven different places causes unbelievable maintenance
nightmares, we'd all go back to:

100-read-It.
       read any-file at end go to 100-read-it-exit.
       perform 200-do-this thru 200-do-this-exit.
* no reads in here
       perform 300-do-that thru 300-do-that-exit.
* no reads in here
       go to 100-read-it.
 100-read-it-exit.
       exit.

At least when I get an invalid IO on a read, I only need to look at one
place.  With the "structured code" (at least in what I work with) I could
look at 15 different reads, depending on what other routine was being
performed.

Besides, if you look at the machine code that is assembled, you'll see
plenty of "go to" statements in structured code in the form of assembler
"branch" statements.  To me, six of one and half dozen of another.

Denny
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structured Programming

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-07T19:29:09-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9fp263$67v2$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<w8%L6.10374$K5.863735@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <5HpS6.62501$4f7.4749984@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <9fg66f$a4r$3@mail.pl.unisys.com> <9fk121$6ioi$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com> <3B1E2EA3.627EFB7C@brazee.net>`

```
Howard,

I most certainly respect your expertise in this area and I most certainly do
not want to open up the "never ending" debate on structured code, but in the
code I work with, the perform read routine can be in many different places.
You know:

Perform Open-Files.
Perform Do-it.
  then within Do-It, there are various places
  where the single defined "Read" routine is
  performed also.  Reports, compares and so
  forth
Perform End-Job.
etc., etc.

Thus, when an I/O error crops up, you have to determine from which perform
the read was "performed".  In any case, it's only an opinion of mine based
on the source I work with, not necessarily the mainstay of current code.  I
have a program that has been running with the sample code I provided, and
it's been rolling along since 1984.  Didn't even have to Y2K it, since in
1984, I figured I might as well do the date like it's supposed to be.

Of course, it's kinda all academic, since Unisys COBOL provides the line
number of the source where the program blew up, so looking at dumps is not
much of an issue any more.

Denny
```

```
Usually the first thing shown in the dumps is the perform stack.
If you have a Compile listing with offsets, you can follow the perform 
stack back to which paragraph/sentence issued the failing read perform.


At least this works in the mainframe environments that I have been in.
Can't say much for the PC world.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structured Programming

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-06-10T13:10:03-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0106101210.41637f20@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9fp263$67v2$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com> <20010608113935.04512.00000133@nso-fl.aol.com>`

```
sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky) wrote in message news:<20010608113935.04512.00000133@nso-fl.aol.com>...
> In article <9fp263$67v2$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com>, "Denny Brouse"
> <denden@prodigy.net> writes:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> >


In IBM's COBOL II, PERFORMed paragraphs do not usually require
branches... the compiler plops the object code for the PERFORMed
source code right into the object code right after the PERFORM
statement (which itself is not compiled), making the code IN-LINE!
There are some limitations... it will duplicate the performed code no
more than 50 times, and there may be a limit on how deep into the
logic (PERFORM within PERFORM within... etc) they will make the
duplicated code go.  This makes it VERY efficient!

I was under the impression that this was part of the '85 standard; I
am sure that my friend from Illinois will clear up my confusion.
(Thanks, Bill! *Wink*).

Stephen J Spiro
Wizard Systems


PS: Hey, YOU could be on the J4 COBOL Standards Committee, and help
yourself and our profession!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structured Programming

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-10T16:42:23-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9g0pk5$64$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9fp263$67v2$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com> <20010608113935.04512.00000133@nso-fl.aol.com> <4145699b.0106101210.41637f20@posting.google.com>`

```
"Stephen J Spiro" <stephenjspiro@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:4145699b.0106101210.41637f20@posting.google.com...
> sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky) wrote in message
news:<20010608113935.04512.00000133@nso-fl.aol.com>...
> > In article <9fp263$67v2$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com>, "Denny
Brouse"
> > <denden@prodigy.net> writes:
 <snip>
>
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The Standard *never* specifies "implementation" or "optimization"
requirements.  ('85 Standard, earlier ones - or later ones).  Therefore
"inlining" of object code is NOT something that the Standard talks about.
"Inline performs" of SOURCE code *is* something that was introduced with the
'85 Standard but may or may not be "implemented" (via object code) that is
inline, out-of-line, or via a CALL/Branch mechanism.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structured Programming

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-06-21T15:01:57-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0106211401.54ef510a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9fp263$67v2$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com> <20010608113935.04512.00000133@nso-fl.aol.com> <4145699b.0106101210.41637f20@posting.google.com> <444b2a14.0106210716.199eafff@posting.google.com>`

```
pchq45@hotmail.com (Peter Chong) wrote in message news:<444b2a14.0106210716.199eafff@posting.google.com>...
> stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro) wrote in message > 
> > In IBM's COBOL II, PERFORMed paragraphs do not usually require
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Just thought from COBOL II IN-LINE PERFORM... ;-)

*-------------
Actually, I was referring to OUT-OF-LINE PERFORM.... the IBM mainframe
compiler puts them IN-LINE in the object code, not the source code.

Indentations are a problem with all of the "nested" structores (IF,
EVALUATE, PERFORM, etc).  When it gets awkward to read, Just code an
out-of-line PERFORM, and continue nesting from Column 12 again.  I've
seen 40 (more than that, even...) END-IF's in a row. Worse, a bunch of
END-IF's, an ELSE, then a bunch more END-IF's. Sheesh, trying to match
up the ELSE with the appropriate IF is a pain in the neck! I would
fire a programmer who wrote like that.

Stephen J Spiro
Wizard Systems
```

```
Stephen J Spiro wrote:

>
> Indentations are a problem with all of the "nested" structores (IF,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Accepting your comments about style, it is still a 'horror' if you are looking at 40 plus conditionals, whether
using IF or EVALUATE. Back to Decision Tables - determine what the rules are, can you then shorten the 'logic'
by testing groups :-

       01 Something-Type                pic 9(03).
            88 AType                           value 1..
            88 BType                           value 2.
            88
            88 GType                           value  7.
            88 AtoGType                     value 1 thru 7.
            88 HType                           value 8.
            88
            88 LType                            value 12.
            88 HtoLType                      value 8 thru 12.
            88 MType etc....

        Evaluate true
              when AtoG type - either sub-set of Evaluates or perhaps preferably,
                                           Perform AtoG-Paragraph OR Invoke "aToG-types"
               when HtoLType............
               when MtoQType .....
                when etc.........
        End-evaluate

Jimmy
```

```
Whoops ! You done it again Buster <G> 'Connection to File Denied" - then a snotty message for you - you are
violating their service agreement.

Stephen - however, I would like to get a copy to read. Can always e-mail me privately.

Jimmy
```

```
IIRC, the compiler checks unnecessary when statement until met the
proper condition. so, more higher probabilty of hit the when statement
to move up to top of evalauate statement stack to save some un
necessary check of when statements. Higher chance to hit when
statement to first and to lower change to hit when statement to last.
;-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structured Programming

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-22T21:37:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B33BA49.AC7B4762@home.com>`
- **References:** `<9fp263$67v2$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com> <20010608113935.04512.00000133@nso-fl.aol.com> <4145699b.0106101210.41637f20@posting.google.com> <444b2a14.0106210716.199eafff@posting.google.com> <4145699b.0106211401.54ef510a@posting.google.com> <3B328464.89533758@home.com> <444b2a14.0106221247.5ad49ef1@posting.google.com>`

```


Peter Chong wrote:

> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message > Accepting your comments about style, it is still a 'horror' if you are looking at 40 plus conditionals, whether
> > using IF or EVALUATE. Back to Decision Tables - determine what the rules are, can you then shorten the 'logic'
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> ;-)

Agree if you use Pareto's 80/20% rule. But in the real world us mere mortals think logically in numeric or alphabetic ascending sequence, as  in the code I used to
illustrate. Also agreed, logical 'greatest hit sequence' applies in the mainframe world when handling zillions of records and can have a dramatic impact on run times. Us
little guys in the PC world ......?

I wonder how many times people do fine-tune IFs or EVALUATEs. Sometimes  you instinctively know you've got more of one than another. Department store retail would suggest
more cash than credit - but I wonder. It even gets muddied by direct debit as a branch of 'cash'. Also don't forget they plug their own credit cards to entice you back -
so who knows what the percentage split is. Probably varies from store group to store group or chain to chain, e.g. Sears may have very different percentages to say,
Walmart..

In big installations I would think most fine tuning will originate in the Maintenance Department where they have the Operations people bitching at them about the slowness
of a particular run.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structured Programming

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-22T17:21:10-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9h0gd6$n5o$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9fp263$67v2$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com> <20010608113935.04512.00000133@nso-fl.aol.com> <4145699b.0106101210.41637f20@posting.google.com> <444b2a14.0106210716.199eafff@posting.google.com> <4145699b.0106211401.54ef510a@posting.google.com> <3B328464.89533758@home.com> <444b2a14.0106221247.5ad49ef1@posting.google.com> <3B33BA49.AC7B4762@home.com>`

```
For IBM mainframe shops (especially large applications that are
"time/response sensitive") "tuning" of IF's and EVALUATEs is (and has been
for years) one of the easiest and best things that can be done.

"Way back" in the days of OS/VS COBOL and CAPEX (before CA-Optimizer) there
was an "easy" way to get a listing showing where (what source code line OR
object code sections) you were spending the most "time" (resources) in.
Therefore, it was fairly typical (at least in the shops that I worked in) to
do a systems test (not unit test) where you ran several days worth of "real
world" data thru this reporting feature and "moved" any IF (later evaluate)
statements around so that the "most common" tests were first.

OBVIOUSLY, this didn't make sense if the test was only done a "couple" of
times in a multi-hour batch run.  But if, on the other  hand, you were in a
"tight loop" and the same test was done multi-millions of times and you were
trying to get an 8-hour batch job done in 7-hours, this was certainly ONE
easy way to make some progress.  (Obviously, there were LOTS of others, and
this one rarely did the trick by itself).

If you don't have CA-Optimizer, but do have the IBM "debug tool" this same
functionality is still available with the FREQ command (and can  be used in
a "batch debugging" session).  This only tells how many times a specific
statement is executed, not how much time/resource is spent with it.  For
that, I think people still use STROBE or Omegamon.

Similar tools are available with some (not all) other COBOL products and
environments, but they certainly are still used in the IBM mainframe world.
```

```
Joseph J Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote 
> Stephen J Spiro wrote:
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> I would buy a obtain a program that produced a listing with level bars in it, before I fired a perfectly good programmer.

People who code like that are NOT perfectly good prograqmmers.   They
may write code that WORKS, but that is not sufficient in a commercial
programming environment.
PROFESSIONAL programmers write code that works, is reasonably
efficient (given the appropriate trade-offs), and is easy to maintain.
 The people who put 40 END-IFs in a row are not professionals.  In
every case that I can recall, they had already been fired.

Stephen J Spiro
Wizard Systems
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structured Programming

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-24T11:05:32-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9h5364$76rg$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<9fp263$67v2$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com> <20010608113935.04512.00000133@nso-fl.aol.com> <4145699b.0106101210.41637f20@posting.google.com> <444b2a14.0106210716.199eafff@posting.google.com> <4145699b.0106211401.54ef510a@posting.google.com> <3B33323B.7040406@iinet.net.au> <4145699b.0106221008.2b02c939@posting.google.com> <3B35FBAD.5070104@iinet.net.au>`

```
You make a good point, Joe.  39 of those END-IFs may have already existed
before the maintenance programmer was given the task of adding one more
condition.  In a situation like that, the programmer was likely allowed a
short time to implement the change.  Several years ago, I was making a Y2K
change and in the same program I spotted a non-Y2K related nested, compound
IF that was very difficult to follow.  I replaced it with a couple of
EVALUATE statements and proved that logically its results were the same as
the old IF.  I was told by the project manager to not implement the modified
code for the reason that that same IF statement appeared in several other
programs and that this change was beyond the scope of the immediate task.
Good intentions don't always end in good results.  I left the EVALUATE
statement in the program but commented it out so sometime in the future, it
might have a chance to be implemented.
```

```
Because in one case the guy who wrote it came back for a visit, and I
asked him.
He was very proud of his unmaintainable code.  Thought it was clever
and efficient.
Another one I met on another assignment; he was a little more
disingenuous -- he admitted it was for "job security" -- although it
obviously didn't work, in his case.
Many mainframe shops have a standard which requires a note with all
maintenance in production programs.  This code (where I saw it), was
not the result of such maintenance.

I see it less and less these days; apparently managers tolerate it
less and less.  Or programmers who did it found themselves responsible
for maintaining similar code written by OTHER people, and realized the
drawbacks...


Stephen J Spiro
Wizard Systems
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structured Programming

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiammD@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-09T13:48:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B222913.F3D5DCDA@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<w8%L6.10374$K5.863735@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <5HpS6.62501$4f7.4749984@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <9fg66f$a4r$3@mail.pl.unisys.com> <9fk121$6ioi$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Denny Brouse wrote:
> 
> Here, here!
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> "branch" statements. 

Denny, please, this is a big red herring. "Structured Programming"
refers to the source only and addresses issues such as readability,
maintainability, etc. It has nothing to do with the binary file created
by compiling & linking, which will always contain branches or jumps. A
machine code which contained instructions such as "DO WHILE" would be
CISC, indeed.

LiamD 

(my standard disclaimer, my experience is limited to IBM mainframes &
related software)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structured Programming

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-12T23:50:44-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ga66p$b0h0$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<w8%L6.10374$K5.863735@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <5HpS6.62501$4f7.4749984@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <9fg66f$a4r$3@mail.pl.unisys.com> <9fk121$6ioi$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com> <3B222913.F3D5DCDA@optonline.net>`

```
Liam wrote:

<snip>
Denny, please, this is a big red herring. "Structured Programming"
refers to the source only and addresses issues such as readability,
maintainability, etc. It has nothing to do with the binary file created
by compiling & linking, which will always contain branches or jumps.
<unsnip>

Liam,

I apologize for the "red herring" thing.  Perhaps I need a better example of
"structured" code than what I personally deal with, i.e.:

PERFORM 000-OPEN-FILES.
PERFORM 100-DO-WHATEVER.
PERFORM 800-CLOSE-FILES.
PERFORM 900-EOJ.

Maintainability is my issue here.  It is super to add a new file to
000-OPEN-FILES and 800-CLOSE-FILES, but the 100-DO-WHATEVER section is where
(in perhaps my case only) it gets awfully sticky. As I stated in my post to
Howard, it just seems to take me longer to insert enhancement code and/or
fix existing problems in the programs that are "structured" (qualified as in
the example above) than in the older "modular" type programs.  The main
thing I seem to have a problem with is inserting new code in deeply nested
"IF" statements that were created expressly for the purpose of eliminating
the "go to" statement.  To me, I still need to understand where the "If" and
the "else" goes to, regardless of whether it is explicitly defined or not,
in order to insert new code that won't break down with the next new
enhancement.  And if the program breaks, I need to understand how it got to
where it was when it broke.  And in my environment, I don't have the luxury
of "time" to figure this out.

I'm sure it's simply because I wasn't raised with the newer style.  And I
humbly concede that "what is", "is", and I just need to deal with it.  At
least until retirement <G>.

Denny
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structured Programming

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <rwstyles@worldnet.att.net>

```
    Exactly!

    This is why I still tend to use this sort of structure when
writing
a "process file" sort of program.  The sort of program below is
easy to understand and modify later, even though it necessarily
includes a GO TO in 100-DO-WHATEVER.

    Of course, it could be written with a perform until on that
paragraph, and an EXIT PARAGRAPH internally.

   But since "NO GO TO'S" is a religion to some people,
they don't care. The old "I am right, you are wrong" mindset.

  Nobody really needed a pair of giant 800 year old statues
anyway.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structured Programming

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-06-14T11:15:23-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0106141015.13307ac9@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<w8%L6.10374$K5.863735@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <5HpS6.62501$4f7.4749984@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <9fg66f$a4r$3@mail.pl.unisys.com> <9fk121$6ioi$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com> <3B222913.F3D5DCDA@optonline.net> <9ga66p$b0h0$1@newssvr06-en0.news.prodigy.com> <GF3W6.17$LA1.81638@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
I learned programming around 1975...  When I was still a junior
programmer, my employer, Irving Trust Co, in NYC, brought in some
people to conduct a two-week course in structured programming. 
Everyone in the department took the course.  Much of what we used was
IBM-prepared material.
This was the first time I had been exposed to any methodology; and I
realized that I had been writing "garbage that worked" up until then.

Structured Modular programming involves much more than the elimination
of GO TO.  It is actually very much like OO programming --- each
logical process is encapsulated in a module (in structured
programming, we would say that each PERFORMed routine has "cohesion".)
 There are a lot of rules.

The one which causes the elimination of GO TO, is the rule that says
every logical construction must have only one entrance and one exit. 
In COBOL, this is done with the PERFORM or CALL statements.
  
There are only 3 kinds of logical constructions that are used:
PROCESS (Do....)
IF ... THEN (Do....) ELSE (Do....)
DO... WHILE.
The DO .. UNTIL is a special case of DO .. WHILE.

Another rule is that you code from the top down.  This is NOT the same
as "Top Down Programming".  It means you do not code any module unless
you have already coded all of the logical connectors to all of the
logically higher elements in the program.

 Along with a few other rules, I have found that writing programs this
way
1. Makes design easier. The program naturally organizes around the
structure.
2. Makes coding easier.  You do not have to keep track of which GO TO
statements need to be 'fleshed out'... it has been so long since I
coded any other way, I can't remember what I am doing easier!  I DO
know that, the more  I discipline myself to do the code in accordance
witrh the rules, the easier it is... I have less and less code to go
back to and change.  Most of the serious programmers that I know, who
use this methodology, agree with me on that.
3. Makes maintenance easier.  I am proud to say that I have often been
told by people who came after me, that my programs are easy to change
and update.

I wish I could find a good reference book to recommend, but I have not
been paying attention to that sort of book for a long time...


Consider a program organized like this:

PROCEDURE DIVISION.

0000-MAINLINE.

    PERFORM 1000-INITIAL-HOUSEKEEPING.
    PERFORM 2000-READ-AND-PROCESS
        UNTIL DRIVER-FILE-AT-END.
    PERFORM 9000-CLEANUP.

1000-INITIAL-HOUSEKEEPING.

    PERFORM 1100-OPEN-FILES.
    PERFORM 1200-DATE-ROUTINES.
    PERFORM 1200-INITIALIZE-REPORT-HEADERS,
    PERFORM 1400-MAYBE-MORE-STUFF.

1200-DATE-ROUTINES.

    PERFORM 1210-SYSTEM-DATES.
    PERFORM 1220-MASTER-DATE-FILE.
    PERFORM 1230-FORMAT-DATES.

EACH level logically includes everything below it.  When I first wrote
this example, I did date routines first.  Then I realized that the
MASTER-DATE-FILE had to be opened.  It was extremely simple to move
the OPEN routine in front of the DATE routine. (I COULD have done the
OPEN in the DATE routine, but I think this way is easier to maintain
... all of the related elements are together.) Had I been using
straight line code with GO TO, I would have had to insert the OPEN
code in front, and possibly wound up with some spaghetti ... this
early in the program.

(It could be argued that OPENing the DATE file is logically part of
the MASTER-DATE-FILE processing, and that I could have done it there. 
However, OPENing the file is a system/compiler requirement [not all
computer languages require an explicit OPEN statement], and is
physically but not LOGICALLY related to obtaining data from the file. 
I place all of the open statements together because they are all the
same kind of system/compiler-required operations.)

Stephen J Spiro
Wizard Systems
```

```
Learnt Structured as part of a 1 year programming course (we did other
stuff, too - English, Accountancy, etc etc etc).

The other thing I have found as an advantage is that I believe it helps you
to learn other programming languages - or rather, it makes it easier to code
in them whilst knowing bugger all about the language.

Because you have a specific and detailed structure to hang your mistakes on.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Structured Programming

- **From:** "Paul Pigott" <paul.pigott@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-07T16:34:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<k2OT6.68613$4f7.5206041@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<w8%L6.10374$K5.863735@news1.rdc1.il.home.com> <5HpS6.62501$4f7.4749984@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <9fg66f$a4r$3@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
You're absolutely correct.  Or, as some wag wrote, "A Real Programmer can
code Fortran in any language."  The presence of GOTOs no more indicate
spaghetti logic, than their absence indicates structure.  It is simply that
the extensive use of GOTOs tends to lead one to spaghetti logic quicker.

Paul

Jim Watts <james.watts@unisys.com> wrote in message
news:9fg66f$a4r$3@mail.pl.unisys.com...

> >The classical definition that I learned of structured programming (at
least
> >in the COBOL world) is programming that does not make use of the GOTO
> >statement.  Relying on logical data structures (PERFORM UNTIL,

> >EVALUATE...END-EVALUATE) to process data, rather than branching all over
the
> >place to lots of different paragraph names with lots of different GOTO
> >statements.  It was developed in order to avoid spaghetti logic.

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

to

or

call
> >> them a procedure if you want.  Sometimes these paragraphs are called a
> >> process.  Usually Prcedure is what the entire procedure division does.
> >>
> >> In my experience or lack thereof newer books just invent terms so you
have
> >> even more terms to understand.  I just stick to the terms that refer to
> >the
> >> language they were designed for.  For instance in C++ you have
functions
> >and
> >> in Java you have methods.  COBOL isnt that mystical.
> >>
> >> On the other hand the term Procedure is used in my newer RPG book to
refer
> >> to single procedure programs with a single entry point.  According to
the
> >> book, "Procedures are IBM's most recent RPG enhancement for developing
> >> modular, reusable code."  (Programming in RPG IV by Judy Yaeger).
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
```

## Thread: Table Manipulation to Center Text for Output — 6 msgs

### re: Table Manipulation to Center Text for Output

- **From:** "Rukie" <agricus@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-03T14:34:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jWrS6.45254$qs3.20447475@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com>`

```
for example:

01 WS-TEXT            PIC X(80)
        VALUE "HELLO WORLD".

01 WS-TEXT-LINE REDEFINES WS-TEXT.
        05 WS-TEXT-LETTER OCCURS 80 TIMES
                    PIC X(01).

is there a way through table manipulation to center the text in the table
for output?


please email me at agricus@home.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Table Manipulation to Center Text for Output

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-06-03T11:02:27-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B1A5F92.D5D4012E@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<jWrS6.45254$qs3.20447475@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com>`

```
Rukie wrote:

> for example:
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> please email me at agricus@home.com

 One way is to  redefine your output line 40 times with two sub-fields under
each redefine: sub-field 1 is of length 1, 2, 3 etc. up to 40; the second
being a filler to make up 80 ch.  Then count the letters in your input text
string - in this case "Hello world" - and work out which of the redefines you
should move it to.

Crude but effective.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Table Manipulation to Center Text for Output

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2001-06-03T14:34:52-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0106031334.619b5aa1@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<jWrS6.45254$qs3.20447475@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com> <3B1A5F92.D5D4012E@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
regarding Peter Lacey's suggestion about Rukie's question

Without wishing to do all the homework, I would prefer to use reference
modification rather than a large number of redefinitions.

Robert
```

```
:>is there a way through table manipulation to center the text in the table
:>for output?

1. Calculate the length of the text (may involve dropping leading and trailing
spaces)

2. Determine the calculations required to center a string of length X in a
field of length Y.

3. Do you own homework.
```

<a id="t-240d84022a"></a>
## Thread: Shall I take Cobol? — 24 msgs

### Shall I take Cobol?

- **From:** "sergey drachev" <sdrachev@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-06-03T10:36:52-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MTrS6.596$g62.275360@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Hi,

I'm studying Computer Programming and Systems Analysis at a private computer
school in Toronto, ON, and the program includes:

1. Computer Operations and Applications (2001)
2. Programming Logic and Design
3. Cobol (2001)
4. Visual Basic
5. Unix
6. Visual C++
7. Networking Essentials
8. Systems Analysis

My question is "Shall I take Cobol (3GL) or shall I change it for a 4GL like
C++ or Java?

I want to specialize in developing custom databases, and I don't know if
Cobol is going to be useful, or if there's enough work for Cobol
programmers. My friend - a beginning programmer - from New York, who just
finished college, says Cobol is not a good idea.

Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: Shall I take Cobol?

- **From:** "eRRaTiK" <regulate@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-03T15:44:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7XsS6.98929$ff.756932@news-server.bigpond.net.au>`
- **References:** `<MTrS6.596$g62.275360@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Initially, when I started my course in COBOL (because I "had to") I didn't
think it was a good idea either. I had the preconception, probably from
hearing other people talking, that COBOL was a "dead language", "popular in
the 70's", etc. I must say however, having completed the course (my final
exam is in two weeks) my opinion of the language and its usefulness has
changed entirely.

A very interesting point my lecturer brought up in the final lecture was
that many languages come and go, hundreds are created but only a half a
dozen or so have lasted as commercially or academically viable systems
languages. COBOL being one of them. It is of particular relevance in the
commercial arena. Many legacy and mainframe systems utilise COBOL's robust
and strong index file system to maintain complex databases, and hundreds and
thousands of records. COBOL is one of those languages that develop as
technology develops, unlike many languages that don't have much support when
it comes to new technology.

A question I think a lot of people need to ask themselves:
If it's such a useless language, why has it lasted so long and why do people
continue working on trying to improve it?

As for job prospects, I just did a search on an employment site using the
keyword 'Cobol' and the search returned at least 15 positions that required
some knowledge of Cobol programming. Does that answer your question about
jobs out there?

Cobol and databases go well. Did you know that SQL server uses multiple
index tables (the concept that was popular in Cobol long before anybody even
heard of SQL) and that SQL and Cobol can communicate and share data
effectively?

I can also tell you that I've programmed in Java and C++ and (from what I've
seen) neither languages provide an emphasis on database development, they're
more likely to be used for application development.

Of course, what it comes down to is what you want to do and where you want
to work. Perhaps you could search around newspapers, employment agencies,
and employment websites or maybe even ask the course
instructors/co-ordinators what they would recommend based on where you want
to go?

Hope this helps,

eRRaTiK

"sergey drachev" <sdrachev@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
```
