###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Handling files with varying length records. Help - it's driving me nuts!!

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-06T17:40:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B1E6BA1.44E43396@home.com>`
- **References:** `<PibT6.5491$Fk7.43026@news.indigo.ie> <9fk49j$4jrda$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de> <5CA7835527E81AE4.5092EABE23B9C511.FDCAC9656C3F2C04@lp.airnews.net>`

```


SkippyPB wrote:

> >If I understand correctly, you are taking a file in (let's say it's 100
> >bytes long).  Adding another hundred bytes (making it 200 bytes long), then
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> ponder.

Fergus,

As you are an old hand I don't want to teach grandmother to suck eggs - but just
in case you missed the subtlety here - the business of different record sizes.
It's a problem I came up against a couple of months back, and both Bill Klein and
Pete Dashwood helped me through it.

In OO I wanted a generic class to handle Line Sequential (but same concept as
Sequential). So I have this 'common' program which says "record varying in size
from a to b depending upon c" All my Line Sequentials are in fact FIXED length
but to use that common program I have to tell it is getting "a varying depending
on" - and I pass the size factor to the common program before I start processing
for EACH Line Sequential I'm using.

Here's an interesting one - I feed Ascii-delimiteds(CSVs)  to it and they are
FIXED length, say 100 chars, 248 chars etc. Didn't work ! Because the compiler
picks up on the fact that the Line Sequentials are FIXED length and doesn't 'buy'
my occurs varying. So initially to get CSVs into the system had to have SEPARATE
programs to accept the FIXED length CSVs. Once into the application then my 'own'
files can use the occurs varying.

Take your input and output - Let's assume your header record is 100 chars, but
then you are adding stuff to it for output - is this a fixed amount or varying ?.
(Can you specify the maximum size it will grow to). You can't use the same syntax
:-

    Input file - varying from a to b depending on c( c = 100)
    Output file - varying from a to b depending on c( c = 100)

You must have optimum  size for output file; so the second entry above becomes :-

    Output file - varying from a to b depending on c ( where c = 150,  2,000,
                                                        2,500, 3,000  etc.)

Above may be off track - but just in case.....

Jimmy
```

<a id="t-4617d11b2a"></a>
## Thread: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods) — 48 this month / 52 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-05T19:19:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Just for the "record" the following source code will compile "cleanly" and
is semi-valid with IBM mainframe compilers (discussion afterwards), (please
assume correct "columns" if they don't show up in your browser)

        Identification Division.
          Program-Id.  TestPgm.
        Data Division.
         Working-Storage Section.
        01  Test-Field   Pic X  Value "Y".
       Procedure Division.
        Main-Para.
            If Test-Field = "N"
               Next Sentence
            Else
              Display "Always"
            End-If
            Display "In same sentence"
       *       No period - full-stop here
        New-Para.
             Display "New Paragraph *and* Sentence"
        Fall-Thru.
              Stop Run.

****

Reasons -

1) IBM has a documented extension that allows NEXT SENTENCE with END-IF

2) IBM has an "undocumented" extension (which gets at least a W-level
message) that allows the "final" period before a paragraph-name (starting in
the A-margin) to be omitted.  (This "extension" *will* go away with the next
COBOL Standard - when the A-/B-margin distinction goes away)

Again, I do *not* recommend the use of either of these, but did want to
comment on whether or not this was "valid" source code (and the fact that
this depends on compiler used and acceptance of error/warning/informational
messages).
```

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>

```
> Ignoring all the variances you can make with periods (full stops - just in case
> she's reading this !), Next Sentence etc., I would suggest the use of End-if is
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


You have two 'else', does this imply that you have:

          IF ( condition )
              action
              ELSE IF ( condition 2 )
                  action
              ELSE
                  action
                   END-IF
          END-IF      

or it is a figment of the tabs you used ?

I use:

         IF ( condition )
             action 1
         ELSE
         IF ( condition 2 )
             ......
         ELSE
             .....
         END-IF
         END-IF

> in exactly the same way as using Evaluate :-
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>     End-evaluate
 
Well I prefer:

      EVALUATE
      WHEN ....
          ......
      WHEN
          ....
      END-EVALUATE

(note also that I use Modula-2 style where reserved words are always
upper case [my editor generates all reserved words in upper case for me
with keystroke macros], and user words are mixed case.)


I have been doing some programming in Python where the indentation is
_everything_.  There are no scope terminators _except_ the indentation. 
At first this was frustrating, especially until I got the editor to
replace tabs with spaces automatically.

After a while one gets used to this and it becomes entirely natural to
use the enforced indentation correctly.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3B1DD625.7A50077C@ont.com> <3B1F3794.9FB631D3@Azonic.co.nz>`

Richard Plinston wrote:

> You have two 'else', does this imply that you have:
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No, without getting too detailed it was meant to convey what you have above. What you
have below is perfectly acceptable, but to my eye, I like to catch the indentations.

>
> I use:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I think I'm correct in saying Don Tees uses similar to above. I recall an example where
he was testing a whole series of flags - then if you try to start indenting, (my style),
you would run off the end of the page !

When it is not the simplest IF, such as :-

        if    QuitFlag
               EXIT METHOD/PARAGRAPH/PERFORM/.PROGRAM etc.
        End-if

for preference I use EVALUATE - I just like the way it 'forces' you to indent for
clarity.

I like your technique of CAPITALIZING reserved words, (something Wilson Price does in
that book Pete just recently complimented). I don't enforce it on myself, using
primarily lowercase but ALWAYS capitals to start :-

        Working-Storage Section
        Local-Storage Section
        Procedure Division

and to highlight,  always use :- EXIT-METHOD or EXIT-PERFORM  Similarly, for the odd
paragraph performs I may have in an OO method (paragraphs are really the exception, not
the norm), I'll use :-

*>---------------------
Method-id. "something".
*>--------------------
etc.......
        if  this ?????
            perform GET-THE-COUNT
        etc........
      Some more stuff...............

EXIT METHOD. *> If I don't include this it will drop through and execute
                            *> GET-THE-COUNT

GET-THE-COUNT.
        add something to something2 etc...
    .
End Method "something".
*>--------------------

I still use the DOS Text editor that came with MS COBOL V5 - but if I was coding
entirely in the Net Express IDE, in addition to being able to 'colour' (pick your
foreground/background), to highlight typos, reserved words, Level 78s etc..(you can go
really TECHNICOLOR on this feature - making CINEMASCOPE look like an amateur !) - forget
what it is called, but there is a 'spellchecker' which lets me set defaults - possibly
if I typed 'evaluate' I could get it to spit out 'EVALUATE' just like your Modula-2
technique below.

>
> (note also that I use Modula-2 style where reserved words are always
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> use the enforced indentation correctly.

Let's quote Michael, "It's the artist, not the paintbrush". Mind you, if it is a really
good quality paintbrush...... <G>

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-06-24T15:13:38-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0106241413.6df46f12@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3B1DD625.7A50077C@ont.com> <3B1F3794.9FB631D3@Azonic.co.nz> <3B1EA8F6.37791D0D@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote 

> and to highlight,  always use :- EXIT-METHOD or EXIT-PERFORM  

*-----------------
Yakh! EXIT-METHOD is the equivalent of EXIT-PROGRAM. There should be
only one per Method.  One entrance, one exit.  You can have spaghetti
code in methods as easily as in a main program.
There is NEVER an excuse for EXIT-PERFORM (or EXIT-PARAGRAPH or EXIT
SECTION). The same functionality can be provided by coding an
appropriate VARYING or UNTIL in your PERFORM statement.  These new
"features" in the next standard are an abomination which completely
counters the trend in the language to encourage/enforce structured,
modular programming.  One entrance, one exit. We get rid of NEXT
SENTENCE and introduce THREE new anti-modular constructs. Yes, it
really is a "committee product".


Stephen J Spiro
Wizard Systems
```

First, let me say that my comment wasn't directed at you directly,
Stephen.

I've been sort of a silent reader of this newsgroup for a while now, and
perhaps I don't understand the purpose of the newsgroup.  I thought the
purpose might be to keep users of COBOL up to date on latest
developments, and perhaps trade techniques and tips for becoming better
programmers.  Certainly, there is some of that going on.  However, it
seems that most of the time people are arguing silly things like IF vs.
Evaluate, or Perform vs. Perform-thru or whether to nest an IF or use
AND.....things like that.

One of the strengths of COBOL (IMHO) is it's flexibility.  There are
always going to be people that don't code in a "logical" manner, but
that is true about any programming language.  The programmers that
really care about what they're doing, and are concerned about creating
good, easily maintainable code are going to do it, whether or not they
use Perform or Perform-thru.

What do I consider important??  I consider important anything I don't
already know.  For instance, I've never used most of the intrinsic
functions available in COBOL.  I'd like to hear if and how others have
used them.  Tips or techniques that people have used to solve certain
problems.  I use certain techniques to make my programs easier to debug
in the event of an abend.....I'd like to hear what others do in that
same regard.  Bottom line, anything that I can learn from someone else
that helps make me a better and more efficient programmer.



Stephen J Spiro wrote:

> "David P. Bretz" <DBretz@mescoma.com> wrote

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Member, J4 COBOL Standards Committee
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-25T14:23:15-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9h832v$7vo$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3B1DD625.7A50077C@ont.com> <3B1F3794.9FB631D3@Azonic.co.nz> <3B1EA8F6.37791D0D@home.com> <4145699b.0106241413.6df46f12@posting.google.com> <3B36A907.98A26C73@mescoma.com>`

```
Just a comment on the introduction of the enhanced EXIT statements and
"structured programming".

As has been clear in recent discussions, there is a LOT of different
definitions of WHAT exactly Structured Programming (not Structured
methodology) means.  At least one definition says that you need to have a
"single entrance/single exit" for each procedure.

The new EXIT statements (unlike the NEXT SENTENCE phrase and the GO TO
statement) actually ENFORCE the behavior.  Because the "exit" spot is an
un-named point (end of PERFORM, PARAGRAPH, or SECTION) and there is (by
definition) ONLY ONE per procedure, this is viewed by SOME as enforcing
"structured programming".

For those shops that (today) "enforce" the GO TO xxxx-EXIT paragraph
structure and this is the ONLY time that a GO TO is allowed and there are no
paragraphs/sections between the GO TO and the EXIT paragraphs, this new
"format" works the same way and is just as structured (or unstructured -
depending on your definition) as the older technique.  The advantage that it
has, is that it is IMPOSSIBLE to create spaghetti code with the new features
because you are unable to put a "new" exit or go-to in the middle of the
existing procedure.

  ***

From a "real world" perspective, I really like the new features as it allows
a (structured) method of doing an "immediate get out" of a procedure - when
a specific error (or other) situation is detected.  This gets rid of the
need of either GO TO or deeply nested (or repeated) IF statements.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-26T13:07:58+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b37dfed_3@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3B1DD625.7A50077C@ont.com> <3B1F3794.9FB631D3@Azonic.co.nz> <3B1EA8F6.37791D0D@home.com> <4145699b.0106241413.6df46f12@posting.google.com> <3B36A907.98A26C73@mescoma.com> <9h832v$7vo$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
So, If I understand you correctly, Bill there can only be ONE  "EXIT
PERFORM" or EXIT PARAGRAPH
statement (for each accompanying PERFORM or paragraph, obviously) in the
code?

What happens if I have a condition where I want to do something different
before exiting...like this:

some paragraph.
      ....
      yada yada yada
      if some condition
          do something-1
                if it failed
                  exit paragraph
                end-if
      else
          do something-2
                if it failed
                   exit paragraph
       end-if
*> it didn't fail....
       do something-3
       exit paragraph
       .
next-paragraph.

I have 3 exit paragraph statements. Is this legal?

As these could also be interspersed with EXIT SECTION, and EXIT PERFORM
statements, doesn't it strike you as being a bit of a mess to have to look
through all this stuff searching for possible exits?

If I Section the code and use a GO TO a single exit point (as I do
currently, and I don't care what the rest of you think about it...<G>) at
least I only have to look for GO TOs, not a number of different EXIT
statements.

In practise most of my sections are fairly small anyway, but they DO have a
single exit at the end of them (whether it is branched to or not...it's just
habit. I feel comfortable doing it that way, my programs work, and no-one
has ever complained about maintaining them <G>)

To be fair, these days it is really rare for me to use GO TO to get me out
of a section of code or a Method, but I reserve the right to when
circumstances require it. (I know there are those of you out there who will
argue it is NEVER required, but can we just agree to differ on this? I'm
heartily sick of piddling arguments about code that persuade nobody and we
all end up coding the way we want to anyway, so, don't expect me to enter
into correspondence on this...)

This post is about the proposed use of EXIT PERFORM, EXIT PARAGRAPH, and
EXIT SECTION, NOT about use of Sections, paragraphs, and GO TOs.

Appreciate any comments, Bill.

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>

- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3B1DD625.7A50077C@ont.com> <3B1F3794.9FB631D3@Azonic.co.nz> <3B1EA8F6.37791D0D@home.com> <4145699b.0106241413.6df46f12@posting.google.com> <3B36A907.98A26C73@mescoma.com> <9h832v$7vo$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3b37dfed_3@Usenet.com>`

```
No what I am saying is that all EXIT PARAGRAPH statements within the same
paragraph go to the same exit point.  Similarly, all EXIT PERFORMs within
the scope of a single PERFORM go to a single exit for that PERFORM and all
EXIT SECTIONs go to a single exit of that section.

This means that a "single exit point" per procedure is enforced.  While GO
TO and Next Sentence can both have MULTIPLE "exit points" from within the
same procedure.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-06-26T12:25:45-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0106261125.61aa728@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3B1DD625.7A50077C@ont.com> <3B1F3794.9FB631D3@Azonic.co.nz> <3B1EA8F6.37791D0D@home.com> <4145699b.0106241413.6df46f12@posting.google.com> <3B36A907.98A26C73@mescoma.com> <9h832v$7vo$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3b37dfed_3@Usenet.com> <9h8qhg$dme$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote 
> No what I am saying is that all EXIT PARAGRAPH statements within the same
> paragraph go to the same exit point.  Similarly, all EXIT PERFORMs within
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bill Klein

Come on, Bill! Paragraphs end at the last sentence. An EXIT PARAGRAPH
takes you out of the paragraph wherever statement is. We are not
talking about some kind of conceptual meta-code, we are talking about
COBOL source code, and EXIT PARAGRAPH is not the last sentence in the
paragraph in the source code.  GO TO an exit is not structured code,
either.

       x    y
#--->||||||||     # is the perform statement
A      V    V     x is the EXIT PARAGRAPH
|<-----|    |     y is the last statement in the paragraph
|<----------|     A is an "up" arrow
                  V is a "down" arrow
                       | are statements
       x    y
|||||||||||||---->#    # is the next paragraph on a fall-thru
       V          A    x is the EXIT PARAGRAPH
       |-------- >|    y is the last statement in the paragraph
                        


I understand that all of these constructions may be convenient, and
may, sometimes, even simplify the code, particularly in small
programs/modules.  But they also make it possible  to expand into
really bad code. Make that PROBABLE.
I will stick with strict rules for programming; my experience is that
those programs are easy to maintain until somebody introduces
non-structured elements.

Stephen J Spiro
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-26T14:54:02-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9hap9o$52$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3B1DD625.7A50077C@ont.com> <3B1F3794.9FB631D3@Azonic.co.nz> <3B1EA8F6.37791D0D@home.com> <4145699b.0106241413.6df46f12@posting.google.com> <3B36A907.98A26C73@mescoma.com> <9h832v$7vo$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3b37dfed_3@Usenet.com> <9h8qhg$dme$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <4145699b.0106261125.61aa728@posting.google.com>`

```
Come ON Stephen,

 The definition of the EXIT PARAGRAPH is *very* carefully written so that it
DOES go to an "implicit continue" immediately after the last statement in
the paragraph.  This means that there is NO way to cause "confusion" in
maintenance.  There is nothing that you can add or subtract that is going to
make this have "crossing" logic paths.  This is WHY having a "single exit"
in a procedure is "maintained" and enforced (unlike both GO TO and NEXT
SENTENCE which *can* lead to multiple exit points from the same procedure
and crossing logic paths).

The EXIT PARAGRAPH statement *never* allows for a "fall-thru" from the
bottom of a paragraph UNLESS that paragraph were already coded to do a
fall-thru.  This is WHY the wording of where the "implicit continue" occurs
was so carefully written in the FCD.

P.S.  There is ONE problem which I raised long ago - and which others have
"acknowledged" which never really got fixed.  If you do a PERFORM of a
PARAGRAPH in another SECTION and then do an EXIT SECTION (rather than EXIT
PARAGRAPH) there are some problems with "logic flow" in those cases.
Personally, I would have "preferred" that there be a simple EXIT PROCEDURE
statement that exited whatever procedure was currently being PERFORMed - but
there were a few reasons why this was rejected.
```

```
William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:9hap9o$52$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net...

> Come ON Stephen,
>
>  The definition of the EXIT PARAGRAPH is *very* carefully written so that
it
> DOES go to an "implicit continue" immediately after the last statement in
> the paragraph.  This means that there is NO way to cause "confusion" in
> maintenance.  There is nothing that you can add or subtract that is going
to
> make this have "crossing" logic paths.  This is WHY having a "single exit"
> in a procedure is "maintained" and enforced (unlike both GO TO and NEXT
> SENTENCE which *can* lead to multiple exit points from the same procedure
> and crossing logic paths).

Ummm ... Yes, I'm sure it was *very* carefully written, but there may be a
'way to cause "confusion" ' in trying to figure out what this verb ought to
do.  This ambiguity was pointed out by an international comment accompanying
the recent FCD ballot, and it was discussed at the last J4 meeting.

The ambiguity involves the implicit CONTINUE statement that's associated
with an EXIT PERFORM (which occurs immediately after the appropriate
END-PERFORM phrase), and the implicit CONTINUE  that's associated with EXIT
PARAGRAPH (which occurs immediately after the last statement in the current
paragraph), in the case that the PERFORM ... END-PERFORM is the last
statement in the paragraph.

A proposal to clarify the wording of General Rule 10 (and 11) of the EXIT
statement to resolve this ambiguity has been submitted for discussion at the
next J4 meeting.

       -Chuck Stevens
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9hdker$6sh$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3B1DD625.7A50077C@ont.com> <3B1F3794.9FB631D3@Azonic.co.nz> <3B1EA8F6.37791D0D@home.com> <4145699b.0106241413.6df46f12@posting.google.com> <3B36A907.98A26C73@mescoma.com> <9h832v$7vo$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3b37dfed_3@Usenet.com> <9h8qhg$dme$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <4145699b.0106261125.61aa728@posting.google.com> <9hap9o$52$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <9hb65d$ekq$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3b395d4f_1@Usenet.com>`

```

Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3b395d4f_1@Usenet.com...

> >
> ROFL!
>
> So how many angels can stand on a pin head, then? What about a meeting to
> decide where to have the next meeting..?

Several different issues here, Peter.

First:  Is there a real problem with the FCD in this area?  J4 thinks there
is.   I am inclined to agree.

Second:  The issue has been raised by a national standards body, who does
*not* think it is as trivial or as meaningless as you seem to with your
comment.

Third: The issue has been raised *formally* by a national standards body.
As such, it needs to receive a *formal* response.  And *formal* responses
require J4's *formal* acquiescence.

> Can you not see that if this were PUBLIC it would be resolved in minutes?

No, nor do I necessarily agree that the question as to whether there's
really a problem with the draft in this area, and if there is a problem what
the 100% accurate and 100% unambiguous way to reword the relevant passages
in the draft is.   I've seen the results too many times of a cavalier
approach to documentation changes in a compiler manual; how much more
important is care and caution and due diligence in the production of a
standard?

I don't have a problem with interested parties corresponding via E-mail on
the issues, but I'm not convinced that the consensus of an undefined group
can be counted on to resolve such issues (or more importantly larger ones).

> (most of us won't stand for this kind of nonsense...)

Arguably true, Peter.  The question is whether it's nonsense or not.  I
personally feel that a formal criticism of the standard by a national
standards body is neither nonsense nor deserves to be treated as if it were,
and that a formal response to the formal criticism is appropriate.

The FCD is available on the internet.  So is the comment that raised the
issue.  How would you propose *this specific* problem be addressed?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

Chuck Stevens wrote:

>
> I don't have a problem with interested parties corresponding via E-mail on
> the issues, but I'm not convinced that the consensus of an undefined group
> can be counted on to resolve such issues (or more importantly larger ones).

Chuck,

I'm sure you didn't intend it to read that way, but the above does sound a
little elitist.
I'm slightly at variance with Pete on the 'mechanism'. Certainly at the present
time I would accept the 'formalized' approach of 'sub discussion groups'
thrashing out a topic and coming up with a solution - which I think should be
taken as read. Still uncomfortable with that, I'll buy a J4 member being the
official monitor for the thread. (Now Pete is really pissed off ! I want it to
happen so will compromise; experience can dictate the future mechanism).

Now let's take Stephen up on his "Join up lads, you can do your thing at J4". As
I understand from Stephen - yer pays yer dues, show up at sufficient meetings to
show you are for real, and then you are officially accepted. (No doubt dear old
ANSI has a trump card in there somewhere to stop some of what I'm suggesting
below). Two scenarios (1) As it is now and (2) What could be.

(1) Pays yer money and show up etc........ In addition to the 12 apostles and
the good lady you are now a member of J4. Agreed there IS user input but in a
formalized structure, which I'm sure you all impartially consider. Nevertheless
as it stands, an UNELECTED body of 13 + 1 determines what 3 MILLION programmers
can use.

(2) You don't want to play ball and bend the rules ? We COULD - but we wont - 30
like-minded souls who just don't buy into the current process pay their dues and
join up etc...... Having served the requisite 'trial period', next meeting one
of them proposes, "Next Item on the Agenda. J4 WILL (not that stupid word
'shall') organize into an e-mail forum with monitors/adjudicators per topic.
Let's vote 'Yes' or 'No', then determine the rules".  All 30 new members vote
Y-E-S. What are you 13 going to do ? It wont happen because it means a fair
number of bucks being shelled out by the 30 individuals, unless of course they
could dip into some 'consolation' fund.

The purpose of the forum approach is not for a tete a tete - but to speed up the
COBOL review process, allowing for multi-tasking, (yes, 'Bill's phrase'), and
the more frequent introduction of changes to stay on par with other languages,
or even what Mr. Microsloth may throw at us.

Jimmy
```

- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3B1DD625.7A50077C@ont.com> <3B1F3794.9FB631D3@Azonic.co.nz> <3B1EA8F6.37791D0D@home.com> <4145699b.0106241413.6df46f12@posting.google.com> <3B36A907.98A26C73@mescoma.com> <9h832v$7vo$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3b37dfed_3@Usenet.com> <9h8qhg$dme$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <4145699b.0106261125.61aa728@posting.google.com> <9hap9o$52$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <9hb65d$ekq$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3b395d4f_1@Usenet.com> <9hdker$6sh$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
OK, I've responded below, contrasting what you describe through J4 with what
I would like to see in a Public Forum.

I'm sorry if I appeared flippant, but your post did cause me genuine
amusement. It is really no wonder things take so long...

First up, here's what we're arguing about...

*---------------------------------------------------------------------------
------------------------
> >  The definition of the EXIT PARAGRAPH is *very* carefully written so
that
> it
> > DOES go to an "implicit continue" immediately after the last statement
in
> > the paragraph.  This means that there is NO way to cause "confusion" in
> > maintenance.  There is nothing that you can add or subtract that is
going
> to
> > make this have "crossing" logic paths.  This is WHY having a "single
exit"
> > in a procedure is "maintained" and enforced (unlike both GO TO and NEXT
> > SENTENCE which *can* lead to multiple exit points from the same
procedure
> > and crossing logic paths).
>
> Ummm ... Yes, I'm sure it was *very* carefully written, but there may be a
> 'way to cause "confusion" ' in trying to figure out what this verb ought
to
> do.  This ambiguity was pointed out by an international comment
accompanying
> the recent FCD ballot, and it was discussed at the last J4 meeting.

In a public forum, the comment that was posted would be VISIBLE and we could
all decide whether it was pertinent or not. A vote could be taken if there
was disagreement or concern over this and then the work could move on.

It doesn't need to be "very carefully written" (at least not in the first
iteration of it...) and there is NO "'way to cause "confusion" ' in trying
to figure out what this verb ought to do", if what it ought to do is stated
in plain, simple English.

>
> The ambiguity involves the implicit CONTINUE statement that's associated
> with an EXIT PERFORM (which occurs immediately after the appropriate
> END-PERFORM phrase), and the implicit CONTINUE  that's associated with
EXIT
> PARAGRAPH (which occurs immediately after the last statement in the
current
> paragraph), in the case that the PERFORM ... END-PERFORM is the last
> statement in the paragraph.
>
 So WHO introduced the concept of an "implicit CONTINUE" in the first place?
It's bollocks!

There is no CONTINUE statement, implicit or otherwise. Control is
transferred from the Perform scope or the Paragraph scope and processes the
next statement which follows these scopes. It is simple, normal, COBOL
behaviour that we're all familiar with.

But no, that's too simple. We have to justify these constructs against the
alternative GO TOs , so let's talk about "crossing logic paths" and show
that our new constructs don't do this by enforcing a single EXIT point. We
can add substance to the "single exit point" contention by saying it is like
an implicit CONTINUE... and so on ad nauseum.

Then we have the "special cases" (what I refer to as the "Black, Jewish,
Lesbian, Dwarfs" ) that we spend 80% of the available time arguing about or
more, if it's an interesting argument <G>. In this case the BJLD is "What if
the EXITs from PERFORM and PARAGRAPH overlap in scope?" Shock! Horror!
Gee... we better postpone that one until next meeting in 4 months so we can
all go away and consider it....

IF it was posted in a public forum that was empowered to resolve it, it
could be sorted in a few days.

DON'T justify the constructs with doubtful considerations regarding style,
just DOCUMENT them!

Like this...

COBOL Verb: EXIT PARAGRAPH.

Description: Transfers control from the current paragraph to the first
executable statement following it.

Notes:

1.  You can have as many of these in a paragraph as you need. Each one will
behave as described above.

2.  If the paragraph is part of a PERFORMED range and control would be to
the end of the PERFORM range, the "next executable statement" will be the
one following the PERFORM. (See examples, below)

... I'm not going to waste time posting it; you get the picture. There is a
big difference between DOCUMENTING something and DISCUSSING it.

There is no need for the dry, pedantic, legalese that permeates the
Standard, either. It can be done in simple English, amplified with "in
context" examples. The average COBOL user is NOT an English Lit. Master.


> A proposal to clarify the wording of General Rule 10 (and 11) of the EXIT
> statement to resolve this ambiguity has been submitted for discussion at
the
> next J4 meeting.
>
>ROFL!

>So how many angels can stand on a pin head, then? What about a meeting to
>decide where to have the next meeting..?

>Can you not see that if this were PUBLIC it would be resolved in minutes?
>(most of us won't stand for this kind of nonsense...)

*-------------------------------- end of statement as to what we're arguing
about ------------------------

Now, addressing your response...

> > ROFL!
> >
> > So how many angels can stand on a pin head, then? What about a meeting
to
> > decide where to have the next meeting..?
>
> Several different issues here, Peter.
>
> First:  Is there a real problem with the FCD in this area?  J4 thinks
there
> is.   I am inclined to agree.
>
I don't know or care. I'm not interested in what J4 thinks, I'm interested
in getting hold of a Standard that can be used.

OK, for the moment the only game in town is J4 and they have a FCD that
probably has a lot of valuable stuff in it (if you can read it without
falling asleep...I can't).

Why not put it in a Forum, broken into sections with a thread for each
section, empower the User base to make changes to it under control of a
Moderator, and get the bloody thing out...?!

"Is there a real problem with the FCD in this area?" NO! It is the SAME
problem that has permeated the thing ever since its inception: Can't issue
it until it is "right", Can't get it right because it's too complex.

That's why I advocate iterating the approach to it and gradually refining
it, within the timeboxes allocated.

Start with some simple basic descriptions like the one above. Ruthlessly
chop out any pontificating pompous prose and translate it to plain English,
and ban ANYONE with a Legal background from being involved in it! (OK, I was
joking with the last one...)

> Second:  The issue has been raised by a national standards body, who does
> *not* think it is as trivial or as meaningless as you seem to with your
> comment.

Still don't care. If it had been simple in the first place NOBODY would be
raising an issue. (At least the issues that WERE raised would not be
pedantic little nitpicks about "possible confusion").

And the "argument from Authority" impresses me not one jot.

>
> Third: The issue has been raised *formally* by a national standards body.
> As such, it needs to receive a *formal* response.  And *formal* responses
> require J4's *formal* acquiescence.
>
Which supports my argument that the Procedures and Rituals embraced by J4
are counter productive. Who says it has to be that way? ANSI/ISO/J4. Who
wants it that way? ANSI/ISO/J4, and the other National standards bodies who
should stick to describing screws and bolts and leave our Language alone....


> > Can you not see that if this were PUBLIC it would be resolved in
minutes?
>
> No, nor do I necessarily agree that the question as to whether there's
> really a problem with the draft in this area, and if there is a problem
what
> the 100% accurate and 100% unambiguous way to reword the relevant passages
> in the draft is.   I've seen the results too many times of a cavalier
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
How much better is having SOMETHING than having NOTHING?!

I agree that care, cauton, and "due diligence" all have a place and I am NOT
advocating a cavalier approach. I'm saying it all needs to be timeboxed and
MANAGED. Then you go with what is there at the end of the timebox. (until
the next time you get a chance to iterate and refine the process - maybe a
year later).


> I don't have a problem with interested parties corresponding via E-mail on
> the issues, but I'm not convinced that the consensus of an undefined group
> can be counted on to resolve such issues (or more importantly larger
ones).
>
No, of course, you all welcome free help and support as long as you retain
CONTROL of it.

The "consensus of an undefined group" led to the establishment of the United
States of America... And such groups have achieved similar remarkable
results throughout History.

It is when an unelected elite take control of something they have no right
to control, that such "undefined groups" become more defined.

> > (most of us won't stand for this kind of nonsense...)
>
> Arguably true, Peter.  The question is whether it's nonsense or not.  I
> personally feel that a formal criticism of the standard by a national
> standards body is neither nonsense nor deserves to be treated as if it
were,
> and that a formal response to the formal criticism is appropriate.
>
It is a nonsense for numbers of valuable people to spend time arguing about
something this trivial. In the meantime neither COBOL nor the Users of it
are being served.

I accept that, having raised a formal criticism, it must be responded to
formally. My issue is with why the criticism arose in the first place, and
why we are supporting such an archaic and closed mechanism for accepting
and dealing with criticism.

> The FCD is available on the internet.  So is the comment that raised the
> issue.  How would you propose *this specific* problem be addressed?
>
I've already covered at length what I want to see.  And why.

It is a matter of record. It will probably never happen, but someone has to
voice the discontent felt by many over the current set up.

Pete.



Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services

                http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-28T05:31:17-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tjluf5jllortf2@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3B1DD625.7A50077C@ont.com> <3B1F3794.9FB631D3@Azonic.co.nz> <3B1EA8F6.37791D0D@home.com> <4145699b.0106241413.6df46f12@posting.google.com> <3B36A907.98A26C73@mescoma.com> <9h832v$7vo$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3b37dfed_3@Usenet.com> <9h8qhg$dme$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <4145699b.0106261125.61aa728@posting.google.com> <9hap9o$52$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <9hb65d$ekq$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```

Chuck Stevens wrote in message <9hb65d$ekq$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>...
>William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
>news:9hap9o$52$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net...
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>  The definition of the EXIT PARAGRAPH is *very* carefully written
[...]
>
>Ummm ... Yes, I'm sure it was *very* carefully written, but there may be a
>'way to cause "confusion" ' in trying to figure out what this verb ought to
>do.  This ambiguity was pointed out by an international comment
accompanying
>the recent FCD ballot, and it was discussed at the last J4 meeting.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>statement to resolve this ambiguity has been submitted for discussion at
the
>next J4 meeting.

While your at it, there is a conflict between EXIT GR 10 (and 11) and
PERFORM GR 3.

Given the example:

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
A SECTION.
AA.
    PERFORM B
    EXIT PROGRAM.
B SECTION.
BA.
    PERFORM BZ
    EXIT SECTION.
BZ.
    EXIT PARAGRAPH.

The FCD is not clear concerning the sequence of elements to
occur after paragraph BZ.

One interpretation is:
1. Return from perform of section B.
2. Return from perform of paragraph BZ.
3. Implicit continue from EXIT SECTION.
4. Implicit continue from EXIT PARAGRAPH.

If the word 'immediately' from EXIT GR 10 (and 11) is meant to
indicate priority, the possible sequence becomes:

1. Implicit continue from EXIT SECTION.
2. Implicit continue from EXIT PARAGRAPH.
3. Return from perform of section B.
4. Return from perform of paragraph BZ.

Logically, the above sequences are inconsistent with the idea that
sections are containers for paragraphs (FCD page 389, 14.5.1,
"A section consists of ... paragraphs."). The logical sequence is:

1. Implicit continue from EXIT PARAGRAPH.
2. Return from perform of paragraph BZ.
3. Implicit continue from EXIT SECTION.
4. Return from perform of section B.

The defect may be repaired by making the following changes
to the FCD.

Page 389, 14.5.2 Paragraphs, change the last sentence to read,
"A paragraph ends immediately before the next paragraph-name,
the end of the containing section, or the end of the procedure division."

[The text referring to declaratives is removed since no paragraph
in the declaratives portion of the procedure division would share
a boundary with the keywords END DECLARATIVES.]

Page 455, 14.10.13.3 EXIT GR 10, change in part to read,
"... an implicit CONTINUE at the end of the current paragraph."

Page 455, 14.10.13.3 EXIT GR 11, change in part to read,
"... an implicit CONTINUE at the end of the current section."

Page 496, 14.10.27.3 PERFORM GR 3, change in part to read,
"a) ... return is immediately after the end of procedure-name-1.
 b) ... return is immediately after the end of procedure-name-2."

The above changes also eliminate the conflict with the implicit
continue of EXIT PERFORM when PERFORM ... END-PERFORM
is the last statement of a paragraph.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-28T11:34:03-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9hftau$mc7$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3B1DD625.7A50077C@ont.com> <3B1F3794.9FB631D3@Azonic.co.nz> <3B1EA8F6.37791D0D@home.com> <4145699b.0106241413.6df46f12@posting.google.com> <3B36A907.98A26C73@mescoma.com> <9h832v$7vo$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3b37dfed_3@Usenet.com> <9h8qhg$dme$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <4145699b.0106261125.61aa728@posting.google.com> <9hap9o$52$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <9hb65d$ekq$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <tjluf5jllortf2@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Your suggestion is a good one, Rick; thank you.  I think the following
*might* be a bit better, though, because I think it's a bit more complete
and explicit:

EXIT GR10: " If an EXIT statement with the PARAGRAPH phrase is executed, the
EXIT statement causes control to be passed to an implicit CONTINUE statement
within, but immediately following the last implicit or explicit statement
of, the current paragraph."

EXIT GR11 to be reworded similarly:  " ... within, but immediately following
the last implicit or explicit statement of, the last paragraph in the
current section".

In general, I view "statement" in such contexts as synonymous with "implicit
or explicit statement".  In fact, an earlier instantiation of the draft
official recommendation simply retained "following the last statement of
..."; the intent of the later addition of "implicit or explicit" was to
improve clarity.

I agree with you as to the intended execution stream (your third sequence),
and given both the slight revisions to EXIT SECTION and EXIT PARAGRAPH
described above, and the understanding that "following the last statement of
..." describes a location after any implicit statements that follow the
explicit ones, I *think* the rules as revised above resolve the ambiguities
you point out.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

Chuck Stevens wrote:
> 
> Your suggestion is a good one, Rick; thank you.  I think the following
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> of, the current paragraph."

Does this mean (and I think it does) that each EXIT PARAGRAPH within a
paragraph will 'create' a new instance of an implicit CONTINUE after the
previous implicit CONTINUE ?

Now the real question is: does a PERFORM of that paragraph also create
an 'implicit CONTINUE' that is the end point of the PERFORM ?  If it
does then the EXIT PARAGRAPH is to after this (ie following the last
implicit CONTINUE created by, say, the PERFORM).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-28T13:50:52-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9hg5ba$1al$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3B1DD625.7A50077C@ont.com> <3B1F3794.9FB631D3@Azonic.co.nz> <3B1EA8F6.37791D0D@home.com> <4145699b.0106241413.6df46f12@posting.google.com> <3B36A907.98A26C73@mescoma.com> <9h832v$7vo$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3b37dfed_3@Usenet.com> <9h8qhg$dme$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <4145699b.0106261125.61aa728@posting.google.com> <9hap9o$52$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <9hb65d$ekq$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <tjluf5jllortf2@corp.supernews.com> <9hftau$mc7$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3B3C28F3.9ADD72D5@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3B3C28F3.9ADD72D5@Azonic.co.nz...

> Does this mean (and I think it does) that each EXIT PARAGRAPH within a
> paragraph will 'create' a new instance of an implicit CONTINUE after the
> previous implicit CONTINUE ?

No, because CONTINUE is a no-op.  It means only that it would "act as if"
such a CONTINUE had been coded.  All EXIT PARAGRAPHs within a given
paragraph will transfer to the same place.  All EXIT SECTIONs within a
SECTION will transfer control to the same place.  And the place to which an
EXIT PARAGRAPH that occurs in the last paragraph of a section transfers
control is the same place to which an EXIT SECTION in that paragraph
transfers control.

When an EXIT PARAGRAPH is executed from within an *in-line* PERFORM located
at the end of a paragraph, the iteration requirements of the PERFORM are
bypassed.

> Now the real question is: does a PERFORM of that paragraph also create
> an 'implicit CONTINUE' that is the end point of the PERFORM ?  If it
> does then the EXIT PARAGRAPH is to after this (ie following the last
> implicit CONTINUE created by, say, the PERFORM).

That's already covered by the scope of PERFORM, I believe, because of the
phrase "last statement of" used (twice) in the same PERFORM GR3 that you
pointed out earlier.  "Last implicit or explicit statement of ..." might be
a useful clarification here (and in a few other places as well), but I
believe it's correct as is, and there are a few other areas that actually
need correcting still.  I'm also not convinced that the more elaborate and
explicit wording in each and every such instance makes the standard overall
easier to understand ...

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-29T17:01:41+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B3CA665.EA37DCE8@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3B1DD625.7A50077C@ont.com> <3B1F3794.9FB631D3@Azonic.co.nz> <3B1EA8F6.37791D0D@home.com> <4145699b.0106241413.6df46f12@posting.google.com> <3B36A907.98A26C73@mescoma.com> <9h832v$7vo$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3b37dfed_3@Usenet.com> <9h8qhg$dme$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <4145699b.0106261125.61aa728@posting.google.com> <9hap9o$52$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <9hb65d$ekq$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <tjluf5jllortf2@corp.supernews.com> <9hftau$mc7$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3B3C28F3.9ADD72D5@Azonic.co.nz> <9hg5ba$1al$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:
> 
> Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> such a CONTINUE had been coded. 

Well a No-Op (NOP) instruction is still an instruction that takes up
space.  An implicit statement is not the same as a virtual statement.

> All EXIT PARAGRAPHs within a given
> paragraph will transfer to the same place.  

The issue I have is that:

> >  an implicit CONTINUE statement
> > within, but immediately following the last implicit or explicit statement
> > of, the current paragraph."

The 'implicit CONTINUE' is an 'implicit statement', this means that the
next EXIT PARAGRAPH must go to an 'implicit CONTINUE' that is after all
the implicit statements including the last 'implicit CONTINUE' that was
the target of the previous EXIT PARAGRAPH, or of any other mechanism,
such as the PERFORM.

If the CONTINUE is a NOP instruction then these are _not_ the same
place.

I quite agree that logically they are, or should be, the same place, I
am only arguing that the new wording does _not_ make anything 'clearer'.

>  I'm also not convinced that the more elaborate and
> explicit wording in each and every such instance makes the standard overall
> easier to understand ...

Exactly.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-29T07:31:15-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9hhsen$as8$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3B1DD625.7A50077C@ont.com> <3B1F3794.9FB631D3@Azonic.co.nz> <3B1EA8F6.37791D0D@home.com> <4145699b.0106241413.6df46f12@posting.google.com> <3B36A907.98A26C73@mescoma.com> <9h832v$7vo$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3b37dfed_3@Usenet.com> <9h8qhg$dme$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <4145699b.0106261125.61aa728@posting.google.com> <9hap9o$52$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <9hb65d$ekq$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <tjluf5jllortf2@corp.supernews.com> <9hftau$mc7$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3B3C28F3.9ADD72D5@Azonic.co.nz> <9hg5ba$1al$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3B3CA665.EA37DCE8@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
When written, it was felt that the "implicit CONTINUE" was "inserted" at
run-time and NOT compile-time.  Therefore, you can only be under ONE EXIT
statement - and that EXIT statements "dynamically" creates the implicit
CONTINUE.  This is not something that gets created when each SOURCE code
EXIT PARAGRAPH/SECTION is encountered.

The reason (as I recall) that "implicit" was included was that there are
some other times that an "implicit" statement can be inserted.  One that I
can think of (that actually raises some other questions) is the IMPLICIT
EXIT PROGRAM statement at the end of the final paragraph (if "fall thru
logic is in effect") of a subprogram.   The question is whether an EXIT
PARAGRAPH should go "before" or "after" that statement.  I think it is
"before" - but I won't swear to it.
```

William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> When written, it was felt that the "implicit CONTINUE" was "inserted" at
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> EXIT PARAGRAPH/SECTION is encountered.

'Inserted at tun-time' is a clever trick.  Shouldn't it then be a
'virtual CONTINUE' rather than an 'implicit CONTINUE' ?
 
> The reason (as I recall) that "implicit" was included was that there are
> some other times that an "implicit" statement can be inserted.  One that I
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> "before" - but I won't swear to it.

Well the EXIT PARAGRAPH is quite specific that it follows all other
explicit or implicit statements.  The question is whether the PERFORM of
that paragraph establishes a return point and the wording of the EXIT
PARAGRAPH has it jumping to a point beyond (following) that return
point.

Of course we all know what the EXIT PARAGRAPH should be doing, it is
just that I am questioning whether the wording actually says this.

Shouldn't that be 'implicit STOP RUN' beyond the end of the final
paragraph ?  Ot at least an implicit GOBACK (yuck, nasty IBMish thing).

> > > >  an implicit CONTINUE statement
> > > > within, but immediately following the last implicit or explicit
> > > > statement of, the current paragraph."

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-29T10:43:16-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tjp51r3n79jf28@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3B1DD625.7A50077C@ont.com> <3B1F3794.9FB631D3@Azonic.co.nz> <3B1EA8F6.37791D0D@home.com> <4145699b.0106241413.6df46f12@posting.google.com> <3B36A907.98A26C73@mescoma.com> <9h832v$7vo$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3b37dfed_3@Usenet.com> <9h8qhg$dme$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <4145699b.0106261125.61aa728@posting.google.com> <9hap9o$52$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <9hb65d$ekq$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <tjluf5jllortf2@corp.supernews.com> <9hftau$mc7$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```

Chuck Stevens wrote in message <9hftau$mc7$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>...
[...]
>I agree with you as to the intended execution stream (your third sequence),
>and given both the slight revisions to EXIT SECTION and EXIT PARAGRAPH
>described above, and the understanding that "following the last statement
of
>..." describes a location after any implicit statements that follow the
>explicit ones, I *think* the rules as revised above resolve the ambiguities
>you point out.

To have meaning, EXIT PARAGRAPH and return from
perform of a paragraph must be bound to the end of the
paragraph.

To have meaning, EXIT SECTION and return from perform
of a section must be bound to the end of the section.

As long as the FCD defines and uses the end of the last
paragraph of a section as the end of that section, the
ambiguity will exist.

The ambiguity becomes a problem only when performing
the last paragraph of a performed section from within that
section.

Your revision to EXIT GR 11 assures that EXIT SECTION
will have behavior that is different when performing the last
paragraph of a performed section; that is, in the last
paragraph, EXIT SECTION will behave as EXIT
PARAGRAPH -- elsewhere in the section it will be behave
as EXIT SECTION.

As a programmer, I can work around these problems by
inserting an unreferenced paragraph as the last paragraph
in the section. I would prefer removal of the defect from the
FCD.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-06-26T19:44:46-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0106261844.98de2b5@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3B1DD625.7A50077C@ont.com> <3B1F3794.9FB631D3@Azonic.co.nz> <3B1EA8F6.37791D0D@home.com> <4145699b.0106241413.6df46f12@posting.google.com> <3B36A907.98A26C73@mescoma.com> <9h832v$7vo$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3b37dfed_3@Usenet.com> <9h8qhg$dme$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <4145699b.0106261125.61aa728@posting.google.com> <9hap9o$52$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
One of the main reasons for the prohibition against multiple exits
from a module is this:

A large paragraph (or section) may be broken down into two or more
paragraphs (or sections) by a maintenance programmer, "to make it
easier".  If overlooked (easy to do), the EXIT PARAGRAPH (or EXIT
SECTION), NEXT SENTENCE or even GO TO  may no longer go to the correct
place. If there is only a single exit in the SOURCE code, you do not
have this problem.

I don't care what kind of weasely definition/rule comes with EXIT
PARAGRAPH and EXIT SECTION. They are awful GO TO's, and I will
recommend that my clients prohibit it in their shops.

Stephen J Spiro
Wizard Systems
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-29T07:56:14+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B3C268E.35F0CE50@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3B1DD625.7A50077C@ont.com> <3B1F3794.9FB631D3@Azonic.co.nz> <3B1EA8F6.37791D0D@home.com> <4145699b.0106241413.6df46f12@posting.google.com> <3B36A907.98A26C73@mescoma.com> <9h832v$7vo$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3b37dfed_3@Usenet.com> <9h8qhg$dme$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <4145699b.0106261125.61aa728@posting.google.com> <9hap9o$52$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <4145699b.0106261844.98de2b5@posting.google.com>`

```
Stephen J Spiro wrote:
> 
> A large paragraph (or section) may be broken down into two or more
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> recommend that my clients prohibit it in their shops.

Over a long period most programs will grow as new requirements are added
to meet changing business needs.  The growth may be in specific modules
and this may make the modules unwieldy and difficult to maintain.  The
solution may be, as you say, to break the module down into more easily
handled parts in a way not envisioned in the original design.

There are mechanisms which make breaking a module up (or down) difficult
and costly in terms of programmer time and, especially, testing and
debugging time.  One such mechanism is the 'perform section (or thru)
and go to exit'.  On the one hand it appears simple to just 'jump to
exit' to avoid the 'complications' of nesting the code within and IF ...
END-IF, on the other there is the very high cost of reconstructing the
code as two or more modules.

These costs may involve having to (by some convention) assign new
hierarchical level numbers for the labels, moving the code to new places
in the source determined by the number, creating the bracketing for the
code (headers, exits, etc), and most importantly rewriting all the logic
to cater for different exits, with flags being passed back so that a 'go
to exit' can take place when necessary.

This high cost, and the resulting messy code, is often blamed on the
'fetish' of trying to remove GO TOs.  In fact attempts to remove GO TOs
_without_ breaking down the module often leads to less desirable code,
such as 500 line IF ... END-IF. I can see that many 'high overhead'
modules will think that the EXIT paragraph/section is a saviour that
will allow removing the GO TO by replacing it with an EXIT SECTION.  

However, as you say, the EXIT SECTION is no better than a GO TO ~Exit,
except for the tiny assistance that you cannot specify the wrong ~Exit
and have the program rage out of control.

These do not, however, help at all in making the program more modular,
and certainly are just as much an impediment to breaking modules up as
GO TO is.  Thus modules will continue to grow as code is added during
maintenance and restructing becomes more costly and less likely to
occur.  The programs become less modular as the same number of modules
takes in more code and more requirements.

Programmers in high module overhead environments will not understand
your 'breaking into two or more paragraphs' at all.  They do not see
this as a solution to any problem because it is simply too expensive for
them to even think of doing.  

Of course I break down modules all the time to simplify them, but I do
not use any mechanism that gets in the way of doing this.  By using a
low overhead module construction I can rip out a nested IF or break a
module in half in a trice, all it takes it to write a new label name,
copy line and add PERFORM then block move the code a few lines, or leave
it where it is depending on what follows.

Because I do not use any high overhead mechanism (such as section, thru,
go to, exit paragraph, etc) I am assured that breaking down modules
(paragraphs) in this way will never lead to loss of control or unwanted
logic flow.  This means that I never need check, or even remember, when
to do fix-ups.

The difference between low overhead module and high overhead module
programming is so great that practitioners of the latter will not
understand how the former could possibly work, so they will not
understand why you object to EXIT section/paragraph, after all it does
allow them to replace the GO TO (by an implied GO TO) so it must make it
more 'modular'.  People have been telling them to get rid of GO TO for
decades, well now they can, they won't see beyond that.
```

```
On Fri, 29 Jun 2001 07:56:14 +0100, Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
wrote:

[some text deleted]

>Of course I break down modules all the time to simplify them, but I do
>not use any mechanism that gets in the way of doing this.  By using a
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>decades, well now they can, they won't see beyond that.


I have a question for you or anyone...  Here is a generalization of some code I
wrote just today.  Being the 'good' programmer I am I wanted to avoid any gotos.
EXIT PARAGRAPH is not available in my environment.  And even if it was,
apparently that's bad programming as well.


procedure division.
0000-entry.
    evaluate mode-of-entry
      when td-queue
        perform 0100-handle-td-queue
      when interval-control
        perform 0200-handle-ic
      when terminal-entry
        perform 0300-handle-terminal
     end-evaluate
     goback.

0100-handle-td-queue.
     perform 0110-read-td-queue
     perform until no-more-records
         perform 1000-process-record
         perform 0110-read-td-queue
     end-perform
     exit.

0200-handle-ic.
     perform 0210-receive-ic-data
     perform 1000-process-record
     exit.

0300-handle-terminal.
    perform 0310-receive-from-terminal
    perform 1000-process-record
    exit.

1000-process-record.
    perform 1010-check-for-discard
    if discard-record
        continue
    else
        perform 1100-really-process-record
    end-if
    exit.

1010-check-for-discard.
*   this paragraph checks to see if the record is older than 120 seconds
*   and sets discard-record to true if it is.  Otherwise it sets keep-record
*   to true.

1100-really-process-record.

*   this paragraph does whatever processing I want to do.
*   It could be 50 lines or so.

Now take a look at 1000-process-record and 1100-really process record.  Wouldn't
it make just as much sense to say:

1000-process-record.
    perform 1010-check-for-discard
    if discard-record
        exit paragraph
    end-if
*   followed by 50 lines of whatever record processing I had in
*   1100-really-process-record above.
    exit.

Now of course another thing I could have done was instead of just doing my three
different "perform 1000-process-record" things I could have done

    perform 1010-check-for-discard
    if keep-record
        perform 1000-process-record
    end-if

But that's three iterations of the same exact code - BLAH!

Note that I could not simply put that code once after my evaluate statement in
the 0000-entry paragraph because, while 0200 and 0300 process only one record,
0100 processes multiple records from a queue.

Thoughts?  Arguements?

Frank Swarbrick
home: infocat@sprynet.com / work: frank.swarbrick@efirstbank.com
"I'm very seldom naughty." --Willow Rosenberg "Buffy the Vampire Slayer"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-26T12:32:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Eh%Z6.379$aX5.70021@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3B1DD625.7A50077C@ont.com> <3B1F3794.9FB631D3@Azonic.co.nz> <3B1EA8F6.37791D0D@home.com> <4145699b.0106241413.6df46f12@posting.google.com> <3B36A907.98A26C73@mescoma.com> <9h832v$7vo$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3b37dfed_3@Usenet.com>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3b37dfed_3@Usenet.com...
> So, If I understand you correctly, Bill there can only be ONE  "EXIT
> PERFORM" or EXIT PARAGRAPH
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>

In the example above, Pete, EXIT PARAGRAPH is redundant (no statements at
end of tests) ; but as a veteran
BASIC programmer, I can tell you I see the "problem of multiple exits" all
the time. Sure, it works, but it's a dog to maintain.

In COBOL, you might consider using EVALUATE TRUE instead of a series of
IF..ELSE..END-IF

i.e.,

IF condition-1
     do-something
     exit
 ELSE
        IF condition-2
           do-something
           exit
       ELSE
          do-something
          (no exit)
      END_IF
 END-IF

can be rewritten...

EVALUATE TRUE
   WHEN condition-1
     do-something
    (set exit-flag to true)   <<< if statements must execute in paragraph
after EVALUATE
   WHEN condition-2
     do-something
   WHEN OTHER
      do-something
 END-EVALUATE
(if not exit-flag
    rest of statements in paragraph)
END -IF)

CONTINUE.      << end of paragraph

I find this much easier to read, understand and maintain.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-25T18:16:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B377F9E.5DBE7500@home.com>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3B1DD625.7A50077C@ont.com> <3B1F3794.9FB631D3@Azonic.co.nz> <3B1EA8F6.37791D0D@home.com> <4145699b.0106241413.6df46f12@posting.google.com>`

```


Stephen J Spiro wrote:

> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Not going to let you get away with that one you purist <G>. Agreed I'm
talking OO, but pull the following to bits :-

Now of course others will say, but there is a better way of doing this
with SQL, and you can view my constant testing of SQLSTATE as overkill.
But why 'trundle' your way through the whole 'mini-program' when you can
immediately EXIT given an error conditon ? Similarly this also includes
an EXIT PERFORM for the same reasons. (Oops ! On reflection I *should*
automatically exit the perform as a result of setting RoutineFinis to
true).

The alternative, which is more or less what you are suggesting, is to use
nested IFs and keep going until you hit an error conditon. I just don't
see where that would make it any easier, and certainly not as clear as
EXIT METHOD - right here and now !

(Just as background - note I'm not building the collection in the SQL
Class, but for a standard approach I'm returning the records by invoking
the caller os-DBI or MatTableDBI (Database Interface) which handles all
collection creation, additions, changes and deleteions. I'm doing this by
using two-way handles MatTableDBI can invoke MatTable(SQL) and the latter
can invoke MatTableDBI).

Jimmy

*>----------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "make-collection".
*>----------------------------------------------------------
Local-storage section.
01 ls-flag                       pic 9.
   88 RoutineFinis               value 1.
   88 RoutineNotFinis            value 0.

Linkage section.
01 lnk-result                    pic 9(03).

Procedure Division returning lnk-result.

 move zeroes to lnk-result
 invoke super "check-current-db"
        using ws-dbID returning lnk-result

 if lnk-result <> zeroes
    EXIT METHOD
 End-if

 EXEC SQL
      DECLARE C3 cursor for SELECT
                MatID,
                MainType,
                SubType,
                SpecNo,
                GradeNo
      FROM      MatTable

 END-EXEC

 if      SQLSTATE <> "00000"
         move MatID to ws-ErrorID
         invoke self "set-errmsg"
         move 99 to lnk-result
         EXIT METHOD
 End-if

 EXEC SQL OPEN C3 END-EXEC

 if      SQLSTATE <> "00000"
         move MatID to ws-ErrorID
         invoke self "set-errmsg"
         move 99 to lnk-result
         EXIT METHOD
 End-if

 set RoutineNotFinis to true
 Perform until RoutineFinis

   EXEC SQL
     FETCH C3 INTO
       :MatID, :MainType, :SubType,
       :SpecNo, :GradeNo
   END-EXEC

   Evaluate true

     when SQLSTATE = "00000"
       initialize            ws-record
       move MatId         to ws-MatId
       move SpecNo        to ws-SpecNo
       move GradeNo       to ws-GradeNo
       move MainType      to ws-MainType
       move SubType       to ws-SubType

       invoke os-DBI "new-list-record" using ws-record

     when SQLSTATE = "02000"               *> no more rows
       set RoutineFinis to true
       EXIT PERFORM
     when other set RoutineFinis to true
       move MatID to ws-ErrorID
       invoke self "set-errmsg"
       move 99 to lnk-result
       EXIT METHOD
   End-evaluate

 End-perform

 EXEC SQL CLOSE C3 END-EXEC

 if      SQLSTATE <> "00000"
         move MatID to ws-ErrorID
         invoke self "set-errmsg"
         move 99 to lnk-result
         EXIT METHOD
 End-if
 .

End Method "make-collection".
*>----------------------------------------------------------
```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in 

> Stephen J Spiro wrote:
> 
…[114 more quoted lines elided]…
>        EXIT PERFORM

*---------------------
Why bother with the EXIT PERFORM? After you 'set RoutineFinis to
true', you fall thru to the End-Perform, and your "until" condition is
satisfied.
*---------------------

>      when other set RoutineFinis to true
>        move MatID to ws-ErrorID
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> *>----------------------------------------------------------

The GO TO's and EXIT's always look easier in small programs....  My
contention is that they cause spaghetti as the programs grow thru
maintenance.  And if they are an encouraged style in methods (which
probably SHOULD always be small programs), less-than-professional (90%
of all) programmers will use the same style where it is much easier to
get in trouble.

My comment on your EXIT PERFORM is embedded in the code.




Stephen J Spiro
```

Stephen J Spiro wrote:

> *-----------------
> Yakh! EXIT-METHOD is the equivalent of EXIT-PROGRAM. There should be
> only one per Method.  One entrance, one exit.  You can have spaghetti
> code in methods as easily as in a main program. <snip>

Stephen,

This is a follow-up to my previous message but giving a more valid EXIT
PERFORM    :

        invoke myCollection "size" returning ls-size
        perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > ls-size

            Evaluate true
                when aaa = RecordType1 invoke self "do-rec1"
                when aaa = RecordType2 invoke self "do-rec2"
                when other EXIT PERFORM
            End-evaluate
    End-perform

(OK you can get nit-picky - just an example, but assume above collection
is a SortedCollection on RecType).

Still makes sense to me <G>

Jimmy

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-26T10:35:52-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tjh7et8cvc7nbd@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3B1DD625.7A50077C@ont.com> <3B1F3794.9FB631D3@Azonic.co.nz> <3B1EA8F6.37791D0D@home.com> <4145699b.0106241413.6df46f12@posting.google.com>`

```

Stephen J Spiro wrote in message
<4145699b.0106241413.6df46f12@posting.google.com>...
[...]
>There is NEVER an excuse for EXIT-PERFORM (or EXIT-PARAGRAPH or EXIT
>SECTION). The same functionality can be provided by coding an
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>modular programming.

Structured programming had its origin in work that attempted
to find means for mechanical proofs of correctness of programs.
It was discovered that adherence to a small set of well-defined
structures makes such mechanical proofs more easily acheivable.

Humans are not machines! Human comprehension (or "human
proof of correctness")  is more easily acheived through simplicity.
The EXIT statements, by removing the complexity required for
mechanical proofs, simplifies programs and improves human
comprehension of such programs.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-26T11:22:59-04:00

#### ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

- **From:** chammond@lc.cc.il.us
- **Date:** 2001-06-06T16:32:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
The only reason to use the end-if is if there is an if statement with a next
sentence and there is actually some other code that follows it.  Even if an
End-if is used the book says if the next sentence phrase is executed control
will not pass to the next command after it but instead it will pass to the
statement after the closest following the closest following period.
I can not see why you would not put a period after the first display command. 


Often next sentence is used to avoid using a compound condition with 
"not" logic, which is kind of a bad idea for logic flow.  While this may not
really be wrong or incorrect, it may cause unexpected results in practice. 
The reason is it takes the careful use of parentheses which is sometimes
overlooked.  Even my old logic teacher said never to use end-if.  It is kind
of a throw-back to Pseudo-code or basic.  A period ends a statement in COBOL
anyway.

In article <9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>, William M. Klein
<wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> writes:
>Just for the "record" the following source code will compile "cleanly" and
>is semi-valid with IBM mainframe compilers (discussion afterwards), (please
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
>


 -----  Posted via NewsOne.Net: Free (anonymous) Usenet News via the Web  -----
  http://newsone.net/ -- Free reading and anonymous posting to 60,000+ groups
   NewsOne.Net prohibits users from posting spam.  If this or other posts
made through NewsOne.Net violate posting guidelines, email abuse@newsone.net
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-06T17:51:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B1E6E31.E8D93F47@home.com>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com>`

```


chammond@lc.cc.il.us wrote:

> The only reason to use the end-if is if there is an if statement with a next
> sentence and there is actually some other code that follows it.  Even if an
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Ignoring all the variances you can make with periods (full stops - just in case
she's reading this !), Next Sentence etc., I would suggest the use of End-if is
more beneficial for consistency of style, showing indentations so that as you look
at your program everything is 'paired off' :-

        if    .....
            else.....
            else...
        End-if

in exactly the same way as using Evaluate :-

    Evaluate true
        when ....
        when ....
    End-evaluate

The 'End' syntax was introduced to COBOL to avoid 'gotchas'.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-06T12:01:09-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B1E6FE5.842D0A09@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com>`

```


chammond@lc.cc.il.us wrote:

> The only reason to use the end-if is if there is an if statement with a next
> sentence and there is actually some other code that follows it.  Even if an
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> overlooked.

I no longer code NEXT SENTENCE (my pre-compiler does though).   But I will use
CONTINUE to avoid using a compound condition, if that results in more clear code.
I will also use it to keep a series of IF statements consistent, if that makes the
code more clear.   I also over use parenthesis, so that some maintainer doesn't
have to guess if what I wrote is what I meant.   Don't make him parse what you
wrote.

IF MY-CHOICE
OR A > B
     CONTINUE
ELSE
     ADD 1 TO B
END-IF.

If the user sees the logic this way, keep it in the program, don't manipulate it
around to avoid the CONTINUE.


> Even my old logic teacher said never to use end-if.  It is kind
> of a throw-back to Pseudo-code or basic.  A period ends a statement in COBOL
> anyway.

I believe END-IF can save problems with nested IF statements.  It is especially
useful when someone sticks in a debug display within a procedure.


PERFORM
       ....
       ....
       IF MY-ACCOUNT < '5000000'
            DISPLAY "FOUND ONE!!!"
       END-IF
       ....
END-PERFORM
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and missing periods)

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-11T20:54:28+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B2521F3.3FFAE797@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<9fjsu2$4c0$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <9fllv7$5ko$1@news.netmar.com>`

```
chammond@lc.cc.il.us wrote:
> 
> The only reason to use the end-if is if there is an if statement with a next
> sentence and there is actually some other code that follows it. 

That is actually the poorest reason to use 'END-IF'.  It is non-standard
ANSI/ISO code and is poor style, usually leading to bugs which may be
difficult to find and fix.

> Even if an
> End-if is used the book says if the next sentence phrase is executed control
> will not pass to the next command after it but instead it will pass to the
> statement after the closest following the closest following period.

Exactly. DON'T DO THIS.

> I can not see why you would not put a period after the first display command.

Because he was making a point that you failed to understand, or even
notice.  

(Actually I am happy because it does provide evidence for an unrelated
argument about who is being the more confusing   ;-)).
 
> Often next sentence is used 

NEXT SENTENCE should only be used in ANSI-74 style Cobol where there are
no END-IFs.  If a more modern compiler (say one only 15 years old) is
available then CONTINUE should always be used and not NEXT SENTENCE.

> to avoid using a compound condition with
> "not" logic, which is kind of a bad idea for logic flow.  While this may not
> really be wrong or incorrect, it may cause unexpected results in practice.

It would only cause 'unexpected results' if you couldn't work out what
the code is doing, or how it should be coded correctly.  As you become
more experienced then you should be able to get to a point of writing
some code and having it execute as you expected.

> The reason is it takes the careful use of parentheses which is sometimes
> overlooked.  

Yes, use parentheses wherever possible, I always use them even on simple
conditions:

     IF ( Customer-Type = "R" )
         ....

> Even my old logic teacher said never to use end-if.  

Get your money back that was very poor advise.

> It is kind
> of a throw-back to Pseudo-code or basic.  A period ends a statement in COBOL
> anyway.

Well exactly.  The period ends every outstanding statement.  With
scope-terminators (END-~) it is possible to write much more concise code
that can be more readable and more reliable.

          PERFORM
              VARYING Ix FROM 1 BY 1 
                UNTIL Ix > Table-Limit

              IF ( Table-Code(Ix) = "M" )
                  ADD 1     TO M-Count
                  IF ( M-Count > 10 )
                      MOVE "Y"   TO M-Indicator
                  END-IF         
              ELSE
              IF ( Table-Code(Ix) = "R" )
                  ADD 1     TO R-Count
              END-IF
              END-IF
              ADD 1         TO Examine-Count
          END-PERFORM

You can't end the inner IF with a full-stop because it will aslo end the
outer IF and the PERFORM.
```
