#### ↳ Re: AcuCorp Vision Files

- **From:** "Sal Cambareri" <sal@lasermax.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-06T10:40:04-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WTtT6.3346$CT2.1296789@nntp3.onemain.com>`
- **References:** `<lLiT6.6544$n7.213331@e420r-atl2.usenetserver.com>`

```
For more than 10 years I have been working with two software houses who ship
their products to run on a variety of hosts (mainly Unix, Aix, NT, OS2 and
Citrix) with the AcuCorp runtimes and Vision (version 3 and version 4) file
systems.  I have found Vision fast, efficient and dependable. I have only
encountered the smallest of problems with it.  For example, while the Vision
data files are almost universally portable, on a Vax/VMS system we had to
use the VMS native ISAM  package (in place of Vision) to get reasonable
performance (at the cost of some features.)

Is there something in particular you want to know?

Regards,
Sal
```

#### ↳ Re: Information about Cobol

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-07T00:27:13+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b1e22a9_2@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<3b1dcb29.1365439@news.eunet.be>`

```
Peter,

A number of people through this forum have expressed concern that many of
the books on COBOL are WAY out of date and give a very misleading impression
of the Language.

Thane Hubbell is a fairly frequent contributor here, and his book "SAM's
Teach yourself COBOL in 24 Hours" has had unanimously favourable reviews.
(Despite the misleading title, it is NOT a quick and dirty day course in
COBOL. <G>)

I am currently working my way through Wilson Price's "Elements of
Object-Oriented COBOL" (Second Edition).

I cannot recommend this book highly enough. It is simply outstanding. In
fact, I am so impressed, I am trying to interest my local Polytech in
considering it as a standard text.

Wilson has produced a book which is simple, filled with excellent examples,
and follows a logical progression which introduces Object Oriented
programming in a COBOL context. It is completely up to date, and uses real
modern COBOL sample code. I see this book being of use to existing COBOL
programmers who want to understand the Object Oriented paradigm, and to
people like Java programmers, who already understand OO concepts, and are
interested in extending their skill set to include COBOL.

Some while ago I considered writing a book on OO COBOL, but after reading
this one I am persuaded there is nothing I could add...<G>

It is published by ObjectZ and can probably be obtained form the
www.cobolreport.com Web Site.
(No, I am NOT on commission...<G>)

The only other book on COBOL which I have ever been really impressed with
(and I have read, proof-read, and reviewed many) was written by Dr. Fritz D.
McCameron who taught COBOL at Louisiana State in the late sixties. I should
think this book is now long out of print, but, as a young programmer I found
it explained the concepts of things like nested IFs, complex PERFORMs, and
the need for structure (before the famous papers on Structured programming)
in simple language, with copious easy examples. In fact I still teach (and
use) his rules for nesting IFs to this day. The book, should you be lucky
enough to find a copy, is called "COBOL Logic and Programming".

Hope this helps,

Pete.

"Peter Alluyn" <pa@schaubroeck.be> wrote in message
news:3b1dcb29.1365439@news.eunet.be...
> Hello,
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Peter


Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>

```
book, it was Arranga/Coyle that showed me the light as regards OO, getting away
from the confusion caused by the N/E on-line help.

PS: I'm not on commission either - even though I write to both authors <G>

Jimmy
```

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2000/07/02
Version: 3.01
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Sunday, July 2, 2000

The COBOL FAQ has moved.  To view the most recent version, please check out the
web version at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send private
email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Recorded new location of the FAQ
 o Updated some old (bad) web links
 o Added some new COBOL books (particularly those written or contributed to - by
frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).
 o Added information about LegacyJ (PERCobol) and changed the company name from
"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".
 o Updated information for changes in product and company names and addresses
where available
 o Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
 o Added links to Xinotech
 o Added information on Norcom and GUI ScreenIO
 o Added (back) information on Wang
 o Added (back) information on the Tiny-COBOL ('74 Standard) project
```

#### ↳ Re: Read Cobol Data

- **From:** chammond@lc.cc.il.us
- **Date:** 2001-06-06T16:05:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9flkcv$4ag$1@news.netmar.com>`
- **References:** `<9fl0kd$9ia@netnews.hinet.net>`

```
In article <9fl0kd$9ia@netnews.hinet.net>, Weng <aa@bb.cc> writes:
>I want read cobol data(*.dat),  anybody can tell me about 3rd party tools
>
>
*.dat is a text file.  You will still have to know if it has carriage returns
or line feeds.  Some *.dat files may be continuous data.  No matter what the
data is you need to know what kind of fields are contained.  Sometimes the
fields are fixed length and sometimes the fields are comma delimited or have
variable length fields.

Keep in mind there is no such thing as a COBOL file.

COBOL programs use the available file types available for the operating
system.
All of the COBOL files we use are fixed length records, with fixed length
fields.  

You still have to know the file record structure.
```

_Thread started [2001-05](2001-05.md#t-1029a7da95)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A very strange affair indeed? OK I NEED YOU EXPERT COBOL PROGRAMMERS TO HELP ME :oP

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-07T07:01:20-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B1F7B20.2F45A329@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<b0e9ca5c.0105230157.52107d5c@posting.google.com> <9eh44l$ned$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <WjWO6.43609$T7.3526895@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <ee8d0e5c.0105232352.74916846@posting.google.com>`

```


CoBoL CoNfUzEs mE wrote:

> Hey Guys me again I am trying to take on board the things you say
>
> But I am not sure if my compiler supports functions

Easy enough to test.


> The main thing I need to do make sure the date is either in the format of
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> jan 2nd 2000

First, find out the business rules for what you want.   For instance:

     Whether or not your parsing will be case sensitive  FUNCTION UPPER-CASE
will help if not.
      Whether or not you want to allow multiple spaces, commas, etc.

Second, create your test plan.

Third, start your design and coding:

If you don't have functions, do a table translate on each character to get
everything upper case for your test.  If you need case sensitive matching,
you will have more work to do.

Then you can separate your words, either with an unstring, or looping through
the characters.

Then you might want to check out the first word against a table of months.

Then check to see if the third word is numeric and within your valid range.

The most complicated check will be the second word.  Split it between numeric
and non numeric parts.   Look at the last digit of the numeric part, and see
if the alphabetic part matches 0 & th; 1 & st; 2 & nd etc.    Then look at
the number and see if it is within the range allowed for the month.  Do you
know how to check for leap year?
```

```
Jerry P wrote:

> "Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk> wrote in message
> news:9eh44l$ned$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk...
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> is inefficient (and most other places as well).

Only if the "efficient" code is harder to maintain.   It is hard to call
with the STRING/unstring commands.   Some programmers are comfortable with
it, others need to pull out the manual.   And the parsed loop may be hard
or easy to understand or to carry subtle bugs.   Efficiency isn't bad - it
just isn't the most important characteristic of the code.
```

- **From:** gary drummond <gdrumm@ont.com>

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> Jerry P wrote:
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> just isn't the most important characteristic of the code.

How true. I had to code data entry programs that the customer would 
inherit, on a key-2-disk system. If the checkbox worked-fine, but
if not, the code had to be supported by the 
"I learned COBOL yesterday, before lunch!" bunch. For a data entry
system, or an interactive system, coding for easy maintenance is a
much better way to go. Now if it was a batch system, processing 20K
to 100K each night, it might be a toss-up. I often hack more than 
one way for testing, and pick the one I can be sure I'll be able to
figure out a year or two from now, or that can be fixed if the input
changes later. 

Gary
PS-I just saw a post about indexing VS subscripting, and if the timing 
was so close, I'd do it the easier way. (Which one is easier is left 
as an exercise for the reader :^)
```

```
Want to hear an even more interesting one.  In the "old" days, we had
a system that sometimes needed some of the records adjusted.  This was
a retail system.  Sometimes it was necessary to take a customers data
and "cancel" an order, or to mark a whole department as sold, or
things like that.  We wrote something we called a "Clean".  It was a
COBOL program with a copybook read from sysin,  The account
specialists (not COBOL programmers at all!) could actuall write COBOL
code - IF statements with GO TO's -- including putting in their own GO
TO statement - and it would compile and run.  I do not think that they
even know now that they were writing COBOL source code!  It looked
like English to them!
```

<a id="t-1f003fffa4"></a>
## Thread: invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and mi... — 16 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Re: invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and mi...

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-06T17:25:47+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B1E598A.D34434FC@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
-> Just for the "record" the following source code will compile
"cleanly" and
> is semi-valid with IBM mainframe compilers ...
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> messages).

You appear to be constructing an entirely semantic argument about what
the term "valid Cobol" means.

In my view what you describe is not 'valid COBOL' but may be valid
source code for a language that is very similar to Cobol called "IBM MVS
COBOL/2" or some such, though whether it actually is valid may depend on
compiler options or other settings.

That is, just because your compiler swallows it and produces am
executable in a deterministic way does not make this 'valid COBOL', it
only makes it 'valid compiler input'.
```

#### ↳ Re: invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and mi...

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-06T11:02:45-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9flk74$54a$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3B1E598A.D34434FC@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Valid COBOL is the COBOL that the compiler vendor SAYS is valid for that
compiler.  ANSI/J4 has actually done an "interpretation request" where they
CONFIRMED this definition.

The question was related to a compiler that "accepted"

    Label Records IS
      or
    Label Record ARE

which is "illegal" according to the ANSI Standard and was *not* documented
as an extension by that vendor in their Language Reference Manual.

J4 (who - at least in the US - I consider the "authority" on such matters)
said,

A) This is a "conforming implementation"
B) As long as they did not document this as an extension, they did NOT need
to flag it as an extension - NOR were they required to produce *any*
compiler error (warning, informational or error severity).

I did not (and do not) "LIKE" that decision - but the bottom line is
conforming source code for a conforming compiler is WHATEVER the compiler
vendor says it is.

Let me know if you need/want web access to the J4 interpretation in which
this was "confirmed".
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and mi...

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-07T07:42:16+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B1F2247.6FDB3D26@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3B1E598A.D34434FC@Azonic.co.nz> <9flk74$54a$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> Valid COBOL is the COBOL that the compiler vendor SAYS is valid for that
> compiler.  

COBOL is not a vendor defined language (not even IBM).

What you are claiming is that what is 'valid COBOL' will depend on which
compiler you use, or indeed what options and settings.

> ANSI/J4 has actually done an "interpretation request" where they
> CONFIRMED this definition.
> [...]
> A) This is a "conforming implementation"

You are confusing 'conforming implementation' where a valid Cobol
program (that is one following all the standards) is processed correctly
and then extrapolating this to expect that whatever the compiler will
accept is 'valid Cobol'.

Just because some source code is valid for the compiler of a
cobol-derived language that some vendor supplies does not make it 'valid
COBOL'. And this is the case even if the compiler will pass all the
tests and has been declared conforming.

'Valid COBOL' is what passes a comparison with the COBOL standard.

'Valid IBM MVS COBOL/2' is what the compiler accepts.

I would expect the IBM compiler to accept all 'valid COBOL' programs
(perhaps with some documented provisos).  But this does not mean the
reverse that everything the compiler accepts is thus 'valid COBOL'.

The same situation exists in other languages.  A C/C++ compiler will
process correctly an ANSI C program or a C++ program.  By your
definition all C++ would thus be 'valid C'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and mi...

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-06T17:51:07-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9fmc54$1j2$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3B1E598A.D34434FC@Azonic.co.nz> <9flk74$54a$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B1F2247.6FDB3D26@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3B1F2247.6FDB3D26@Azonic.co.nz...
> William M. Klein wrote:
> >
  <snip>
>
> Just because some source code is valid for the compiler of a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 'Valid COBOL' is what passes a comparison with the COBOL standard.

Richard - you are simply WRONG.  This is not what the ANSI (or ISO) COBOL
Standard says - or what any COBOL standards commitee has EVER said.

What you are describing is what is "correctly" refered to as "conforming
source code".  By definition (of the ANSI/ISO Standards) *any* implementor
is allowed to add as many extensions as they want and still call the source
code that uses such extension "valid COBOL".  That is part of the definition
of what the COBOL programming language IS.

It is true that any "conforming compiler" *must* provide an OPTION to flag
any (all) documented extensions.  (IBM, MERANT, Fujitsu, etc all do this).
There is *no* requirement in the ANSI/ISO COBOL Standard (past or present)
that vendors *must* "catch" all source code that violates the rules of the
Standard or their Language Reference. (This is the fact that was recently
"confirmed" by J4).

The next Standard *does* include a NEW requirement that vendors must provide
an "option" to flag syntax that violates the "syntactically detectable"
violations of their own COBOL (including Standard and Extensions) - HOWEVER,
even with the new rules, it will still be the vendor in many (most?) cases
that determines what is "syntactically detectable".

   ***

Bottom-Line:
  If you want to talk about COBOL source code that is "limited" to that
defined in the current ANSI/ISO Standards, then refer to it as "ANSI/ISO
conforming source code".  If you want to refer to whether specific COBOL
source code is or is not "valid" - then you MUST refer (only) to that source
code that the VENDOR says is valid.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and mi...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-07T14:06:06+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B1F7C3D.BA724241@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3B1E598A.D34434FC@Azonic.co.nz> <9flk74$54a$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B1F2247.6FDB3D26@Azonic.co.nz> <9fmc54$1j2$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> Richard - you are simply WRONG.  This is not what the ANSI (or ISO) COBOL
> Standard says - or what any COBOL standards commitee has EVER said.

Please quote the paragraph in the ANSI COBOL '84 Standard that defines
the word 'valid'.


> If you want to refer to whether specific COBOL
> source code is or is not "valid" - then you MUST refer (only) to that source
> code that the VENDOR says is valid.

What nonsense.  I get to choose the Vendor and I choose one that says
the code that I was talking about is 'invalid code'.

Therefore, even by your 'definition' it is invalid.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and mi...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-07T02:53:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B1EED42.8B10459D@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3B1E598A.D34434FC@Azonic.co.nz> <9flk74$54a$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B1F2247.6FDB3D26@Azonic.co.nz> <9fmc54$1j2$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3B1F7C3D.BA724241@Azonic.co.nz>`

```


Richard Plinston wrote:
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and mi...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-07T13:59:13-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9foitu$oe4$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3B1E598A.D34434FC@Azonic.co.nz> <9flk74$54a$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B1F2247.6FDB3D26@Azonic.co.nz> <9fmc54$1j2$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3B1F7C3D.BA724241@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard, (and I doubt that any others are actually interested in this - but
I am posting the references just in case)

  The documents that you are going to want to read (look at) concerning what
is and is not "COBOL" are the J4 documents:
   01-0022 (E-308.2) - Undetected User Errors (IQ-2204)
   01-0017 (E-308.1) - Undetected user errors
   01-0018 - Documents related to E-308.1
   01-0062 - Report of the X3J4 ad hoc group on interpretations

All of those can be found from:
   http://www.ncits.org/tc_home/j4htm/m229/j4m229.htm
(Use the document number to find each document)

I do NOT know (and have not looked to see) if they do or do not actually use
the word "valid" (or "invalid") but they certainly DO give the J4 answer to
any claim (that I can understand) that "valid COBOL" is something that exits
in the abstract and is not determined by each vendor.

As I say, I do believe that what you are talking about is "ANSI/ISO
conforming COBOL source code" - and that is well defined in the '85 Standard
(and those before and after).  See Page I-9 of the '85 Standard that says
(in part),

"1.6 DEFINITION OF A CONFORMING SOURCE PROGRAM

A conforming source program is one which does not violate the explicitly
stated provisions and specifications of Standard COBOL. In order for a
source program to conform to Standard COBOL, it must not include any
language elements not specified in this standard. The execution of a
program, the source text of which conforms to Standard COBOL, is predictable
only to the extent defined in this standard. The results of violating the
formats or rules of Standard COBOL
are undefined unless otherwise specified in this standard.

In order for a source program to conform to a specified subset of Standard
COBOL, it must include only language elements of that subset.

There are, in Standard COBOL, situations in which the results of executing a
statement are undefined or unpredictable (see page XVII-96, Undefined
Language Element List). A COBOL source program which allows this to happen
may nevertheless be a conforming program, although the resultant execution
is not defined by Standard COBOL."

   ***

Compare this with the earlier statement (on page I-8) which states,

"1.5.2.5.2 Extension Language Elements

An implementation that includes language elements in addition to the subset
and levels of optional modules for which support is claimed meets the
requirements of Standard COBOL. This is true even though it may imply the
extension of the list of reserved words by the implementor, and thereby may
prevent proper translation of some programs that meet the requirements of
Standard COBOL.

Documentation associated with an implementation must identify any standard
extensions (language elements not defined in the supported subset or
supported levels of optional modules but defined elsewhere in Standard
COBOL) or nonstandard extensions (language elements or functions not defined
in Standard COBOL) that are included in the implementation.

A conforming implementation of Standard COBOL must provide a warning
mechanism, which optionally may be invoked by the user at compile time to
indicate, if appropriate, that a program contains nonstandard extensions
that are included in the implementation."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and mi...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-07T09:10:24-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9fnuis$2p5k$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<3B1E598A.D34434FC@Azonic.co.nz> <9flk74$54a$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B1F2247.6FDB3D26@Azonic.co.nz> <9fmc54$1j2$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

William M. Klein wrote in message <9fmc54$1j2$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>...
[...]
>Bottom-Line:
>  If you want to talk about COBOL source code that is "limited" to that
>defined in the current ANSI/ISO Standards, then refer to it as "ANSI/ISO
>conforming source code".  If you want to refer to whether specific COBOL
>source code is or is not "valid" - then you MUST refer (only) to that
source
>code that the VENDOR says is valid.

Bill, I agree, but I want to take the explanation one step further.

"What COBOL is" is stated in the acknowledgment.

"COBOL is an industry language and is not the property of any
company or group of companies, or of any organization or
group of organizations. ..."

If the complete acknowledgment has been placed at the
beginning of a language reference, any specification that
follows is COBOL. This is true even if it looks like C, APL,
Ada, or classic COBOL. Furthermore, there is no requirement
that any COBOL compiler conform to any COBOL standard.
It also means that multiple, conflicting, COBOL standards
could exist at any time.

Hopefully, this makes it more clear that the compiler
manufacturer determines "valid COBOL" and a standard
determines "conforming COBOL".

Individuals determine whether any COBOL is a "true" or
"real" COBOL. :-)
------------------
Rick Smith
```

```
Richard,
  You of course (especially in a non-moderated newsgroup) may call PL/I
"valid" COBOL and the NIST conformance tests "invalid COBOL" - that just
doesn't make either of these correct statements.  Furthermore, in order to
insure that your implications (confusing "valid" which is not well defined
and "conforming" which is) do NOT confuse others, I will continue to correct
you when you use the WRONG term.

If there were no term for what you were talking about, then I would say -
fine go ahead and define your own terms and see if others agree (or if your
posts are clear).  HOWEVER, the fact remains that the people who DO define
the ANSI/ISO COBOL Standards have defined "conforming source code" to be
what you have (incorrectly termed) "valid COBOL".  They have also explicitly
stated that every vendor "defines" their own COBOL implementation - and that
such an implementation (with any number of extensions) remains a conforming
implementation (and thus any SANE person would call their "accepted" source
code "valid").

When you use the correct terms, I won't comment; when you use incorrect or
misleading ones, I will continue to correct the misconceptions that you try
to spread - whether you believe them or not.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and mi...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-09T18:57:35-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9fud5f$gdr$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3B1E598A.D34434FC@Azonic.co.nz> <9flk74$54a$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B1F2247.6FDB3D26@Azonic.co.nz> <9fmc54$1j2$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3B20E468.AB60E68C@Azonic.co.nz> <3B233220.82DCBE64@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard,
  I just love it.  Now COBOL that includes documented extensions is "junk
COBOL" (see below).

Conforming source code is EXACTLY what you are talking about.  It is
"conforming" source code that may not include a NEXT SENTENCE in an IF
terminated by an END-IF.  There is *no* other (defined) term for this
distinction.

Extensions to the COBOL Standard (defined by ANSI/ISO) are EXPLICITLY
allowed in a "conforming" implementation.  That you don't like SOME
conforming implementations or want programmers to limit their applications
to that subset (without acknowledging that even THAT subset is not
"portable" as far as either compile-time or run-time impact) seems strange -
if not counter-productive.

I have (repeatedly) pointed out that all conforming compilers MUST include a
compiler option to flag "non-conforming source code".  I know of almost no
programmers who ever use this option - as the "restricted" conforming source
code has such limited "real world use".  However, it does exist (in
conforming compilers) and is something that you might point people to - if
you want to have them check for conformance of their source code to the
defined ANSI/ISO standard.

If you want to "encourage" the use of "portable" source code, you might do a
little research into extensions that have "common implementations" and
especially those included in the draft of the next Standard.  Such "items"
include:
  - GoBack statement
  - USAGE POINTER (and/or procedure-pointer)
  - SET ADDRESS OF
  - LOCAL-STORAGE SECTION

By your definition, this is "junk COBOL" until some time at the end of next
year - when it will suddenly become "valid COBOL" (in your definition).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and mi...

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-10T11:15:22-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9g0307$te7$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<3B1E598A.D34434FC@Azonic.co.nz> <9flk74$54a$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B1F2247.6FDB3D26@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

Richard Plinston wrote in message <3B1F2247.6FDB3D26@Azonic.co.nz>...
>William M. Klein wrote:
>>
>> Valid COBOL is the COBOL that the compiler vendor SAYS is valid for that
>> compiler.
[...]
>'Valid COBOL' is what passes a comparison with the COBOL standard.

Which COBOL standard?

The BLOCK CONTAINS clause is defined in the ANSI 85
COBOL standard; but is excluded from the X/OPEN COBOL
standard. Is BLOCK CONTAINS valid COBOL or not?

If we can accept that each COBOL standard and each COBOL
language reference represents a distinct definition of COBOL,
then "valid COBOL" is that which conforms to a specific
definition of COBOL. The idea is that one cannot determine
whether COBOL source is valid without knowing which
definition applies.

It might be better to avoid the term "valid COBOL". ;-)
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and mi...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-11T10:40:03+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B2491F3.B8C0EAAE@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3B1E598A.D34434FC@Azonic.co.nz> <9flk74$54a$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B1F2247.6FDB3D26@Azonic.co.nz> <9g0307$te7$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:
 
> "What COBOL is" is stated in the acknowledgment.
> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> determines "conforming COBOL".
 
Actually you completely missed the whole issue on 'dialects'.  You are
quite correct that you could have a product that claims to be Cobol, but
if it looked more like C then it would be a dialect, possibly called
'Ricks Cobol'.  In which case a C program, even thogh it can be
processed by the 'Ricks Cobol compiler', does not turn into being
'Cobol' it only turns into being of the dialect 'Ricks Cobol'.

> Individuals determine whether any COBOL is a "true" or
> "real" COBOL. :-)

Given that 'true', 'real' and 'valid' are all not defined anywhere then
if you can claim to be able to determine what is 'true Cobol', then I
can claim to be able to determine what is 'valid Cobol' (or actually
'invalid') in exactly the same way.

Bill is quite right that _IF_ I had used 'conforming' is a way that
_disagreed_ with the standard then he _may_ be able to argue that I was
wrong.

> then "valid COBOL" is that which conforms to a specific
> definition of COBOL. 

Is this a formal definition that you found in the standard, or did you
just make this up ?

You also need to be more careful, you used the word 'conform'.  I have
been repeatedly berated that 'conform' is to be used when referring to
the ANSI/ISO Standard.  Now it seem that you will use the term when
referring to dialects and versions.

Specifically Bill told me that I MUST use the term 'conforming Cobol'
instead of 'valid Cobol' when talking about ANSI/ISO Standard, yet you
seem to be free to use that term when referring to versions and dialects
that may be non-standard.

If there is specific version or dialect of Cobol then what would be
useful if if the actual dialect is specified.  Bill's full-stop-less
paragraphs may be 'valid IBM MVS Cobol/2' (or even semi-valid whatever
_that_ means).

> The idea is that one cannot determine
> whether COBOL source is valid without knowing which
> definition applies.

Exactly what I have been saying.  But, of course, when the version is
not specified then are you saying that it cannot be discussed ?

In any case what gets right up my nose is pompous prats attempting to
make every informal discussion into a legal argument with several page
quotes from the standard and lecturing me on what I can and cannot say.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and mi...

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-11T20:29:08+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B251C04.E808ACCD@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3B1E598A.D34434FC@Azonic.co.nz> <9flk74$54a$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:

> By your definition, this is "junk COBOL" 

I would be interested as to how you contrived to make my words into a
'definition'.

>   I just love it.  Now COBOL that includes documented extensions is "junk
> COBOL" (see below).

You are confused yet again.  The cobol in question is not 'junk cobol'
because it contains extensions, it is junk because it is difficult to
extract what it actually means and even harder to determine what it was
intended to mean.

It is easy to contrive a nested IF structure where the inner IF has a
NEXT SENTENCE and the outer IF has an END-IF, and this would be valid
standard cobol.  This alone would make it 'junk cobol' (without _any_
extensions) if the programmer relied on the NEXT SENTENCE to go to some
point beyond the end of the scope of the IFs.  This is because the NEXT
SENTENCE acts as a GO TO to the next full-stop wherever it may be
(except where an extension such as MF's OLDNEXTSENTENCE directive
changes its behaviour BTW).  The full-stop then acts as an _anonymous_
label.  This in itself is a very productive bug-nest.

The extension of not requiring a full-stop at the end of a paragraph
then potentially makes the target of the NEXT SENTENCE not only
anonymous, but _invisible_ (if I understand the compiler behaviour
correctly). This means that you need a language lawyer for that dialect
to understand what is _going_ to happen and a mind-reader to know what
the programmer _intended_ to happen.

Perhaps the compiler adds an implicit full-stop and this acts as a
target for the NEXT SENTENCE, perhaps the programmer thought that it
would take an actual full-stop on an executable statement to be the
target.  How would you ever know ?

Would _you_ even care ? As long as they use the correct terminology and
quote the relevant paragraphs when discussing it you may think that the
code is ok to use.

The only safe thing to do with such code is to dispose of it (junk it)
and rewrite from the specifications, otherwise it will just be a
bug-factory for each new coder to fall into the traps.

> Conforming source code is EXACTLY what you are talking about.  It is
> "conforming" source code that may not include a NEXT SENTENCE in an IF
> terminated by an END-IF.  There is *no* other (defined) term for this
> distinction.

Again you are confused.  There is a contrived way in which the standard
allows a NEXT SENTENCE to be 'included' in an IF ... END-IF, that is by
having it in a nested IF included in the IF ... END-IF.

You have been let down by sloppy use of words.  

You <<MUST>> say that 'If the END-IF is specified in an IF statement
then the NEXT SENTENCE phrase must not be specified'.  'Include' is not
part of the standard, There is *no* other (defined) way of saying this.

And I may keep repeating this at you in a thoughly boorish way until you
repent and admit that you were WRONG, WRONG, WRONG.

 

> Extensions to the COBOL Standard (defined by ANSI/ISO) are EXPLICITLY
> allowed in a "conforming" implementation.  That you don't like SOME
> conforming implementations or want programmers to limit their applications
> to that subset 

It has nothing to do wanting programmers to not use extensions, so you
rant is entirely misdirected (as usual).  It is about writing better
programs that are more easily maintained.  Though this doesn't seem to
be a concern of yours, you prefer people using the 'correct' terms.


> (without acknowledging that even THAT subset is not
> "portable" as far as either compile-time or run-time impact) seems strange -
> if not counter-productive.

It is nothing to do with portable.  You drew entirely the wrong
conclusions about what made 'junk' code and then extrapolated this in an
entirely haphazard and incompetent way.

 
> I have (repeatedly) pointed out 

ad nauseum ..

> that all conforming compilers MUST include a
> compiler option to flag "non-conforming source code".  I know of almost no
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> defined ANSI/ISO standard.

Unfortuately, if the manual doesn't distinquish between ANSI/ISO
standard and actual implementation the programmers won't actually be
able to understand why they are getting these messages.

> If you want to "encourage" the use of "portable" source code, you might do a
> little research into extensions that have "common implementations" and
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>   - LOCAL-STORAGE SECTION

What's your point ?  I know which are actual extensions and which will
be in the next standard.  Except for GOBACK (which always seemed to be
an IBMish thing and little point where EXIT PROGRAM and STOP RUN did the
same), I have used them for some years, but always knew they were
extensions.


> By your definition, this is "junk COBOL" until some time at the end of next
> year - when it will suddenly become "valid COBOL" (in your definition).
 
No. Wrong.  I never made any definition of 'junk', and my use of 'junk
Cobol' is not about extensions at all.

Your 'conclusion' is completely wrong and shows an incompetence in being
able to usefully understand what is written, to be able to extrapolate,
or to draw accurate conclusions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: invalid" source code (END-IF, Next Sentence, and mi...

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-11T14:20:53-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9g35mq$ld7$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3B1E598A.D34434FC@Azonic.co.nz> <9flk74$54a$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B251C04.E808ACCD@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard changes his definitions and terms yet again.  In the note (to which
I am replying), he states (some sections snipped - see the full note for
"context")


>
> > By your definition, this is "junk COBOL"
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >   I just love it.  Now COBOL that includes documented extensions is
"junk
> > COBOL" (see below).
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

 <snip>
>
> > (without acknowledging that even THAT subset is not
> > "portable" as far as either compile-time or run-time impact) seems
strange -
> > if not counter-productive.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Now lets look at what Richard actually said in the note to which I was
replying,

"If you actually wanted to reduce confusion you would be telling what the
standard says about Cobol instead of parading how wonderfully obscrure
*YOUR* compiler can be in accepting what I would call 'junk Cobol'."

The "highlighted" "*YOUR* compiler" certainly seems to be stating that "junk
COBOL <sic>" is related to what a specific compiler allows (or doesn't
allow).  This *must* have to do with extensions and not with what is in the
ANSI/ISO Standard (which - I think we ALL agree *can* be obscure - but need
not be).

Terms that "make sense" to me and that I think that most CLC people would
agree to are:
   (Please reply to these terms in SEPARATE thread)

 - Conforming COBOL - source code conforms to ANSI/ISO Standard (current
ANSI/ISO Standard).
 - 'nn Conforming COBOL - source code that conforms to the "19nn" or "20nn"
ANSI/ISO Standard
 - Conforming XYZ COBOL - source code conforms to the XYZ Standard (e.g.
X/Open, MIA, SAA, etc)
 - Clear (and usually maintainable) COBOL - source code that can
(subjectively) be picked up and understood/maintained by a programmer OTHER
than the one who originally wrote it.  (Sorry, Tony - this doesn't include
your code that is "clear" to you, but not to many/most others)
 - Valid XYZ COBOL - COBOL which gets a "clean compile" with the XYZ
compiler.  The XYZ compiler vendor describes the rules for this source code
and whether informational or warning message may or may not be issued for
such code.  (The phrase "valid COBOL" may be used without mentioning XYZ -
if the context makes it clear which compiler - and possible release and
possibly operating system are being used.  The same is true for the "XYZ" in
"Conforming XYZ COBOL").
 - Portable COBOL - COBOL that compiles "cleanly" and produces the same
run-time results in multiple environments.  In most cases, one actually
needs to (at least initially) talk about which environments are in question,
as almost no "real world" COBOL is fully portable to ALL known ANSI/ISO
conforming compilers/operating systems.
```

<a id="t-ad51eaffff"></a>
## Thread: convert cobol — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### Re: convert cobol

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-06T18:02:10+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B1E6212.EACC265E@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
> It may be outdated for the customers, but not for me. I am not going
> to convert a 300,000 line package for someone who is love with their
> Macintosh or Web-TV or PalmPilot or PDP-8 or garage-door opener.

That's OK no one is going to ask you to (except, possibly, your
customers).

It may well be that you have yourself locked in to your platform.

However, you seem to have totally missed the point about 'cross platform
portability'.  There is no 'convert 300,000 lines'.  With, say, AcuCobol
(which I don't actually use) or even MF with Application Server, there
is no 'convert' there is not even 'recompile', and as long as some rules
have been followed and the code is competent there may not even be a
need to 'retest'.

I develop under Multiuser-DOS (because I like it and it does everything
I need without crashing) doing the code-compile-test cycle, then I
export it to SCO where it gets recompiled (though I actually could just
send .int) and from there sent to Linux if required.  If necessary it
gets put on a Windows machine and runs there (because I use SP2 it can
work as a Windows application).

When I first put one of my systems to Unix I had it running in half a
day and that included recoding some of the grubby stuff such as listing
files for selection.
 
> We don't make a product for the masses; our software costs
> significantly more than the hardware it runs on.

Then a few hundred dollars in run-time fees wouldn't hurt your market
much.  Or, more importantly, won't hurt your competitors much.
 
> When we get an inquiry (fortunately rare) about whether our software
> runs on a Mac, the prospect gets the same reaction as a car buyer
> would get from a Corvette dealership when he says: "Hey, I already own
> these truck tires. Can you make me a sports car that fits 'em? I want
> sumthin' com-pat-a-bly with my pickup!"

That, of course, is a nonsense analogy, and you seem to have it the
wrong way around.  You are making the customer buy the hardware (car) to
fit your software (tires) regardless of how compatible it is/isn't with
_anything_ he may already have.

But that's OK, if you don't need the market or want it then someone else
will fit the requirement using a cross-platform solution perhaps.

But, I can't recall that anyone here actually asked you to convert to
cross-platform, so why are you being so defensive about it ?

Just as long as you aren't insisting that we shouldn't be doing that
(which you come close to sometimes by saying it isn't valuable).
```

```
> That's OK no one is going to ask you to (except, possibly, your
> customers).

No, my 'customers' don't ask. Occasionaly a prospect will ask.

>
> It may well be that you have yourself locked in to your platform.

Yep. Sure have.

>
> However, you seem to have totally missed the point about 'cross
platform
> portability'.  There is no 'convert 300,000 lines'.  With, say,
AcuCobol
> (which I don't actually use) or even MF with Application Server,
there
> is no 'convert' there is not even 'recompile', and as long as some
rules
> have been followed and the code is competent there may not even be a
> need to 'retest'.

'Rules' to some means 'Restrictions' to others.

>
> I develop under Multiuser-DOS (because I like it and it does
everything
> I need without crashing) doing the code-compile-test cycle, then I
> export it to SCO where it gets recompiled (though I actually could
just
> send .int) and from there sent to Linux if required.  If necessary
it
> gets put on a Windows machine and runs there (because I use SP2 it
can
> work as a Windows application).

Hey, way cool.

>
> When I first put one of my systems to Unix I had it running in half
a
> day and that included recoding some of the grubby stuff such as
listing
> files for selection.

Excellent. If your business model includes Unix, not only did you
accomplish the 'conversion' in half a day (at not much cost), but you
now have a product you can offer to other Unix users. And I'm really,
really glad it works for you. I, on the other hand, have never
knowingly been in the same room with Unix and I never will.

>
> > We don't make a product for the masses; our software costs
> > significantly more than the hardware it runs on.
>
> Then a few hundred dollars in run-time fees wouldn't hurt your
market
> much.  Or, more importantly, won't hurt your competitors much.

We don't use runtime fees. And even if we were oblidged to do so, you
are correct: it would not hurt our market much. We don't have
competitors. Oh, there are some companies that have a primitive
version of what we do but it's like comparing a text editor to MSWord.

>
> > When we get an inquiry (fortunately rare) about whether our
software
> > runs on a Mac, the prospect gets the same reaction as a car buyer
> > would get from a Corvette dealership when he says: "Hey, I already
own
> > these truck tires. Can you make me a sports car that fits 'em? I
want
> > sumthin' com-pat-a-bly with my pickup!"
>
> That, of course, is a nonsense analogy, and you seem to have it the
> wrong way around.  You are making the customer buy the hardware
(car) to
> fit your software (tires) regardless of how compatible it is/isn't
with
> _anything_ he may already have.

Yeah, I guess the analogy works both ways. Point is, a thinking
prospect will focus on the desired solution - a combination of
hardware and software - and will obtain the combination that
accomplishes the task. To insist on a hardware restriction may (in our
case, certainly) limit the prospect's reaching his goal.

>
> But that's OK, if you don't need the market or want it then someone
else
> will fit the requirement using a cross-platform solution perhaps.

I'm not worried. If a competitor sprang up with that business plan,
I'd love it. In fact one of our competitors (I just found out) is
abandoning their text-based QNX system and intend to develop in (wait
for it now...) Linux. I almost died trying to stifle a Beaufort Force
VII giggle when they told me.

>
> But, I can't recall that anyone here actually asked you to convert
to
> cross-platform, so why are you being so defensive about it ?

Sigh. I know. I'm cursed with the compulsion to point out the Emperor
Has No Clothes.

>
> Just as long as you aren't insisting that we shouldn't be doing that
> (which you come close to sometimes by saying it isn't valuable).
>

No, no, no, I'm not insisting anyone do anything! I'm just forcefully
presenting facts sufficiently strong in themselves to compel a
rational mind to the certain conclusions.

If some developers want to take a different tack, I say "Go for it!"

I've always loved the slogan: "Unleash the Power of ConcurrentDOS!"
```

### O.T. Oracle physical placement of tables?

- **From:** gary drummond <gdrumm@ont.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-06T10:56:51-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B1E6EE3.1E0F79BA@ont.com>`

```
This would be better asked in an Oracle news group, but based on the
responses I get from DBAs, I would get too many answers that don't
mean anything, or questioning looks like "Why do you care?".

My question is "Does anyone know of any resource that explains how to
force Oracle to position certain table/indexes?/..., or the 'data', 
in particular places-in one or more filesystems on a Unix system?

If I can tell the DBA how to do it, I can size and position the
data for maximum I/O throughput, but as I mentioned, all I get is
why or ???. I can slice/dice/mirror any of the files and filesystems, 
but I can't get any of the "super" DBAs to tell me how! Is this 
something that isn't available to them, or what?

Gary
```

```
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Gary
When I worked on a mainframe, I could place the DB areas that were 
hit the hardest, in the middle tracks of few strings of disks. The 
other areas were placed on the outer or inner tracks depending on 
block and record sizes. I just wanted to know how to physically 
position oracle volumes.

When the database gets slow, the apps people point at the DBAs, and
the DBAs point at the SysAdmin. Nobody wins.

I talked to my son and some Oracle people from his shop. They just
configure for the best I/O they can afford, and when the DB get slow,
they export and rebuild it. I did borrow some books, and a copy of
Oracle's CBT. There must be some type of tuning available.

Gary
```

```
Eisenhowser was the president while I was in my developmental years...I

lost, and still lose...

I'm sorry, but if I can find ANYTHING to improve performance of a system
I am responsible for, I will try. I'm just that way, and nothing will
change it. 

I did, by rebuilding the raid and veritas configurations, cause the
positioning of an Oracle DB to improve it's performance, but it was
short-lived. It seems that many Oracle features adjust themselves, but
*like* indexed files have in the past. We all know that reloading
indexed files, after too many updates, doesn't increase performance :~)

Give me a break...I'm still learning "why" relational is "better" for
hierarchical types of  data (So far-only from the hardware vendors'
viewpoint!). When I learn enough, I'll post it in the Oracle group. I
just wanted the "trenches" to respond to this, in language that a simple
32-year veteran of the IT world can understand. My DBA training (And 15
years support experience) was not on relational DBs, and I haven't
learned all of the buzzwords to hide the "answers-I-don't-know" yet!

Gary
```

```
Actually, it's from the RDBMS vendors' point of view.

I personally think a hierarchical DBMS is superior for hierarchical data.
Problem is, the major vendors have put all their money into RDBMS's.

Also, the pointy-clicky-touchy-feely new generation of programmers (the ones
who think "Eisenhower" is a either a Civil War general or the name of a
river in Germany) would probably get freaked out looking at, say, a DL/1 DB.
```

```
Michael,

Care to expand on your comments about DBMS as opposed to RDBMS - or perhaps you
can point me to sites that already spell it out. Thinking back some 30 years I
can think of a classical case where RDBMS full Normalization would be an
absolute MUST and would have been very effective, (instead of using COBOL
files). Full normalization -  taking what I'm currently attempting, I have a gut
feeling would present problems - not alleviate them. Only testing it will prove
the theory.

Somebody wrote recently , 'You start out by normalizing, then de-normalize'.
That's a bit daft - like building a two-storey house when what you really want
is a bungalow.

As regards DBMS sounds awfully close to me, to a well designed hierarchical
system of COBOL files. From your standpoint does RDBMS 'prohibit' things you
would have done with DBMS. And as you've already commented 'Design, Design,
Design.... Then comes the programming".

Jimmy, Calgary, AB
```

```
> Michael Mattias wrote:
>
> >
> > I personally think a hierarchical DBMS is superior for hierarchical
data.
> > Problem is, the major vendors have put all their money into RDBMS's.
> >
>
> Care to expand on your comments about [a hierarchical] DBMS as opposed to
RDBMS - or perhaps you
> can point me to sites that already spell it out.

My comments in strictly in terms of application data structure. If the data
are truly hierarchical - that is, there is some row/record for which there
is never a good reason to access that record except when you also "from an
application perspective" will always be accessing the parent, then why not
have a DBMS which mirrors the application?

For example, I saw a nifty IMS database a couple of years ago in insurance.
The parent was the policy; under the policy were timestamped records
indicating what coverage was in effect in a given time period. This allowed
tracking of when the policyholder added an item of property to casualty
insurance, changed a deductible option, etc. Policyholder could never claim
"loss" for an item which was not added to the policy until after the date of
loss, nor claim a $200 deductible if he did not upgrade from $500 deductible
AFTER the policy period started but PRIOR to the loss.

Never any reason to go after the "coverage as of xxxx date" records without
also going after the parent.

That's all I meant. Today I'd probably write this application relational,
anyway. The Hierarchical skills seem to have left with.... Eisenhower.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: O.T. Oracle physical placement of tables?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-12T13:26:12+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b256f6c_5@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<3B1E6EE3.1E0F79BA@ont.com> <1CAT6.6$wH.33191@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net> <3B1F2A29.FD808743@ont.com> <mvUT6.1219$ep3.235029@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <3B22A845.E48CA81F@ont.com> <yIJU6.2600$qh3.489455@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <3B23CE52.B295F270@home.com> <VL0V6.20$r93.7947@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
No, Michael,

there are still a few of us around who understand Hierarchical databases
(and even the Network model [IDMS f'rinstance...).

I made a living for many years as an IMS DBA, wrote generalised software to
access IMS hierarchies and even found obscure codes to modify the DL/1 calls
into holding position at various levels of the hierarchy.

Your observations below regarding hierarchical structure are very valid
(although you neglected to mention the downside ...the need to traverse the
hierarchy to get to dependant data, but that's another story...).

When Relational DBs first appeared, many of us who had been using the
Hierarchic model successfully for a number of years were quite dismayed by
the apparent lack of support for hierarchic data...

We loathed Relational with a passion and resisted it fiercely. Fortunately,
the early Relational implementations were very inefficient and we could
benchmark our Hierarchies against them and win every time (of course the
data was always slanted to favour the Hierarchic Model...<G>).  However, as
implementations of the Relational Model became more efficient and Big Blue
dictated that it WOULD be used, we were forced to take it seriously and,
eventually, I for one, became persuaded of its applicability.

Today, the use of Foreign Keys provides for Hierarchic support within the
Relational model, and we have much more understanding, knowledge, and proven
Methodology with which to design  our DBs, so supporting Hierarchies within
RDB technology is no problem.

The fact is (as anyone who has ever read "Zen and the Art of Motorcycle
Maintenance" will attest) that hierarchic structures are a VERY good way to
organise information and much "natural" data follows this pattern. It is
natural and intuitive for Humans to absorb information in a "Top Down"
manner, gradually expanding on sound basic concepts or overviews. It is easy
to design systems on the same basis.

The success of Object Orientation is partly because it allows an incremental
inheritance Hierarchy.

There is NOTHING wrong with a Hierarchic approach in many cases.

The danger is in assuming that a Hierarchic Database will IMPLEMENT this
approach BETTER than a Relational Database. While, on the face of it, this
might appear to be a reasonable assumption, practice has shown that because
of technical limitations in the Hierarchic software, combined with huge
advances in the Relational software (especially in recent years) and better
design Methodology based on the Relational Model, today it is quite feasible
to implement a hierarchic structure with an RDB and get not only the
benefits of the hierarchy, but also the RELATIONAL benefits.

ADMISSION OF BIAS: I use ONLY the Relational model for new system
development (even where Hierarchic data is involved). I have never found it
to be inadequate when implemented properly.

Pete.



"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
```

```
you make a theoretical normalized model, you de-normalize for

reasons to avoid, heavily dependent on the data and how it is used.
```

- **References:** `<3B1E6EE3.1E0F79BA@ont.com> <1CAT6.6$wH.33191@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net> <3B1F2A29.FD808743@ont.com> <mvUT6.1219$ep3.235029@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <3B22A845.E48CA81F@ont.com> <yIJU6.2600$qh3.489455@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <3B23CE52.B295F270@home.com> <3B2528CE.D9D68E46@boeing.com>`

```


Susan Allen wrote:

> "James J. Gavan" wrote:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> reasons to avoid, heavily dependent on the data and how it is used.

Agree with your comments Susan. Regardless of tool there is no 'pure tool' and only
one way in which to use it. We don't need to be omniscient to know that down there
in Seattle you will use a whole different bunch of tools and design concepts to
cover JIT Inventory - as opposed to engineers using ENTIRELY different software for
cockpit design.

I just told Thane fairly recently about Flour Daniel (engineers) here in Calgary but
HQ'd in the States. An English engineer told me he had been working on an oil/gas
plant design for over six months, before he found out the original model they were
fine tuning, was in fact a 'virtual' created by their computer software - coupled
with an extensive DB, inclusive of everything down to the nuts and bolts !

I've no doubt you could parallel that one in Seattle <G>

This is merely a memory representation of something that occurred nearly 30 years
ago - which we as analysts/programmers reluctantly inherited. (I don't have the
finite details because I was the Systems Manager not the analyst),

01 StoreRecord.
    05 PrimeKey.
        10 StoreNumber                 pic 9(03).
        10 DepartmentNumber        pic 9(04).
    05 WeeklySales     pic 9(???? occurs 53.
    05 WeeklyPlannedStock occurs 53....
    05 WeeklyClosing Stock occurs 53
    05 WeeklyCommitment   occurs 53
    05 WeeklyOpenToBuy occurs 53
    05 WeeklyMarkdowns occurs 53
    05 Weekly ...................................... I could go on and on.

At the design level it is badly flawed :-

(a) One huge conglomerate record for an average of 60 departments for 60 department
stores

(b) Fashion Merchandise etc. - yes you need all those features. But even some
fashion departments are 'seasonal' see (c) below

(c) "Easter Bunny Time" and Christmas World etc. - seasonal departments that only
need some of those fields for the duration of their seasonal operation.
Historically, yes, you need TotalSales and the ClosingStock, the latter for the
audited statements.

(d) Foodhall - no we aren't talking 'supermarkets' but specialties - particularly
with perishables - order and sell before expiry date

(e) Catering - coffee shops, restaurants/dining rooms - again order what they
currently need. Planned stock and what is planned as OpenToBuy - are totally
irrelevant.

Categories (d) and (e) were 'pushed' into fitting into the overall design - sorry
the word is not 'design' - they were thrown in as the bean counters thought of
something else to add to the record..

(f) Nearly forgot - Shop-in-Shop, (independently run 'departments') - that's where
the retailer rents out floor  space to well known manufacturers such as Aquascutum ,
plus all those cosmetic, perfumery  houses that recruit/run their own sales staff -
the store gets a commission cut off the sales. Again fits into the above record, but
only weekly sales are relevant, primarily for the commission and also performance in
terms of 'Gross sales per square foot"..

As a total amateur in DB, I would suggest this COBOL file application would have
been an absolute natural for full Normalization.

What happened in practice, when we really got our hands on this and re-designed it -
the analyst proposed a COBOL "normalization"

    - Primekey (Store/Dept)
    - RecordType ( 1= Sales, 2 = Stock etc....)
    - Values - (can't recall if this was individual weeks or occurs 53)

We also printed statistics in Department/Store sequence - don't recall, either we
did sorts or had alternate keys designed.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
gary drummond <gdrumm@ont.com> wrote in message 
> When I worked on a mainframe, I could place the DB areas that were 
> hit the hardest, in the middle tracks of few strings of disks. The 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
In the Mainframe, Logical Disk vs. Physycal Disk are tightly
connected, base on
where you hit on the spot of disk, the mechanical head has to be fly
by a servo voltage and wait for latency for spindle so and so, but,
newer DB uses an massive memory or cached Disk array to compensate
those speed issue, almost to invisible to user under the good tunning
on the DB side.
 
> When the database gets slow, the apps people point at the DBAs, and
> the DBAs point at the SysAdmin. Nobody wins.
> 
What I know in the TOAD (Oracle tool) from the Quest software, you
could watch your process to check how many disk I/O you get. If the
process get less I/O than you expect with no reason call DBA, and also
you could tune your SQL statement using this utility. also depend on
app, call PL/SQL to reduce network traffics and select only what the
application need data items not whole table,  so on so...
```

#### ↳ Re: Question

- **From:** Jeff Campbell <jcampbell@ins-msi.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-06T20:15:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B1E88FC.2386E975@ins-msi.com>`
- **References:** `<9flr84$qu3$1@venus.telepac.pt>`

```
Paulo wrote:
> 
> Hi, I need to know how many records a indexed file have.
> I work with Fujitsu Cobol 5, there is some way to do this ?
> 
> Thanks.

According to a table in Appendix B of the Fujitsu 5 "Cobol Language
Reference - Volume 2" the maximum size of an indexed file is
1,073,741,824 bytes. The maximum record size is 32,760 bytes.

There is not an express limit on the 'number' of records. As long as
your file is composed of records less than 32,761 bytes and the total
data size of all records is less than 1,073,741,825 bytes you should
be ok.

I think there is an optional DLL that can be copied from the
installation CD that increases the file capacity to nearly 4 GB.
The maximum record size remains the same, though.

HTH,

Jeff Campbell
n8wxs@arrl.net
```

#### ↳ Re: Question

- **From:** chammond@lc.cc.il.us
- **Date:** 2001-06-13T21:04:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9g8kgv$pk0$1@news.netmar.com>`
- **References:** `<9flr84$qu3$1@venus.telepac.pt>`

```
In article <9flr84$qu3$1@venus.telepac.pt>, Paulo <palsg@hotmail.com>
writes:
>Hi, I need to know how many records a indexed file have.
>I work with Fujitsu Cobol 5, there is some way to do this ?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Why not just count the records as you read the file?  Make a counter, set it
to zero and add one to it every time you perform a read in your program.  We
often do this as a diagnostic tool when we build reports and files, so we can
see the records read versus the records Written or printed.

Another option is to find out the total number of bytes in the file and then
divide by the number of bytes in a record (Assuming you are using fixed
length records).  If you can copy one record and save it in a separte file it
will tell you the general size and you can estimate the record count.  It
isnt too hard to just write a little program that just counts records, if the
file isnt too large. 

Someone may have a file utility that can look at the file and find out real
quick.
```

```
> >Hi, I need to know how many records a indexed file have.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Why not just count the records as you read the file?  Make a
counter, set it
> to zero and add one to it every time you perform a read in your
program.  We
> often do this as a diagnostic tool when we build reports and files,
so we can
> see the records read versus the records Written or printed.

Good idea. A header record for the file (say a key of low-values) that
contains:

Active Records
Read Operations
Write Operations
Rewrite Operations
Opens
Closes

along with absolute diligence on the programmer's part to accurately
tabulate and record these values could prove very helpful in
subsequent tune-ups.

>
> Another option is to find out the total number of bytes in the file
and then
> divide by the number of bytes in a record (Assuming you are using
fixed
> length records).  If you can copy one record and save it in a
separte file it
> will tell you the general size and you can estimate the record
count.  It
> isnt too hard to just write a little program that just counts
records, if the
> file isnt too large.

That won't work on an indexed file because the file size is the sum of
(the number of records times the record length) plus all the internal
positioning information the ISAM file system needs. In some cases, it
is possible for the latter to exceed the former. After a period of
use, most ISAM files contain either significant wasted space or
significant key-chasing. I don't think anyone has yet devised an ISAM
file system whose efficiency couldn't benefit from a periodic
reorganization.


>
> Someone may have a file utility that can look at the file and find
out real
> quick.
>
>

posts
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-14T17:09:15+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b284a30_3@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<9flr84$qu3$1@venus.telepac.pt> <9g8kgv$pk0$1@news.netmar.com> <U5TV6.21619$JS1.1204125@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```

"Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com> wrote in message > That won't work on an
indexed file because the file size is the sum of
> (the number of records times the record length) plus all the internal
> positioning information the ISAM file system needs. In some cases, it
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> reorganization.

VSAM Key Sequenced Datasets (which replaced ISAM to a large extent in the
IBM Mainframe environment ) were developed in response to the need for a
self reorganizing Indexed file structure.

They use binary fission to acquire space at two separate logical levels:
within a "block" (control interval) and within a sub-file space (control
area). Free space is administered by a series of chained pointers at both
levels. "Overflow" (in the sense understood when referring to ISAM files)
can NEVER occur (as long as disk space is available, obviously), and the
datasets NEVER need to be off-loaded and re-loaded or re-organized.
(Although sometimes this used to be done on small systems to  reclaim unused
free space).

It is an ingenious and highly efficient file organization.

Pete.
```

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:
> 
> "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com> wrote in message > That won't work on an
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>                 http://www.usenet.com

I learned COBOL and indexed files in 1970/71, and never understood 
why there was so much concern about the files growing. I didn't 
find out for another year or so, when someone mentioned that the
filespace on other systems were formatted for ISAM. Our system
was designed with fixed block I/O, so ISAM was designed that way 
(like VSAM) from the start. 

When I was assigned to a GM site, we saved the logs for the programs 
that used the files in sequential mode only. When the physical 
I/O caused the wall clock time get too high, we dumped and 
restored them (Reorg), but because of the space reserved at 
file creation time, the size usually increased...

Gary
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2001-06-17T18:03:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010617140303.03072.00000914@ng-cj1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3b284a30_3@Usenet.com>`

```
>From: "Peter E. C. Dashwood" dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz 
>Date: 2001-06-14 01:09 Eastern Daylight Time

>VSAM Key Sequenced Datasets (which replaced ISAM to a large
>extent in the IBM Mainframe environment ) were developed in 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I did not understand your statement above.  

Almost all my experience was on Burroughs/Unisys.  Recently,
I did a year and a half at the DMV where they had an IBM VSAM
environment.  

The DMV rebuilt all its VSAM files every single night.  

One of my own programs caused problems because a master
file kept stopping with some kind of  "out of space error."  It
turned out that the alternate key only had space for 10 new
additions.  One of the users was leaving a lot of fields empty
while they were adding records, planning to go back and
fill them in later.  This practice resulted in an unexpectedly
high number of duplicate alternate keys being added, and
the program ceased to work about every other day.

I did not see any evidence that VSAM never needs a reorg. 


Ross
http://www.geocities.com/ross_klatte/
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-17T20:32:14+02:00

```
On 17 Jun 2001 18:03:03 GMT, Ross Klatte wrote:

>I did not understand your statement above.  
>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>
A well designed VSAM  file doesn't "need" reorganization, but reorganizing
often results in a better performance.

The "out of space" error you mentioned is a clear example of a bad design of
the dataset, the application, or both.


Willem Clements
Brainbench MVP for COBOL II
http://www.brainbench.com
```

```
In article <9g8kgv$pk0$1@news.netmar.com>, chammond@lc.cc.il.us writes:

>
>Another option is to find out the total number of bytes in the file and then
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

This would probably work if all your fields are PIC X.
A neat trick in the Fuji implementation of the ISAM file is that all 
(it appears) numeric fields are compressed in some manner to save space.
I had a 12M flat master file that I rebuilt defined as Indexed and 
the total file ended up being somewhere around 512K.  Granted 
that I only had 1 character field and all  the others were numeric 
of varying sizes.
```

```
"Sff5ky" <sff5ky@aol.comxxx123>
>
> This would probably work if all your fields are PIC X.
> A neat trick in the Fuji implementation of the ISAM file is that all
> (it appears) numeric fields are compressed in some manner to save
space.
> I had a 12M flat master file that I rebuilt defined as Indexed and
> the total file ended up being somewhere around 512K.  Granted
> that I only had 1 character field and all  the others were numeric
> of varying sizes.

Wondering whether you were demented, I repeated your experiment.

I took a flat file consisting of a 10-byte key and 60 binary
half-words of data (130 bytes in total) and converted it to a Fujitsu
ISAM file. As a flat file, it covered 54.5 Mb. As a FJ ISAM file, it
shrank almost HALF to 29.5 Mb.

I'm impressed by your observation and Fujitsu's file system's internal
and invisible compaction.
```

```
> It is a pretty slick thing , whatever Fujitsu is doing with the ISAM files.

MF (Merant) Cobol has an optional data compression package that I have
used with some success.  I think it does run-length encoding so is best
suited for data that includes runs of spaces or zeroes. It allowed me to
get an 8MByte product file onto a diskette for issuing as a product
catalog.
```

```
M/F compression directives. But some 18 months or more back, saw a query on
Answerlab as to why M/F's own Sort module didn't work - answer - can't use it
with data compression. (Actually, I've never used the Sort).

The other was far more pertinent - was looking through on-line help and
'accidentally' came up against restrictions with compression. Regrettably can't
remember the specifics - but it lodged very firmly in my mind "Don't mess with
Directives". So while compression is good, particularly if you are following it
up with a zip to a diskette - I am veering away from compression.

Still, going for a DB and SQL, it's not the same baby as COBOL files anyway, is
it.

Jimmy
```
