## Thread: What exactly is DMSII and LINC? — 6 msgs

### What exactly is DMSII and LINC?

- **From:** "COGNICASE - Patrick Saunderson" <patrick.saunderson@cognicase.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-06T19:45:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ALvT6.2862$jD6.266530@news>`

```
I would apprecaite a detailed answer to both items.

Thanks,
```

#### ↳ Re: What exactly is DMSII and LINC?

- **From:** "Tim Scrivens" <tim.scrivens@nz.eds.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-07T09:30:36+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9fm7eb$vmq$1@hermes.nz.eds.com>`
- **References:** `<ALvT6.2862$jD6.266530@news>`

```

"COGNICASE - Patrick Saunderson" <patrick.saunderson@cognicase.com> wrote in
message news:ALvT6.2862$jD6.266530@news...
> I would apprecaite a detailed answer to both items.
>
> Thanks,
>
How detailed is detailed?

Data Management System II
DMSII is a mature, proven database management system designed for
enterprise-wide data management. DMSII supports large databases and
high-volume online transaction processing and accommodates installations of
virtually any size.

DMSII offers exceptional flexibility in accommodating a broad variety of
data models. The database can be a hierarchical, network, or flat structure
or can be normalized to a relational model. You define the database using
the DMSII Data and Structure Definition Language (DASDL) or the
menu-assisted interface that is part of the optional Advanced Data
Dictionary System (ADDS). The ADDS interface eliminates the need to define
database characteristics by using a conventional syntactic language.

DMSII supports a variety of information accessing techniques. It
incorporates validation, audit/recovery, and access control capabilities and
supports several optional utilities for database analysis, monitoring,
integrity certification, online reorganization, and online archiving.

DMSII Extended Edition (DMSII XE)
DMSII SE is a mature, proven database management system designed for
enterprise-wide data management. DMSII SE supports large databases and
high-volume online transaction processing, and it accommodates installations
of virtually any size. DMSII SE offers exceptional flexibility in
accommodating a broad variety of data models-hierarchical, network, flat, or
relational. DMSII SE also supports a variety of information accessing
techniques. It incorporates validation, audit/recovery, and access control
capabilities, as well as supporting optional utilities for database
analysis, monitoring, integrity certification, online reorganization, and
online archiving.

In addition to providing all the specialized programs of DMSII SE, DMSII XE
has three goals:

Linear scalability. As each processor is added to a system, it should
ideally yield the same amount of performance increment as the processor that
preceded it.
Multiterabyte capacity. DMSII XE provides multiterabyte data capacity at the
data set level. Each data set can hold more than 12 terabytes of data. DMSII
XE implements structures with large amounts of data rather than relying on
application logic to piece together several structures with less capacity.
Database availability. DMSII XE seeks to reduce the amount of time during
which a database or structure is unavailable because of required maintenance
or recovery tasks. DMSII XE addresses this goal by providing an online
garbage collection facility for disjoint index sequential sets and subsets.

General Features
Features Common to DMSII SE and DMSII XE
DMSII supports a variety of information accessing techniques. You can store
groups of data records (called data sets) several primary ways, including

A "standard" file structure
Records are generally not ordered in this structure type. To provide
efficient keyed access to a particular data record, indexes are often used.
A single standard data set can have multiple indexes, allowing keyed access
for many different selection conditions.

A direct file structure
Records are stored and accessed by a unique user-supplied "record number,"
eliminating the need for a separate index to find a particular data record.
Direct data sets allow other indexes to be specified, which permits
efficient searches by other keys.

A hashed file structure
A designated key of this record type is hashed and the records are stored in
the hashed order. The hashed file structure provides fast access without an
additional index structure. No indexes are allowed on this structure type.
The hashed key can be alphanumeric as well as numeric.

An ordered file structure
A designated key of this record type is used to order the records as they
are stored.

Records in the data sets must be efficiently located when they need to be
accessed. Records in most data sets can be accessed by using several types
of indexes. Most common are the index sequential and index random access
methods. Index sequential access uses binary searches on multiple levels of
index tables to allow flexible, efficient access to data records. Index
random access uses a hashing algorithm on a key to efficiently locate a
particular data set record.

Information relationships can also be established. Link relationships can be
specified between members of data sets on a one-to-one basis. DMSII also
supports chain relationships, which provide a one-to-many type of
relationship. A number of different link types are available, including
unprotected, verified, self-correcting, symbolic, and counted links.
Selection of the proper type is based on trade-offs among integrity
requirements, volatility, and resource consumption.

DMSII incorporates a variety of validation, audit/recovery, and access
control capabilities. Comprehensive audit/recovery facilities provide
restart information for user programs, automatically recover the database
when faults occur, reconstruct portions of the database, and remove aborted
transactions. Full recovery of all completed transactions is available
within DMSII when it enforces independent transactions. You can synchronize
a COMS audit trail with the DMSII audit trail to provide full synchronized
recovery.

Access control is offered through the Master Control Program (MCP). This
interface permits system access only to authorized requestors. In addition,
you can establish access control to identify the databases a requestor may
access, the data sets that requestor may access within a database, the
records the requestor may access within a data set, and the information
items the requestor is permitted to view within a record. Access control
also can limit a requestor to inquiry-only access as well as limit the DMSII
commands that can be used.

Several DMSII utilities are available that support database analysis,
monitoring, integrity certification, online reorganization, and online
archiving. These products enhance the overall usability of DMSII and help
clients approximate a 24-hour online, 7-day-a-week operation. All DMSII
generated messages use the MultiLingual System (MLS), which allows each site
to translate messages into the local language. The released language is
English.

DMSII XE Extensions
Variable Audit Buffers

Varying the number of audit buffers when audit files are sectioned enables
the database to absorb short periods of intense audit activity. The number
of audit buffers automatically changes as the number of audit file sections
increases or decreases, with approximately 10 audit buffers allocated for
each section. Although the system adjusts the number of audit buffers
automatically, you can also manually assign the number.

Sectioned Audit Files

Sectioned audit files involve the use of multiple physical files that
together form a single logical audit file. You can define up to 63
individual audit sections; however, the combination of all the sections make
up the logical audit file. All sections of an audit file must be present for
DMSII XE to recognize that the audit file is present. Some advantages
offered by sectioned audit files are

Improved audit trail throughput
Concurrent multiple audit file I/Os
Increased bandwidth of the audit trail
Sectioned Data Sets

As with sectioned audit files, a sectioned data set is one in which the
logical data set structure is physically composed of multiple physical
files. You can define up to 255 sections for a data set; however, it is the
combination of all the sections that make up the logical data set.

For HMP 4.0, sectioned data sets are provided for disjoint standard data
sets only. Records in the data set are distributed among the sections using
a round-robin algorithm.

Use of sectioned data sets has the following advantages:

Reduction or elimination of throughput restrictions imposed by the
architecture of internal locks within DMSII.
Increased bandwidth of DMSII physical I/O.
Expanded data set capacity-multiple physical files expand the data set
capacity from 48 gigabytes per structure to 48 gigabytes per section. With a
possible 255 sections per data set, each data set has a potential capacity
of 12 terabytes.
Visibility of existing application program logic-logically, sectioned data
sets appear identical to nonsectioned data sets. Consequently, when
migrating to sectioned data sets, no application program changes are
required. With the exception of the possible appearance of an RSN item
(which is visible to the application only if declared in the DASDL),
application programs are unaware that a data set is sectioned.
Sectioned Sets

A sectioned set is one that has been divided, based on criteria specified in
DASDL, into several discrete components that enable access or manipulation
of each section independently of the other sections.

When multiple application programs access a data set by way of a set, some
contention for set resources occurs. As the number of programs rises, so
does the amount of contention. Set sectioning reduces, or in some cases
eliminates, set resource contention.

Sectioning of sets is critical for achieving scalability on

Systems with many processors
Databases with large data sets
For HMP 4.0, sectioned sets are provided for disjoint indexed sequential
sets only.

TranStamp Locking

TranStamp locking is a new type of locking algorithm for data sets that are
defined in DASDL with a special keyword. The new TranStamp locking algorithm
makes the data record an integral part of the locking process. With the aid
of a unique transaction identifier, TranStamp locking provides a substantial
increase in record-lock related performance.

To allow for ease of migration, the use of TranStamp locking is optional for
nonsectioned data sets. However, the full benefit of TranStamp locking is
not realized unless used by all structures in the database.

Some of the advantages offered by TranStamp locking are

The data record is made part of the locking scheme, which eliminates much of
the overhead required to manage internal lock tables.
The size of the lock table is reduced from one entry per locked record to
one entry per transaction.
The limit on the number of records that can be locked by a single program is
eliminated because the lock table requirements for a given program are
fixed.
The overhead associated with END-TRANSACTION operations is reduced because
all records can be freed by invalidating the TranStamp identifier associated
with the lock (as opposed to freeing each locked record).
Record Serial Numbers (RSNs)

An RSN is a unique number assigned to each data set record. An RSN is
guaranteed to be unique within a data set, but not within the database. That
is, once an RSN is used within a data set, that RSN is never used again
within that data set, but it can be used for a record in another data set.

RSNs enable an internal optimization within sets that allow duplicates but
that do not declare DUPLICATES FIRST or DUPLICATES LAST.

Online Set Garbage Collection

Online Set Garbage Collection returns unused structure space to the system
while your database is up and running.

The following are advantages offered by Online Set Garbage Collection:

Consolidates unused space in sets or subsets
Rebalances index structures to optimize access through sets
When you use garbage collection, the database remains online and available,
and any failure of the garbage collection task has no impact on the
database.


All if this DMSII/DASDL stuff was stolen from:
http://ecc400.com/unisysold/clearpath/sw_datamgmt_cl_dmsiixe.htm

My work uses it as the database for a 100+ G mainframe application, with a
transaction load of just over 2 million transactions/day.

LINC is an NZ invented 3.5 GL.  It has been around sufficiently long that we
are now on version 17.  It has advantages of speed of coding, and is usually
used to create an on-line system (with supporting batch).  Easy to use, fast
to code.  When you "compile" LINC, it creates COBOL (VERY ugly COBOL - 80
characters wide, continuation characters used, and full-stops without going
to a new line - just basically a solid 72 characters of code, right down the
page), and then uses that to compile an object version.

Here is a press release from one of the inventors (for what it is worth, he
is touting a new product in it).

http://www.jade.co.nz/news/prcov1999_hero.htm
```

#### ↳ Re: What exactly is DMSII and LINC?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-07T08:48:34+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B1F31D2.E1A19B2D@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<ALvT6.2862$jD6.266530@news>`

```
> I would apprecaite a detailed answer to both items.

LINC is/was a 4GL system developed in New Zealand in the 1970s.  It was
purchased by Burroughs which later became part of Unisys.  The 'LINC
Centre' in Christchurch (I think it was) closed down a few years ago so
the product ran for about 20 years.

It was originally supposed to be a system where the users could define
the system in terms they understood in the LINC language and then LINC
would generate all the file definitions and program source in Cobol.  In
fact just like all the other 4GLs.  It seems that it was quite
successful in a number of situations and many of its users swore by it
(or perhaps they said 'at' it  ;-).

Like all 4GLs they failed because users, even very good ones, only know,
or can only express, 60% to 80%, of what the system they use actually
does.  This means that analysts are needed to dig out (or invent) the
rest.  Also 4GLs usually only generate 80% of what the system is needed
to do, so programmers are needed for the rest.

Now in any project 80% of the work takes 80% of the resources, the last
20% of the projects takes the remaining 80% of the time and and
money.     ;-)

The net result is that 4GLs (an LINC) only produced savings where the
problem matched the solution perfectly.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What exactly is DMSII and LINC?

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2001-06-06T22:11:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010606181111.11294.00003584@ng-mp1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3B1F31D2.E1A19B2D@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
>From: Richard Plinston riplin@Azonic.co.nz 
>Date: 2001-06-07 03:48 Eastern Daylight Time

DMSII is the Burroughs/Unisys database.  
It is a proto-relational database.  
All the tables (called "datasets") are usually separate entities
within the database.  Every dataset must have a primary key
and may have alternate keys.  Keys can be constructed from
disparate fields.  Duplicate keys are permissible as primary
keys.  
All "joins" or "foreign keys" have to be written in the program
code.  
DMSII comes with an ad hoc inquiry program.  Simple
inquiries are simple.  Complex inquiries can get pretty 
damn complicated--suitable for use only by programmers.
There are 3rd party tools that can provide ad hoc inquiries, 
including filters and joins, with at least no more difficulty
than SQL.  
For those seeking the thrill of hierarchical structure, you
can use the "embedded dataset."  

Ross
http://www.geocities.com/ross_klatte/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What exactly is DMSII and LINC?

- **From:** Sean Case <gsc@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2001-06-07T21:52:08+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gsc-05ED53.21520807062001@nostril.pacific.net.au>`
- **References:** `<ALvT6.2862$jD6.266530@news> <3B1F31D2.E1A19B2D@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
In article <3B1F31D2.E1A19B2D@Azonic.co.nz>,
 Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

> LINC is/was a 4GL system developed in New Zealand in the 1970s.  It was
> purchased by Burroughs which later became part of Unisys.  The 'LINC
> Centre' in Christchurch (I think it was) closed down a few years ago so
> the product ran for about 20 years.

The LINC Development Centre closed down, but LINC development continues.
The product is now based in Sydney, at the Australian Centre for Unisys
Software.

It's also now called Unisys e-@ction Enterprise Application Environment,
but that's another story...

Sean Case
```

#### ↳ Re: What exactly is DMSII and LINC?

- **From:** Sean Case <gsc@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2001-06-07T21:47:28+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gsc-4E0F71.21472807062001@nostril.pacific.net.au>`
- **References:** `<ALvT6.2862$jD6.266530@news>`

```
In article <ALvT6.2862$jD6.266530@news>,
 "COGNICASE - Patrick Saunderson" <patrick.saunderson@cognicase.com> 
 wrote:

> I would apprecaite a detailed answer to both items.

DMSII is the database management system for the Unisys ClearPath NX
series mainframes (the ex-Burroughs ones).

LINC is a 4GL sold by Unisys for various platforms, including both
their mainframe lines (ex-Burroughs and ex-Sperry), several flavours
of Unix, and Windows NT.

If someone has offered you a job working with both of these, then
they are presumably running LINC on an NX machine.

You can probably get much more information on both these products
in comp.sys.unisys.

Sean Case
```

## Thread: odbc — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### odbc

- **From:** saugatad@hotmail.com (saugata)
- **Date:** 2001-06-07T02:17:09-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b9ae33cd.0106070117.15de426d@posting.google.com>`

```
Anybody please give me some idea that ;-
1. If I'm using cobol in unix then how can I incorporate database
connectivity ?To be very specific, how oracle table/tables can be get
connected thru a cobol program?
2.In windows/dos envronment , how can I connect cobol with oracle ?i.e
how can I read data from a table using a cobol program.
```

#### ↳ Re: odbc

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavab@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-07T16:17:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B1FA99F.B0EE53DB@home.com>`
- **References:** `<b9ae33cd.0106070117.15de426d@posting.google.com>`

```


saugata wrote:

> Anybody please give me some idea that ;-
> 1. If I'm using cobol in unix then how can I incorporate database
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> how can I read data from a table using a cobol program.

Go to google.com and as a search enter "SQL" or better "ESQL". However,
your COBOL compiler must have an SQL pre-processor to allow you to insert
E(mbedded)SQL statements into your COBOL programs. Your appropriate COBOL
manuals/on-line help for either Unix or Windows will also need to contain
some reference to the Oracle DB, showing how to 'connect' the COBOL and
Oracle software.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

a starting point.

I know that were I looking for information on "open/read/close," I'd never
even think of looking under OPEN, READ or CLOSE. It's just too damn obvious
to be correct.

"EXTERNAL" attribute if you want to do some of these operations in each of
the programs - but against the same file.

#### ↳ Re: mastercard conversion to ipm

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-08T17:03:26-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9fre94$9k4$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3b1f6ac9$0$56548$45beb828@newscene.com>`

```
IPM is MasterCard's Version of ISO-8583 Variable-Length Bit-Mapped messages,
which (as you've indicated) replaces the current INET (settlement)
fixed-format messages. EuroPay's been using them for years. Bit-Mapped INAS
(MasterCard Authorisations) messages have been around since 1985. Does your
question ultimately have to do with Parsing/Formatting these messages ? Do
you need help writing one of these beasts ?

E-Mail me off-line if you have specific questions.

Cheers,

Bill

"James Walker" <jwalker@novia.net> wrote in message
news:3b1f6ac9$0$56548$45beb828@newscene.com...

mastercard
> processing from inet to IPM format,
> please contact me.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

driver
> program..... and now it's to be standalone and now has to open/read/close
> input files ,m which it hasn't done in the past.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: mastercard conversion to ipm

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-09T06:34:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B21C322.798B3871@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3b1f6ac9$0$56548$45beb828@newscene.com> <9fre94$9k4$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
WOB wrote:
> 
> IPM is MasterCard's Version of ISO-8583 Variable-Length Bit-Mapped messages,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Before I say anything else, you should be aware that I am a COBOL
programmer for MasterCard International, and I have worked with
ISO-8583 Bit-Mapped messages since about 1990-1991.  

MasterCard began processing ISO-8583 messages natively in 1988, if I
recall correctly, but it wasn't until 1992 that all the older
fixed-length INAS (InterBank National Authorization System) message
formats were made obsolete.  INET (International Network for
Electronic Transfers) still uses fixed-length message formats to this
day, although IPM is coming later this year.  

I am not fully conversent on the differences between ISO-8583 and IPM,
but I believe IPM makes greater use of subelements (subtags) than
ISO-8583.  As I recall, subelements began to creep into ISO-8583
message data elements in the mid-1990's.  MasterCard publishes a
hardcopy manual titled "Customer Interface Specifications" that
describes the ISO-8583 message format.  It's a very big book.  As far
as I know, this book should be available to banks and other financial
institutions that are customers of MasterCard.  They have a department
of Manuals & Publications that may sell or otherwise distribute this
book.  I have a copy because I need to reference it in my work.

WOB (Bill) is quite correct.  Writing an ISO-8583 (or IPM) message
parser or formatter utility is a fairly difficult piece of work.  I've
seen several of these beasts, for batch & CICS, generally as separate
utilities for parsing (breaking out the fields) and formatting
(compressing fields into one message).  Some were written in IBM S/370
Assembler, and some were written in COBOL.  MasterCard may have had
parser/formatter utilities in S/370 assembler for batch that were
distributed to member banks, but I'm not sure if they are still
available.  
I'm aware of parsers & formatters written in C for Unix
implementations, but I've never seen the code for those.  I've never
written one of these beasts, although I have cloned or enhanced a
couple, and I've certainly used them extensively.

Basically the Data Elements are numbered and have some defined
formats.  Some data elements are fixed length, such as DE-014 (Card
Expiration Date), and some are variable-length with a two-digit or
three-digit length prefix.  The initial bitmap tells you how many
bitmaps are present, and the bitmaps tell you which data elements are
present in the compressed message.  Subelements are contained within
defined data elements, and normally consist of a two-digit subelement
identifier (subtag), a two-digit length, and a data value.  The
disadvantage to subelements is that they are another level down.  You
have to parse out the data element before you can parse its
subelements.

There may be commercially available message parsers and formatters for
both ISO-8583 and IPM message formats, although I have no information
on vendors.  But, hey, if you can get a free one from WOB, more power
to you!

It makes no sense re-inventing the wheel, although performance
efficiency is definitely a consideration in parsing/formatting
messages, since the volume of records can be quite large.

STANDARD DISCLAIMER:  I am NOT an official corporate spokesperson for
my benevolent employer, and anything I said was my personal opinion. 
If you want an official corporate spokesperson, I think I know two or
three.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: mastercard conversion to ipm

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** wobconsult@sprynet.com (WOB Consulting)
- **Date:** 2001-06-10T08:28:25-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<173f80ae.0106100728.6ac2f675@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3b1f6ac9$0$56548$45beb828@newscene.com> <9fre94$9k4$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3B21C322.798B3871@worldnet.att.net> <3b222f94.1402542@news1.attglobal.net> <B4FF4F87EDEB8DEA.CAEE4C4E533B082D.F9BA077E381255C4@lp.airnews.net> <3B233A97.6BC8EB98@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message news:<3B233A97.6BC8EB98@worldnet.att.net>...
> SkippyPB wrote:
> > 
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> specific parser available yet at a good price.

Gents,

I've been writing parser/formatters for ISO-8583 Authorisation
networks since 1985. Visa, MasterCard, EuroPay, JCB, AMEX and most
recently, the Australian Standard AS2805. They've been written in
Assembler, COBOL and PL/I. Of course, Assembler is the fastest method,
but you can write some very efficient re-entrant code in COBOL and
PL/I. The benefit of not writing in Assembler is maintenence. They
ain't making new Assembler people anymore. Reference modification and
the SUBSTR verb in PL/I make it simple and finding people to maintain
it is that much easier. The only catch is dealing with different data
types, of which, AS2805 is the KING. Visa uses EBCDIC, BCD and HEX.
MasterCard/EuroPay are all EBCDIC. JCB is a potpouri of Visa and
MasterCard. AMEX is EBCDIC but uses the 1993 standard of ISO-8583 (as
opposed to the 1987 standard) and AS2805 uses ASCII Display, EBCDIC,
BCD and HEX. The customer wanted this Parser/Formatter written in
Assembler, but I urged them not to. They got their way.

Page ===> http://www.aboutlegacycoding.com/ I have placed four
sub-programs that can be used with the ISO-8583 standard. They are:
BIT2BYTE, BYTE2BIT,CHAR2HEX and HEX2CHAR. I've used these sub-programs
in some of the COBOL Parser/Formatter sub-systems.

I suspect that James could use them in his IPM endevor.

FWIW, all of this work was done on a Mainframe, either in an MVS or
VSE environment.

Cheers,

Bill O'Boyle
WOB Consulting, Inc.
Atlanta
```

customers.  It should come with manuals, so perhaps James Walker's

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: mastercard conversion to ipm

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-09T21:40:53-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e2q5it4mton9kde0jr5dbgl7tag2mt78od@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3b1f6ac9$0$56548$45beb828@newscene.com> <9fre94$9k4$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3B21C322.798B3871@worldnet.att.net> <3b222f94.1402542@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
On Sat, 09 Jun 2001 14:17:08 GMT, thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
wrote:

>Arnold,
>
>How widely used (if you have any idea) outside of Master Card, is the
>ISO-8583 format?  Is there any real market (new adopters) for a
>parser? 

VisaNet also uses ISO-8583 for their messages.  The main difference between Visa
and MasterCard is that MasterCard uses all EBCDIC (or ASCII??) for their
messages (in other words, a numeric value might be x'F0F0F0F0F5F9F9F5' for
00005995 (implied decimal point)) while Visa uses what they call a 'numeric
packed' field, ie x'00005995'.  This makes Visa's a bit harder, at least for
mainframes, since there's no natural COBOL pic clause for that data type.

At my shop we communicate with both Visa and MasterCard (Cirrus).  We have a
vendor (CV Systems Corp - www.cvsystems.com) for our card transaction processing
system, and they wrote the 'formatter' (in COBOL) for both types of messages.
While it is somewhat confusing to learn at first, once you have a basic
understanding it's not really as difficult as it seems.  I'd post more detail
but since we didn't write the code I'm not sure it would be 'kosher' to describe
how they parse the messages.  But basically you'd have little subroutines for
each possible 'data type'.  One for fixed length alphanumeric, one for variable
length alphabetic, one for fixed length numeric, etc.  You then have a table for
each field that says which data type that field is, and you then perform the
proper subroutine.  (The hardest one, of course, is when the data type itself
has a bitmap followed by subfields.  Yikes!)

Frank Swarbrick
home: infocat@sprynet.com / work: frank.swarbrick@efirstbank.com
"I'm very seldom naughty." --Willow Rosenberg "Buffy the Vampire Slayer"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: mastercard conversion to ipm

```

<infocat@sprynet.com> writes:

>00005995 (implied decimal point)) while Visa uses what they call a 'numeric
>packed' field, ie x'00005995'.  This makes Visa's a bit harder, at least for
>mainframes, since there's no natural COBOL pic clause for that data type.
>

I'm not sure where you get that it is harder for a mainframe to deal with 
numeric packed fields.  The Burroughs/Unisys mainframe has always 
had COMP fields which are 'unsigned packed numeric' fields.  The only 
time a sign is forced, is if the programmer indicates the need for the sign
digit in the PIC.   IBM uses COMP as BINARY (5995 would be represented 
entirely differently) and COMP-3 as signed packed numeric (where 5995C 
would be the default way of showing the number).  So even in the IBM
environment it is possible to derive an unsigned packed numeric field
(via a little arithmetic slight of hand or over statement of the decimal
offset).  (Knowing when to use such a routine/procedure is the important piece
of 
information, as you indicated in your message.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: mastercard conversion to ipm

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: mastercard conversion to ipm

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-10T09:12:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B23399F.D5F44F03@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3b1f6ac9$0$56548$45beb828@newscene.com> <9fre94$9k4$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3B21C322.798B3871@worldnet.att.net> <3b222f94.1402542@news1.attglobal.net> <e2q5it4mton9kde0jr5dbgl7tag2mt78od@4ax.com>`

```
Frank Swarbrick wrote:
> 
> On Sat, 09 Jun 2001 14:17:08 GMT, thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> mainframes, since there's no natural COBOL pic clause for that data type.

I believe MasterCard uses all EBCDIC, even on machines where the
native codeset is ASCII.  Unlike Visanet ISO-8583, a MasterCard
ISO-8583 message only contains character data outside the bitmaps (no
binary, packed-decimal, or BCD fields).  Even credit card magnetic
stripe tracks are converted to EBCDIC, which include field-separators
that translate to relatively uncommon EBCDIC characters.

> 
> At my shop we communicate with both Visa and MasterCard (Cirrus).  We have a
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> has a bitmap followed by subfields.  Yikes!)

There are several parsing strategies.  The parse I use most frequently
has a COBOL copybook of nearly 1000 lines to describe the uncompressed
message.  I know of one IPM parser that simply generates a table with
one entry per Data Element (including all subelements) that contains
the offset into the compressed message and al length.  Then the data
is actually referenced by a table lookup, and the offset and length
are populated into a reference-modified MOVE statement.  

DocDwarf might be able to convert that to IKFCBLOO, but otherwise that
strategy requires an ANSI-85 compliant COBOL compiler.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: mastercard conversion to ipm

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2001-06-11T13:14:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zu3V6.9211$DW1.374773@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<3b1f6ac9$0$56548$45beb828@newscene.com> <3B23399F.D5F44F03@worldnet.att.net> <oHJU6.9123$DW1.368954@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3B245C5F.62810CD6@worldnet.att.net>`

```
In article <3B245C5F.62810CD6@worldnet.att.net>,
Arnold Trembley  <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:
>NA wrote:
>> 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>but it certainly hasn't gone away.

Ahhhhh, Mr Trembley, one can play 'what if' until the cows come home...
and you are, by my experience, quite right; running a chunk o' code
through a program which changes POSITIONINGs to ADVANCINGs and EXHIBITs to
DISPLAYs does not make the majority of... stuff 'go away'.

Consider, for example, the bewildered 'youngster' who cannot understand
why the elegance of an inline PERFORM and reference-modification was
'passed over' in favor of:

MOVE INREC TO WS-INREC.
PERFORM 103725-EXIT
 VARYING SUB1 FROM 1 BY 1
  UNTIL WS-INREC-CHAR(SUB1) = WS-FLD-DELIM-CHAR
   OR SUB1 > WS-MAX-INREC-RECLEN.
IF SUB1 > WS-MAX-INREC-RECLEN
    PERFORM 152914-BAD-INREC-FMT THRU 152914-EX
    GO TO B700-200000-PROCESS-INRECS-EX.
ADD 1 TO SUB1.
IF SUB1 > WS-MAX-INREC-RECLEN
    PERFORM 152914-BAD-INREC-FMT THRU 152914-EX
    GO TO B700-200000-PROCESS-INRECS-EXIT.
IF WS-INREC-CHAR(SUB1) NOT NUMERIC
    PERFORM 173659-INVALID-CUSTNO THRU 173659-EX
    GO TO B700-200000-PROCESS-INRECS-EXIT
ELSE
    PERFORM 187548-GET-CUST-LIM-N-HIST-RECS THRU 187548-EX.

dd
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: mastercard conversion to ipm

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-12T07:31:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B25C4FF.B1586AE2@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3b1f6ac9$0$56548$45beb828@newscene.com> <3B23399F.D5F44F03@worldnet.att.net> <oHJU6.9123$DW1.368954@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3B245C5F.62810CD6@worldnet.att.net> <zu3V6.9211$DW1.374773@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
NA wrote:
> 
> In article <3B245C5F.62810CD6@worldnet.att.net>,
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> DISPLAYs does not make the majority of... stuff 'go away'.

Hey, I wrote a program to do that once.  It even converted NOTE and
EXAMINE (although it didn't handle EXAMINE very well) and completely
ignored TRANSFORM.  It did an excellent job on OS/VS COBOL "MOVE
CURRENT-DATE..." and the output frequently compiled under VS COBOL II
without error (and even executed!).  But it wasn't smart enough to
insert explicit scope terminators, renumber the paragraphs, or to
restructure the programs to use NOT AT END.

> 
> Consider, for example, the bewildered 'youngster' who cannot understand
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> dd

One might almost think you had worked on some of our older COBOL
programs from the 70's and 80's.  There are still a few that look too
much like your example.

I have recently been modifying COBOL programs to make them
AMODE(31),RMODE(ANY), and I still run into the occasional program that
has never been converted from OS/VS COBOL to VS COBOL II (much less
COBOL for OS/390 & VM).  Gotta get ready for z/OS!
```

	For a while there Fujitsu COBOL was free, and now it seems not
to be so - or perhaps is that for the latest version?

started up again - actually just trying to refresh my limited skills
in it (PC platform).

So I'm looking for a reasonably capable compiler (even an interpreter
- I'm not that fussy). Just something pretty complete and low priced
(less than $100 US dollars), doesn't have to be Win32 but that would
be nice.

any resources you know of ??? - thanks

  Keith

nice compiler.

general purpose language - EVERY language does things differently and
some a little or a lot better than others.

or even Big Iron experience (is that the biggest advantage for COBOL -
ie maintenance.

 Keith

#### ↳ Re: COBOL best suited for what ??

- **From:** chammond@lc.cc.il.us
- **Date:** 2001-06-07T18:09:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9fog18$3jd$1@news.netmar.com>`
- **References:** `<3b1f8163.25529522@news.optusnet.com.au>`

```
In article <3b1f8163.25529522@news.optusnet.com.au>, Keith
<kwarekf@optusnet.com.au> writes:
>Not really the "shoot yourself in the foot" list but  ::
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
COBOL is best for file I/O, printing reports, and possibly in accounting and
data manipulation.  It is really best suited for large scale, high speed
computer systems.  It is also used for updating and accessing Database
storage.  I saw a class recently at a university for databse design with
Oracle and COBOL being used together.

The best thing about COBOL is it is less complex, easier to use, and simpler
to learn.  Since it is used by so many businesses all over the world, it is
kept to a standard.  If the programmer avoids the Compiler specific add-ons
it can be quite portable and cross-platform capable. This language tends to
be more common in the Mainframe world.  It was originally developed for large
businesses using mainframe computers.  


 -----  Posted via NewsOne.Net: Free (anonymous) Usenet News via the Web  -----
  http://newsone.net/ -- Free reading and anonymous posting to 60,000+ groups
   NewsOne.Net prohibits users from posting spam.  If this or other posts
made through NewsOne.Net violate posting guidelines, email abuse@newsone.net
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL best suited for what ??

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-09T05:43:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B21B726.6C1E4767@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3b1f8163.25529522@news.optusnet.com.au> <9fog18$3jd$1@news.netmar.com>`

```
chammond@lc.cc.il.us wrote:
> 
> In article <3b1f8163.25529522@news.optusnet.com.au>, Keith
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> businesses using mainframe computers.

COBOL was designed to read like English for ease of understanding, and
to allow continuing maintenance of installed programs.  Imagine
reading a C program aloud to someone over the phone, compared to
reading a COBOL program aloud.

While COBOL is best suited to business accounting problems I have
heard of creative uses.  I remember reading about 25 years ago an
article by a hobbyist who needed a cross-assembler to build object
code for a microprocessor (it may have been a Motorola 6800 or a
Mostek 6502).  He wrote the cross-assembler in COBOL on a mainframe,
and it generated binary code to run on the target microprocessor. 
That would have been an interesting COBOL program!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL best suited for what ??

- **From:** "Charles" <charles@godwin.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-06-11T20:16:22-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PadV6.14864$QE4.1840246@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<3b1f8163.25529522@news.optusnet.com.au> <9fog18$3jd$1@news.netmar.com> <3B21B726.6C1E4767@worldnet.att.net>`

```
The story of Realia COBOL compiler is similar.

The compiler was written in COBOL in the early 1980s. It was initially
compiled on a small IBM mainframe machine and emitted Intel compatible code
that was downloaded to a PC. (8088 in those days). It then was used to boot
strap itself by recompiling itself on the PC.

all further development was on the PC.

We still have the compiler written essentially all in COBOL and do
regression tests on the compiler by compiling the compiler and comparing the
results.

Later the Realia CICS emulator was also implemented in COBOL.

The COBOL run-time however was written in assembler and more recently C.

## Thread: acucobol and mapped drives? — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### acucobol and mapped drives?

- **From:** "Dennis Edward" <temp081100@ipipeline.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-07T17:08:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YxOT6.1899$5R2.93965843@news1.van.metronet.ca>`

```
I'm looking at setting up a system where acucobol programs running on
individual pc's will access vision files that are on a common mapped drive
(samba, but I doubt that makes a difference). Has anyone experience with
doing this in a multiclient environment? Good or bad,  I'd like to hear from
you.

Temporary address: temp010607@pctc.com

TIA
```

#### ↳ Re: acucobol and mapped drives?

- **From:** "Ray Smith" <Ray.Smith@fujitsu.com.au>
- **Date:** 2001-06-08T10:08:22+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9fp159$tgu@newshost.fujitsu.com.au>`
- **References:** `<YxOT6.1899$5R2.93965843@news1.van.metronet.ca>`

```
Hi,

I have never done what you are attempting.

AcuCorp have a product called AcuServer which will do what you want
much better than mapped drives.

AcuServer is a server program that accepts COBOL IO requests from clients,
processes them on the server and returns the results to the program that
requested
the it.

This cuts the network IO to one send / receive per request.

There is obviously a cost involved in purchasing AcuSever and a little more
configuration (not much!) but the results will be greatly improved and scale
much better.

The great thing about all this is you can try it mapped drives and if it
doesn't
work very well you always have the option to use AcuServer.  I don't think
you even need to recompile your program - just setup a config file to say
you
want to use Acuserver (you will also need an AcuServer enabled runtime which
from memory I think comes with the AcuServer "server").

Ray Smith
(Please Note:  I am in no way connected to AcuCorp - just a happy user)


Dennis Edward <temp081100@ipipeline.net> wrote in message
news:YxOT6.1899$5R2.93965843@news1.van.metronet.ca...
> I'm looking at setting up a system where acucobol programs running on
> individual pc's will access vision files that are on a common mapped drive
> (samba, but I doubt that makes a difference). Has anyone experience with
> doing this in a multiclient environment? Good or bad,  I'd like to hear
from

>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: acucobol and mapped drives?

- **From:** "Acucorp News" <shaun_gough@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-07T17:43:12-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1451C740A890D411B65C00104B95B1C0214072@ras.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<YxOT6.1899$5R2.93965843@news1.van.metronet.ca>`

```
Dear Dennis.

Acucorp has many customers that access Vision file from a mapped drive using
a NFS product with no problems.
But, Acucorp does not support products in this environment as NFS use their
own record locking and file caching, which can result in problems with
accessing the data or even file corruption.

To prevent the above, Acucorp recomends that you do not use NFS products,
but instead use AcuServer that will allow full locking and caching abilities
from within the Acucorp products.



"Dennis Edward" <temp081100@ipipeline.net> wrote in message
news:YxOT6.1899$5R2.93965843@news1.van.metronet.ca...

> individual pc's will access vision files that are on a common mapped drive

> doing this in a multiclient environment? Good or bad,  I'd like to hear
from
> you.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: acucobol and mapped drives?

- **From:** "Joe" <joe@noneofyourbiz.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-08T02:04:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bpWT6.56463$gA.2120735@monger.newsread.com>`
- **References:** `<YxOT6.1899$5R2.93965843@news1.van.metronet.ca>`

```
We have a few customers who do this.  There are some settings in the Samba
config that you need to play with to get file locking to work reliably and
to avoid file corruption.  If I recall correctly, we needed to set "oplocks
= false" and "blocking locks = false" in the smb.conf file.

You may also need to set "read raw = yes" and "write raw = yes" but try
without it first.  And make sure you have the latest Samba.

news:YxOT6.1899$5R2.93965843@news1.van.metronet.ca...

> individual pc's will access vision files that are on a common mapped drive

from
> you.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: acucobol and mapped drives?

- **From:** "Dennis Edward" <temp081100@ipipeline.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-08T14:38:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ur5U6.1907$5R2.94804625@news1.van.metronet.ca>`
- **References:** `<YxOT6.1899$5R2.93965843@news1.van.metronet.ca> <3rWT6.8250$Rq4.2338883@news1.news.adelphia.net>`

```
To those who suggested AcuServer:

A problem with that, is that you can buy a basic Acucobol runtime, OR you
can buy an Acu4GL runtime, OR you can buy an AcuSQL runtime, OR you can buy
an AcuServer runtime, but you can't combine them (all runtimes support
vision files as well, of course). We *have* to get AcuSQL, so we *can't* get
AcuServer. We're going to be converting all our our files to DB2 in the long
term, but for now a mapped drive gives us an intermediate solution.


"fdr" <fdr@adelphia.net> wrote in message
news:3rWT6.8250$Rq4.2338883@news1.news.adelphia.net...
>
>
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: acucobol and mapped drives?

- **From:** "Drake Coker" <drake@san.rr.com.nospam>

- **Message-ID:** `<azvW6.1466$35.1308351@typhoon.san.rr.com>`

```
Actually Dennis, you have some misinformation there.  A single ACUCOBOL-GT
runtime can support all the data sources at once.  You can certainly use
AcuSQL and
AcuServer in a single program.  If someone at Acucorp is telling you
otherwise, tell
them to come talk to me ;)

Drake Coker
Chief Scientist
Acucorp, Inc.

<a id="t-4eb5d963a9"></a>
## Thread: COMP fields - VAX COBOL — 2 msgs

### COMP fields - VAX COBOL

- **From:** "The Riddler" <riddler_cubed@nospam.yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-08T07:14:49-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9fqc3b$nhq$1@news.cis.ohio-state.edu>`

```
I'm sure this has been answered before, I just can't track it down. I've
checked some of the COBOL sites looking for doc and tutorials, but nothing
that helps me out with this. A point to the right direction would be greatly
appreciated.

If I had access to the system I'd just write some code to do this and look
myself, but I don't. I only see the raw data after it's ported to another
platform.

I basically understand COMP fields. A value is translated to binary and
stored using 4 or 8 bytes when saved to media. But how *exactly* does it
look? I'm talking VAX, which looks like it's ASCII as opposed to EBCDIC, if
it matters.

If I have a COMP 9(6) field with a value of 160, will the 4 bytes look like
"00 00 00 A0"? Would it be different if the field were signed? What if it
was signed and the value was negative? I vaguely remember some 2's
complement form or something.

Finally, what if there is an implied decimal point? Is the number stored as
if there were no decimal point and then it's up to COBOL to place the
decimal? Meaning if the field were COMP 9(4)V99 with a value of 1.60 would
it be on disk as "00 00 00 A0"?

Thanks so much for any guidance.
```

#### ↳ Re: COMP fields - VAX COBOL

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-08T14:50:24+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ge1itcv3nfjol7p6ic8n6ig97ik7leueg@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<9fqc3b$nhq$1@news.cis.ohio-state.edu>`

```
On Fri, 8 Jun 2001 07:14:49 -0400 "The Riddler"
<riddler_cubed@nospam.yahoo.com> wrote:

:>I'm sure this has been answered before, I just can't track it down. I've
:>checked some of the COBOL sites looking for doc and tutorials, but nothing
:>that helps me out with this. A point to the right direction would be greatly
:>appreciated.

:>If I had access to the system I'd just write some code to do this and look
:>myself, but I don't. I only see the raw data after it's ported to another
:>platform.

:>I basically understand COMP fields. A value is translated to binary and
:>stored using 4 or 8 bytes when saved to media. 

Could be more or less, depending on the COMP type and picture.

:>                                               But how *exactly* does it
:>look? I'm talking VAX, which looks like it's ASCII as opposed to EBCDIC, if
:>it matters.

ASCII/EBCDIC should not matter for COMP fields.

Big Endian/Little Endian may make a difference.

:>If I have a COMP 9(6) field with a value of 160, will the 4 bytes look like
:>"00 00 00 A0"? 

Most likely

:>               Would it be different if the field were signed? 

No.

:>                                                               What if it
:>was signed and the value was negative? I vaguely remember some 2's
:>complement form or something.

FF FF FF 60  (though I have heard of hardware that has a negative zero = ff ff
ff ff) so -160 would be ff ff ff 5f)

:>Finally, what if there is an implied decimal point? 

Changes nothing.

:>                                                    Is the number stored as
:>if there were no decimal point and then it's up to COBOL to place the
:>decimal? 

The COBOL program must know where the decimal is.

You can use floating COMP forms where the decimal point is recorded. Of course
these forms do not keep exact fractional values.

:>         Meaning if the field were COMP 9(4)V99 with a value of 1.60 would
:>it be on disk as "00 00 00 A0"?

Yes.

:>Thanks so much for any guidance.

No problem.
```

<a id="t-789d77a0e5"></a>
## Thread: Why I can't make a COBOL dynamic call with LE V2R9 — 4 msgs

### Why I can't make a COBOL dynamic call with LE V2R9

- **From:** ddirion@club-internet.fr (Didier Dirion)
- **Date:** 2001-06-08T05:19:58-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8f48f863.0106080419.180b5744@posting.google.com>`

```
I have the following architecture

COB-1    ---..>dynamic-call--->>>    (COB-2stub / C-Module and C++
module)

This architecture run good before we move to OS/390 LE V2R9.

With this new version of LE we have the following error :

IGZ0174S IGZ0174S A dynamic call to COB-2stub failed because the load
module is a DLL.

Or my program is not a DLL because I use the link option LET .
The LET option specifies that a generated program object should be
marked as executable even if the return code is not zero: for example,
if symbols are unresolved. For example,LET(4) marks the generated
program object as executable even if there are errors of severity 4 or
less. LET is the equivalent of LET(8).


So I don't understand why I have the error IGZ0174S. 

Can you help me ?
```

#### ↳ Re: Why I can't make a COBOL dynamic call with LE V2R9

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2001-06-08T15:31:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010608113142.11331.00000365@ng-ce1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<8f48f863.0106080419.180b5744@posting.google.com>`

```
Didier Dirion writes ...

>I have the following architecture
>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>Can you help me ?

A program is considered to be a DLL by binding (link editing) with the
DYNAM(DLL) option. Did your systems staff change the default options for the
program binder when they installed V2R9? Did you re-link your stub? How does
"COB-2stub" call the C-Module and C++ module? 

Check these issues out and let us know what you find.

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Why I can't make a COBOL dynamic call with LE V2R9

- **From:** ddirion@club-internet.fr (Didier Dirion)
- **Date:** 2001-06-14T10:27:56-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8f48f863.0106140927.3de98ae1@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<8f48f863.0106080419.180b5744@posting.google.com> <20010608113142.11331.00000365@ng-ce1.aol.com>`

```
COB-1 can be compiling with COBOL II V1R4, or COBOL for MVS & VM V1R2,
or COBOL for OS/390 and VM V2.1.

C-module and C++ module are compiling with the option DLL (C only) and
EXPORTALL. I use LE V1R7 for compile (OS390 V2R6).

COB-2stub is compiling with COBOL for MVS & VM V1R2 (OS390 V2R6)  and
options NODYNAM,LIB,OBJECT,RENT,APOST,DATA(31),OFFSET.

The LE V2R9 docvumentation give me the solution :
Compile COB-2stub with PGMN(LM),DLL,EXPORTALL options.
Compile COB1 with DLL,PGMNAME(LM), prelink step include the sidedesk
result of COB-2stub prelink step (SYSDEFSD card).
The result works fine.


What I need to do when I don't have COBOL for OS/390 and VM V2.1.
compiler ?

Why my architecture works fine with previous version of OS/390 LE V2R9
and not with OS/390 LE V2R9 ?

Thanks a lot for your help and answer .

Best Regards 

Didier
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why I can't make a COBOL dynamic call with LE V2R9

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2001-06-14T19:10:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010614151009.03175.00000575@ng-cj1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<8f48f863.0106140927.3de98ae1@posting.google.com>`

```
Didier Dirion writes ...


>COB-1 can be compiling with COBOL II V1R4, or COBOL for MVS & VM V1R2,
>or COBOL for OS/390 and VM V2.1.
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I do not believe it is a problem with LE levels or versions, but with compiler
levels or versions and how you bind your programs.

OS/390 recognizes these ways for one program to call another:
  * static call (programs bound into a single load module or program object)
  * dynamic serial (programs separately bound; use system LOAD services to
connect)
  * dynamic parallel (programs separetly bound; use system services to create
       sub-tasks or threads that operate independently; not supported in COBOL)
  * DLL (all programs bound separately; use system DLL services to connect)

If you attempt to call a program that is bound as a DLL, you must use the first
or last approach to connect the programs. What has historically been called a
dynamic call won't work; you need to call using DLL linkages.

If you compile with the DLL option, it says "make all dynamic calls in this
program use DLL linkages"; it does not say "make this program a DLL".

COBOL for MVS & VM (and earlier) do not support DLL linkages. COBOL for OS/390
& VM (both currently available releases) do support DLL linkages. The only
reasons I could see that your program worked before are: a) you did not have
your C program compiled or bound as a DLL before; or, b) you used static calls
earlier.

We discuss this in great detail in our course Inter-Language Communications in
OS/390. Here is a link to the course description; from there, you can link to
the detailed outline.

===> http://www.trainersfriend.com/M220O.htm

Hope this helps.

Kind regards,



 
Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

## Thread: Return to: index vs subscript performance — 14 msgs

### Return to: index vs subscript performance

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-06-10T22:15:00-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0106102115.26784fe4@posting.google.com>`

```
From: Jerry P (jerryp@bisusa.com)
Subject: Re: index vs subscript performance 
Newsgroups: comp.lang.cobol
Date: 2001-03-26 19:55:22 PST 
 
Jerry P wrote:



I just performed an experiment:

PERFORM 100000 TIMES
   SET INDX TO 0
    PERFORM VARYING J FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL J > 10000
      SET INDX UP BY 1                            [Line 'x']
      MOVE 'A' TO A-ARRAY(INDX)        [Line 'y']
      MOVE 'B' TO B-ARRAY(J)                 [Line 'z']
      END-PERFORM
   END-PERFORM


With Lines 'x'  'y'  and 'z' commented (no MOVEs executed), the
overhead averaged 5.98 seconds.

With Line 'x' and 'y' commented, such that we move by SUBSCRIPT,
average execution time 11.22 seconds

With Line 'z' alone commented, such that we move by INDEX, average
execution time 11.55 seconds.


Conclusions:
1. Subscripting is faster by 0.33 seconds for 100 million single bytes
moved, 3.3 seconds per gigabyte.
2. It is doubtful whether anyone cares.


The tests were run using the Fujitsu compiler (debug mode) on an
900MHz PC.

Your results may vary, but as for me, I say to hell with indexes; they
should be banned from COBOL.  Where do I write?

_________*

Jerry, my man, you surprise me.  This was an unfair test. To test the
INDEX, you should code

    PERFORM VARYING INDX FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL INDX > 10000
        MOVE 'A' TO A-ARRAY(INDX)        [Line 'y']
           END-PERFORM 

Your SETting INDX UP BY 1 
DOUBLES the overhead, because in your version, J is being incremented
as well!

Try it again, and let us know how it benchmarks on your machine.


Stephen J Spiro
Member, J4 COBOL Standards Committee

PS: YOU could be a member of the standards committee, and have a lot
of fun! YEAH!
```

You're right, of course. I salute those who have labored in the
efficient-compiler morass; my life is ever so much easier because of
their efforts.

Because of competition, diligence, or luck, the compiler-writers have
presented us with two techniques with virtually identical efficiency.
If I go left or right, I'll get to the destination in the same length
of time. To the left, I have to quack like a duck while walking
backwards, and carrying a greased weasel. To the right, I get a free
hotdog.
```

:>   02  A-ARRAY OCCURS 10000 PIC X.
:>01  B.
:>  02  B-ARRAY OCCURS 10000 PIC X.

With such a picture the index is identical to the subscript.

Try pic x(11).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Return to: index vs subscript performance

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-06-21T23:47:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B328773.808897A7@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<4145699b.0106102115.26784fe4@posting.google.com> <rd8ait0g04itrb0cttoc4snf1io84sd3r5@4ax.com> <ikwV6.273780$sP6.15236785@news3.aus1.giganews.com> <405eits7ieb0f03eogehjk2gmpa7u44d3d@4ax.com>`

```
Not only that but his test code should be written 
PERFORM 
   VARYING J FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL J > 10000
   VARYING INDX FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL INDX > 10000

plus the SET INDX TO 0 and SET INDX UP BY 1 should be removed.  

The revised test with the arrays sized as Binyamin has suggested and an
assumed INDEXED BY clause on A-ARRAY would allow the test to be run in a
comparable fashion with only the subscript or only the index being
incremented.  Both changes should swing the comparison in favor of
indexing.  However with the pipelining and modern day high-speed integer
binary arithmetic (assuming J is defined in an optimal computational or
binary mode for the computer and we are dealing with machine and not
intermediate code), the difference still might be trivial.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services, Inc.
      cfmtech@istar.ca or clark.morris@ns.sympatico.ca        

Binyamin Dissen wrote:
> 
> On Tue, 12 Jun 2001 22:02:54 GMT "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com> wrote:
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
> Director, Dissen Software, Bar & Grill - Israel
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Return to: index vs subscript performance

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-21T18:55:02-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9gu1g4$g0t$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<4145699b.0106102115.26784fe4@posting.google.com> <rd8ait0g04itrb0cttoc4snf1io84sd3r5@4ax.com> <ikwV6.273780$sP6.15236785@news3.aus1.giganews.com> <405eits7ieb0f03eogehjk2gmpa7u44d3d@4ax.com> <3B328773.808897A7@istar.ca>`

```
The "recommended" change in source code isn't quite right - as you can't
have two "varying" phrases in the same in-line PERFORM.  In fact, in the '85
Standard (but NOT the draft of the next one), you can't even have an "AFTER"
phrase for an inline PERFORM - only out-of-line ones.

Having said that, I don't think it REALLY impacts the "thrust" of this
argument, but did want to clarify this - in case anyone actually tries to
compile and test the code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Return to: index vs subscript performance

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-06-22T09:21:43+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9guv55$atqv5$1@ID-39799.news.dfncis.de>`
- **References:** `<4145699b.0106102115.26784fe4@posting.google.com> <rd8ait0g04itrb0cttoc4snf1io84sd3r5@4ax.com> <ikwV6.273780$sP6.15236785@news3.aus1.giganews.com> <405eits7ieb0f03eogehjk2gmpa7u44d3d@4ax.com> <3B328773.808897A7@istar.ca> <9gu1g4$g0t$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I think the whole discussion depends (used to depend) on the compiler AND
the machine architecture.

The old WANG VS 74 (and I believe 85) compiler used to be smart on
incrementing indexes with literals.  If indexing an array of 500 byte
elements, the WANG would compile SET INDX UP BY 2 as ADD 1000 TO INDX.   So
when using literal values, any multiplication was done at compile time not
run time.   Of course if you set an index to a variable value it had to
generate an inline multiply.   In those days (the 80s) the run time saving
using indexes was generally good enough for us to use indexes as a standard
to cut out the multiplication of subscripts on each use.

The AS400 OPM COBOL compiler (back in 1990) wasn't that smart.  It would
generate the any SET INDX to use a multiply.   In fact, if indexing through
an array of 1 byte elements it would still generate a multplication by 1.

However this didn't really matter.  Because of the way the AS400 describes
its data, subscripts are used raw.  It doesn't have to multiply them by the
element size to get to the correct element.  So on the AS400 subscripting is
(was?) always faster than indexing.

So on the WANG indexing was better and on the AS400 subscripting was better.
Nowadays, for commercial procesing, I don't think it really matters at all.

Rick

William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:9gu1g4$g0t$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net...
> The "recommended" change in source code isn't quite right - as you can't
> have two "varying" phrases in the same in-line PERFORM.  In fact, in the
'85
> Standard (but NOT the draft of the next one), you can't even have an
"AFTER"
> phrase for an inline PERFORM - only out-of-line ones.
>
…[56 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > :>> :>With Line 'z' alone commented, such that we move by INDEX,
average
> > > :>> :>execution time 11.55 seconds.
> > >
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…

#### ↳ Re: freeware window editors

- **From:** "Joe" <joe@noneofyourbiz.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-12T00:46:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

If you just need syntax highlighting, you can use VIM (Vi IMproved) at
www.vim.org.

It's a Unix "vi" clone and there is a Windows version (GUI and console
version - I use the console version).  It comes with a number of files that
configure it for syntax highlighting of different languages (C, Cobol,
Pascal, Perl, etc.).

Of course, you have to like vi if you want to use it.  Some people don't.
(I do.)

Minora wrote:
> 
> Are there any good freeware Cobol editors available for windows.

IIRC there was an editor that came with the free Fujitsu version 3
compiler.  It was adequate but not good.

If you will do a lot of programming then invest in the best chair and
the best editor that you can find because you will spend a lot of time
in both.  You could work in notepad and sitting on an orange box, but it
is not a good idea.

Of course, what is 'best' depends on what you are used to and what you
have trained yourself to use.

I have used WordStar like editors for 20 years (WS, Turbo-Pascal, rtc)
and so am most comforatable with that basic key set.  Others prefer
vi-like or emacs-like keys.  Get an editor that can be configured to any
key set and try out all of them.  Definately get one that doen't _need_
the mouse.

I use TSE and have it configured with colouration for Cobol, short keys
for most reserved words (eg  F9-P giver PERFORM, F0-V gives VARYING).

Many editors have limited time or limited facility versions for
downloading.  For example www.semware.com have a yestdrive version of
TSE for DOS or Windows that you can download.
```

<a id="t-6e6e45abcb"></a>
## Thread: "Modifying terms" for use with the word COBOL — 7 msgs

### "Modifying terms" for use with the word COBOL

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-11T14:27:04-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9g362e$g2s$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Given the (relatively unproductive) thread that is going on and also given
my (personal) desire that the majority of those of us who "post regularly"
to this newsgroup use "common terms" for the same "types of COBOL" - I
thought I would start a thread to solicit comments on the following terms
and definitions.  As this is an "unmoderated" newsgroup, there is NOTHING
that any of us can do to enforce these terms, but if we agree on the
appropriate terms, then we can be more consistent and less confusing to
others.

 - Conforming COBOL - source code conforms to ANSI/ISO Standard (current
ANSI/ISO Standard).

 - 'nn Conforming COBOL - source code that conforms to the "19nn" or "20nn"
ANSI/ISO Standard

 - Conforming XYZ COBOL - source code conforms to the XYZ Standard (e.g.
X/Open, MIA, SAA, etc)

 - Clear (and usually maintainable) COBOL - source code that can
(subjectively) be picked up and understood/maintained by a programmer OTHER
than the one who originally wrote it.  (Sorry, Tony - this doesn't include
your code that is "clear" to you, but not to many/most others)

 - Valid XYZ COBOL - COBOL which gets a "clean compile" with the XYZ
compiler.  The XYZ compiler vendor describes the rules for this source code
and whether informational or warning message may or may not be issued for
such code.  (The phrase "valid COBOL" may be used without mentioning XYZ -
if the context makes it clear which compiler - and possible release and
possibly operating system are being used.  The same is true for the "XYZ" in
"Conforming XYZ COBOL").

 - Portable COBOL - COBOL that compiles "cleanly" and produces the same
run-time results in multiple environments.  In most cases, one actually
needs to (at least initially) talk about which environments are in question,
as almost no "real world" COBOL is fully portable to ALL known ANSI/ISO
conforming compilers/operating systems.

   ***

Depending on what modifications others have and/or how wide the "acceptance"
of these (or replacement) terms are, I may or may not add them to the FAQ to
"encourage" their use in future posts.
```

#### ↳ Re: "Modifying terms" for use with the word COBOL

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-12T12:55:45+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b256860_3@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<9g362e$g2s$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill,

I've watched with absolutely no enjoyment the exchange between yourself and
Richard.

Both of you are extremely valuable contributors in this NG, I have respect
for the knowledge and usually very sound advice provided by both of you, and
I ALWAYS read threads contributed by each of you.

However, recently, it has become like two bald men fighting for a comb...

While terminology is important, (it is pretty difficult to have ANY kind of
meaningful discussion unless terms are agreed), it surely shouldn't become
so intense that the discussion descends to personal abuse...

I read the post below, Bill, I see and understand why you posted it, but I
have to be honest here...it confuses me!

I don't know about the other readers of this NG, (hopefully some of them
will respond to your post with their feelings about it) but personally, I
don't need to have definitions of exactly what I mean when I use the term
"valid COBOL" or "Clear COBOL" or "conforming COBOL" because it really
doesn't matter. As for accepting the ANSI/ISO standard as being " valid
COBOL", well you know how I feel about that...basically, just heartsick.

I believe that when COBOL is discussed here it is pretty obvious what
specifically is being discussed. There is no need to obfuscate it with a
series of pedantic definitions so we can go off at a tangent and argue about
those instead of the points actually under consideration.

The COBOL FAQ serves an extremely valuable function and, in my opinion, it
would be a misuse of it to bog it down with a series of "definitions" as to
what constitutes certain (originally) informal descriptions of COBOL.

BOTTOM LINE:

1. Although it may be Life and Death to you and Richard (at the moment), I
believe that the majority of people here really don't care, and certainly
don't need MORE definitions of things that really don't need defining.

2. It is a pity to see two really intelligent and valuable people engaged in
such a waste of time... But, as you observed, Bill, it is an unmoderated NG
and I respect totally the right of both of you to pursue whatever you decide
to pursue....

The purpose of this post as been to try and re-introduce an element of
perspective and get you both to re-examine whether you REALLY want to pursue
this...?

My two cents...

Pete.

OTHER

## Thread: convert cobol — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### Re: convert cobol

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-11T19:44:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B252051.DEE77DF4@home.com>`

```


Bob Wolfe wrote:

> "James. J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:
>
…[63 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Bob,

Thanks for comments and those from Kevin. I was somewhat 'loose' in my
definition of events being returned - and should have been more
specific, they give back 'results/text' as a result of a Windows event -
whereas I currently will take that event object and return it to the
Business Class as an object reference, pic 9 or pic x, as appropriate.

> COBOL sp2 also supports all of the other major compilers as well.  The
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Now a minor correction on yours above <G>. Pete Dashwood has
successfully developed component modules, (his DECLGEN he offered folks,
is a good case in point) and I can run it because he provides the
FREEBIE Fujitsu runtime (Hint ! Hint ! - somebody is missing out on a
potential market for their product). As yet I haven't used a component
of his 'linked' to Net Express, but there is absolutely no reason why
that shouldn't work either.

Now of course, if and when OO truly gets off the ground, there's no
reason, using a switch acknowledging different operating systems, that
you can't address a different OS. While your SP2 approach is to
acknowledge each different OS in one package,  I would suggest, that in
the majority of instances, most developers are coding primarily for one
platform. I know there will be exceptions, but again think they are
probably very much the minority. On the plus side both SP2 and Screen IO
both allow a developer to switch to his current vendor of choice without
extensive re-writes.

As an example can you visualize Howard coding in OO for Big Blue, but at
the same time producing, (or wanting) a version for PC desktops - which
pieces of furniture do I move out of the house to make way for his vast
disk/tape storage <G>

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: convert cobol

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-12T17:30:29+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b25a8ca_6@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<3B252051.DEE77DF4@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:3B252051.DEE77DF4@home.com...
>
> Now a minor correction on yours above <G>. Pete Dashwood has
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The main objection to using Components at the moment is the requirement for
different run time systems.

If the component is written in VB it requires a VB runtime; C++ requires a
C++ runtime and so on...

These two are not so bad because many systems already have these run times
in their Windows\system directory with all the other Dynamic Link Libraries
that are necessary to implement Windows.

(If you don't know it's there then you don't grieve over it...<G>)

One reason why Java Components (Beans) are so effective is because they
DON'T require a "run time" as such (they do require the JVM, but anyone who
has a Browser, probably has this implemented already...)

The main impact of the forthcoming .NET will be its ability to run a single
set of intermediate code and thus remove the need for all these different
run times.

That is one reason why I believe the future belongs to component technology;
once the "inconvenience" of different run times is overcome, there will be
no stopping it.

(Even WITH this inconvenience, many of us are using Components effectively
and deriving great benefit from doing so...)

> Now of course, if and when OO truly gets off the ground, there's no
> reason, using a switch acknowledging different operating systems, that
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The exact scenario Jimmy describes above (coding OO COBOL simultaneously for
mainframe and PC platforms) should eventuate within the next few years as a
common runtime becomes available. It is very likely the .NET intermediate
code will be produced by mainframe platforms as well as PCs, and this will
lead to running components on the mainframe which may have been written in a
Language which won't even compile on the mainframe!

The Object Code will be all that matters, and this will be encapsulated into
functions and wrapped as Components.

Object Oriented COBOL is more than up to this task, but as long as we have
no standard for it, and no effective mechanism whereby we can define and
iteratively refine a base for it (other than individual vendor's
implementations) it is a sad reality that COBOL will probably "miss the bus"
in this area.

I am already reviewing whether I shall continue to develop components in
COBOL (even when it is definitely the best language for a particular task)
because I have become disillusioned with the constant bickering, pedantry,
and failure to actually achieve anything, which we have seen coming out of
J4. (despite their protestations regarding their heroic efforts).

Frankly, the thought that the Language I have known and loved for 35 years
is now in the hands of people who couldn't organise a piss up in a Brewery,
frightens me...

Maybe with a common run time and excellent vendor implementations (such as
we can expect from Merant and Fujitsu, to name but 2) there will still be a
case for using OO COBOL to develop business components, but it is by no
means certain.

I hope I'm wrong...only time will tell.

Pete.



Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

>
> The main objection to using Components at the moment is the requirement for
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> <snip>

Lots of relevant stuff and I don't need to reiterate what Pete has already
written. To amplify what he has said I wanted to quote from two sources -  be
damned if I can find reference to them.

The first was a very relevant introductory paragraph on Java. So in a very bad
paraphrase, gong from memory, this is what it said :-

"When manufacturers design fridges, freezers, TVs, radios etc etc...... They can
start from ground zero - completely design from scratch. They don't. Having
arrived at an overall design, both due to time factor and cost, and one might
add a proven record that the component works, they look for component parts
which integrate with their design, available from allied industries"

Then the 'lost' quote went on to make the same point about the Java language
providing components........

The second - I believe from InfoWorld, originally passed from Thane to Pete,
then Pete to me :-

The article addresses the fact that the mainland states (is that the correct
phrase ?), are addressing the problem of re-inventing the wheel. They each have
auto/vehicle licensing systems. Parallel -  but local subtleties, because of
state legislation. However, so much of it is 'common components'. Simply put
they are going to design and swap components. Agreed this is an area tied into
one specific 'industry' - but reflect on its impact on programming, but not
constrained to one language, and when spread over all the various categories of
industry.........?

The article above doesn't say it - but COBOL, because of its business acumen -
is up there, front and centre !

Thane or Pete - the link please (Sounds like a Quiz program <G>)

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b2b69ee.227853504@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<3B252051.DEE77DF4@home.com> <3b25a8ca_6@Usenet.com>`

```
On Tue, 12 Jun 2001 17:30:29 +1200, "Peter E. C. Dashwood"
<dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:


>The exact scenario Jimmy describes above (coding OO COBOL simultaneously for
>mainframe and PC platforms) should eventuate within the next few years as a
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Note: For those of you interested MSDN.MICROSOFT.COM and several
echoing sites (including www.adtools.com) are hosting the documents
that are created as part of the standardization process of not only C#
but also the intermediate runtime code.  This is the first time I am
aware of that something akin to object code has been standardized.

>Object Oriented COBOL is more than up to this task, but as long as we have
>no standard for it, and no effective mechanism whereby we can define and
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

In the draf the base class is well defined.  If you are lamenting the
lack of a common class library, I understand,    Serious query:  What,
in your opinion should be included in such a common class library?

>I am already reviewing whether I shall continue to develop components in
>COBOL (even when it is definitely the best language for a particular task)
>because I have become disillusioned with the constant bickering, pedantry,
>and failure to actually achieve anything, which we have seen coming out of
>J4. (despite their protestations regarding their heroic efforts).

I see no "constant bickering" at J4 - and in fact the International
comments we received bear out the fact that we have a good, sound
document.  One interesting fact - The Japanese have always held a
critical eye to the OO portion of the draft.  They have always
submitted requests and clarifications and pointed out any errors or
pitfalls.  In there international comments, there was nary a mention
of anything about the OO portions of the draft.  I found this VERY
encouraging.

As far as your other comments - it doesn't matter at this point.  We
can regret the past all day long, but that gets us nowhere.  Let's
consider the future.  The draft is basically "Frozen" and has been
since we went to FCD.  We are in the "correction" stage at this point,
and the frequency of meetings has increased.  We have 2 more working
meetings before WG4 in November, at which time WG4 will make their
recommendation at to our comment resolution.  There will then be a
vote on the final document and it will be forwarded to ISO.
```
