###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ODBC Driver for COBOL Indexed Files

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-18T16:36:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B2E2DC9.3CB615B3@home.com>`
- **References:** `<d%cV6.14749$QE4.1828241@news20.bellglobal.com> <20010611202604.11055.00003402@ng-mc1.aol.com> <e6fX6.14033$Az2.52996@news1.frmt1.sfba.home.com>`

```


Aaron Cardon wrote:

> To my knowledge, NetExpress does not provide any ODBC drivers that are
> capable of accessing their indexed data files.  Please correct me if I am
> wrong.
>

Aaron,

Now that you have been more specific and quoted N/E V 3.1. I don't know, but
would have thought it extremely unlikely that there is any ODBC that is
geared to reading COBOL files. Such a generalized tool would have to
acknowledge the different formats of COBOL files coming with each vendors
'compiler - inevitably an ODBC driver is geared to the format of a specific
DB..

You are already aware that N/E has a pretty detailed on-line book regarding
DB and SQL. It seems to me if you want to bite the bullet on this one, select
your DB of choice, then create Ascil-delmiteds(CSVs) from your COBOL files
and feed them into the DB.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ODBC Driver for COBOL Indexed Files

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<d%cV6.14749$QE4.1828241@news20.bellglobal.com> <20010611202604.11055.00003402@ng-mc1.aol.com> <e6fX6.14033$Az2.52996@news1.frmt1.sfba.home.com> <3B2E2DC9.3CB615B3@home.com>`

James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:3B2E2DC9.3CB615B3@home.com...
>
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

am

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Now that you have been more specific and quoted N/E V 3.1. I don't know,
but
> would have thought it extremely unlikely that there is any ODBC that is
> geared to reading COBOL files. Such a generalized tool would have to
> acknowledge the different formats of COBOL files coming with each vendors
> 'compiler - inevitably an ODBC driver is geared to the format of a
specific
> DB..
[snip]

Jimmy,

But such an ODBC driver exists.  Its name is Relativity.  Its innovations
are subject of U.S. Pat. No. 5,826,076, British Pat. No. GB2302430, and
other patents pending.  The title of the US patent is "Computer-based
information access method and apparatus to permit SQL-based manipulation of
programming language-specific data files".

I guess I need to plug Relativity far more often in this newsgroup!  :o)

http://www.liant.com/products/relativity/

Relativity is available for Liant's own RM/COBOL, Micro Focus, and Pervasive
Btrieve.  AcuCOBOL and Fujitsu's newer file formats are NOT supported.

Regards,
Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

#### ↳ Re: ODBC Driver for COBOL Indexed Files

- **From:** contact@mikewatson.co.uk (Mike Watson)
- **Date:** 2001-06-14T07:37:41-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<781ddb68.0106140637.315ad82f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<4haV6.312$CZ4.35927@news1.frmt1.sfba.home.com>`

```
Aaron,

Liant Software offer a product named Relativity. Relativity allows
legacy data from sophisticated Micro Focus or RM/COBOL applications to
be retooled as a full-featured relational database. The data can then
be joined with other disparate data and accessed by 'ANY' JDBC or ODBC
tool seamlessly and transparently.
For more information then please take a look at the following URL:
http://www.liant.com/products/relativity/

I hope that this is of some further help to you.
Regards
Mike Watson
Liant Technical Support [London].

that can read Cobol data files (MF, ACU, RM, FSC)

Vadim Maslov

We extract data from COBOL dat files. We dont know what compiler produced
the application. But the files are presented to us in Ascii, so we just read
them as text files then rewrite them to a database.
We do have the file format of the original COBOL file.
If we didnt have the File format we could guess most of it.
David M

## Thread: AcuCobol + SP2: Any user experiences? — 12 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### AcuCobol + SP2: Any user experiences?

- **From:** "Grinder" <no.spam@no.spam.spam.spam.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-11T18:59:24-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qVcV6.42$s4.3955@e420r-atl4.usenetserver.com>`

```
Is there anyone out there using AcuCobol in tandem
with SP2 that would have some time to share their
experiences?
```

#### ↳ Re: AcuCobol + SP2: Any user experiences?

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 2001-06-12T00:24:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010611202442.11055.00003401@ng-mc1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<qVcV6.42$s4.3955@e420r-atl4.usenetserver.com>`

```
Hi,

I have used Sp2 for about four years (not with AcuCOBOL) and it works great. 
The support is fantastic.  I know that they work with a number of different
COBOL vendors and I have never heard anything bad from any user that I have
talked to.

Bob Hennessey
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: AcuCobol + SP2: Any user experiences?

- **From:** merhottin@aol.com (Merhottin)
- **Date:** 2001-06-12T02:48:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010611224845.29782.00000059@ng-fr1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<20010611202442.11055.00003401@ng-mc1.aol.com>`

```
     I have to agree with Bob.  Never had any problems, and support is
terrific.  I have always gotten a response back in less then 2 hours, but
usually within the hour. They have even looked at some of the screens I was
having problems with, and offered solutions.  
     I do not use AcuCOBOL either, but sure  there isnt much difference.  SP2
offers 30 day trial, with full support during that time.  Definatly worth
trying.  Feel free to email me, but you can also email flexus.

John
```

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-06-12T10:07:18-07:00

```
I use Sp2 with Fujitsu COBOL normally.  I have a client using
MicroFocus and another using AcuCOBOL GT.

My preference is for Fujitsu because of the robust and reliable code,
and the lack of a runtime fee in the US.  AcuCOBOL is a fine product,
but has a runtime fee.  However, if your desire is multiple OS
support, AcuCOBOL seems to lead the pack.

"Grinder" <no.spam@no.spam.spam.spam.net> wrote in message news:<qVcV6.42$s4.3955@e420r-atl4.usenetserver.com>...
> Is there anyone out there using AcuCobol in tandem
> with SP2 that would have some time to share their
> experiences?

I use SP2 with Acucobol. They work well together. The reason I choose
the Flexus product was for the thin client. At the time Acucobol had
no such equivalent.(They are bringing out a thin client product in
beta this month) I like the SP2 screen development environment but we
may switch to native Acucobol screens for a large screen conversion
project we have coming up. The reason we might switch has nothing to
do with SP2 not being adequate. I would actually prefer to use the SP2
product but our conversion project will involve screen and code
conversion from WANG to Acucobol and the tools that do this don't
support SP2. Since Acucobol runs on so many platforms and will now
support thin client architecture we feel confident that switching will
work out fine.
```

Gary - couple of questions for you...

I just happen to have a query outstanding from a potential customer on
a Wang to sp2 conversion.  Have you performed other Wang conversions? 
We may need to speak privately.

How does AcuCOBOL itself help you with a WANG conversion?  Is there
emulation built in?

How do you handle the normally "vast" amounts of WANG type "jcl" that
usually accompany WANG systems?

Next, I'm anxious to know more about the AcuCOBOL Thin Client
offering?

Do they offer remote printing like the Flexus products?  What about
data encryption and compression of the transmitted data?  How do they
handle bitmaps and images?  Do they have any type of version control
for "Temporary" files and the like?  What about the ability to launch
client side executables, or the ability to interface with the client
side hardware such as a receipt printer or other special hardware?

Thanks!

<a id="t-5acb8c8353"></a>
## Thread: Win32API to shutdown another program — 3 msgs

### Win32API to shutdown another program

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 2001-06-12T00:28:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010611202851.11055.00003403@ng-mc1.aol.com>`

```
Good Afternoon,

Is anyone aware of how to use Win32API to detect if a program is running and
then be able to shutdown that program down ?  The program that I want to detect
and shutdown is a non COBOL program (P.C. Anywhere).

Thanks,
Bob Hennessey
```

#### ↳ Re: Win32API to shutdown another program

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-12T01:57:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VFeV6.91068$Be4.29568148@news3.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<20010611202851.11055.00003403@ng-mc1.aol.com>`

```
Caveat emptor. Caveat omnis. Here there be dragons!!!

To terminate a process:

1) Call OpenProcess(PROCESS_ALL_ACCESS, TRUE, ProcId)
2) Call TerminateProcess(hProcess, 0)

see Microsoft KB Article Q90749 for details.

The harder part is determining the process Id for the application. 

Under Windows 9x you can use the ToolHelp32 functions: CreateToolHelp32Snapshot(), Process32First() and Process32Next().

Under Windows NT you use the platform services API (PSAPI): EnumProcesses(), EnumProcessModules(), GetModuleFileNameEx() and GetModuleBaseName().

see Microsoft KB Article Q175030 for more info.

There may be more recent articles that provide a more elegant solution. You might also need to use SetPrivilege() to give your application debug privileges and the power to cause havoc to your system.

Karl


"Bob7536" <bob7536@aol.com> wrote in message news:20010611202851.11055.00003403@ng-mc1.aol.com...
> Good Afternoon,
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Win32API to shutdown another program

- **From:** "Grinder" <no.spam@no.spam.spam.spam.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-11T23:40:36-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<O4hV6.1474$o4.73577@e420r-atl1.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<20010611202851.11055.00003403@ng-mc1.aol.com>`

```
Oscar provides a lot of good information.  One point worth
mentioning though, is the use of TerminateProcess.  This
is equivalent to Ctrl-Alt-Deleting the application.

Some applications, Lotus Notes for one, can realize that
they've abended and will refuse to run until a restart is
performed.

The "civil" way to close an application is to send a WM_QUIT
message to it's main window.  As it may be difficult to determine
the main window (some apps don't really have a main window,)
it's generally accepted practice to send WM_QUIT to all top
level windows.

becomes time to change them because of "maintenance" changes.

I hope for their sake, they don't 
"throw the baby out with the bathwater", and save the old source for
updating it in a year or two!

Gary
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ The future of COBOL was Re: Beginner's Language?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-06-24T17:43:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B362696.998075DA@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<9cukad$nn68@news-dxb> <q9n6ft0pbn903d5gqqbjfjns0ae0kfvra9@4ax.com> <9d6b6e$1bt$1@nh.pace.co.uk> <87snihxiwc.fsf@frown.here> <9dbi83$sji$1@nh.pace.co.uk> <87heyu7cqd.fsf@frown.here> <9dc20p$hh15e$1@ID-37382.news.dfncis.de> <sashank.varma-0905011801100001@129.59.212.53> <9dclnt$9ic1@news.cis.okstate.edu> <3B02C05B.BA4B63C6@raytheon.com> <slrn9g5hdr.asg.stephan@pcrm.win.tue.nl> <3B08965C.6F34D5C9@ix.netcom.com> <Xns90A8E9104ebfg01bluewinch@195.186.4.29> <3B0AA48E.E8650E3F@home.com> <3B25A2A4.2AD24817@ont.com> <3B259D8C.BCD40349@home.com>`

```
Jimmy, I wish you were right.  However what Marie is saying for her
environment mirrors what is happening in a lot of places for good
reasons.  It is expensive to write, maintain and document a system
regardless of language so packages are replacing in-house systems in
many organizations.  People are even getting smart and minimizing
changes so that they can go to the next release.  For code on the client
side where the user interface is more important, C/C++ may be the way to
go although I think that Java may be more appropriate.  The COBOL
community hasn't come to grips with HTML and its delimited text
organization (as opposed to the fixed field that I know and
understand).  COBOL has nothing native for dealing with databases
(although I would like to see a view treated as a file and think that
there could be tremendous productivity gains from doing so in COBOL). 
Thus, they are not going to be making as many changes as when they
wrote, owned and maintained the system and if they did the job right,
the maintenance should be relatively easy regardless of language.  

The one caveat to my statements above is that someone had better make
certain that the package(s) is(are) doing the arithmetic correctly, the
rounding and the handling of intermediate results.  Binary floating
point can give different results from fixed point decimal (more
accurated in some cases but that is another question). 

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc.     

"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> gary drummond wrote:
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Gary
```

```
"Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote in message
news:3B362696.998075DA@istar.ca...
> Jimmy, I wish you were right.  However what Marie is saying for her
> environment mirrors what is happening in a lot of places for good
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Clark,

this is a good summary of what is actually happening and I agree with you
that the move is not "anti-COBOL" it is "anti-maintenance".

While I have some serious reservations about what they are doing, I cannot
fault their reasons for doing it.

Using C++ on the Clients MAY be a solution but it isn't the one I would go
for. Tying reports to ORACLE PL/SQL is something else I would be careful
about (especially if they are using ORACLE procedures on the database.)

But these are really "nit picks" in the overall context. You cannot blame
them for phasing out expensive development and maintenance operations and
moving to packages that meet their requirements.

The one that really worries me is Marie's statement that they will replace
the Patient system (currently in COBOL) with a new package.

I have worked in Hospital systems and I know that the "Patient Index" is the
heart of the whole system. Everything else interfaces to and derives data
from it. In other words this particular system is "mission critical".

I would've thought it was a VERY high risk to replace this with a "Big Bang"
package installation, rather than a phased implementation where each phase
can be audited and verified before proceeding.

It looks to me like some Marketing people have done a very good job over a
few good lunches...<G>

Hope it turns out OK for them and, (more importantly) the patients...

Pete.




Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B396A08.5E3873A8@home.com>`
- **References:** `<9cukad$nn68@news-dxb> <q9n6ft0pbn903d5gqqbjfjns0ae0kfvra9@4ax.com> <9d6b6e$1bt$1@nh.pace.co.uk> <87snihxiwc.fsf@frown.here> <9dbi83$sji$1@nh.pace.co.uk> <87heyu7cqd.fsf@frown.here> <9dc20p$hh15e$1@ID-37382.news.dfncis.de> <sashank.varma-0905011801100001@129.59.212.53> <9dclnt$9ic1@news.cis.okstate.edu> <3B02C05B.BA4B63C6@raytheon.com> <slrn9g5hdr.asg.stephan@pcrm.win.tue.nl> <3B08965C.6F34D5C9@ix.netcom.com> <Xns90A8E9104ebfg01bluewinch@195.186.4.29> <3B0AA48E.E8650E3F@home.com> <3B25A2A4.2AD24817@ont.com> <3B259D8C.BCD40349@home.com> <3B362696.998075DA@istar.ca>`

```


"Clark F. Morris, Jr." wrote:

> Jimmy, I wish you were right.

Just checked - not too sure what you wish I was right about <G>

> However what Marie is saying for her
> environment mirrors what is happening in a lot of places for good
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yes I'll accept that packaging has to be the way to go. Not for any altruistic reason,
the banks have latched on to Payroll as a service - they've got their hands on your
money. For small to medium, packages also make sense as the way to go. (But even in the
area of POS I see many different products being used by the Mom and Pop stores).
Packaging includes big 'groups' like hospitals, subject to national medicare
regulations. Hospitals have classic administrative domains and it just doesn't make
sense to have say 6 or more hospital computer departments in the same city re-inventing
the wheel.

But even with packaging - Company B puts the effort into having a package which was
written in C, customized. Then Company A takes them over, and they have SAP - guess what
happens to the C application ?

However, packaging does have its limits. Agreed Accounts Payable sounds very similar for
all organizations, essentially paying for purchases/expenses, printing cheques  and
giving a summary to the P & L. Now diverge a little.

(a) Retail - matching to orders placed, subtract from commitment (the dollar value of
orders placed and still outstanding), and that in turn increases the Open-to-buy based
on Current/Planned Stock. I can't guarantee but I believe most major retailers in the
Americas and Europe work in retail dollars/pounds/DMs etc., so those various items above
are expressed at retail value, invariably taking a Cumulative Mark-Up to calculate stock
at cost for annual financial purposes.

(b) Oil/Gas - again a large amount of matching to orders, but they are also interested
in AFEs for expenditure on projects, plus they may be joint-owners with several other
exploration companies, so they bear say 60% of the expense and need to pass the
additional 40% on to their partners, (the 40% being split unevenly amongst the partners,
possibly different percentages per project).

Just two industries that use Payables, but results in a very diverse operation, albeit
they both have vendor files, outstanding invoices and have to print cheques. Perhaps you
can clone the two from a base system - perhaps you can't. (No doubt SAP would claim you
can).

Also there is some packaging which is a pure rip off. Treasury Branch for oil/gas. Some
six people wheeling and dealing with' funny money', futures, and trying to keep the debt
ratio in check. Price for such packages $0.5 million upwards. (I'm definitely in the
wrong business !)

Question is - if there are those C packages - where are the competing COBOL packages.
There's 3 million of us and certainly not more than 1 million of them. So why go the C
route - is COBOL perceived to be lacking or inflexible ?

Two things you mentioned - HTML and a COBOL Database.

You can get picky, but HTML is a branch of the GUI family. Neither J4 at this point in
time, nor many COBOL developers believe COBOL should embrace GUIs - so don't hold your
breath on this one - unless you will take vendor 'extensions' as the substitute. (The
key problem being that most believe COBOL should be 'vanilla' flavoured - i.e. in no way
acknowledge the different and major O/Ss. That kind of puts the kibosh on GUI-ing).

I referred to Ed Arranga's book on OO, published 1996. He mentioned a COBOL database in
passing - it hasn't happened nor been mooted to the best of my knowledge - so don't hold
your breath on that one either.

Now before our good friends Bill and Stephen jump in, let me say it for them : "Clark,
if you have ideas for enhancements, make a proposal to J4...." <G>
I think you would stand a better chance with Pete's e-mail-forum IF it ever happened.

Jimmy
```

```
> You can get picky, but HTML is a branch of the GUI family. Neither
J4 at this point in
> time, nor many COBOL developers believe COBOL should embrace GUIs -
so don't hold your
> breath on this one - unless you will take vendor 'extensions' as the
substitute. (The
> key problem being that most believe COBOL should be 'vanilla'
flavoured - i.e. in no way
> acknowledge the different and major O/Ss. That kind of puts the
kibosh on GUI-ing).

Maybe a twig--but not a branch.  It's ironic--thin clients implemented
in web browsers are typically closer to a dos-style interface than
windows.  The native forms support in HTML is quite weak.  ActiveX and
Jave applets are tourists and allow a web page to come alive, but HTML
is almost entirely static.

XML (and to a lesser degree XHTML) is being touted as being the next
revolution in information management.  The hype is exhausting, but
there are some strong concepts that could be used to unify systems.
If a strong XML interface could be developed for use with Cobol
environments, it would provide a value common ground.

My apologies to previous posters in this thread if I'm being
redundant.
```

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Joseph J Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2001-06-28T23:20:17+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B3B4B31.9FCDB5D6@iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<9cukad$nn68@news-dxb> <q9n6ft0pbn903d5gqqbjfjns0ae0kfvra9@4ax.com> <9d6b6e$1bt$1@nh.pace.co.uk> <87snihxiwc.fsf@frown.here> <9dbi83$sji$1@nh.pace.co.uk> <87heyu7cqd.fsf@frown.here> <9dc20p$hh15e$1@ID-37382.news.dfncis.de> <sashank.varma-0905011801100001@129.59.212.53> <9dclnt$9ic1@news.cis.okstate.edu> <3B02C05B.BA4B63C6@raytheon.com> <slrn9g5hdr.asg.stephan@pcrm.win.tue.nl> <3B08965C.6F34D5C9@ix.netcom.com> <Xns90A8E9104ebfg01bluewinch@195.186.4.29> <3B0AA48E.E8650E3F@home.com> <3B25A2A4.2AD24817@ont.com> <3B259D8C.BCD40349@home.com> <3B362696.998075DA@istar.ca> <3B396A08.5E3873A8@home.com> <4ev_6.30463$f4.1292116@e420r-atl1.usenetserver.com>`

```
Grinder wrote:
> 
> 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> redundant.

But to use XML, you need to create the dictionary and run half a dozen
utilities, which create the XML scanner,... etc etc etc etc.

I like the approach being take by the Linux community. HTML like tags
are being used in the open source spreadsheets and word processors. ONLY
those programs understand these tags. As a result, they don't have to go
through all the XML pre-bullshit. They just define the tags and use
them.

This has always been in the HTML standard. If you do not understand a
tag, you ignore it and just present the data as best you can.

My boss today lamented that his laptop could quite possibly run all the
computing requirements for the city - but instead it wasted its time
trying to understand how to move one pixel from point a on the screen to
point b on the screen.

The problem with XML is that it is TOO much of a solution and as such
90% of it is not required to enable the construction of programs that
communicate with each other. It is too generic, when what is needed is
something more specific to the task of communication. 

The same is true for the browser. Once Javascript and Java were coopted
into the browser, the HTML tag developers stopped creating new tags for
the native browser. The solution to everything became, write a
Javascript or Java applet. But you can only present and obtain data in
so many ways. A proper full forms based html interface will outperform a
java or script applet every time.

I see this happening all over the place. COBOL does not get new verbs
and language elements because the possiblilty of OO coming along and
letting the user create their own "verbs". But this is merely the
formalization of a library of routines. I applaud this because,
hopefully, when the class libraries have been standardized, we will all
have better tools to do the job. I would have prefered that these
"Programming Domain" classes be made native to the language. That is as
discrete language elements or verbs and not as a generic interface to be
"invoked". Then the OO stuff could be used for the "problem domain"
whatever that was.

In short, the proliferation of complex tools is not helping the issue of
developer productivity.
```

Joseph J Katnic wrote:

> "James J. Gavan" wrote:
> >
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yes I do now. And we already have that in Fujitsu and Micro Focus OO COBOL.
Can't speak for F/J - but your reference to linked lists is not a feature of
Micro Focus, (which is a 'reflection' of Smalltalk that does include linked
lists). But I believe the mechanism of linked lists is adequately covered in the
M/F Collection hierarchy.

>
> What do you mean by 'business' and what does it have to do with a
> programming language?
>

Surprisingly enough the word COBOL makes a passing reference to 'Business'.
Where did it all start. That US Census on punched cards (the government's
interest
in knowing numbers to conduct government 'business'). First practical commercial
use was punched cards - used predominantly by accountants ('business'). Where
did the mainframes start - FORTRAN the 'business' language for engineers. Then
came COBOL to replace the accountants' punched cards. Do I need to go on.

Agreed, programming has extended outside of traditional 'business' - simulation,
NASA with specific languages, graphics, imaging, etc.. But I would still
suggest, quoting Microsoft's figures, that 21.6 million programmers are
predominantly employed using programming for the generic group 'business'. And
'business' = making money. Even all the hype about Java and connecting to the
Net is associated with the 'business' need to communicate.

Jimmy
```

## Thread: Micro Focus - soon to be "out of" MERANT — 9 this month / 11 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Micro Focus - soon to be "out of" MERANT

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-12T08:56:38-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9g574k$2fb$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
FYI

"12/06/2001 07:30:34
Merant : �45m sale to Golden Gate

Merant has agreed to sell its legacy development, transformation and
integration business to Golden Gate Capital and Parallax Capital Partners
for $62.5m (�45m), payable in cash on
completion, subject to adjustment for changes in book value of the business
prior to closing.

Golden Gate Capital is a $700m private equity firm dedicated to investing in
high-growth businesses in change-intensive industries.

It is proposed that Golden Gate will acquire Merant's legacy development,
transformation and integration business through a new British Virgin Islands
based company, Micro Focus
International, which will be operated by the current management team of the
Micro Focus business.

David Dominik, a Managing Director and co-founder of Golden Gate, said:

"We have teamed up with Parallax Capital Partners, who will assist Micro
Focus in its transition to an independent company."

Background to and details of the Disposal

Micro Focus is an independent provider of COBOL on the desktop and
distributed environments--a market which presents considerable opportunities
within existing mainframe
environments,

For fiscal year 2001, Micro Focus revenue was �84m ($123.6m), contribution
to the group was �7.5m ($11.7m) before income taxes and goodwill
amortisation, and net tangible
assets at 30 April 2001 were �7m ($10m).

On completion, the disposal is expected to result in a small pre-tax gain,
after restructuring charges.

ICV Edited News
END (KRT)

07:30 Jun 12 2001
"
```

#### ↳ Re: Micro Focus - soon to be "out of" MERANT

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-12T09:09:39-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
For more, see:

  http://www.merant.com/resource_center/press/releases/061201b.asp

which says (in part),

"Tony Hill, Senior Vice President and General Manager of the legacy
development, transformation and integration business at MERANT, will head
the new company. "This is a great opportunity for our customers and
employees," said Hill. "The Micro Focus name is synonymous with leadership
in COBOL development throughout the world. We have a large and loyal
customer base. We have market proven technology. And we have the best talent
and expertise in the world. We will now have the ability to focus every
calorie of our energy on driving the success of our customers and growing a
strong and profitable business. Our partnership with Golden Gate and
Parallax gives us the financial resources to invest in our core technology
and expand our business through additional acquisitions."
"
```

So to use a WMK phrase - 'Bottom Line' - translation - does this statement

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus - soon to be "out of" MERANT

- **From:** "Editor" <Editor@aboutlegacycoding.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-18T10:28:04-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9gl35a$2v2m$1@allbad.news.cais.net>`
- **References:** `<9g574k$2fb$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3B2655F6.58A40933@home.com>`

```
Did anybody else notice the way Micro Focus's business is described in this
release?

It's not described as a COBOL Compiler company, or a development tools
company.  It's described as a: "legacy development, transformation and
integration business."

That sounds like a services business to me, not a tools company.

Could be just the word smithing of a novice PR person at Golden Gate trying
to throw in all the latest buzz words, but quite frankly, that whole
description bothers me.  If they focus on services and not tools, they will
be competing with the very people they normally sell compilers to and
through.
With the exception of Microsoft (who is so arrogant they can get away with
it), very few companies can be both tools vendors and service companies.  It
divides their focus, but more importantly it alienates their strongest
evangelist - the contract developers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus - soon to be "out of" MERANT

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-18T13:30:51-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9glhcs$ndd$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9g574k$2fb$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3B2655F6.58A40933@home.com> <9gl35a$2v2m$1@allbad.news.cais.net>`

```
There is nothing "definitive" about any of this, but the people that I know
who have worked in development (and still do) on the "legacy" tools, all
seem pretty happy about what they are hearing internally.

I think they don't limit it to "COBOL" as Micro Focus (before MERANT)
included
  - Assembler
  - PL/I (limited)
  - CICS and IMS
  - remote data access
  - DB2
  - ISPF

for their "mainframe" tools.

For Workstation tools, they included a "full range" of tools for Windows
(and Unix) environments.  My *guess* is that these will go with the "new"
company as well.
```

"William M. Klein" wrote:

> There is nothing "definitive" about any of this, but the people that I know
> who have worked in development (and still do) on the "legacy" tools, all
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

As I said Bill - wish them every luck. (BTW - Happy 25th Birthday, Micro Focus).
Given their 25 year history with COBOL, same goes for Ryan McFarland, (and I am
not in anyway decrying mainframe vendor compilers), look at the following table
:-

First some numbers. 2-3 million COBOL programmers (Gartner), somebody else
(Murtech ?) speculated 90% mainframe - let's assume 80% mainframe. Then there
are 21.5 million programmers in total - see cobolreport.com, claim by Microsoft,
plus in same article Sun claims 2.5 million Java users. Let's get cautious and
divide figures by three, but it doesn't change the percentages any  :-

        COBOL    - Mainframe (80%)        :    800,000               11.16 %
                          - PCs/Unix                    :
200,000                 2.79 %
        Java                                                :
833,333                11.62 %
        Other languages                               : 5,333,333
74.43 %
                                                                ------------
        Total                                                :
7,166,666            100.00%

Speculation - if when Micro Focus was aligned with Apple and the Apple COBOL had
come to fruition, first would we have seen two heads-on fairly equal
competitors,  Apple and Microsoft, and even allowing for language overlaps, i.e.
there are COBOL programmers using other languages and vice versa, perhaps the
above table to-day might just look like the following ? A logical extension of
the Apple alliance would have seen GUIs as an automatic necessity for COBOL.

Way back when, on Radio Shack, I had a graphics package from RM callable from
RM/COBOL '74 !. (For Tom Morrision - I'm not referring to the Liant package
available with RM/COBOL ' 85, but the RM module issued in a Radio Shack brown
cover for the old clunkers - Model II ).

Note - I'm not implying Micro Focus was the 'innovator' - just that a natural
COBOL progression MAY have followed from the Apple/Micro Focus association.

Taking his cue from Smalltalk, Steve Jobs would probably also have pushed the
OOP approach - result - COBOL with OO as an integral module, two decades ago.


        COBOL    - Mainframe                   : 1, 800,000               25.12
%
                          - PCs/Unix                    :
3,033,333                42.32 %
        Other languages including Java         : 2,333,333               32.56 %

                                                                ------------
        Total                                                :
7,166,666            100.00%

Dammit ! Today I wouldn't bemoan the fact that COBOL tuition is disappearing in
Calgary, rather, I would see recurring articles in the local paper where kids
were griping about the 3 year waiting lists to get into school and learn COBOL.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

i have an exam tomorrow and i don't know how i can put a decimal value into
a variable with ACCEPT.

the picture of the variable should be 9V9 if i want to use numbers between
0.0 and 9.9 isn't it?

when i enter 3.5 the value in my variable is 0.0.  why is this.  thank u for
any help, i'm really in trouble.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Decimal values

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-12T21:57:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YewV6.273744$sP6.15235077@news3.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<bQrV6.29648$mR5.3518368@afrodite.telenet-ops.be> <_FsV6.5505$pb1.213974@www.newsranger.com> <fUvV6.30912$mR5.3660832@afrodite.telenet-ops.be>`

```
Compiler errors, along with memory bit-rot, should be the last thing
to worry about.

Editing symbols (as in 9.9) are used to format output, not input
(except, sometimes, with exotic compilers).

It is up to you to convert the user's input to something useful. In
your case:

01  USER-VAL        PIC X(5).
01  COMPUTER-VAL    PIC 9(3)V99.

ACCEPT USER-VAL.
COMPUTE COMPUTER-VAL = FUNCTION NUMVAL(USER-VAL).

Of course, this fails if the user types "ASSHO"

try the following....


w-s

    value-to-display    pic 9.9.


procedure

    move value-to-accept to value-to-display
    display 'value entered was:' value-to-display

#### ↳ Re: Decimal values

- **From:** Barry Steinholtz <Bazza@OptOnline.Net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-12T21:57:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B26903B.F5357CCE@OptOnline.Net>`
- **References:** `<bQrV6.29648$mR5.3518368@afrodite.telenet-ops.be>`

```
Hi:

     Your problem is that anything coming in from ACCEPT must be treated as
alphanumeric, then within the program changed back to numeric. In other words,
your 3.5 is taking 3 physical places with the decimal point. You'll have to
figure out the rest for yourself.

Barry Steinholtz

CyberWizzard wrote:

> Hello,
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> any help, i'm really in trouble.
```

## Thread: [OT] ViaSoft Materials? — 2 msgs

### [OT] ViaSoft Materials?

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2001-06-12T17:28:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vjsV6.9410$DW1.382701@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```

All righty... I've just found out that, buried in a sub-menu's sub-menu,
my present client has ViaSoft's Existing Systems Workbench (ESW).

I haven't used this in a half-dozen or more years but I recall that it
raises productivity by reducing debugging time by an order of magnitude or
so... and, of course, the client is unwilling to acknowledge the fact that
such a valuable tool has been allowed to lie fallow and will not allocate
any time/budget for training.

Might someone be able to offer suggestions as to where I might find a
'Teach-Ur-Self-ViaSoft' cheat-sheet and do some of my own homework?  I've
been able to allocate my AKR and compile a program... but beyond that I am
at an impass.

Thanks much!

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: [OT] ViaSoft Materials?

- **From:** "James" <mangogrower@telocity.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-12T21:17:34-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GdzV6.3$p7.27836@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`
- **References:** `<vjsV6.9410$DW1.382701@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
TSO Command:
PRODINFO
will list the Viasoft releases
TSO LISTALC and save the list
searcht it for *.Viasoft.*

these will be the Clists/Rexx and Panels

usually you can execute
the Clist/Rexx mbrs: Insight for the analysis,
                                Smartest for the debugger
                                Sm*Ed* for the Viasoft Editor

Once you find the Viasoft Clist/Rexx, browse thru for
ESW or Existing or WorkBench

Once you get into it, you can use the online help!
If you need more info after these steps, let me know

#### ↳ Re: Print preview

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-13T14:00:12+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b26ca4d_1@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<9g5scf$8uk$1@venus.telepac.pt>`

```
I use OLE automation to send the document to MS WORD. It can then be print
previewed, printed, FAXed, e-mailed, or whatever the user wants, with NO
effort on my part and without re-inventing the wheel...the system has a Word
Processor, why duplicate this functionality in COBOL?

Use the same facility to do renewal letters for Membership. Wrote a couple
of MS WORD macros, set up a template for monthly Membership renewals and
another for Yearly Membership renewals. COBOL program uses SQL to select
Members who are due for renewal within User selected period and load them to
an ACCESS table which is defined as a Data source to MS WORD Mail Merge. OLE
Automation is invoked to start WORD and run the Mail Merge... User can
easily amend text of templates or even individual letters, preview, FAX,
e-mail etc. as above. All letters end up on the WORD print queue and print
in the background while the system is doing something else...

Works for me...<G> (And the users LOVE it...)

All of the above is easily "doable" from PowerCOBOL Version 5.

Pete.

<a id="t-4a12ae013f"></a>
## Thread: 10 Reasons Why JAVA is better than cobol — 153 this month / 155 total

### 10 Reasons Why JAVA is better than cobol

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2001-06-12T23:09:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010612190911.12598.00000033@ng-mh1.aol.com>`

```
1.  JAVA can read a string as an integer with the elegant
    int iVar=Integer.valueOf(stringVar).valueInt;
    cobol has the boring
    03 xVar                pic XXX.
    03 iVar redefines xVar pic 999.
B-o-r-i-n-g.  

2.  JAVA has a wide assortment of methods to read input data--InputStream,
    FileInputStream, FilterInputStream, readChar, readChars--just to name
    a tiny handful.  
    cobol has nothing but one miserable little READ verb.  
B-o-rrr-inggg. 

3.  JAVA reads data the way it is supposed to be read--one byte at a time. 
    cobol lumps everything into one monolithic monstrosity called a "record." 
Boring.  Boring and primitive.  

    
Ross
http://www.geocities.com/ross_klatte/
```

#### ↳ Re: 10 Reasons Why JAVA is better than cobol

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-13T00:28:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B26B457.5EC09EF7@home.com>`
- **References:** `<20010612190911.12598.00000033@ng-mh1.aol.com>`

```


Ross Klatte wrote:

> 1.  JAVA can read a string as an integer with the elegant
>     int iVar=Integer.valueOf(stringVar).valueInt;
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Of course you are being deliberately provocative - but BS anyway ! (loved your
tongue in cheek abut Brits abusing the American language <G>).

Now math is not my great skill, but 1, 2 and 3 - where are the other seven ? To
save unnecessary repetition - ever heard of OO COBOL - OK your current compiler
doesn't have it, it is not as yet an 'official' part of the standard but is
available from Fujitsu, Merant(Net Express), IBM (Visual Age) and if you wanna
take a trip, Hitachi - but they only currently sell to the Japanese market. And
believe me pardner - it WORKS! FAST !

And regarding fast - I continuously read about the JVM and its performance. You
gotta make an executable to make it compare to COBOL.

Don't know about you buster - I like things in Innglish - you know the Brit type.
To me your so called 'elegant' Java is absolute gibberish. I'd be dabbling around
the British Museum studying Egyptology if I was enthralled about cryptography.
Plain English - plain communication.

>
> 2.  JAVA has a wide assortment of methods to read input data--InputStream,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I don't use - but since DOS days Micro Focus handled byte stream files. How many
ways do you want to read, reminds me of "Fifty ways to leave your lover".
As you say Java, many ways - what you didn't mention, does it have the self
contained feature read PrimeKey or Alternate Key or Split-Key.

I can't think of an occasion when I'd want to readChar (one byte). Are we
comparing apples with apples - in COBOL we are talking about real world business
applications.

If you MUST HAVE your various read methods, absolutely nothing to stop you
writing COBOL methods to do that.

> 3.  JAVA reads data the way it is supposed to be read--one byte at a time.
>     cobol lumps everything into one monolithic monstrosity called a "record."
> Boring.  Boring and primitive.
>

"Supposed to be read" - who said so. Where's your argument the the following can
be addressed more efficiently in Java :-

   01 Readings-record.
        05 PrimeKey etc...
        05 occurs 10.
            10 Reading occurs 24 pic 9(02)v9(03),

Read record using PrimeKey - whammo - I've got it all back in one instruction. OK
adjust that to rows using DB and ESQL - but parallel result.

4. Don't like some of the verbosity associated with OO COBOL. Net Express has the
class Vocabulary, kinda functions like the copyfile :-

        replace ==(tag)== by ==xyz==.

If I *really* disliked using :-

    invoke ThisObject "get-abc" returning thisthing  *>(either text or object)

Having defined the vocab 'exchange' I could substitute :-

    makeACall ThisObject "get-abc" returning thisthing *> (either text or object)

Compiler does a look-up and substitutes word 'invoke'.

Agreed - COBOL and Java DO compliment one another and certainly some are using
Java to front-end COBOL business logic with Java GUIs. (You shouldn't overlook
the current complaints by Java developers - it has unnecessary bloat to handle
GUI-ng). I'm not in the least anti Java - I just LIKE COBOL and it does the job !

Ross, with a surname like 'Klatte' you must be thinking Double Dutch. Get outta
here - it aint April 01 yet !

Jimmy, Calgary AB










>
> Ross
> http://www.geocities.com/ross_klatte/
```

I'm not an apologist for Cobol, but I find it hilarious that:

1)  A chief complaint about math languages, from a cobol
     perspective, is their tendancy to propagate rounding errors.

2)  You think 10 = 3.

"Ross Klatte" <klatteross@aol.commmm> wrote in message

#### ↳ Re: 10 Reasons Why JAVA is better than cobol

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-13T13:50:03+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b26c6b5_2@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<20010612190911.12598.00000033@ng-mh1.aol.com>`

```
Very droll...

Shame there are 7 reasons missing...

Here they are:

4. Java is innately Object Oriented and requires no Repository.

5. Java extends Classes in a simple logical way. (although it can only
inherit from ONE parent Class at a time; unlike OO COBOL which supports
multiple inheritance. Funny, I've never needed multiple inheritance and NOT
having it simplifies the whole OO inheritance model. Guess If I did need it
I'd use C++....)

6. Java is less verbose than COBOL and much quicker to write. It is also
easier to test and debug (mainly because there is much less of it...) and
requires NO Run Time fees to distribute.

7. Java runs on ANYTHING including the washing machine and the toaster...(if
you have a smart toaster).

8. Java is not held back by a moribund Standards committee who will take 10
years to pontificate over what should be in it.

9. People who use Java can actually participate in the development of the
language on-line and directly to Sun's site.  Java Programmers can even
contribute Classes for inclusion with the next release of the Language (and
they do...)

10. Java can be used to build components (Beans) which will run on Clients,
Servers, or the Web. The software development kit to do this is FREE and SO
is the Run Time. (cf with COBOL...)

I could go on...<G>

more below...


"Ross Klatte" <klatteross@aol.commmm> wrote in message
news:20010612190911.12598.00000033@ng-mh1.aol.com...

>>>
> 1.  JAVA can read a string as an integer with the elegant
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
<<<
Unfair comparison! The COBOL code which purports to be equivalent simply
defines the data. The Java code actually loads iVar...
In both cases the resulting data conversions are transparent to the
Programmer.

I could also achieve the above with the following Java statement:

int iVar = string2num.convert(String)

The true COBOL equivalent would be:

invoke string2num "Convert" using String
                                                            iVar
end-invoke

...where "string2num" is a Component I wrote which does a lot more than the
simple numeric conversion effected by the COBOL MOVE...

The point is, are the above two examples REALLY so different?

>>>
> 2.  JAVA has a wide assortment of methods to read input data--InputStream,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> B-o-rrr-inggg.
<<<
Are you suggesting that having a "wide assortment of methods to read input
data" is actually a DISADVANTAGE for a computer programming language? A
curious take on it...

>>>
> 3.  JAVA reads data the way it is supposed to be read--one byte at a time.
>     cobol lumps everything into one monolithic monstrosity called a
"record."
> Boring.  Boring and primitive.
>
<<<
COBOL also supports Byte Stream input (at least Merant and Fujitsu  versions
do...I'm not getting into a discussion over "valid COBOL...<G>)

The File Stream I/O in Java is a Programmer choice and, if you use it, it
can be used to load "structures" ("records" in COBOL speak...).

While I realise your post was tongue  in cheek, Ross, there are many
"threatened" COBOL programmers who will see denigration of the perceived
"opposition" as a more comfortable path than actually sitting down and
expanding their skill set by learning Java.

I have never, and do not currently, advocate Java as a REPLACEMENT for
COBOL. It is a COMPLEMENT to COBOL. BOTH of these languages will be required
in the toolbox of the future.
(I have covered all this at length elsewhere so I won't go into it again
here.)

I strongly recommend COBOL programmers to get into Java, mainly for the
following reasons:

1. The marketplace of tomorrow will not support the "One Language Wonder"
mentality. ("I can do everything I want to do in COBOL..."). If you intend
to be a Professional Programmer you will need to have the best tools for the
job. COBOL is the best tool for solving commercial business data processing
problems; it is NOT the best tool for enabling certain other aspects of the
new technology.

2. New methods and approaches to commercial business data processing may
render even COBOL unnecessary (except maybe "behind the scenes"). Component
based technology will enable non-technical Business Users who never
programmed anything in their lives, to quickly and easily build commercial
systems using just iteration, interaction, and Business knowledge.

3. Java is a "Fast Path" to understanding Object Orientation. OO is going to
figure large in everybody's future, whether you like it or not... Pick up
this knowledge now, and the quickest way to do it is to learn Java. You can
then easily extend the newly acquired concepts into OO COBOL AS WELL as
Java.

4. Like COBOL, Java is fun to use and it looks good on a CV, costs you
nothing (apart from time and effort - and not too much of that...<G>), and
it provides insight into another way of programming from the one you are
used to.

There should NOT be a CONTEST between Java and COBOL. It's like the saw
insulting the hammer because it doesn't know how to cut... When you need to
nail something it takes much longer hitting it with a saw than if you have a
hammer handy...

I love COBOL and have done for 35 years. However, it is not a blind
love...<G> My experience is richer for having acquired Java skill.

If anyone reading this is persuaded, even slightly, by the above, then I
would recommend the following starting points:

1. "Java Made Simple" by Peter McBride (ISBN 0-7506-3241-0)

A very good introduction to the basic concepts (including OO) of Java.

2. "SAMS Teach Yourself Java in 24 Hours" by Rogers Cadenhead (ISBN
0-672-31630-7)

This is sprinkled with a wicked sense of humour which makes the book
extremely entertaining as well as informative.

Either or both of the above wll give a COBOL progammer a really good
grounding  and appreciation of Java. Having covered them, you might then
move on to the 1000 page "Java2 Complete" which is really invaluable for
advanced OO programming.

"The BEST tools for the Job..." That's what professional programming and
Component building are all about...

I would be very happy to receive private mail with your experiences on using
the above, and will gladly help if you are stuck on any points.

Pete.

"Ross Klatte" <klatteross@aol.commmm> wrote in message
news:20010615054623.11466.00003852@ng-mp1.aol.com...
> >From: "William M. Klein" wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com

> It's easy to see why computer geeks,
> mathemeticians, academics, etc. prefer object orientation to
> sequential procedure orientation.

Personally, I'm in "none of the above" yet I prefer OO because of its
logical elegance and eloquence. That doesn't mean that I would "evangelize"
OO...(no need to...it will take over the World without any help from me). I
see it primarily as the essential tool to build components with.

I would NOT suggest that the people using sequential procedures are "wrong"
(and I still code a certain amount using this paradigm), but I would argue
that OO is better for certain purposes. (It just so happens that these
purposes are very dear to my heart...<G>)

> But it mystifies me that
> a large number of reputedly level-headed businessmen have
> gone along with that view.

Why? There are proven cost benefits from the proper application of OO.

> It is, as you say, pointless to
> compare a jackhammer with a chainsaw, at an absolute
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I suggest that you are talking about some short-sighted Managers who believe
everything they read and are dedicated followers of fashion because they
don't know any better... Your true hard headed businessman runs a pilot
project and evaluates it for himself. He then either accepts or discards it.
The kind of Manager who simply goes with whatever is "cool" in this week's
edition of "Computer Weekly" cannot last long...


> Anyway, I have figured out that I can write my entire Java
> program as a single switch / case statement with forty-leven
> break label's, so I don't even need to abandon my GO TO
> mentality, if I don't want to.
>

Ah, I'll work an evaluate into my sample code, just to make you feel at
home...<G>

> P.S.  I have been studying Java for 4 months now, and I
> still don't understand the "this."  Any enlightening tips?
>
Well, "enlightenment" is a big ask, but let's take the first faltering steps
towards Nirvana <G>....

First off, you are reading the wrong books if it isn't clear after four
months... See my suggestions for reading elsewhere in this thread...

"this" in Java serves exactly the same purpose as "self" in OO COBOL.
("POW-SELF" in PowerCOBOL, which is intrinsically Object Oriented.)

It means "use the object we are currently in". Another way to look at it is
"pass this object as the subject of the Method I am invoking".

Here's a Java example:

Java uses the delegation model to handle Event processing. Events are passed
from source controls to LISTENER objects. That means you will connect a
"listener" to the control that raises the event (e.g. a button click). When
an event occurs, the "Listener" object will "hear" it.  You do this by
invoking the "addActionListener()" method. This method belongs to the
control which raises the event (in this case, a button). But what if the
object you want to be the "Listener" is the actual Applet you are writing ?
You will not necessarily have an Object Reference to the current object (in
this case the Applet).

So, you use "this", which is like an implicit Object Reference to the
current object.

Having written the Java code to add the button to your Applet, you then
invoke the addActionListener method and pass "this" as the reference. Like
this...

public void init()
    ...
    button1 = new Button("Click Me!");
    add(button1);
    button1.addActionListener(this);
    ...
From now on whenever button1 is clicked, your Applet will be notified. How
you actually action the event or tie it back to whichever control raised it,
is beyond the scope of this post, but it involves overriding the
ActionListener interface's ActionPerformed method.

Here's an OO COBOL example, for comparison:

In OO COBOL we only really use "self" when we wish to specify a Method which
is LOWER (or equal, but then it is redundant) in the inheritance hierarchy.

consider 3 classes: ClassA, ClassB, and ClassC

ClassC inherits from ClassB which inherits from ClassA. (This is very useful
for combining "common" elements of a Class and then "extending" it to
accommodate specific extensions. ClassA would define the basic attributes,
ClassB would extend this, and ClassC would extend it even further.)

A concrete example?

ClassA: Pets
Attributes: species, name, colour
Methods: New, GetSpecies, GetName, GetColour, SetSpecies, SetName,
SetColour, FeedIt

ClassB (inherits ClassA): Haslegs
Attributes: numberLegs
Methods: Groom, GetNumberLegs, SetNumberLegs

ClassC (inherits ClassB): Dogs
Attributes: registration, breed
Methods: GetRegistration, SetRegistration, GetBreed, SetBreed, Roll Over,
Fetch, Walkies, Speak

Now let's say that Dogs which are NOT registered must not be groomed...

I am coding the "Groom" Method in ClassB (HasLegs).  I need to know if what
I'm grooming is a dog, and if it is, whether it is registered...

*> This looks UP the hierarchy...
invoke SUPER "GetSpecies"  returning local-species
end-invoke

evaluate local-species
    when  'dog'
*> This looks DOWN the hierarchy...
         invoke SELF "GetRegistration" returning local-reg
         end-invoke
         if NOT registered
            display "unregistered dogs cannot be groomed, sorry..."
            exit method
        else
           display "nice doggie...stroke stroke..."
        end-if
     when 'horse'
            display "curry comb... curry comb...easy boy..."
     when other
           display "stroke...stroke...don't bite me...stroke... stroke"
end-evaluate
exit method

Note that the HasLegs Class does NOT have EITHER the 'GetSpecies' or
'GetRegistration' Methods...they are provided by inheritance, and 'SUPER'
and 'SELF' control the "direction" of the inheritance search.

(the Java equivalents are 'SUPER' and 'THIS')

Another important use of SELF and SUPER is in cases where the inheritance
hierarchy has several methods  with the same name (quite possible with the
polymorphic nature of OO). It is necessary to "qualify" which one you want
(especially important if there is a like named method both above and
below...)

I am pleased to see you extending your skill base by learning Java.
Persevere with it; it is different to COBOL, and even OO COBOL. Try not to
be too quick to write off things which don't work the way you might
expect... I am learning more about both languages every day, and many of the
things I thought bizarre at first are now part of accepted practise...

There is a good base of OO expertise available through this NG and most
questions should get answered.

I hope the above has clarified "this" for you.

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 10 Reasons Why JAVA is better than cobol

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-15T12:05:20-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B2A4E60.5294064B@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<9gbvri$v9e$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <20010615054623.11466.00003852@ng-mp1.aol.com> <3b2a3e70_3@Usenet.com>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

> But it mystifies me that

> > a large number of reputedly level-headed businessmen have
> > gone along with that view.
>
> Why? There are proven cost benefits from the proper application of OO.

Each choice also has some increased costs as well.  Some times the benefits of
OO outweigh its costs - but it hasn't been demonstrated that this is the case in
an enterprise business environment.

Of course things change.  Right now, there is no way any enterprise I have been
involved with will accept changing a component without testing everything which
uses that component (with real live tests).   Since testing is a high cost item
with the trend for it to get higher, we don't find large enterprises putting
business rules into objects.   Things which don't matter so much, such as a GUI
button on a screen are a different matter.  Not much system testing is
required.  And if the system is small enough that one person can know it, the
testing is more manageable.

And not allowing one to modify components is not generally considered "the
proper application of OO".

An exception may come from a company building an integrated business environment
for sale.  If J.D. Edwards creates an OO product and sells it to a thousand
companies, then they can have a permanent staff doing rigorous testing of the
system.  System testing from adding an integrated component is no greater than
what is happening anyway for isolated components.  The benefits of integration
for a product which has to adjust to a lot of customers are easier to see and
measure.  In this environment, there really are no significant increased testing
costs.  So the cost/benefit ratio of going OO are different again.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 10 Reasons Why JAVA is better than cobol

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Grinder" <no.spam@no.spam.spam.spam.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-15T23:54:50-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kBBW6.12199$I6.259641@e420r-atl1.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<9gbvri$v9e$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <20010615054623.11466.00003852@ng-mp1.aol.com> <3b2a3e70_3@Usenet.com> <3B2A4E60.5294064B@brazee.net>`

```
> Each choice also has some increased costs as well.  Some times the
benefits of
> OO outweigh its costs - but it hasn't been demonstrated that this is the
case in
> an enterprise business environment.

I'm currently engaged in the upkeep of two applications.  One
is procedural Cobol, the other, Visual Basic.  On a weekly basis
I see the benefit of VB's OO-ish approach over the flat style of
the Cobol application.

If a bug is found, it is most often easy to find in the VB application.  A
strict organization of object responsibility allows for the likely bit to
code
immediately suggest itself.  This is a direct result of allowing code
to be organized by what it does, instead of when it does it.

Functions and procedures, methods and properties compartmentalize
code further--into short, specific chunks of logic.  It's easier to
comprehend
ten 20 line routines, than one 200 line program.  Indeed this comparison
is imbalanced, as it takes significantly less OO code than its inlined
counterpart.

For bug fixes and enhancements alike, code revisions are generally
centralized--you don't have to hunt through lots of source code to look
for a pattern to amend.  You can centralize Cobol code with copybooks,
but we've found that name collision limits this approach.

> Of course things change.  Right now, there is no way any enterprise I have
been
> involved with will accept changing a component without testing everything
which
> uses that component (with real live tests).

I agree that being able to do this is a luxury, but one that can be afforded
if you maintain a static (or at least expanding) interface to your objects,
you can regularily change internals without breaking suscribing
applications.

At a minimum, a growth organization should be driven to make bug fixes,
even if they're not engaged in development.  OO makes this easier.

>                                                               Since
testing is a high cost item
> with the trend for it to get higher, we don't find large enterprises
putting
> business rules into objects.

I think this is a goal worth striving for.  If you can distill your business
logic
into a concentrated set of code, with suitable interfaces, you have an edge.
You can keep your market response peppy, while allowing the infrastructural
components--like the GUI and data layers--to shake-out.
__________________

OO is more complex, but it's not a sophistication you escape with procedural
code--it just wanders into the relationships between your applications and
libraries.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 10 Reasons Why JAVA is better than cobol

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-16T06:15:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B2AFA19.F3156790@home.com>`
- **References:** `<9gbvri$v9e$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <20010615054623.11466.00003852@ng-mp1.aol.com> <3b2a3e70_3@Usenet.com> <3B2A4E60.5294064B@brazee.net> <kBBW6.12199$I6.259641@e420r-atl1.usenetserver.com>`

```


Grinder wrote:

> > Each choice also has some increased costs as well.  Some times the
> benefits of
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well Howard he sure makes a good case for OO and he's talking VB. He certainly
addressed your 'maintainability mantra'. Mr."Grinder", if you can get your hands
on an OO COBOL compiler - you should be ecstatic.

> You can centralize Cobol code with copybooks,
> but we've found that name collision limits this approach.
>

Within OO COBOL I find copybooks are an extremely  useful feature - but
'collision' doesn't occur because you can have your copybooks either 'Global
(W/S) or 'Localized' to a method :-

    Class :
    *>-----------------------
    Working-storage section.    *> "Global"
    copy 'mycopy.cpy' replacing ==(tag)== by ==WS==.
    *>-----------------------
    method-id. "mymethod".
    *>-----------------------
    Working-storage section.
    copy 'mycopy.cpy' replacing ==(tag)== by ==WS==.
    Local-storage section.
    copy 'mycopy.cpy' replacing ==(tag)== by ==LS==.
    Linkage section.
    copy 'mycopy.cpy' replacing ==(tag)== by ==IN==.
    copy 'mycopy.cpy' replacing ==(tag)== by ==OUT==.

    Procedure Division using IN-rec returning OUT-rec.

Note the 'Local' W/S entry currently available in Net Express will NOT be a part
of the new standard. The rest is.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Emergent OO Cobol Standard

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-06-17T16:48:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b2cdf2c.323418015@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<9gbvri$v9e$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <20010615054623.11466.00003852@ng-mp1.aol.com> <3b2a3e70_3@Usenet.com> <3B2A4E60.5294064B@brazee.net> <kBBW6.12199$I6.259641@e420r-atl1.usenetserver.com> <3B2AFA19.F3156790@home.com> <j9TW6.52119$r4.457444@e420r-atl3.usenetserver.com>`

```
None of the vendors OO extensions match the draft standard.  Some are
close, but none have all the features of the draft, and some have
things that we have since disallowed.


On Sat, 16 Jun 2001 19:53:13 -0500, "Grinder"
<no.spam@no.spam.spam.spam.net> wrote:

>> Well Howard he sure makes a good case for OO and he's talking VB. He
>certainly
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 10 Reasons Why JAVA is better than cobol

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-16T07:20:36-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B2B5D24.D8F90EA9@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<9gbvri$v9e$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <20010615054623.11466.00003852@ng-mp1.aol.com> <3b2a3e70_3@Usenet.com> <3B2A4E60.5294064B@brazee.net> <kBBW6.12199$I6.259641@e420r-atl1.usenetserver.com>`

```


Grinder wrote:

> > Each choice also has some increased costs as well.  Some times the
> benefits of
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> the Cobol application.

It could be.   The advantages of OO are certainly more manifest in the types of
environments where a Visual Basic solution is possible, while the disadvantage
that I mentioned does not apply.  But why use an OO-ish approach when OO CoBOL
is available?    The only time I did extensive work in an OO-ish language it was
Paradox for windows.  It was the hardest thing to debug that I have ever worked
with, with each piece of code separate from the rest.  That separation had no
corresponding advantage that I could see (such as having a debugged component in
a library.


> If a bug is found, it is most often easy to find in the VB application.  A
> strict organization of object responsibility allows for the likely bit to
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> comprehend ten 20 line routines, than one 200 line program.

This argument applies just as well to structured CoBOL.  OO has other advantages
which count for more.


> Indeed this comparison is imbalanced, as it takes significantly less OO code
> than its inlined counterpart.

I have to ask people who use OO CoBOL - how true is this statement?


> For bug fixes and enhancements alike, code revisions are generally
> centralized--you don't have to hunt through lots of source code to look
> for a pattern to amend.  You can centralize Cobol code with copybooks,
> but we've found that name collision limits this approach.

I have seen and used the exact opposite argument.   For bug fixes and
enhancements, old type CoBOL code is centralized - except for copybooks, data
dictionaries, and calls everything is in one place - the program!  The more you
spread out the source, the less centralized it is, and the harder it is to hunt
through lots of source to look for the code which applies.


> > Of course things change.  Right now, there is no way any enterprise I have
> been
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> applications.

I don't think testing is a luxury.  Maybe that's why enterprises haven't
switched to OO, as my belief is more common in enterprise environments than
yours is.

> At a minimum, a growth organization should be driven to make bug fixes,
> even if they're not engaged in development.  OO makes this easier.

It could be.  But the bigger the system, the higher the testing component of
these bug fixes.

> OO is more complex, but it's not a sophistication you escape with procedural
> code--it just wanders into the relationships between your applications and
> libraries.

Which means, we have more dependencies.  In a typical enterprise shop, we have
lots of semi-duplicated code.   One customer wants almost the same report as
another customer, so we clone a program.   Keeping two similar programs is
costly, and the OO people are right to point to that cost.   But we don't need
to get the first customer to sign off on maintenance done to the second
customer's program.  If a business rule changes for one customer, we don't have
to figure out a generic way to make it different for that customer - we simply
change that customer's program.

As I keep saying - each choice we make has some costs and some benefits.   The
choice we make for one environment isn't always the same choice we would make in
a different environment.  And as costs change, environments change and we should
re-evaluate our decisions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 10 Reasons Why JAVA is better than cobol

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2001-06-16T14:35:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010616103510.11216.00001731@ng-ce1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3B2B5D24.D8F90EA9@brazee.net>`

```
>From: Howard Brazee howard@brazee.net 
>Date: 2001-06-16 09:20 Eastern Daylight Time
>Grinder wrote:

>> For bug fixes and enhancements alike, code revisions are 
>> generally centralized--you don't have to hunt through lots of 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>source to look for the code which applies.

A colleague of mine who had half a dozen years in COBOL and
now has half a dozen years in Java emphasizes that
well-written and well-documented code in Any-language-X is
easier to maintain than badly-written and non-documented 
code in Any-language-Y.  But assuming the code is badly
written and undocumented (and if it were otherwise, why
would they be getting 200 per hour for him?), then Java is
more difficult to maintain than COBOL.  He says, "Everything's
scattered all over to hell and back."  

Is it easier to find a needle hidden in a haystack, or to find a 
needle hidden in one of ten thousand tablespoons filled with
hay?  If you can use a book of matches and a magnet, it
will be probably be quicker to find it in the haystack.  




Ross
http://www.geocities.com/ross_klatte/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 10 Reasons Why JAVA is better than cobol

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-16T20:15:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B2BBF3D.4936AC27@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3B2B5D24.D8F90EA9@brazee.net> <20010616103510.11216.00001731@ng-ce1.aol.com>`

```


Ross Klatte wrote:

> A colleague of mine who had half a dozen years in COBOL and
> now has half a dozen years in Java emphasizes that
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> will be probably be quicker to find it in the haystack.

Ross,

There really is no mystery to this. You and I can sit down and write a
program, Structured COBOL, OO COBOL, VB,  Java whatever. They are all
just tools. We can use them effectively or make a complete balls-up.

It has to start with DESIGN. What are the overall objectives of the
application and where does our particular 'piece' fit in. Because of
wishy-washy user input or poor EDP leadershp, ('IT" I believe is now the
politically correct term),  an application has the potential to be a
non-starter, even before somebody starts writing the first line of code.

Whether or not you are using non-OO or OO languages the 'problem' still
has to go through the same set of hoops, and the same analysis techniques
apply. Because of OO methodology, without question you have much more
flexibility in terms of reusable components (methods). But it gets back
to how is the design structured.
Part of that process of design for OO has to be, that you MUST come up
with a sensible hierarchy of classes, picking on logical groups of
actions, calling them super classes with variations suborned as
'sub-classes'.

If you don't figure out a logical structure for these units, which you
are constantly able to refer to (invoke), then agreed you finish up in a
maniacal haystack. (One of my regrets - don't see real haystacks around
any more - used to love sliding down them as a kid).

Does that imply I've figured it out. No sir !. Because the COBOL OO biz
is so new, without models to work from, there are several occasions where
I have back-tracked to arrive at a more meaningful and more easily
understood structure of classes. Don Tees who is using Fujitsu could also
confirm that he has done the same thing.

You can take a crack at OO COBOL in two ways, only use OO classes, or use
OO classes combined with 'Structureds'. Purely for consistency of coding
style I opted for the OO class approach. In my case it has paid off; the
style in effect allowing me a 'reusability' approach when viewing
disparate operations - if you like, different Business actions called
from a main menu.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 10 Reasons Why JAVA is better than cobol

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-18T07:03:13-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B2DFC11.EC022DB7@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<3B2B5D24.D8F90EA9@brazee.net> <20010616103510.11216.00001731@ng-ce1.aol.com> <3B2BBF3D.4936AC27@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:

> There really is no mystery to this. You and I can sit down and write a
> program, Structured COBOL, OO COBOL, VB,  Java whatever. They are all
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> non-starter, even before somebody starts writing the first line of code.

True - but it depends on what is being designed.   If you are designing an
application, your criteria are pretty straightforward.  (although even there
are legacy and skill level issues) here When you design a system, you have
some new considerations.  It is a tough call to determine how integrated of a
system you want.  Sharing components has its upsides and its downsides.
These very important considerations may not be part of the analysis of an
application, except as being standards established by the system design.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 10 Reasons Why JAVA is better than cobol

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Grinder" <no.spam@no.spam.spam.spam.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-16T19:29:13-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<D5TW6.51821$r4.456816@e420r-atl3.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<9gbvri$v9e$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <20010615054623.11466.00003852@ng-mp1.aol.com> <3b2a3e70_3@Usenet.com> <3B2A4E60.5294064B@brazee.net> <kBBW6.12199$I6.259641@e420r-atl1.usenetserver.com> <3B2B5D24.D8F90EA9@brazee.net> <3b2b9cdd.240894940@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
> >> Indeed this comparison is imbalanced, as it takes significantly less OO
code
> >> than its inlined counterpart.
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> if you had changed all of your code to a series of CALL statements.

I see on distinction between objects and sub-programs--data.  An object
is built with the idea that it's suscriber is using to maintain data with
its
associated methods.

Subprograms, operate on data, but must be specially structured to
maintain data between series of calls that should not be predictable by
that subprogram.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 10 Reasons Why JAVA is better than cobol

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Grinder" <no.spam@no.spam.spam.spam.net>

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:3B2DFD15.66B22646@brazee.net...

Sure--it applies to business objects most easily.  In a 3-tiered structure,
