###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 10 Reasons Why JAVA is better than cobol

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Editor" <Editor@aboutlegacycoding.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-19T11:15:51-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9gnqau$30p4$1@allday.news.cais.net>`
- **References:** `<sUSV6.21342$JS1.1187767@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <20010613195619.04650.00000284@ng-ba1.aol.com> <RT2W6.28799$JS1.1721545@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9gar20$pms$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <3b295d3d_3@Usenet.com> <9gl487$ott$1@allthetime.news.cais.net> <rXAX6.87537$4f7.7100565@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Russell,
I'm sure that the .JPG's that a fine upstanding citizen like you were
looking at were on the alt.fan.johnwayne newsgroup right? *grin*

I don't think it's a matter of different newsgroups so much as the content
of that news group.  Administrators can set the number or days or the number
of bytes of stuff that a news server holds.  And they can set this by group.
So many of them will hold 7 or 10 days of stuff in mostly text based groups
but only 3 days in the binary groups.

However, the larger problem is file size.  A text only message like this one
only takes a few k.  A binary like a jpg takes 20- 100k or more.  So the
chance that a bad packet or dropped line will interrupt a large jpg is many
times greater than for a text message.
```

per newsgroup.  Some newsgroups will go over 500 in less than a
day.
Other groups can have messages last 3 or 4 months.

   Disk space is so cheap now that it looks as if some ISP's
simply
are not paying attention.  The newsgroup service seems to be an
afterthought.
For what it is worth, if you don't need to do any long unattended
downloads,
and don't care about that @#$% always on top ad window, the ATT
4.95
offer is good.  The ATT Worldnet newsgroup support is excellent.
Pity about
the busy signals.

   I am not affiliated with ATT.


<snip>
```

Thanks Buddy.

I appreciate the explanation.

I believe nevertheless that NG messages are echoed from server to server. As
my server is in New Zealand (or at least, one of them is) and the Usenet
server is in North America, you will forgive my induction that these echoes
occur "around the World"...<G>

From your own, and some other posts, I readily accept  your contention that
you in the USA are probably no better off than the rest of us who aren't...

There's a kind of cold comfort in that...<G>

Pete.

"Editor" <Editor@aboutlegacycoding.com> wrote in message
news:9gl487$ott$1@allthetime.news.cais.net...
> Pete,

in
> N/A have any special privlidges.
>
> NTTP protocol, like HTML protocol is not "guaranteed delivery."  With
HTML,
> if the net is extremely busy, or you are down a poor connection, or some
> jerk with a backhoe dug up part of the backbone, then you get 404 errors
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> With news messages (NTTP protocol), as messages are relayed between
servers,
> if the net is slow or a packet gets damaged, etc. the message simply
doesn't
> get through.
>
> The quality of a news server (i.e. how many messages you see) is sometimes
a
> function of how close to the backbone and how good their peering
connections
> are.  Up until last month I was on a news server on PSI (who recently went
> Bankrupt).  I was lucky if I saw 10% of the messages in the group.  I know
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> web based systems are better.  For example http://www.etin.com/ I
frequently

>

the

#### ↳ Re: 10 Reasons Why JAVA is better than cobol

- **From:** kanoako@netnet.net (Kano Ako)
- **Date:** 2001-06-15T14:09:13-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<677e6c81.0106151309.100dbf8e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<20010612190911.12598.00000033@ng-mh1.aol.com>`

```
klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte) wrote in message news:<20010612190911.12598.00000033@ng-mh1.aol.com>...
> 1.  JAVA can read a string as an integer with the elegant
>     int iVar=Integer.valueOf(stringVar).valueInt;
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> B-o-r-i-n-g.  

Costs you a function call.


> 
> 2.  JAVA has a wide assortment of methods to read input data--InputStream,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> B-o-rrr-inggg. 

I have not used a cobol read in a long time or can I even remember the
last time I saw one.


> 
> 3.  JAVA reads data the way it is supposed to be read--one byte at a time. 
>     cobol lumps everything into one monolithic monstrosity called a "record." 
> Boring.  Boring and primitive.  

Wrong!  Byte by byte reading is primitive, record reading is more
natural.

It is more common that applications require groups of bytes rather
than single byte by single byte.  So with byte by byte reading one
must collect bytes  until the information unit is completely gathered.
 In fact I consider the innate ability of COBOL to define, redefine
and breakdown records to be its strongest feature!

Anyway I recognize you as a troll since your standard of a good
language seems to be that which is not boring vs that which is boring.

Anyway
KA
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 10 Reasons Why JAVA is better than cobol

- **From:** kanoako@netnet.net (Kano Ako)
- **Date:** 2001-06-18T15:48:39-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<677e6c81.0106181448.2cad2151@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<20010612190911.12598.00000033@ng-mh1.aol.com> <677e6c81.0106151309.100dbf8e@posting.google.com> <3B2A9614.57CAA129@home.com>`

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message news:<3B2A9614.57CAA129@home.com>...
> Kano Ako wrote:
> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> your applications where you only WRITE - not even a measly REWRITE <G>

Uhh in my work the preferred method of writing and reading sequential
datasets is via GSAM, i.e. using IMS to write and read.  This way if a
job abends then it is easy to re-start the job where it left off.  In
any event at the moment I am doing online (via IMS as well).  It  has
been months since I have done a read or a write, even when I was
working on batch programs.  In fact the guy who would do the
production JCL would get on my case if he saw my jobs coming in with
the cobol standard read & write.

ttyl
KA
```

The "exclusive" use of GSAM (even in an IMS-only shop) to the exclusion of
QSAM and VSAM (which implement "standard" COBOL I/O) is unusual - from my
experience.  I suppose it COULD be done as a "shop standard" - or at least
for one application, but I (personally) have never heard of this actually
happened before.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 10 Reasons Why JAVA is better than cobol

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-21T02:21:08+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b30b18a_3@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<20010612190911.12598.00000033@ng-mh1.aol.com> <677e6c81.0106151309.100dbf8e@posting.google.com> <3B2A9614.57CAA129@home.com> <677e6c81.0106181448.2cad2151@posting.google.com> <3B2E99E1.42DD5589@home.com> <9go2b6$lok$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3b301fea.82106209@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
When IMS first started making its presence felt (early to mid-70s) it was
common practice to define existing VSAM datasets as GSAM databases so they
could be accessed hierarchically sequentially with IMS.

It was intended as a "conversion" aid and NOBODY expected the GSAM databases
to continue, once applications were converted to "full" IMS.

We invented our own mnemonics for these things...These were dutifully
expanded with a straight face to new trainees... here are the ones I
remember (and that are printable...)

BTAM =  Bloody Terrible Access Method
QTAM =  Quietly Treacherous Access Method
VTAM =  Very Tricky Access Method
TCAM =  The Cat's Attacking Me
BDAM = Bloody Dangerous Access Method
QDAM = Quite Disastrous Access Method
(Logic tells me there MUST have been a VDAM, but I don't remember using it.
I have used and/or programmed all the others in this list...)
BSAM = Blatantly Silly Access Method
QSAM = Quite Silly Access Method
VSAM = Very Silly Access Method
  ISAM =  Incredibly Silly Access Method
GSAM =  Goddamned Stupid Access Method
 KSDS=  Keep Still Don't Struggle
 ESDS=  Extremely Stupid Data Storage
 RRDS=  Relatively Risky Data Shredding

(Makes PC ISAM look a bit anaemic, doesn't it...<G> Guess that's why I moved
to RDB...)

Pete.

> common practice to define existing VSAM datasets as GSAM databases
so they
> could be accessed hierarchically sequentially with IMS.
>
> It was intended as a "conversion" aid and NOBODY expected the GSAM
databases
> to continue, once applications were converted to "full" IMS.
>
> We invented our own mnemonics for these things...These were
dutifully
> expanded with a straight face to new trainees... here are the ones I
> remember (and that are printable...)
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> (Logic tells me there MUST have been a VDAM, but I don't remember
using it.
> I have used and/or programmed all the others in this list...)
> BSAM = Blatantly Silly Access Method
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> (Makes PC ISAM look a bit anaemic, doesn't it...<G> Guess that's why
I moved
> to RDB...)
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

converting
> > that "stuff" to something usable.  I thought it was something the
> > people at xxxxx (censored) invented themselves.  Are you telling
me
> > GSAM is a real animal?  These people used the stuff quite a LOT
from
> > Batch and CICS programs.
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > >The "exclusive" use of GSAM (even in an IMS-only shop) to the
exclusion
> of

from my

at

actually

REWRITE

> > >> > Uhh in my work the preferred method of writing and reading
sequential
> > >> > datasets is via GSAM, i.e. using IMS to write and read.  This
way if
> a
> > >> > job abends then it is easy to re-start the job where it left
off.  In
> > >> > any event at the moment I am doing online (via IMS as well).
It  has
> > >> > been months since I have done a read or a write, even when I
was
> > >> > working on batch programs.  In fact the guy who would do the

in with
> > >> > the cobol standard read & write.

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

#### ↳ Re: 10 Reasons Why JAVA is better than cobol

- **From:** chammond@lc.cc.il.us
- **Date:** 2001-06-20T20:09:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9gqvtg$q2o$1@news.netmar.com>`
- **References:** `<20010612190911.12598.00000033@ng-mh1.aol.com>`

```
In article <20010612190911.12598.00000033@ng-mh1.aol.com>, Ross Klatte
<klatteross@aol.commmm> writes:
>1.  JAVA can read a string as an integer with the elegant
>    int iVar=Integer.valueOf(stringVar).valueInt;
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>    cobol lumps everything into one monolithic monstrosity called a "record."

>Boring.  Boring and primitive.  
>
>   
Java reads data however the file reader you choose reads the file.  In java
you can choose to read one byte at a time if you choose, however that doesnt
do you much good when the data is in text.  Java can read text one character
at a time also.  Then when you have data that is comma separated you may be
better off reading one line at a time if it is a file with the carriage
returns in the  file.  For this you need to read one line at a time.  Of
course you left off buffering.  If you use a BufferedReader you can read data
faster than one byte at a time.  You can also read data one Byte Array at a
time which is even faster in Java.  Reading data one byte at a time is very
innefficient and wastes processing Cycles.  Only an rank Newbee Java
Programmer who wants to slow down their computer would read a file in this
manner. 
>Ross
>http://www.geocities.com/ross_klatte/
>
>

  http://newsone.net/ -- Free reading and anonymous posting to 60,000+ groups
   NewsOne.Net prohibits users from posting spam.  If this or other posts
made through NewsOne.Net violate posting guidelines, email abuse@newsone.net
```

## Thread: Copy PDS to Sequential File (Fujitsu Mainframe) — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Copy PDS to Sequential File (Fujitsu Mainframe)

- **From:** liptakj@hotmail.com (John Liptak)
- **Date:** 2001-06-12T17:08:16-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9db5eaf6.0106121608.3362130d@posting.google.com>`

```
Is there a utility on Fujitsu that will allow me to copy members of a PDS
 (or the entire PDS) to a sequential file.  The target file must be created
 as FB 80.

I tried sort but it does not liek a PDS as input.  I tried a "LIBE" utility 
but that creates a file type of VB with record length of 84.  I dont want 
an 84 byte VB dataset because I always have trouble reading VB datasets in
cobol programs.

Any suggestions?.
```

…[12 more quoted lines elided]…

Are you trying to copy from the mainframe to a PC?  If so, look at
IND$FILE.  Should be on your PC and Host.  Alows you to do exactly
what you're wanting to do.  It is not part of Fujitsu.

#### ↳ Re: Copy PDS to Sequential File (Fujitsu Mainframe)

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-06-16T15:05:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b2b7581.230817037@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<9db5eaf6.0106121608.3362130d@posting.google.com>`

```
John - don't laugh.  I've done this a bunch.

Use FTP!

Say the PDS is called my.pds

FTP and do a 

CD 'MY.PDS'

Then do a 

MGET *

and each PDS member will be FTPed to your local machine, each as a
separate file.

## Thread: COBOL FAQ — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2001-06-14T11:34:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers,alt.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_992518442@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2000/07/02
Version: 3.01
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Sunday, July 2, 2000

The COBOL FAQ has moved.  To view the most recent version, please check out the
web version at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send private
email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Recorded new location of the FAQ
 o Updated some old (bad) web links
 o Added some new COBOL books (particularly those written or contributed to - by
frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).
 o Added information about LegacyJ (PERCobol) and changed the company name from
"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".
 o Updated information for changes in product and company names and addresses
where available
 o Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
 o Added links to Xinotech
 o Added information on Norcom and GUI ScreenIO
 o Added (back) information on Wang
 o Added (back) information on the Tiny-COBOL ('74 Standard) project
```

#### ↳ Re: newbie

- **From:** "Ron" <Ron@NoSpamForMe.Com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-14T16:57:49-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9gbc10$n2a$1@sshuraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<01c0f4d7$99ffad70$120a17ac@HKTGV0005161251>`

```
05  DP   PIC 99.    (data-pointer)
05  MY-FIELD PIC X(11).
05  MY-STRING PIC X(5).

PERFORM VARYING DP FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL DP > 7
  IF MY-FIELD (DP:5) = MY-STRING
     DISPLAY 'I FOUND IT OR WHATEVER YOU WANT TO DO"
  END-IF
END-PERFORM

#### ↳ Re: newbie

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-14T19:40:05-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9gbhnd$ogo$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<01c0f4d7$99ffad70$120a17ac@HKTGV0005161251>`

```
03  WS-FIELD PIC X(11).
03  WS-STRING PIC X(05).
03  WS-SUB PIC S9(04) BINARY VALUE +1.
03  WS-INDEX PIC S9(04) BINARY VALUE ZERO.

PERFORM UNTIL WS-SUB > (LENGTH OF WS-FIELD - 4)
           IF  WS-FIELD (WS-SUB:LENGTH OF WS-STRING) = WS-STRING
                MOVE WS-SUB TO WS-INDEX
                COMPUTE WS-SUB = (LENGTH OF WS-FIELD - 3)
           ELSE
                     ADD +1 TO WS-SUB
                     END-IF
           END-PERFORM.

IF WS-INDEX > ZERO "Then you found the String" and WS-INDEX contains the
field-position.

I'm sure I'll get flack on this one.... ;o)

HTH....

Bill

news:01c0f4d7$99ffad70$120a17ac@HKTGV0005161251...

> x(11) and I need to know if that field contains a certain 5 character

I just found out that UltraEdit will print color.  It is one of the

like UltraEdit as a programmer's editor.  If not, I can always use it for

I'll also save your message and may try it as well.  I saw the following
reviews, but haven't yet seen how much this shareware costs:


    Description
    SynEdit is a text editor that has customizable syntax highlighting
profiles, a clip text browser, code templates, and
    command-line compiler support. It can open files of unlimited size, and
can load up to 255 documents at once. SynEdit is ideal
    for independent coders and students using non-IDE programming languages.
Version 2.3 adds HTML formatting options,
    enhances toolbars, and fixes several bugs. Version 2.4 allows the user to
display SynEdit in the system tray, capture MS-DOS
    compiler output, and has support for PHP3 and ASP syntax highlighting.


    CNET User Opinions

    87%   13%   (230 votes)
      Would you recommend this product? Yes No



YES      Joseph Tate, jtate@ads.REMOVEcom
    "Quick, easy to use customizable and extendable text editor."
    The emacs of the Windows world: This Programmers editor color codes your
C, C++, Perl, Java, HTML, and just about any
    other language for you. If support is not already there, just add
another. (I did one for InstallShield's script language in about 3
    minutes). It has a handy set of editing features including keyword lists,
which you can double click to insert into your text. It
    also has limited support for regular expression searches. It reads both
DOS and UNIX linefeeds, and can output text to both
    formats. It can run commandline compilers, and has a ton of other
features. It is a great tool. At the very least, you should get it
    to replace Notepad.


NO      Dan B.
    "Just another one......."
    Perhaps the only strong side of this editor is coloring support for so
many programming languages and tag display accordingly.
    Unfortunately, after UltraEdit32 this editor leaves to be much desired,
not only in the features it lacks, but in the features it
    implements very poorly.

<a id="t-aba6668e21"></a>
## Thread: Problem calling PL/1 modules in LE — 2 msgs

### Problem calling PL/1 modules in LE

- **From:** gandr37@my-deja.com (gandr)
- **Date:** 2001-06-15T07:58:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<47784e12.0106150658.64a9be8d@posting.google.com>`

```
Greetings,
  We have an IBM mainframe with compiler PL/I for MVS & VM Ver 1 Rel 1
Mod 1. We have the situation below we are unable to resolve and could
use any ideas or clues. This is a batch non-multi tasking process.

Main module A (COBOL) dynamically calls PL/1 modules D and E. Module E
dynamically calls module F (assembler). This flow runs successfully.

A change was made that now main module A dynamically calls modules B
and C which are both COBOL. Module B dynamically calls PL/1 module D
from above and module C dynamically calls module E from above. When
this flow is run we get the following abend on the first call attempt
in module E to module F.

IBM0576S ONCODE=3915  An attempt to use a CALL statement with the
TASK, EVENT, or PRIORITY option was found in a non-tasking
environment.

When the call to module D is removed from module B, this flow runs
fine. It's as if the call to this first PL/1 module (D) is influencing
the call structure in module E.

Again, any ideas, hints, or clues are appreciated.
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem calling PL/1 modules in LE

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2001-06-15T17:42:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010615134210.19503.00002105@ng-fk1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<47784e12.0106150658.64a9be8d@posting.google.com>`

```
gandr37 writes ...

>Greetings,
>  We have an IBM mainframe with compiler PL/I for MVS & VM Ver 1 Rel 1
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>Again, any ideas, hints, or clues are appreciated.

You didn't say, but I presume all the COBOL modules (A, B, and C) are all
compiled with some COBOL compiler that is LE-enabled (i.e.: COBOL for MVS & VM
or COBOL for OS/390 & VM).

In the "Writing InterLanguage Communication Programs" there are two points that
may be of interest:

  * If a COBOL program is to call a PL/I program dynamically, the outermost
    proc in the PL/I  program should include FETCHABLE in the OPTIONS list

    [I know that sometimes you can get away without this, but
     it is not supported]

  * When a load module is dynamically called, control passes to an entry point
    identified as FETCHABLE unless the load module also contains a MAIN
    procedure [presumably not the case here], in which case control passes
    to the MAIN procedure

you should also link the dynamically called PL/I routine to have its entry
point to be the same as the module name.

Hope this helps.

<ad>
You might be interested in some training along these lines. In particular, we
have two courses that address the subject of your post:

Application Development Using LE Services
      Link to course description on our website:
http://www.trainersfriend.com/m212descr.htm

InterLanguage Communication in OS/390
      Link to course description on our website:
http://www.trainersfriend.com/m220descr.htm

</ad>

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

## Thread: Power Cobol — 3 msgs

### Power Cobol

- **From:** Carlos Lages <clages@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-15T16:39:10-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B2A645E.F2F53BD0@attglobal.net>`

```
Hi folks

Does anybody knows how can i do  in Power Cobol  the following:

I have a Forms1 that shows a List of Client (in this forms I have a
ListView) only ID and Name, them I want to use the UP and DOwn Arrows
and Each Line I pointer
Shows All the CLients Informations  in FORMS2. (Address, Phone, Zip
Code, etc)

Is it Possible

Any help is welcome
Carlos Lages
```

#### ↳ Re: Power Cobol

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-15T20:38:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B2A72F3.DCC8F9E5@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3B2A645E.F2F53BD0@attglobal.net>`

```


Carlos Lages wrote:

> Hi folks
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Code, etc)

I've no doubt Pete Dashwood will tell you this is NOT the way to do it
with Fujitsu, but here's pseudo code as I do it in Net Express.
(Substitute your appropriate GUI instructions).

Firstly the full screen :-

        <------------- record layout ------------>    <-------- Listbox
---------->

        A/C #
.....                                                        Smith, Bill
.....

Name....                                                           Smith,
Gracie

Address                                                            Smith,
Harry

etc.............
Smith, Herman

1. Display empty screen. If you already have a list, display in the
Listbox.
2. User has two choices :-
    2a. Select from Listbox - if user has selected an element from your
Listbox
            use the key (A/C #) to display record contents in the Record
area.
    2b. Other option - user is entering a new record so puts in new A/c #
-
          if blank - just re-display empty Dialog. If existing key,
display record from
         your file - suggest you also 'make sure' it shows in your
Listbox
3. No existing record - prompt user to fill in fields. Then this goes to
Step 4.
4. 'New" or existing - accept changes or even 'Deletes'
5. OK or Cancel :-
    5a. OK         if RecordChanged

                            if Record Found
                                write
                            else rewrite

        Make sure changes/additions/deletions are reflected in your
Listbox

6. Go back to # 1 until user signals EXIT.

I have no idea of your F/J coding - but at least above is a fairly
generalized model.. And for reusability you can repeat it over and over
again.

If possible go for three tier system design - i.e. separate
programs/classes :-

(a)Business Logic - EditCustomers
(b) DBI (Data Base Interface) your file/DB handling, and
(c) UI (User Interface) - your dialog(s)

Couple of notes :

When I say display empty screen I mean 'empty record layout'. You should
're-show' what exists in your listbox.

Listbox design - I prefer to keep it small - just what is pertinent to
guide the user, i.e. the name and A/c #. Selecting from Listbox gets me
the PrimeKey(A/C # ) which I then use to access full record details from
file/DB.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Power Cobol

- **From:** "Paulo Vieira" <pvieira@emporsoft.pt>
- **Date:** 2001-06-19T10:30:20+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9gn6fj$d3a$1@venus.telepac.pt>`
- **References:** `<3B2A645E.F2F53BD0@attglobal.net>`

```
There is an example of that in the "Samples" folder of your Power COBOL
installation.
In order to get 2 modeless dialogs that comunicate with each other, you have
to
1)Develop A.dll (your List) in a separate project as a OO module; make shure
you code some event to notify the other form that a record key was selected.
2)Develop your "Details" form (B.EXE or B.DLL) and include A.DLL in it as an
ActiveX control, unchecking the "Visible" property of the control. At the
"Opened" procedure of B.DLL code an 'invoke ??? "Activate" returning
ReturnValue' and voil� the first module pops in a separate window.
3)The next thing is to code a method in B.DLL to populate the "Details"
form. That method shoul be invoked from the event generated by A.DLL when a
customer is selected.
I'll leave to you the details of figuring out how to detect that the user
select an item in A.DLL. If you use "Editable Label", then the control fires
an event when the user presses "enter" over an item of the ListView.
Feel free to email me if there is any trouble. I have done this a lot a
times, so I'm shure it works.

Regards,
Paulo Vieira in Lisboa

http://www.emporsoft.pt
mailto:pvieira@SEM_ISTO.emporsoft.pt

"Carlos Lages" <clages@attglobal.net> wrote in message
news:3B2A645E.F2F53BD0@attglobal.net...
> Hi folks
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: Problem calling PL/1 modules in LE — 2 msgs

### Re: Problem calling PL/1 modules in LE

- **From:** gandr37@my-deja.com (gandr)
- **Date:** 2001-06-18T04:44:33-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<47784e12.0106180344.37abd69@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<47784e12.0106150658.64a9be8d@posting.google.com> <20010615134654.19503.00002108@ng-fk1.aol.com>`

```
Thanks for the reply, Steve. I was certain there was something I
should have included in my original email but didn't.

Everything you requested we try, we've already tried. We have
FETCHABLE in our options list plus adding REENTRANT had no positive
effect. We also already have the actual module name of the PL/I
programs as the entry points. Lastly, the COBOL compiler we are using
is COBOL for MVS and VM 1.2.1.
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem calling PL/1 modules in LE

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2001-06-18T12:23:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010618082304.09359.00001659@ng-fh1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<47784e12.0106180344.37abd69@posting.google.com>`

```
Hmmm. Well there are only three things I can think of at the moment:

1. Linking (binding) your modules: check module attributes, especially entry
point, reusability, AMODE and RMODE; no use of DLL linkages

2. There may well be a compiler bug (sounds most likely in the PL/I compiler or
PL/I runtime component of LE); these don't happen all that frequently, but they
do happen, and the circumstances you are describing seem unusual enough that
there may not have been testing to cover that case.

3. Perhaps there is some runtime setting that is getting in the way; I would
check these (and I don't have any particular reasons, they just seem to be ones
that might influence the behavior you are seeing): ALL31; ERRCOUNT - must be 0
for PL/I apps; PLITASKCOUNT(n) - try either 1 or 0 for 'n';

Just some guesses because I don't really have any more intuitions based on your
information. Good luck.

Kind regards,

-Steve Comstock
The Trainer's Friend, Inc.

Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

#### ↳ Re: Registry...

- **From:** "Grinder" <no.spam@no.spam.spam.spam.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-18T20:51:52-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bdyX6.35866$Ne5.2054975@e420r-sjo3.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<UcnX6.2365$_96.93473@news3.oke.nextra.no>`

```
You can walk the registry to find this, but any one component
is likely a collection of class ids.  For instance, if you want to
know if BestOfWare's SmartUI is installed, look in

HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT for BOWSmartUI.SmartUI.  That means
that this name is registered.  If COM is asked to instance an
object of this type, it will first come here.  It will grab the
CLSID (from the CLSID subkey) and use that GUID to lookup
details of the object's implementation.

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Is there something special I should be aware of when reading the
"(Default)"
> tag ???

>
```

John

John,

The SYSTEM.EXE was not included in RM/COBOL v2.01.  I believe it was first
released with v5.30.00. For grins I tried calling the 5.30 system.exe from a
2.01 runtime and it crashed my DOS box.

You may have to write a assembly language program to do this or upgrade to
v7.00.03 for 32-bit windows.

-Robert Heady

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: rmcobol and dos commands

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-19T22:45:31+01:00

- **References:** `<3b2e1957$0$231@hades.is.co.za> <20010618202744.03410.00000699@nso-cb.aol.com> <7j3uitk217o3ciluvbr22nfk6nef7bn7p2@4ax.com> <3DOX6.114$Xh7.5194@nnrp1.sbc.net>`

the "system" executable was before 5.3.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: rmcobol and dos commands

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-06-19T12:19:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b2f42e0.25511688@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<3b2e1957$0$231@hades.is.co.za> <20010618202744.03410.00000699@nso-cb.aol.com>`

```
One interesting note on this.  I have a client going for Windows
Certification with his software.  In order to get "Platinum"
certification you can't use CreateProcess in your application.  The
"Preferred" way according to MS is to use the ShellExecute API.  The
only problem I have with that is that there seems to be no way to
"wait" for a process to complete before returning to the spawning
process.  With CreateProcess I can use a "monitoring" call to wait for
the process to complete before continuing.


On 19 Jun 2001 00:27:44 GMT, bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536) wrote:

>Hi John,
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: rmcobol and dos commands

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 2001-06-21T01:03:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010620210318.03823.00000003@nso-cq.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3b2f42e0.25511688@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Hi Thane,

I use the Win32API "WaitForSingleObject" to wait until the batch file or
whatever program I have executed to complete.  Is this the monitoring call that
you are talking about ?

Thanks,
Bob
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: rmcobol and dos commands

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>

It looks like ShellExecuteEx returns a process handle in the
ShellExecuteInfo Structure. I haven't tried this, but perhaps this handle
can be used with WaitForSingleObject to do "wait for completion without
using CreateProcess".

Maybe I'll test that later today. I really don't feel like working, anyway.
```

waitforsingleprocess, so it won't work.  Cheesle sent me an idea to
use this API then wait for "something" idle.  I don't have the message
here with me right now to give you the exact details.

ShellExecuteEx which are what is "not allowed" by the Platinum

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu Cobol 3.0

- **From:** gregor.schmidt@gmx.net (Gregor Schmidt)
- **Date:** 2001-06-18T09:36:24-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1ec08887.0106180836.568e646@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,

I have a big problem with FSC 3.0 and access to database via ODBC.

I wanted to compile the Sample10. Therefore I made project
(sample10.prj) and  installed an ODBC datasource to an Access Database
( an Oracle DB too).
Then I used the sqlodbcs tool to create an ODBC information file.

When I compile the project it comes the following output
C:\PROGRAMME\FSCOBOL\PCOBOL32\SAMPLES\SAMPLE10\SAMPLE10.OBJ 
C:\PROGRAMME\FSCOBOL\PCOBOL32\PROJECT.RES 
C:\PROGRAMME\FSCOBOL\PCOBOL32\F3BICIMP.LIB 
C:\PROGRAMME\FSCOBOL\PCOBOL32\LIBC.LIB 
C:\PROGRAMME\FSCOBOL\PCOBOL32\KERNEL32.LIB 
C:\PROGRAMME\FSCOBOL\PCOBOL32\USER32.LIB 
C:\PROGRAMME\FSCOBOL\PCOBOL32\SAMPLES\SAMPLE10\SAMPLE10.OBJ : fatal
error LNK1136: invalid or corrupt file
NMAKE : fatal error U1077: 'C:\PROGRAMME\FSCOBOL\PCOBOL32\LINK.EXE' :
return code '0x22'
Stop.
(You see I'm on a german system ;-) )

I don't know what's wrong.
It's correct that the OBJ is corrupt ( it has a length of 0 bytes ),
but why?
Has somebody an idea or can send me a correct info file?
```

## Thread: How to Call a Dos .bat file as a subprogram — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### re: How to Call a Dos .bat file as a subprogram

- **From:** "Wayne Chen" <agricus@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-19T03:04:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GizX6.315046$oc7.28195390@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com>`

```
How to Call a DOS batch file as a subprogram?
```

#### ↳ Re: How to Call a Dos .bat file as a subprogram

- **From:** "Grinder" <no.spam@no.spam.spam.spam.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-18T22:29:35-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<HPzX6.49093$u34.1983404@e420r-sjo2.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<GizX6.315046$oc7.28195390@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com>`

```
Using the COMSPEC environment variable, concatenate
a command like this:  (assumes COMSPEC=c:\windows\command.com)

c:\windows\command.com /c c:\myfile.bat

This works fairly well, but may have issues if the batch
file has prompts in it.

"Wayne Chen" <agricus@home.com> wrote in message
news:GizX6.315046$oc7.28195390@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com...
> How to Call a DOS batch file as a subprogram?
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: reading a data file in Windows — 2 msgs

### reading a data file in Windows

- **From:** "Harry" <harry_scott@DELETETHISBITlineone.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-19T16:53:20+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9zKX6.595$rk5.5600@NewsReader>`

```
I have a data file that I need to use on a Windows PC.
I am told that the file was created using Cobol, is in ASCII format and is a
sequential file.
Is there any software that I can use to convert this file so that I can use
it in Windows.
At the moment there are non-printable characters which mean that the layout
is no good.

Thanks

Harry
```

#### ↳ Re: reading a data file in Windows

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-19T09:59:36-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B2F84F8.1771@paralynx.com>`
- **References:** `<9zKX6.595$rk5.5600@NewsReader>`

```
Harry wrote:
> 
> I have a data file that I need to use on a Windows PC.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> it in Windows.

Take a look at ParseRat (http://www.parserat.com/)

> At the moment there are non-printable characters which mean that the layout
> is no good.

It probably means that there is binary data as well as text.  ParseRat can convert 
binary data, too.
```

<a id="t-2bcbb032ef"></a>
## Thread: RM/Cobol losing cursor — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### RM/Cobol losing cursor

- **From:** "Hi-Tech Software, Inc." <hitech@kynd.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-19T12:59:27-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9go06g$nrl$1@news.chatlink.com>`

```
Hello Ladies and Gentlemen of the Jury;

I am hoping someone has run into this similar situation and can shed some
light or point us in a direction.

We have a client running an NT network, with about 200 workstations all
running Windows 98.  They are running the text based version of our software
using an RM/Cobol 85 Ver. 5.36 Runtime.  Up until last week everything was
running smoothly.  They tell me nothing has changed on their end and I know
that we haven't changed anything, but all of a sudden when they are doing
data entry, the cursor disappears from the screen.  They can type and hit
the Enter key and the program will proceed to the next field, it just isn't
obvious where they are on the screen and makes it rather hard for input.

Has anyone ever heard of something like this happening before?

We have contacted RM.  They searched their data base and the only thing they
could come up with was to reconfigure the runtime with a TERM-INTERFACE DOS
and then set DEVICE=ANSI.SYS in the CONFIG.SYS.  I tried that on one of our
systems here but when I tried to run a program the runtime didn't handle the
windowing aspects of the program and wigged out the display.  (You could see
the program amongst the hex-values, but I think we would get some
complaints)

I am still not convinced that it has anything to do with our system.  I
believe that they loaded something on the server and pushed it down to the
workstations, but they are still telling me they haven't changed anything.

Any suggestions or help would be greatly appreciated.

Bill
Hi-Tech Software, Inc.
```

#### ↳ Re: RM/Cobol losing cursor

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-19T19:55:09+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8h7vit0e9t9g73g58k87eu8gaj1bq62qnm@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<9go06g$nrl$1@news.chatlink.com>`

```
On Tue, 19 Jun 2001 12:59:27 -0400, "Hi-Tech Software, Inc."
<hitech@kynd.net> wrote:

>Hello Ladies and Gentlemen of the Jury;
>

>We have a client running an NT network, with about 200 workstations all
>running Windows 98.  They are running the text based version of our software
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Has anyone ever heard of something like this happening before?
Yes.


Being a company of this size I will assume they have some kind of
Virus product installed in each and every PC.

A possbile upgrade of this software (which is normally done
automatically) will be one of the possible causes of this problem.

If this is happening in all workstations, and every single day then I
would ask them to disable some or all of this Software options in one
Workstation (of someone they know will not try to introduce a Virus,
or that will not be sugject to external access (email, Web access)).
and see if it still happens.

Apart from this it can happen if you are doing CALL SYSTEM to execute
some other programs and one of this has been changed or doesn't have
all "requirements" available any more.


Sorry if I can not be more helpfull, but these type of things are
always hard to figure out.

cheers

Frederico Fonseca
Memosis
Liant technical support (and distributors) in Portugal
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: RM/Cobol losing cursor

- **From:** merhottin@aol.com (Merhottin)
- **Date:** 2001-06-20T00:07:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010619200733.12461.00000131@ng-ma1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<8h7vit0e9t9g73g58k87eu8gaj1bq62qnm@4ax.com>`

```
Frederico made a good point.  We just upgraded our virus software, and found
problems that interfered with other programs.  While the upgrade did not effect
our COBOL programs, they did effect other programs.  We actually have to
disable the virus protection to run some applications.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b2fd989_1@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<3b2fa007$1@dnewserver.firstcom.cl>`

```
Use comp-2 with no picture.

it is a 64 bit floating pointing point number with the date as days since
1900 to the left of the decimal, and the time as a fraction of a day in
seconds to the right.

Pete.

## Thread: Routine to convert text to mixed case — 12 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### Routine to convert text to mixed case

- **From:** "Brian Cromwell" <blcromw@landsend.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-20T09:56:51-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9gqdjj$7uq$1@grandcanyon.binc.net>`

```
I apologize if this is not the purpose of this newsgroup, but I am looking
to see if there is a common routine that exists to convert character data
from all upper case to mixed case (such as using the Microsoft Word function
Change Case to change selected text to Title Case).  We are converting DB2
data that has previously been used in a CICS application for use by an
internet application, and currently, all of the data is in upper case.  Any
pointers on a quick way to do this would be helpful.  Writing a COBOL
program to do this is fairly simple, but if there is a public routine that
is already available, that would cut some time off of development.

Thank you,

Brian Cromwell
```

#### ↳ Re: Routine to convert text to mixed case

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-20T08:50:10-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B30C632.242@paralynx.com>`
- **References:** `<9gqdjj$7uq$1@grandcanyon.binc.net>`

```
Brian Cromwell wrote:
> 
> I apologize if this is not the purpose of this newsgroup, but I am looking
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> is already available, that would cut some time off of development.

Try ParseRat (http://www.parserat.com/) it can convert to mixed case, while 
intelligently handling "capitalization exceptions" like McDonald.
```

#### ↳ Re: Routine to convert text to mixed case

- **From:** "Grinder" <no.spam@no.spam.spam.spam.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-20T12:32:51-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<775Y6.19475$w4.382733@e420r-atl3.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<9gqdjj$7uq$1@grandcanyon.binc.net>`

```
Sorry I can't offer you code to do this, but wanted to exend a couple
of extra cents I've collected in doing this.

Shifting from upper to proper case is a pretty good idea, but is not
as easy as it would seem.  Ed pointed out case exceptions, like in
McDonald that make this difficult.

The special capitalization cases depend intimately upon the nature
of the data being converted.  Names have a number of specific
conditions, as do addresses.  110 NW Congress Ave, for instance
would have the NW capitalized--a considerating that's not very
reasonable for name conversion.

Descriptive fields are even worse.  I recently converted a number
of medical procedure descriptions.  It was so jammed full of
acronyms, that the resultant text was barely better than the
original.  I ended up having to build a lookup list to properly adjust
these terms, but even that had it's limit.  (ie, is CAT an acronym,
abbreviation or domesticated pet?  You just can't tell from the
text alone.)


"Brian Cromwell" <blcromw@landsend.com> wrote in message
news:9gqdjj$7uq$1@grandcanyon.binc.net...
> I apologize if this is not the purpose of this newsgroup, but I am looking
> to see if there is a common routine that exists to convert character data
> from all upper case to mixed case (such as using the Microsoft Word
function
> Change Case to change selected text to Title Case).  We are converting DB2
> data that has previously been used in a CICS application for use by an
> internet application, and currently, all of the data is in upper case.
Any
> pointers on a quick way to do this would be helpful.  Writing a COBOL
> program to do this is fairly simple, but if there is a public routine that
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Routine to convert text to mixed case

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-20T11:20:46-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B30E97E.6ECC@paralynx.com>`
- **References:** `<9gqdjj$7uq$1@grandcanyon.binc.net> <775Y6.19475$w4.382733@e420r-atl3.usenetserver.com>`

```
Grinder wrote:
> 
> Sorry I can't offer you code to do this, but wanted to exend a couple
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> McDonald that make this difficult.

It can get quite complicated.  A good test name is "IVAN MCDONALD IV"; if it doesn't 
give you "Ivan McDonald IV" your rules are not rigorous enough.

> The special capitalization cases depend intimately upon the nature
> of the data being converted.  Names have a number of specific
> conditions, as do addresses.  110 NW Congress Ave, for instance
> would have the NW capitalized--a considerating that's not very
> reasonable for name conversion.

Right.  You need to apply different rules for address fields than you do for names. Your 
conversion tool should permit that.

> Descriptive fields are even worse.  I recently converted a number
> of medical procedure descriptions.  It was so jammed full of
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> text alone.)

Absolutely true.  I don't know of ANY automated conversion engines which can make that 
distinction based on context alone.  With ParseRat you'd save a different preference 
profile for the circumstances in which you wanted CAT fully capitalized.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Routine to convert text to mixed case

- **From:** "Grinder" <no.spam@no.spam.spam.spam.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-20T14:28:55-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fM6Y6.25874$Nb.423914@e420r-atl2.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<9gqdjj$7uq$1@grandcanyon.binc.net> <775Y6.19475$w4.382733@e420r-atl3.usenetserver.com> <3B30E97E.6ECC@paralynx.com>`

```
> Right.  You need to apply different rules for address fields than you do
for names. Your
> conversion tool should permit that.

I do a lot of this, so I'm agreeable that it's a workable/profitable
approach.  Academically,
though, I like seeing if a problem can be pushed into a closed form
solution.  Meaning, in
this case, if I could write something 100% accurate that would not require
user input.

For names, for instance, I can construct a scenario that prevents this.
"AL" is a common
transliteration of an arabic name prefix--somewhat like "Mc," but more like
"DE" in
Marquis de Sade.  (The choice of example is almost entirely accidental.)
Since the name
acts as a conjunction, it's often lower-cased.

Note: I can escape this particular delimma at many points by making slightly
different
presumptions--like "Marquie De Sade" looks good to me.  But if I continue
with the nit-
picking...

Even by employing stop and/or common word lists, I can't tell if "AL" should
be lower-
or proper-cased.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Routine to convert text to mixed case

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-20T14:46:02-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9gquhc$v87$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Is it Dutch or German (or both) that distinguishes between "von" and "Von"
to show whether one is of "noble birth"?   I would love to see any
"automated" system figure that one out !!! <G>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Routine to convert text to mixed case

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-20T14:33:11-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B311697.3E56@paralynx.com>`
- **References:** `<9gqdjj$7uq$1@grandcanyon.binc.net> <775Y6.19475$w4.382733@e420r-atl3.usenetserver.com> <3B30E97E.6ECC@paralynx.com> <fM6Y6.25874$Nb.423914@e420r-atl2.usenetserver.com> <9gquhc$v87$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Which confirms the point that this is NOT a trivial task. In many cases even a human 
reading the text cannot make a positive determination. An automated system can go quite 
a way, but not the whole way.

It's like gender routines. If you are Johnny Cash's "Boy Named Sue", every automated 
genderizer will call you "Ms".

William M. Klein wrote:
>

Any

> program to do this is fairly simple, but if there is a public routine that
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
----

Here I have a routine. But you must correct manually "MC" or "van" etc. The
routine make all Character Uppercase after blank, comma, etc.

This is a routine specially for german.

Example

Input        "ERNST MAYER GMBH"
Output     "Ernst Mayer GmbH"

Input        "HUGO BOSS AG"
Output     "Hugo Boss AG"

Input        "PETER VON UND ZU TESTHAUSEN"
Output     "Peter von und zu Testhausen"

Hope this help.

Ralph Leiherr

**********************
Working-Section.
      *----------------------------------------------------------------*
      *      Umsetzen Buchstaben        - gross -> klein               *
      *                                 - klein -> gross               *
      *                                 - klein -> gross (ohne ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½)    *
      *----------------------------------------------------------------*
       77  Umsetz-VNAME-OK        Pic     9(01)        Value Zero.
       77  Umsetz-VNAME-1         Pic     X(02)        Value Space.
       77  Umsetz-VNAME-2         Pic     X(02)        Value Space.
       77  Umsetz-VNAME-3         Pic     X(02)        Value Space.
       77  UMSETZ-GROESSE         Pic     9(02)        Value Zero.
       77  UMSETZ-IND1            Pic     9(02)        Value Zero.
       77  UMSETZ-IND2            Pic     9(02)        Value Zero.
       77  UMSETZ-OK-BST          Pic     X(01)        Value Space.
         88  UMSETZ-OK                         Value "a" THRU "z",
                                                     "A" THRU "Z",
                                                     "ï¿½", "ï¿½", "ï¿½",
                                                     "ï¿½", "ï¿½", "ï¿½",
                                                     "ï¿½".
      *
       77  UMSETZ-OK-BST-DTAUS    Pic     X(01)      Value Space.
         88  UMSETZ-OK-DTAUS                   Value "A" THRU  "Z",
                                                     "0" THRU  "9"
                                                     " ", ".", ",",
                                                     "&", "-", "/",
                                                     "+", "*", "$",
                                                     "%".
      *
       01  Umsetz-Nam-Felder.
           02  Umsetz-Name.
               04  Umsetz-Nam-Teil Pic     X(01) Occurs 99 Times.
           02  Umsetz-Nam-Red      Redefines Umsetz-Name.
               04  Filler          Pic     X(01).
               04  Umsetz-Name-R98 Pic     X(98).
      *
           02  UMSETZ-NAME-N.
               04  UMSETZ-NAM-TEIL-N  Pic     X(01) OCCURS 99 TIMES.
      *
       01  UMSETZ-TABELLEN.
           02  UMSETZ-GROSS       Pic     X(29)      Value
               "ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZï¿½ï¿½ï¿½".
           02  UMSETZ-GROSS-RED   Redefines UMSETZ-GROSS,
                                  OCCURS 29 TIMES,
                                  ASCENDING KEY IS UMSETZ-GR-BST,
                                  INDEXED   BY     UMSETZ-IND-GR.
               04  UMSETZ-GR-BST  Pic     X(01).
      *
           02  UMSETZ-KLEIN       Pic     X(29)      Value
               "abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyzï¿½ï¿½ï¿½".
           02  UMSETZ-KLEIN-RED   Redefines UMSETZ-KLEIN,
                                  OCCURS 29 TIMES,
                                  ASCENDING KEY IS UMSETZ-KL-BST,
                                  INDEXED   BY     UMSETZ-IND-KL.
               04  UMSETZ-KL-BST  Pic     X(01).
      *
Procedure Division.

Move 25 to Umsetz-Groesse.                    (Lenght of String, max. 99
Character)
Move String to Umsetz-Name.                  (Input Parameter)
Perform Umsetz-Nach-Klein.                    (Call Subprogram)
Move Umsetz-Name to String.                  (Output Parameter)


      *----------------------------------------------------------------*
      *                 Umsetzen  Buchstaben                           *
      *                 --------------------                           *
      *                                                                *
      *    Parameter    1.)     UMSETZ-NAME         (max. 99 Stellen)  *
      *                 2.)     UMSETZ-GROESSE      (Feldlï¿½nge)        *
      *                                                                *
      *    Aufruf       - Perform UMSETZ-NACH-KLEIN                    *
      *                                                                *
      *    Das umgesetzte Feld steht nach dem Aufruf in UMSETZ-NAME.   *
      *----------------------------------------------------------------*
       UMSETZ-NACH-KLEIN SECTION.
       1.
           If  UMSETZ-GROESSE = Zero
               Move 25 to UMSETZ-GROESSE.
           If  UMSETZ-GROESSE > 01 AND < 99 AND UMSETZ-NAME NOT = Space
               Perform UMSETZ-K VARYING UMSETZ-IND1 FROM 2 BY 1 UNTIL
                                        UMSETZ-IND1 > UMSETZ-GROESSE.
           Perform KORR-UMSETZ.
           GO to 9.
       UMSETZ-K.
           Subtract 1 FROM UMSETZ-IND1 GIVING UMSETZ-IND2.
           Move UMSETZ-NAM-Teil (UMSETZ-IND2) to UMSETZ-OK-BST.
           If  UMSETZ-OK  AND
               UMSETZ-NAM-Teil (UMSETZ-IND1) NOT = Space
               SET UMSETZ-IND-GR to 1,
               SEARCH UMSETZ-GROSS-RED WHEN
                      UMSETZ-GR-BST   (UMSETZ-IND-GR) =
                      UMSETZ-NAM-Teil (UMSETZ-IND1)
                      SET UMSETZ-IND-KL to UMSETZ-IND-GR,
                      Move UMSETZ-KL-BST   (UMSETZ-IND-KL) to
                           UMSETZ-NAM-Teil (UMSETZ-IND1).
      *
       KORR-UMSETZ.
           INSPECT UMSETZ-NAME REPLACING ALL "mbh"    BY "mbH"
                                         ALL " Ag "   BY " AG "
                                         ALL " Kg "   BY " KG "
                                         ALL "Zu "    BY "zu "
                                         ALL "Und "   BY "und "
                                         ALL "Von "   BY "von "
                                         ALL "Fï¿½r "   BY "fï¿½r "
                                         ALL "Fuer "  BY "fï¿½r  "
                                         ALL "I.R. "  BY "i.R. ".
      *
       9.
           EXIT.
      *----------------------------------------------------------------*
```

<a id="t-f2617128ba"></a>
## Thread: Dynamically allocated files — 11 msgs

### Dynamically allocated files

- **From:** farlandjim@netscape.net (Jim Farland)
- **Date:** 2001-06-20T09:23:33-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<25aa81ec.0106200823.668a71a@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello;

Is it possible to dynamically generate files from within COBOL without
assigning DDNames in the JCL.   The files that are dynamically
allocated would not already be allocated on the Mainframe.

Here is an example of what we are trying to do:

1) Read a company table to determine all companies that need to be
processed
2) Read in an input file with data for all companies specified in the
company table.
3) Create unique output files with the input data specific to each
company from the company table.
4) Each new output file created would not be defined in the JCL with a
DDName, and would not previously exist on the Mainframe.


Can Cobol dynamically create files that were not previously defined in
the JCL executing the Cobol program?

Thanks for any help that can be offered in advance;

Jim Farland
```

#### ↳ Re: Dynamically allocated files

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2001-06-20T16:49:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010620124916.08410.00000203@ng-fw1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<25aa81ec.0106200823.668a71a@posting.google.com>`

```
Jim Farland writes ...

>Hello;
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks for any help that can be offered in advance;

Yes. You can issue TSO ALLOCATE commands from a COBOL program to dynamically
allocate files to ddnames that are declared in the COBOL program. So you would
allocate, then open and process, then close, then free.

Check out IKJEFTSR in the TSO programming services reference. Or schedule us to
come in and teach a class (Advanced TSO REXX covers that and more).

Hope this helps,



Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

Jim Farland

#### ↳ Re: Dynamically allocated files

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-20T13:07:33-05:00

Yes it is.

 Steve Comstock has given you the "traditional" ways of doing this in
another post.  However, if you have the "latest and greatest" COBOL compiler
(IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM - V2R2) - then there is an even "easier" (and
possibly more "COBOL intuitive" way to do this).

Check out,


http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igylr205/4.2.3.1.
1

and look for the section beginning,

"Environment variable format (OS/390 QSAM files)--DSN option"

This option was NOT (is not) available with any earlier release of IBM's
COBOL compilers (and is not available except on OS/390 "or above").
```

#### ↳ Re: Dynamically allocated files

- **From:** "Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-06-20T20:50:28+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9gqqtr$681$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<25aa81ec.0106200823.668a71a@posting.google.com>`

```
I use variables to assign file names with my MF WB 2.4 compiler
(SELECT fl-cust ASSIGN ws-fnme.). Is this not possible with
other compilers ?

"Jim Farland" <farlandjim@netscape.net> wrote in message
news:25aa81ec.0106200823.668a71a@posting.google.com...
> Hello;
>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jim Farland
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Dynamically allocated files

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-20T14:08:25-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9gqsbo$j2p$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<25aa81ec.0106200823.668a71a@posting.google.com> <9gqqtr$681$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Almost all PC compilers have a method of "allocating" new files (with file
names not known at compile-time) - this has NOT ever been a part of IBM
mainframe compilers.

In the draft Standard, there is a new format of the Select/Assign clause
(that has
   Select file-name USING variable-name

which is "similar" (but not quite identical) to how many/most PC
implementations work today.

The IBM mainframe implementations (old and new) are quite different.

This is an area of "Standard COBOL" that is (rightly?) left almost entirely
up to the implementor as what a "physical file name" means is so different
on different operating systems.
```

#### ↳ Re: Dynamically allocated files

- **From:** "DBuck" <dbuck@prairieinet.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-20T18:07:54-05:00

- **Message-ID:** `<9gra1o$9pure$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de>`

```

Jim Farland <farlandjim@netscape.net> wrote in message
news:25aa81ec.0106200823.668a71a@posting.google.com...
> Hello;
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You basically have 3 options:

1)  IF you have COBOL 390 or above, you can dynamically allocate the files
directly from your COBOL program
2)  You can dynamically allocate files using an assembler submodule (using
SVC99 - DYNALLOC)
3)  You can dynamically allocate files using TSO (as stated in another
response)

I believe that there   are samples of the last 2 options on the CBT tape...

Dave
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Dynamically allocated files

- **From:** Timothy_S_Coffey@bankone.com (Tim Coffey)
- **Date:** 2001-06-21T05:15:37-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e32280da.0106210415.29f824c0@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<25aa81ec.0106200823.668a71a@posting.google.com> <9gra1o$9pure$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de>`

```
"DBuck" <dbuck@prairieinet.net> wrote in message news:<9gra1o$9pure$1@ID-39920.news.dfncis.de>...
> You basically have 3 options:
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Dave

Or perhaps something as simple as this, which uses a COBOL interface to all
the SVC 99 functions.

MOVE LOW-VALUES TO IDENT-SVC99-PARMS-AREA.       
MOVE 'WAAPCODE' TO IDENT-DSNDA-DDNAME.           
MOVE 'RCVD.DBKP0.NASMSRCE' TO IDENT-DSNDA-DSNAME.
MOVE DSN-TIMESTAMP TO IDENT-DSNDA-DSNAME (20:8). 
MOVE 'TSTDA' TO IDENT-DSNDA-UNIT.                
MOVE 'Y' TO IDENT-DSNDA-ALLOC-CYL-FLAG,          
            IDENT-DSNDA-RELEASE-FLAG.            
MOVE 1 TO IDENT-DSNDA-PRIMARY.                   
MOVE 1 TO IDENT-DSNDA-SECONDARY.                 
MOVE 80 TO IDENT-DSNDA-LRECL.                    
MOVE 24000 TO IDENT-DSNDA-BLKSIZE.               
MOVE 'FB' TO IDENT-DSNDA-RECFM.                  
MOVE 'NEW' TO IDENT-DSNDA-STATUS.                
MOVE 'CATLG' TO IDENT-DSNDA-NDISP.               
MOVE 'DELETE' TO IDENT-DSNDA-CDISP.              
CALL 'S9CALLOC' USING IDENT-SVC99-PARMS-AREA,    
                      IDENT-SVC99-WORKAREA.      

OPEN OUTPUT WAAP-CODE.
```

#### ↳ Re: Dynamically allocated files

- **From:** "Solar Systems" <solar@conterra.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-21T10:02:25-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b31fe0e@news.conterra.com>`
- **References:** `<25aa81ec.0106200823.668a71a@posting.google.com>`

```
It sure is possible to dynamically allocate either new or existing files in
Cobol, using the BPXWDYN  IBM-supplied subroutine (which we also use in
assembler and PL1 programs).  Just set up a table of file allocation
parameters, then go through the table and call the subroutine for each file
to be allocated, similar to this:

Dynamic Allocation in High-Level Languages IBM has made available (as part
of Unix Services for OS/390) a generalized file allocation/deallocation
routine that makes it easy to allocate files in high-level languages, such
as Cobol or PL/1. This subroutine can also be called by Assembler programs
and REXX execs.  Called BPXWDYN, the subroutine is passed a single, varying
length character string of up to 100 bytes in length, containing the ALLOC
or FREE command and its operands, coded exactly as you would enter the
corresponding TSO command.   For the complete syntax rules, see the TSO HELP
section on the ALLOC command.   The subroutine can be called to allocate
input, output, or SYSOUT files. Since this is an IBM-supplied and supported
subroutine, it can be used to replace various user-written subroutines. When
using this subroutine, no JCL DD statements are required when running the
program. If any error conditions occur, they will be listed in the job lob
if the MSG(WTP) operand is included in the parameter string, as shown in the
following example. This example is of a Cobol program calling the subroutine
to allocate, open, read, print, close, and free a member of a PDS. For
simplicity's sake, the varying length parameter is always set to 100 bytes,
the maximum allowed.

       CBL NOLIST,NOSEQ
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. COBDYNAL.
......*===============================================================*
......* This program dynamically allocates and deallocates a file
......* using the BPXWDYN IBM subroutine. No DD statements for the
......* file sould appear in the JCL:
......*===============================================================*
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
       SELECT FILEIN ASSIGN TO FILEIN.
       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD FILEIN
       RECORD CONTAINS 80 CHARACTERS
       RECORDING MODE IS F
       LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD,
       BLOCK CONTAINS 0 CHARACTERS
       DATA RECORD IS FILEIN-REC.
       01 FILEIN-REC.
          05 FILLER PIC X(80).

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       77 INPUT-REC PIC X(80).
       77 IN-COUNT PIC 9(9) BINARY VALUE 0.
       77 SUB-PGM PIC X(8) VALUE 'BPXWDYN '.
       77 ALLOC-CMD PIC X(100) VALUE
            ' ALLOC DD(FILEIN) DSN(P2RSY.TSO.DATA(COMPILE)) CYL(2,1)
MSG(WTP)  '.

       01 COMMAND-STRING.
          05 C-S-LEN PIC S9(4) BINARY VALUE +100.
          05 C-S-DATA.
             07 FILLER PIC X(100).

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       INITIAL-STUFF.
......*===============================================================*
......* MOVE THE "ALLOC" STATEMENT TO COMMAND, THEN CALL SUBROUTINE:
......*===============================================================*
       MOVE ALLOC-CMD TO C-S-DATA.
       CALL SUB-PGM USING COMMAND-STRING.
......*===============================================================*
......* OPEN THE FILE JUST ALLOCATED:
......*===============================================================*
       OPEN INPUT FILEIN.

...... READER.
......*===============================================================*
......* READ AND PRINT ALL THE RECORDS IN THE INPUT FILE:
......*===============================================================*
       READ FILEIN INTO INPUT-REC,
       AT END GO TO E-O-J.
       ADD +1 TO IN-COUNT.
       DISPLAY FILEIN-REC.
       GO TO READER.

...... E-O-J.
......*===============================================================*
......* AT END-OF-FILE, CLOSE INPUT FILE, MOVE "FREE" TO COMMAND:
......*===============================================================*
       CLOSE FILEIN.
       MOVE 'FREE DD(FILEIN)' TO C-S-DATA.
       CALL SUB-PGM USING COMMAND-STRING.
       STOP RUN.
//*********************************************************************

"Jim Farland" <farlandjim@netscape.net> wrote in message
news:25aa81ec.0106200823.668a71a@posting.google.com...
> Hello;
>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jim Farland
```

#### ↳ Re: Dynamically allocated files

- **From:** "Albert W. Paul" <alpaul@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-21T15:24:47-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9gthgf$o62$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<25aa81ec.0106200823.668a71a@posting.google.com>`

```
IBM supplies a routine to do this. It is comes as part of the MVS operating
system software. It functions with all MVS version of COBOL. The following
is the introduction to the documentation attached.

DYNAM provides an interface between a high level language such as COBOL or
PLI, and SVC 99, the system dynamic allocation  routine.  It allows the user
to acquire system resources as the need arises and free them when they are
no longer required.  DYNAM takes simple parameters consisting of character
strings and translates them into the text units required by SVC 99.  In
addition, a special information retrieval function can be called to provide
all the information available about a particular allocation.  This
information is returned in a fixed format data structure.  For more
information about SVC 99 refer to the IBM manual:


"Jim Farland" <farlandjim@netscape.net> wrote in message
news:25aa81ec.0106200823.668a71a@posting.google.com...
> Hello;
>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jim Farland
```

## Thread: Dynamic Tree-View — 5 msgs

### Dynamic Tree-View

- **From:** "Thomas H���rkens" <thoerkens@rsw-orga.de>
- **Date:** 2001-06-21T11:03:56+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<t9dsg9.g85.ln@gw1.rsw-orga.de>`

```
Hi,

Description of my problem:

    I want to build a Tree-View at runtime (ok it's not a problem) with data
from
    data file because the tree-view must be dynamic depending on the
user-login.

    Example:

        User 1 have only the authorization to see the child "Customer"

        User 2 have the authorization to see the childs "Customer" and
"Products"

    The authorization flag is stored in the Data-File.

    My serious problem is the administration of the USAGE POINTER within
    the COBOL-CODE
```

instance of the Tree View control. What this means and how to deal with it I
can provide, but I need to know WHAT COBOL you are using.

Pete.

## Thread: Seeking OLD Honeywell Manual Covers from 1970's — 4 this month / 5 total

### Seeking OLD Honeywell Manual Covers from 1970's

- **From:** gary_jabo@my-deja.com (Gary Jablonski)
- **Date:** 2001-06-21T06:52:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9d9b486e.0106210552.44a14560@posting.google.com>`

```
I'm hoping the readers of this group can help or direct me
to the group that would be more appropriate.  Thanks in advance
for the help.

In the 1970's Honeywell made a mainframe computer. I think the operating
system was called GECOS, or something like that.
Anyway, the thing I remember most was the COVER PHOTOS for the manuals.
There were photos of collections computer chips and other circa 1970 
computer components that were assembled together in an artistic way 
to create a model of an animal.

I seem to rember frogs, dinosaurs, dogs, cats, etc. Unique artwork
that one would remember for a long time.

Am I dreaming? Or have these artistic renderings been lost forever?
Does anyone else remember these COVER PHOTOS?
Do you know where I might get to see some of these (again)?

Please reply to this posting or send e-mail to me.
Please remove the ".NOSPAM" from the e-mail address below.
gary_jabo@my-deja.NOSPAM.com

Many thanks 
Gary Jablonski - Atlanta, GA
```

…[15 more quoted lines elided]…

Hi:

I don't think you are dreaming but the animals, etc. made from bits
of computers were used in advertisements for the H-200 et al.

I don't remember the manuals having those 'sculptures' on the
covers, at least mine didn't.

Thanks

Tony Dilworth

## Thread: How to hide cobol code? — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to hide cobol code?

- **From:** Ron Bell <ronbell@cais.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-21T17:56:25-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B326D89.EAE66764@cais.com>`

```
How do I hide certain lines of cobol code when I compile? There is a
command which I can code in my program which will "hide" portions of
code when I compile and print a listing.  This command is used in
security/top-secret programs when you don't want others to see certain
parts of a program.  Thanks,
ronbell@cais.com
```

#### ↳ Re: How to hide cobol code?

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-21T22:18:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<souY6.19081$iR2.1860262@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3B326D89.EAE66764@cais.com>`

```
COPY copybook SUPPRESS

"Ron Bell" <ronbell@cais.com> wrote in message
news:3B326D89.EAE66764@cais.com...
> How do I hide certain lines of cobol code when I compile? There is a
> command which I can code in my program which will "hide" portions of
> code when I compile and print a listing.  This command is used in
> security/top-secret programs when you don't want others to see
certain
> parts of a program.  Thanks,
> ronbell@cais.com
>
```

#### ↳ Re: How to hide cobol code?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-21T18:33:11-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9gu075$pqb$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3B326D89.EAE66764@cais.com>`

```
What compiler and what operating system?

There is no "generic" way to do this, but I can think of some ways that
MIGHT work - depending on your environment.
```

#### ↳ Re: How to hide cobol code?

- **From:** Louis Krupp <lkrupp@pssw.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-22T00:30:41-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B32E611.13ABD5ED@pssw.com>`
- **References:** `<3B326D89.EAE66764@cais.com>`

```
You could always do what programmers have been doing for
years:

Name your variables creatively.  If you can't do that, come as
close as you can to not naming them at all, e.g., use a single
letter with a bunch of digits.  Don't even think of indenting
your code, not even code subordinate to an IF statement,
because there will be no such code.  You'll be doing it all with
the time-honored GO TO.  In fact, you'll be doing better than
that, because security is important.  Where others might have
been content with mere spaghetti code, you will be using the
ALTER statement.

This technique has stood the test of time.  It has kept the trade
secrets in thousands of programs safe from spies (and everyone
else) no matter how long they look at the source.

Good luck.

Louis Krupp

Ron Bell wrote:

> How do I hide certain lines of cobol code when I compile? There is a
> command which I can code in my program which will "hide" portions of
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> ronbell@cais.com

- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0106221226.4e4df25c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3B326D89.EAE66764@cais.com>`

```
Taking a WILD guess I'm going to say you are talking about Xing out
the code in ISPF.  Just place an XX on the first line you want to
hide, then an XX on the last and press ENTER.

#### ↳ Re: How to hide cobol code?

- **From:** "James" <mangogrower@telocity.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-22T19:06:28-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<maQY6.1178$Tk.227736@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`
- **References:** `<3B326D89.EAE66764@cais.com>`

```
Yes the always irritating 'COPY SUPPRESS' only works for copied code.

If this is at a 'top secret' site, then there should be a procedure for
obtaining
the printout from a specific printer, and the files INCLUDING the
listing, will be restricted by the OS.  Otherwise you can't have A
'Top Secret' designation since CONFIDENTIAL, SECRET, TOP SECRET,
PARTITIONED ACCESS, etc are U.S. Government designations.
ANY site running SECRET or above will have the proper procedures in place.

A non-U.S. Government entity that claims to be operating at Top Secret level
will also have these in place.  It won't be left up to you to find out how!


Ron Bell <ronbell@cais.com> wrote in message
news:3B326D89.EAE66764@cais.com...

> command which I can code in my program which will "hide" portions of
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> ronbell@cais.com
```

#### ↳ Re: How to hide cobol code?

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-06-22T19:43:25-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7DQY6.47327$uR5.5220080@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<3B326D89.EAE66764@cais.com>`

```

"Ron Bell" <ronbell@cais.com> wrote in message
news:3B326D89.EAE66764@cais.com...
> How do I hide certain lines of cobol code when I compile? There is a
> command which I can code in my program which will "hide" portions of
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> ronbell@cais.com

Put the program parts that you want to keep secure in separate data files,
and get them into the programs by using COPY statements.

Although how to do it is going to depend on the platform, just about every
Cobol I have ever seen had some method somewhere that would allow control
over copied data display in the listings.

The fact that the security required source also resides in separate
files than non-secure source allows you better control at the librarying and
archiving level, as well, though that would be true regardless of language.
```

#### ↳ Re: How to hide cobol code?

- **From:** "Schroeder" <jfriedman@nc.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-23T04:55:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sjVY6.8800$Md.2090661@typhoon.southeast.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3B326D89.EAE66764@cais.com>`

```
This may not be a very useful suggestion, but if you put in in the
DATE-COMPILED paragraph (assuming your compiler still supports it), then the
only date shows on the listing and the rest of paragraph is suppressed.  At
least you could hide comments that way.


"Ron Bell" <ronbell@cais.com> wrote in message

#### ↳ Re: How to hide cobol code?

- **From:** alain.reymond-nospam@ceia.com (Alain Reymond)
- **Date:** 2001-06-23T07:47:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b34485c.434094@news.skynet.be>`
- **References:** `<3B326D89.EAE66764@cais.com>`

```
Under some compilers (MF for example), you can use conditional
compilation. If a condition is true, you will compile a portion of
code, if it is false, another one. I think it might be an answer to
your question. 

Under MF, the syntax is 
$if MY-CONDITION = "THIS"
  my code
$else
 another portion of cdoe
$end

You can set the condition in your source code or while calling the
compiler.

Alain

## Thread: rmcobol and dos commands — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: rmcobol and dos commands

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-22T00:14:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<66wY6.1728$qz2.404630@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Well, I tested this today, and it works just fine.

I was able to get WaitForSingleObjectEx to wait properly for BOTH "Open" of
"notepad.exe filename" and for "open" of "filename.txt" (which lauched the
application associated with *.txt files; on my system, that's notepad).

So.. I guess you CAN do process montioring using the "Microsoft-approved"
(i.e., no "CreateProcess") method.

I have this in BASIC code; if anyone can use that, drop me a note. I will
also be posting this on PowerBASICs web site sometime in the next couple of
days. (I'll make a better demo program for that posting).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: rmcobol and dos commands

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-23T12:40:31+00:00

- **References:** `<66wY6.1728$qz2.404630@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <bfdfc3e8.0106221238.6d8c08bc@posting.google.com>`
