```
Well, it worked for me, as expected.  ShellExecuteEx returns immediately, at
which time you go into some WaitForSingleObjectEx code, where you can wait
for a 'timeout value' or wait 'infinite.'

If you want BASIC-language code to convert to your flavor of COBOL, here is
an edited note I sent to someone via regular e-mail, about using this to
invoke a 'file copy' program (I also suggested the copyfile API):

== begin note ==

I posted the BASIC Language source code needed to monitor a process started
with ShellExecuteEx on the powerbasic bbs 6/22/01. Go to
http://www.powerbasic.com/support/forums/Ultimate.cgi

Go to the "Source Code Forum" and look for something posted by Michael
Mattias 6/22/01 under the title, "Win32: Monitor Process with
ShellExecuteEx" (or something real close to that).

The best way to retrieve that code is to hit the "edit this message" button,
right-click, select all, right click, copy, change over to notepad and
paste. After pasting, just hit the "back" button on your browser to
"escape." (Don't worry, you can't really edit the message, since you are not
the author). You should not need to register with the BBS to do this

If you are using a batch file to invoke 'copy', you'll need to open
COMMAND.EXE (win 9x) or CMD.EXE (NT/2K) as the program name. I think 'Xcopy"
is a program, so you would not need to worry about the command interpreter,
but you would need to worry about finding xcopy.exe or xcopy32.exe. The
newer Windows systems hide the old DOS utility programs and do not include
them on the path.

== end note ===
```

<a id="t-aac5d0ffd1"></a>
## Thread: Arithmetic, what are the rules? — 10 msgs

### Arithmetic, what are the rules?

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-06-22T11:52:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B333155.252044D4@istar.ca>`

```
If I learned nothing else from the course in Numerical Analysis and
Programming for Digital Computers, I learned that when you actually do
the arithmetic, I learned that A*(B+C) is not necessarily (A*B) + (A*C)
depending on precision, rounding and truncation rules, etc.  I remember
being intrigued with the implications of the way you define the
problem.  This was reinforced by just recently learning that there are
at least 5 rules for rounding (unless I had forgotten what I learned in
college).  Thus I was happy and relieved to learn that there is work
being done on decimal arithmetic as opposed to binary arithmetic.  There
are proposals for rules for floating point decimal to be used for
business arithmetic.  These proposals come from the realization that
there is not good support for doing arithmetic on
computers that will give results most people expect.  Few computer
architectures provide more than minimal assistance for fixed point
decimal arithmetic let alone floating point.  You can find the proposals
for standard arithmetic and a link for the proposed Java standard at
http://www2.hursley.ibm.com/decimal.  There is also information as to
why this is important.  If you forget the link just go to Google and
search for "decimal arithmetic".

After reading all of the proposals, I have several concerns.

The first is that I don't see any indication of participation by the
accounting and financial communities in the formation of the Java
standard.  This is typical of the problem of the industry where we can't
get the users of our product interested in the implications of the
decisions we make and give us a clear definition of what they want. 
This is further compounded by what I suspect is widespread programmer
ignorance of the rules for handling rounding and intermediate results
for a given organization.  I know that in the thirty plus years I have
been working in business and systems programming (systems administration
in the Unix and NT worlds), I have never been informed of any standards
at the 5 shops I have worked at. At most there has been a vague
reference to keeping the same number of decimal places for all operands
in addition and subtraction along with avoiding the COBOL COMPUTE
statement.  This is because the rules for dealing intermediate results
were unclear.  Indeed, as I stated earlier, it wasn't until this year
that I learned that there are 5 rules for rounding.  Other than one
payroll program where I had to round to the nearest half cent, I have
always used the standard COBOL rounding (round away from zero).  Thus
those who set the rules for an organization probably are not
communicating them to the people who have to implement them.

The second is that I am concerned about the emphasis on floating versus
fixed point.  The Gross World Product expressed in Italian lire
shouldn't require more nineteen or twenty digits plus no more than eight
or nine places of decimal precision so that the current COBOL limit of
31 digit numbers should be enough for most if not all business
calculations.  A floating point number with a 31 digit coefficient seems
to be overkill and use of floating point rather than
fixed decimal precision will give different results from what is
expected in some environments.  In the proposals support is given to the
character representation of both scientific and engineering numbers. 
While there is a definite use for this format, it is not a form that the
average person is used to when looking at numbers.  I will grant that
the variety of fixed decimal formats with at least two different types
of decimal points (period and comma that I know of) and leading or
trailing signs with a variety of representations for them makes it
difficult to come up with an unambiguous way of handling
numbers.  However, one of the major uses of numbers in business is to
communicate with people who are not aware these notations exist, let
alone understand them.  The suggestions should work well with
spreadsheets. 

The third is that the proposals do not address fixed point arithmetic
which is the major part of arithmetic that I have seen in the COBOL
programs over the years.  Most of the operations are simple addition and
subtraction and much of the multiplication retains the same number of
decimal places because at most only one of the operands is not an
integer.  Indeed the question arises as to whether the hardware
optimization should be for arithmetic on formatted character numbers as
opposed to specially formed floating point numbers or even packed
decimal.  This would eliminate a lot of the reformatting that currently
takes place and would not add that much to the actual disk storage
consumed because of the compression already being done.  Data
transmission in many cases is in character format anyway for many
reasons.  In addition, I contend that any savings gained by going to
anything more exotic than packed decimal will be lost in the conversion
overhead.  It could make sense to carry the exponent, sign and
coefficient as separate pieces and load them into separate registers,
where fixed point numbers only have a sign and coefficient.

The fourth concern is that we have no linguistic support that I am know
about to handle either the rules for rounding or the rules for handling
intermediate results.  There are global option settings for intermediate
results in some of the COBOL compilers but none that allow you to
specify any of the following rules:

1.  Round half away from zero.      6.5 -> 7, -6.5 -> -7,
                                    5.5 -> 6, -5.5 -> -6.
    This is the IBM hardware rounding and the COBOL rounding.
2.  Round half toward zero.         6.5 -> 6, -6.5 -> -6,
                                    5.5 -> 5, -5.5 -> -5.
3.  Round half toward + infinity.   6.5 -> 7, -6.5 -> -6,
                                    5.5 -> 6, -5.5 -> -5.
4.  Round half toward - infinity.   6.5 -> 6, -6.5 -> -7,
                                    5.5 -> 5, -5.5 -> -6.
5.  Round half toward nearest even. 6.5 -> 6, -6.5 -> -6,
                                    5.5 -> 6, -5.5 -> -6. 
               
The use of floating point and specification for the rules on number of
digits including the guard digit may obiviate the need for rules on
intermediate results.  This however may require changes in business or
even legal requirements.  While I see nothing in this caveat to hinder
the proposals for Java decimal handling from proceeding my next caveat
may well have an implication for it.  This has more to do with
understanding what changes may be needed and what other standards and
support for business arithmetic may need to be put in place.

The final caveat is that it would be extremely valuable if the rules for
decimal arithmetic were specified the same way for all languages that
support decimal arithmetic.  This would include COBOL, Java, PL1, REXX
and various proprietary languages that I know of.  At this point it
might be that the only way to do this would be to have a user specified
option for the new common arithmetic method(s) and both global
(program-wide) and local (statement leve) ways to make this
specification.  For example, there would be qualifiers for the word
ROUNDED so that the type of rounding would be controlled. 

In closing, the issues of simple arithmetic prove to be complex.  It is
extremely encouraging to see that work is being done on the issues
surrounding decimal arithmetic.  The concerns I raise may have been
dealt with but more people have to be aware and involved.  I am even
more worried about important concerns that I don't know enough to even
have.  As said earlier, a good way to start becoming aware and involved
is to go to http://www2.hursley.ibm.com/decimal, familiarize yourself
with the material there and then communicate with the appropriate
bodies.  I urge everyone to do so.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Arithmetic, what are the rules?

- **From:** "James" <mangogrower@telocity.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-22T19:11:10-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MeQY6.1188$Tk.233978@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`
- **References:** `<3B333155.252044D4@istar.ca> <4TNY6.283495$NK4.19880894@news2.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
The 'Rounding in COBOL' is NOT away from ZERO:

When the size of the fractional result exceeds the number of places provided
for its storage, truncation  occurs unless ROUNDED is specified. When
ROUNDED is specified, the least significant digit of the resultant
identifier is increased by 1 whenever the most significant digit of the
excess is greater than or equal to 5.

In a floating-point arithmetic operation, the ROUNDED phrase has no effect;
the result of a floating-point  operation is always rounded.


The first part above means that if the excess is 1 thru 4, then the least
significant digit of the
resultant identifier is left alone.

10.1 thru 10.4 = 10
10.5 thru 10.9 = 11
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Arithmetic, what are the rules?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-22T21:40:13-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9h0vhu$1bl$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3B333155.252044D4@istar.ca> <4TNY6.283495$NK4.19880894@news2.aus1.giganews.com> <MeQY6.1188$Tk.233978@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`

```

"James" <mangogrower@telocity.com> wrote in message
news:MeQY6.1188$Tk.233978@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net...
 <snip>
>
> In a floating-point arithmetic operation, the ROUNDED phrase has no
effect;
> the result of a floating-point  operation is always rounded.
>

Interesting statement, ...

This is a "well documented" behavior in IBM COBOL's (where floating point is
an "old" extension).  HOWEVER, I see no such statement in the draft of the
next COBOL Standard.  What it does say (on page 406 of the FCD) is,

"14.8.3 ROUNDED phrase
If, after decimal point alignment, the number of places in the fractional
part of the result of an arithmetic operation is greater than the number of
places provided for the fraction of the resultant identifier, truncation is
relative to the size provided for the resultant identifier. When rounding is
requested, the absolute value of the resultant identifier is increased by
one in the low-order position whenever the most significant digit of the
excess is greater than or
equal to five.

When the low-order integer positions in a resultant identifier are
represented by the symbol P in the picture character-string for that
resultant identifier, rounding or truncation occurs relative to the
rightmost integer position for which storage is allocated."

I plan on "following up" on whether this existing IBM statement is also true
(but "implied"?) in the draft Standard or not.  I think it may be "implicit"
in the reference to "the number of places in the fractional part of the
result on an arithmetic operation" but I am not at all positive of this.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Arithmetic, what are the rules?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-26T10:11:00-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9hafn6$jvl$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3B333155.252044D4@istar.ca> <4TNY6.283495$NK4.19880894@news2.aus1.giganews.com> <MeQY6.1188$Tk.233978@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net> <9h0vhu$1bl$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <3b352403_1@Usenet.com>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3b352403_1@Usenet.com...

<<I would've thought that whoever wrote this (and I agree it is not an easy
concept to convey with words alone) might have been moved to give some
EXAMPLEs showing what happens...>>

Examples are a Good Thing, I agree.  At its last meeting, J4 discussed this
general issue (not the specific issue of examples of the application of
arithmetic rules, but the issue of examples in general) at  considerable
length, and my sense of that discussion is that working examples should be
encouraged and solicited from the COBOL user community at large.   Moreover,
I believe some J4 members are investigating setting up a mechanism whereby
the collection and perhaps dissemination of examples can be accomplished.

But the FCD is currently pushing 900 pages, and incorporating examples into
that document for every construct or situation that some reader might find
confusing or vague would almost certainly bloat it way beyond manageability.
So I believe J4's inclination to be that the body of examples should be
published / disseminated separately from the standard itself, not
incorporated into the FCD.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
NO! I disagree SO vehemently here... What the Hell do you mean by "bloat"?

If you mean it will make the document "fat" then split it into different
volumes... (Worked for Gibbons "Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire" and
every decent Encyclopaedia that's ever been published, not to mention the
Greater Oxford Dictionary...)

If you mean that it is just added verbiage which "bloats" the document then
examples certainly DON'T fit in THAT category...

The point is that examples NEED to be IN CONTEXT. Hiving them off to an
appendix or a separate document, renders them almost useless. They should be
considered as at least as valuable as "the priceless prose" written by the
document authors and indeed, should be an essential complement to it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Arithmetic, what are the rules?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-26T14:03:04-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9hamaa$q3f$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3B333155.252044D4@istar.ca> <4TNY6.283495$NK4.19880894@news2.aus1.giganews.com> <MeQY6.1188$Tk.233978@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net> <9h0vhu$1bl$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I have now verified the status of "rounding" with "floating-point" in the
FCD (draft of the next COBOL Standard).

1) If an arithmetic expression uses data-items of any of the new
floating-point usages (e.g. FLOAT-SHORT, FLOAT-LONG), then the evaluation of
the entire arithmetic expression is "implementor defined".  This means that
they MIGHT (but need not) automatically ROUND even when the ROUNDED phrase
is not specified.

2) If an arithmetic expression includes a floating-point literal but all
other items are fixed-point (and not the new binary-xxxx usages), then the
following rules apply:

  A) If the "ARITHMETIC IS NATIVE" phrase is in effect (which it is - by
default for upward compatibility) then the "normal" rules apply - when
truncation is required it occurs; when rounding is specified, it occurs; and
when COMPUTE is used results are "implementor defined" as always.

 B) If the (new) "ARITHMETIC IS STANDARD" phrase is in effect, then
floating-point literals (unlike floating-point USAGEs) does NOT make the
statement "implementor defined".  Truncation and Rounding MUST apply (in a
Standard conforming implementation).


   ***

P.S.  *none* of this applies (of course) to such existing extensions as
COMP-1 or COMP-2 in IBM compilers.  In those cases, the implementor may
continue to define how they work - as they will remain "extensions".
```

---

<a id="t-e15c7f1ecc"></a>
## Thread: COBOL FAQ — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2001-06-22T12:05:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers,alt.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_993211476@rtfm.mit.edu>`

<a id="t-9f50d005a9"></a>
## Thread: A case for multiple inheritence. — 49 this month / 68 total

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

### A case for multiple inheritence.

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-06-22T10:18:57-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0106220918.6e54b419@posting.google.com>`

```
I posted a message indicating I would provide an example of why I like
Multiple Inheritence in COBOL.  Others have questioned it's utility,
and the language du jour doesn't support it.

While it can be abused, making a morass of inheriting classes, it can
also be a boon.  Let's talk theory and I'll try to throw in the
example.

Consider Airplanes.  An Airplane class might grant inheritence to
several subclasses.  The different types of airplanes might inherit
the general characteristics of the Airplane class.  For example:

Airplane
  - Bomber

Airplane
  - Transport

Airplane
  - Fighter

Now, each of these aircraft might be of a different type.

Airplane 
  - Bomber
    - Stealth
      - Stealth Fighter

Airplane
  - Bomber
    - Stealth
      - Stealth Bomber

So far so good.

Now, let's consider the ordinance.  Each of these has different
requirements.
Let's say that we want to specify the bombs.  We could do this:

Airplane -
  - Bomber (Method BombType)
      - Stealth
        - Stealth Fighter


But.. what about fighters?  F16's are fighter bombers and they might
use the same bombs as stealth fighters.  Associating bombs with
bombers won't allow us to have an F16 that inherits from the Fighter
class and still get the BombType method.

One solution, using single inheritence is to place the BombType under
the Aircraft class and then override them in the indivudual subclasses
so that they don't have bombs.  This is silly, and extra overhead for
all the inheriting subclasses that don't have bombs.  It also results
in excessiving "overrides".

Another is just to code the BombType method over and over at the
lowest level where it is necessary - adding extra methods to
subclasses.  This is wasteful and results in excessive maintenance
should something about bombs change.

Instead, we could put the BombType method in a separate class that
handled multiple types of bombs.

Then you simply inherit where necessary and avold all the extra
overhead where it is not necessary.

IE:

Airplane
  - Bomber, - Bombs
    - Stealth
      - Stealth Fighter

Airplane
  - Transport
    - C-130

Airplane 
  - Fighter
    - Fighter-Bomber - Bombs
      - F16

See?  This is what Multiple Inheritence is good for!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: A case for multiple inheritence.

- **From:** "Grinder" <no.spam@no.spam.spam.spam.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-22T18:04:42-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UaQY6.123$PE2.41021@e420r-atl2.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<bfdfc3e8.0106220918.6e54b419@posting.google.com> <3B3396B7.11EDA5CD@home.com>`

```
> 'Grinder" if you are still looking in - care to comment where you have
> found a TRUE NEED for it in other languages - examples please.

Before I comment, I'll come clean as to my aesthetic appreciation of
multiple inheritance.

I'm greedy enough to want it.  Even if I don't use it very often, it does
not complicate the language if you take a single inheritance approach.  I'll
think about it some more, but I can't think of an instance where you gain
anything by knowing that an object cannot inherit from more than one base.
(A single inheritance language compiler can probably do it's job faster than
one that support multiple inheritance, but I know of no examples we could
time to determine this.  Object Pascal compilers beat the pants off of C++
compilers, but C++ has some ambiguities that would likely make this
so--without the complexities of multiple inheritance.)

That said, I can't make a forceful argument against being able to multiply
inherit.

An example of multiple inheritance that I've used before is thus:

Consider a business object.  It has a series of scalar attributes or
properties that define the object.  (It's a lot like a single row of a
recordset if you're familiar with that pattern.)  Values may abstractly be
retrieved from and put to the object, with that object's implementation
being in charge of validation and other computational rules.  It's basically
a "smart array" of named values.

Another object, a business list, is more like the recordset mentioned
parenthetically above.  It has named attributes, but also rows of them.
(Other patterns by name: a grid, spreadsheet, etc.)  In addition, it has
aggregate functions that operate to sum or average a column of values.

Each of these objects have fundamental applications in data management and
may be used in their own right.  Consider a more specific example, though, a
Bill object.  (I mean a financial statement, not William Klein.)

The Bill certainly has a use for object-level attributes: customer, bill
date, disposition, etc.  Contained within that bill is a list of
charges--much like the business list.  In this scenario, you could rapidly
construct a Bill object by inheriting from the business object AND the
business list.

You could certainly construct a base class that implements both of these
schemes, but using multiple inheritance offers quick extensibility.  Perhaps
you need to maintain a list of payors for the bill--inherit another business
list into a new namespace and you're ready to mash.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A case for multiple inheritence.

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-24T05:37:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B357C1D.3BA25E7E@home.com>`
- **References:** `<bfdfc3e8.0106220918.6e54b419@posting.google.com> <3B3396B7.11EDA5CD@home.com> <UaQY6.123$PE2.41021@e420r-atl2.usenetserver.com>`

```
Thane wrote .......

giving a Multiple Inheritance example using airplanes, correctly Bomber, Fighter
and Transport. You must know me well enough by now to know that I'm not sniping.
We both know a little bit about airplanes, me shuffling paper for 12 years in
scratchy air force blue and you 'cos Daddy spent his time pointing nukes from
WBF Montana at the Great White North - and I'm sure one was deliberately
pointing at Don in Assville, Ontario <G> And of course you fly 'em in a private
capacity.

This may seem way off tangent to the topic - but I sincerely believe it starts
at the design stage. So initially I'll go back over slightly 40 years ago when
Unigate (they don't sell a single drop of milk now), employed consultants to
look at their operation and come up with a business plan. This high priced help
produced a very complex and highly detailed design chart of the group's
operations, milk collection, processing, manufacturing, distribution, own fleet,
British Rail and SPD a distribution subsidiary of Unilever. Over and above that
we canned evaporated milk for Carnation (so don't always believe the labels you
see on the cans), real fruit yoghurt for Marks & Spencer, St. Michael brand, and
lacking their own distribution set-up at that time, we sold and delivered Green
Giant in the UK. Put that lot together and you finish up with something looking
even more complex than a map of the London Underground or the New York subway
systems.

Some go for the Big Bang approach. Surprisingly these guys actually got it
right. They looked at the whole complex model and said, let's start at both ends
working towards the middle. The result, the first two computer projects were (1)
Collection of milk from farmers and (2) the bit I was involved in - invoicing
finished 'grocery' products, plus the bulk milk, whey and powders we sold to
bakery/candy/cookie manufacturers, and our own Cow & Gate baby milk powders and
foods. I can't tell you what happened to the 'middle bits' because that
happened, if at all, after I left. I wont digress into details, but the Milk
Collection system was a disaster, purely based on bad design advice. Ours
invoicing, two elements van selling (a mobile warehouse on four wheels) and
advance selling (me) salesmen taking orders and deliveries from distribution
points - it went extremely well but we took an inordinately long two years to
get there because everybody was at ground zero learning about EDP.

Interestingly the consultants took a closer look at their overall design. There
was one element missing - sales of fresh cream (single, whipped, clotted, Double
Devon etc.) direct from creameries (factories) because of refrigerator
requirements and short shelf life. So they hired a small software house, two
excellent programmers who took this on. The timing was right because just as
they were about to start we had defined what Customer records, Product Catalogue
etc. would look like. They looked at our proposals and agreed, and being one
jump ahead of us in programming expertise, they actually computerized these for
us because they needed the same features for their application.

I can assure you - 40 years later - it's crystal clear in my mind that the
consultants had the starting point right. (Lesson - you can't gobble up the
whole problem in one huge bite).

Now the next story is a bit speculative because I'm going from memory of what I
read in local papers, but I'm reasonably certain we are talking about the London
Stock Exchange - and I think occurred within the last ten years.

They brought in a new man, fancy title no doubt, as the head EDP honcho. He
started out like the Unigate consultants looking at the whole, but recruiting
EDP en masse; the final number I think between 250-300. They formulated that
corporate design model but right from day one they fell into the trap of trying
to design the whole for computerization. None of us need to know precisely the
ins and outs of stock exchanges, but we are well aware of such an operation's
complexities. It's a fair bet that they were going the OO route and not using
COBOL - and who know they may even have latched on to one of the languages du
jour which included Multiple Inheritance <G>.

It just went on and on, "Nearly there, not quite finished....". In exasperation
the LSE CEO called in consultants to do an audit. They came back to him with a
two headed message. "Good news, it's coming along fine, although it's cost you 5
million. Bad news, you will have to spend another 5 million pounds before it
happens". Result, he went ballistic and fired EVERYBODY, guilty and innocent
alike.

Now compare the Unigate and LSE approaches.

This is where I had a problem looking at your Aircraft model - I grant you a
valiant attempt, and others jumped in suggesting 'how it can be done', viewed I
would suggest, purely from a programming technique perspective. It is just too
VAST and your model is purely USAF. Throw in the Navy, Army, Marines and
wingless aircraft 'helicopters' and it just gets worse. I also had a problem
with your design model fighter/bomber - it isn't both. Either Fighter/bomber =
Bomber or Fighter/Bomber = Fighter.
To my mind it is Fighter/Bomber = Bomber; the aircraft are designed for that
dual role and lack the operational performance of stand alone fighters. Then my
muddled thoughts fell into place when Pete suggested a separate class 'Ordinance
or Bombs'.
It is a simple and elegant approach to the problem and doesn't require the top
down thinking necessitated by your Aircraft model, and doesn't challenge you to
consider Multiple Inheritance as a solution to a perceived overall need.

Like Unigate - the Bomb class tackles the problem at a modular level. It still
has its own inner complexities, because method message wise we are still down to
individual models of aircraft F16s, F18s or whatever, their payload and what
they can do.
But we have handled one singular aspect of the 'military' business. The rest
will tend to fall into place as we glue on additional modules to the whole
picture and eventually arrive at your Aircraft model, albeit program wise, it
might look considerably different to what you suggest. Anyway 10 out of 10 for a
good effort <G>

As I said in another message - we have Multiple Inheritance in the Standard so
don't mess with it, (i.e. change or delete)  - but I doubt its usefulness.

Does the above make sense - or am I way off in space ?

Jimmy

PS: Just an interesting historical footnote. We are all aware fighters are used
for many roles, aerial reconnaissance, low level ground attack etc. Back in WW2
some of the venerable Spitfires were used for photographic reconnaissance,
primarily along the French coast, zipping in low and taking reams of film. To
make them less discernible from the ground their under-bellies were painted
pink. I'm certain the Luftwaffe and USAAF had fighters providing a similar role.
```

#### ↳ Re: A case for multiple inheritence.

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-23T10:50:46+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b33cde9_4@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<bfdfc3e8.0106220918.6e54b419@posting.google.com>`

```
Thane,

I didn't want to get into a pointless public argument over this,  and,
personally, I have no objection to anyone using multiple inheritance with
COBOL.

But there are risks associated with it (particularly where a number of
Classes are being inherited) and the fact is, it is unnecessary.

I have never used it and never needed to.

You can get all the benefits (without the downside) by simply using an
interface. (This comment applies to both COBOL and Java).

Comments on your example follow...

"Thane Hubbell" <thaneh@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:bfdfc3e8.0106220918.6e54b419@posting.google.com...
> I posted a message indicating I would provide an example of why I like
> Multiple Inheritence in COBOL.  Others have questioned it's utility,
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Well, I don't think "BombType" would be a METHOD, it would be a PROPERTY...
but I'll go along for the ride...<G>

>
> But.. what about fighters?  F16's are fighter bombers and they might
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>


> One solution, using single inheritence is to place the BombType under
> the Aircraft class and then override them in the indivudual subclasses
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Agree it is clumsy. Not sure it is "silly"... depends on what the Method is
that is being overrridden.

> Another is just to code the BombType method over and over at the
> lowest level where it is necessary - adding extra methods to
> subclasses.  This is wasteful and results in excessive maintenance
> should something about bombs change.
>

Agree this is not a god solution. And it defeats the whole object of OO...

> Instead, we could put the BombType method in a separate class that
> handled multiple types of bombs.
>
Yes, let's do that...<G>

> Then you simply inherit where necessary and avold all the extra
> overhead where it is not necessary.
>
Why INHERIT? Simply define an interface to the BOMBS Class and invoke it
when you need it.


> IE:
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> See?  This is what Multiple Inheritence is good for!

Well, yes, I see. But suppose I have a Land vehicle that is also useful for
launching bombs (these days, "bombs" are often actually missiles, so it
isn't unreasonable...)?

It can't inherit any of the Aircraft Classes. But it can certainly interface
to the abstract BOMBS class.

Exit this post with thoughts about cats and ways for the skinning of...<G>

Pete.
```

```
Pete,

Most Java programmers think that the interface can give you this same
functionality - and it's just not true.  The Interface can help, but
even with the interface, Java can only inherit from a single class.

I'm a REALLY BIG FAN of Interface and I can't wait for some COBOL
vendor to implement it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A case for multiple inheritence.

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-24T14:42:37+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b3555d2_5@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<bfdfc3e8.0106220918.6e54b419@posting.google.com> <3b33cde9_4@Usenet.com> <3b34cb31.97631076@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
"Thane Hubbell" <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:3b34cb31.97631076@news1.attglobal.net...
> Pete,
>
> Most Java programmers think that the interface can give you this same
> functionality - and it's just not true.

Er...sorry Mate, but I showed in your example that the interface DOES give
the same functionality.
Wanna see it again  <G>?...
========================  begin previous quote
================================
> >> Instead, we could put the BombType method in a separate class that
> >> handled multiple types of bombs.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> >when you need it.

You get exactly the same functionality by using multiple interfaces as you
would through multiple inheritance, it is just a different way of doing it.
I have yet to see a case where Multiple Inheritance allows me to do
something an interface can't, but I have seen cases where an interface
provides functionality that Multiple Inheritance can't ...see the Land based
bomb launcher below...
> >
> >
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> >Well, yes, I see. But suppose I have a Land vehicle that is also useful
for
> >launching bombs (these days, "bombs" are often actually missiles, so it
> >isn't unreasonable...)?
> >
> >It can't inherit any of the Aircraft Classes. But it can certainly
interface
> >to the abstract BOMBS class.
> >

This last is particularly important because it shows that the interface
approach is actually MORE FLEXIBLE than multiple inheritance.

> >Exit this post with thoughts about cats and ways for the skinning
of...<G>
===========================  end previous quote ============================


> The Interface can help, but
> even with the interface, Java can only inherit from a single class.
>
Yes, I know that and have no problem with it. The point is that it only
NEEDS to INHERIT from a single Class... hence my contention that Multiple
Inheritance in OO COBOL is, strictly speaking, unnecessary. Having said
that, I have no problem with COBOL supporting it...let's not get carried
away by the importance of this or pretend it is a major feature of OO
COBOL...<G>

As for the interface not being supported in COBOL, it IS in the emerging
standard and you can achieve the same effect by prototyping in Fujitsu OO
COBOL. Net Express supports it but, according to Will's book, there is a bug
in the current release which prevents it from working. (As I am not a NE
user, I don't know...maybe Jimmy could comment here?)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A case for multiple inheritence.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Grinder" <no.spam@no.spam.spam.spam.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-23T22:52:12-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<urdZ6.1966$pe.83949@e420r-atl3.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<bfdfc3e8.0106220918.6e54b419@posting.google.com> <3b33cde9_4@Usenet.com> <3b34cb31.97631076@news1.attglobal.net> <3b3555d2_5@Usenet.com>`

```
> You get exactly the same functionality by using multiple interfaces
as you
> would through multiple inheritance, it is just a different way of
doing it.

The difference, though, is that with interfaces you have to do it.
This is the core of why multiple inheritance and interfaces both
exist.  If your implementation can be parameterized, inherit it--if
not polymorph it with interfaces.

>
... the interface
> approach is actually MORE FLEXIBLE than multiple inheritance.

This is true to be sure, but if don't need the guts of your algorithm
to be flexible, why incur the extra coding?  VB does interfaces fairly
well, but I still would like to be able to inherit code--even the MSDN
confesses that an interface-based approach can lead to more code
maintenence.  Admittedly, though, I won't be upset if vb.net uses a
single inheritance model.  (I can't remember from the demos.)

> Inheritance in OO COBOL is, strictly speaking, unnecessary. Having
said
> that, I have no problem with COBOL supporting it...let's not get
carried
> away by the importance of this or pretend it is a major feature of
OO
> COBOL...<G>

It seems to be a matter of personal taste.  Is anyone having a problem
with Cobol supporting it?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A case for multiple inheritence.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-29T08:22:45+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B3C2CC5.F1871AF6@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<bfdfc3e8.0106220918.6e54b419@posting.google.com> <3b33cde9_4@Usenet.com> <3b34cb31.97631076@news1.attglobal.net> <3b3555d2_5@Usenet.com> <urdZ6.1966$pe.83949@e420r-atl3.usenetserver.com>`

```
Grinder wrote:
>
> > > If not then why is F117 so special as to deserve its own CLASS ? 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> warrant it's own class.

If I were writing a flight simulator then I would write it, as most
others have done, to allow new types to be added by the user or by
others.  Building the characteristics into the specific type by making
it a class will prevent this, or at least shows a lack of generality
that would enable add-ons.  It would seem that a new type of actual or
fictional aircraft would require the program to be rewritten.

> > > Why would you create an instance for each individual aircraft at all ?
> > > It seems that you want to create the 'inventory' by running the program,
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>    oAirplane.Model = "P51 Mustang"

Exactly, that is how the program should be.  My objection was entirely
that text names appropriate for attribute values were being used as
class names and instance names in an completely non-abstract fashion.


> Sorry about the syntax--I'm not familiar with OO Cobol, and have
> resorted to using a pseudo OOP language.

and made the example more understandable generally.
```

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Grinder" <no.spam@no.spam.spam.spam.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<56O_6.8920$zT1.246071@e420r-atl3.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<bfdfc3e8.0106220918.6e54b419@posting.google.com> <3b33cde9_4@Usenet.com> <3b34cb31.97631076@news1.attglobal.net> <3b3555d2_5@Usenet.com> <urdZ6.1966$pe.83949@e420r-atl3.usenetserver.com> <3B3C2CC5.F1871AF6@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3B3C2CC5.F1871AF6@Azonic.co.nz...
> Grinder wrote:
> >
> > > > If not then why is F117 so special as to deserve its own CLASS
?
> > > Yes - I would, but only if I am tracking an inventory of actual
> > > aircraft.
> >
> > Both scenarios, in differen contexts, are correct.  I've your
writing
> > an aircraft encyclopedia, the details of any specific model can
> > probably be generalized into a class rather than having an actual
P51
> > class.  On the other hand, if you're writing a flight simulator,
> > there's probably enough specific information about that model to
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> others.  Building the characteristics into the specific type by
making
> it a class will prevent this, or at least shows a lack of generality
> that would enable add-ons.

I was trying to suggest ends of spectrum that validate either
approach.  By not nailing down what the "characteristics" are, it's
not possible to see if they will fit into a hierarchy.  For example,
the aerodynamic properties of all planes can be defined in fundamental
physical laws.  However, doing that is an intensive simulation that
can't be performed in real-time.  So, designers develop simplified
models for particular aircraft to ease the first order calculations.

The models are not very compatible, it's not just a matter of
differing drag coefficents or load distributions--they're radically
different methods of computing common results.

Of course, you could always back out one step on your level of detail,
add a Model property and hope that there's another plane that close
enough that you can set a different Model value (and a few other
parameters) to get your results.

It really boils down to differencing two models.  If those differences
can be parameterized, then that should be built into the class.
Otherwise, you should have two classes or use interfaces to provide
seperate implementations.

> It would seem that a new type of actual or
> fictional aircraft would require the program to be rewritten.

The existing code should be fine, but a new derivation would be
required.  Still it qualifies as a progression of the code, not a
re-write.

> > > > Why would you create an instance for each individual aircraft
at all ?
> > > > It seems that you want to create the 'inventory' by running
the program,
> > > > rather than having it stored in a database.
> >
> > I hope you won't see this as nit-picking, but the language
employed
> > here is a bit cross-pollenated.  Instancing is typically the act
of
> > using a class to create (into memory) an object.  So from your
> > position in the debate, you would not design a class for each
specific
> > model, but rather set properties on an instance of a class to
identify
> > that model:
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Exactly, that is how the program should be.  My objection was
entirely
> that text names appropriate for attribute values were being used as
> class names and instance names in an completely non-abstract
fashion.

It's true that the abstraction presented appears to be taken to to low
of a level of detail.  It can still be used as a basis for debate if
you view the model as a set of relationships rather than a proposed
implementation.

> > Sorry about the syntax--I'm not familiar with OO Cobol, and have
> > resorted to using a pseudo OOP language.
>
> and made the example more understandable generally.

Thanks--OOP is a bit deceptive.  No single concept is particularily
complicated, but they have profound implications to design.  Given
that the ideas are so abstract, it's easy to make slightly different
presumptions about these brief examples.  I don't know how to fix
that, though.

I used to work with a designer that had the habit of discussing
abstractions by saying, "say we have a class A that implements
properties 1 and 2, if we derive class B..."  It squelched detail
questions like "But all patients might not have social security
numbers, how do we key the objects?," but generally left the listener
confused.

I hope I can do better.
```

#### ↳ Re: A case for multiple inheritence.

- **From:** "Grinder" <no.spam@no.spam.spam.spam.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-22T18:09:29-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VaQY6.124$PE2.41079@e420r-atl2.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<bfdfc3e8.0106220918.6e54b419@posting.google.com>`

```
This is no counter to multiple inheritance--but there's another mechanism
that could be of use in this scenario.  Interfaces are different, but just
as valuable as inheritance in object design.  Interfaces can be thought of
as prototype, but not implementation, inheritance.

In Thane's example, Bomber could become an interface that implemented by
whatever Airplane needs it--or by a hot air balloon if need be.

The litmus test, I think, is if the implementation of the desired interface
is common to each suscriber.  If so, inheritance is the way--if not,
interfaces are more suited.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A case for multiple inheritence.

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-06-23T17:05:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b34cbbc.97769814@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<bfdfc3e8.0106220918.6e54b419@posting.google.com> <VaQY6.124$PE2.41079@e420r-atl2.usenetserver.com> <3b342650_6@Usenet.com>`

```
I actually thought of using Interface for this Pete -

However, in the single inheritence languages it still won't work
because even though you can "Combine" classes using interfaces you
can't inherit from said interface of combined classes.  Therefore
using the Bomb interface with bombers won't let the things that
inherit from bombers use said interface unless you have multiple
inheritence.  With single inheritence languages you have to put the
interface to the bombs at the lowest level -- which is why inheritence
is the way to go in this example, but you could combine the two (in
COBOL at least) and use the interface AND inheritence.

<dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
```

```
Excellent post, Thane!

Pete.

"Thane Hubbell" <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:3b34cbbc.97769814@news1.attglobal.net...
> I actually thought of using Interface for this Pete -
>
> However, in the single inheritence languages it still won't work
> because even though you can "Combine" classes using interfaces you
> can't inherit from said interface of combined classes.

Yes, that's very true. However, this won't stop it from working... you
simply invoke the interface at whatever level needs it, rather than
inheriting it... I believe I showed this in your hierarchy.


> Therefore
> using the Bomb interface with bombers won't let the things that
> inherit from bombers use said interface unless you have multiple
> inheritence.  With single inheritence languages you have to put the
> interface to the bombs at the lowest level --
ONLY if you have some rule about using the interface ONCE. You can actually
invoke the interface at ANY level and as often as you like. (I posted a Java
example using "ActionListener" which is a perfect example of an interface.
You can have "Listeners" at ANY level of the inheritance hierarchy...)

> which is why inheritence
> is the way to go in this example, but you could combine the two (in
> COBOL at least) and use the interface AND inheritence.
>
OK. That's an extremely well made point... I'm thinking it over... not
persuaded...yet <G>

But agree there may be a case for multiple inheritance which I missed...<G>

Pete.
```

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in

Arrgh!  Is this right?  I've been using a generalized model of OOP to

am disappointed to hear this.  Is it true that you can't do this
(forgive my syntax):

interface i-export
  method out-to-file
  method out-to-stream

class biz-document derives object
  abstract method print i-export
  abstract method save i-export
  abstract method copy i-export

class patient derives biz-document
  would this class have to implement print, save and copy?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A case for multiple inheritence.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-25T00:38:04+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b35e1f5_3@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<bfdfc3e8.0106220918.6e54b419@posting.google.com> <VaQY6.124$PE2.41079@e420r-atl2.usenetserver.com> <3b342650_6@Usenet.com> <3b34cbbc.97769814@news1.attglobal.net> <3b355968_4@Usenet.com> <gseZ6.2240$pe.97844@e420r-atl3.usenetserver.com>`

```

"Grinder" <no.spam@no.spam.spam.spam.net> wrote in message
news:gseZ6.2240$pe.97844@e420r-atl3.usenetserver.com...
> >
> > "Thane Hubbell" <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I have tried to understand what your syntax means and have modified slightly
so we can all be clear what we are talking about... (my amendments in
Capitals...)

> interface i-export
>   method out-to-file
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>   would this class have to implement print, save and copy?

My feeling is that it wouldn't. But I don't know for sure. Fujitsu
implements the INTERFACE in OO COBOL by means of a PROTOTYPE (It doesn't
recognise the INTERFACE-ID clause in the standard, as far as I can tell) and
you cannot inherit directly from a PROTOTYPE (there is nothing to inherit...
it just defines an interface. Normally you would invoke the interface when
and where you wanted it... In Java you IMPLEMENT the interface at the class
level).

I think you might need to IMPLEMENT the i-export interface in the patient
Class, but again, I don't know for sure.

One way to check the behaviour for OO COBOL would be to define these classes
and the interface and check it with a Class and/or Project Browser which
will tell you if it can inherit or not.

I'm not working at this level on a daily basis so I can't do the necessary
setting up and testing to confirm how it works. Maybe someone else can?

Pete.
```

```
Replies to this got way off track.  So, let me clarify a few things
and then ask you what your question is again.

COBOL does not have "Abstract Methods".  
COBOL does not "Derive" it only Inherits.

COBOL can inherit from an Interface.  If the question is whether or
not every method in the interface must have an implementation -- I'll
have to go look and see, because I don't know the answer off the top
of my head.  Is that the question?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A case for multiple inheritence.

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-26T12:37:41+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b37dc55_6@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<bfdfc3e8.0106220918.6e54b419@posting.google.com> <VaQY6.124$PE2.41079@e420r-atl2.usenetserver.com> <3b342650_6@Usenet.com> <3b34cbbc.97769814@news1.attglobal.net> <3b355968_4@Usenet.com> <gseZ6.2240$pe.97844@e420r-atl3.usenetserver.com> <bfdfc3e8.0106250956.5a020e7d@posting.google.com>`

```
"Thane Hubbell" <thaneh@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:bfdfc3e8.0106250956.5a020e7d@posting.google.com...
> Replies to this got way off track.  So, let me clarify a few things
> and then ask you what your question is again.
>
> COBOL does not have "Abstract Methods".

I believe an "Abstract Method" is one whch cannot be (or is not normally)
instantiated. COBOL Prototypes (interfaces) cannot be instantiated. (They
will often contain a NEW method to instantiate the Class(es) they interface
to, but they are NOT instantiated as objects themselves. I therefore contend
that they ARE abstract methods. What about the class "Legs" in my "Animals"
hierarchy. It exists purely for inheritance and will never be instantiated.
Does this not make it an "Abstract Class"?



> COBOL does not "Derive" it only Inherits.
>
I don't pretend to understand what is meant by "derive" in this context.

> COBOL can inherit from an Interface.  If the question is whether or
> not every method in the interface must have an implementation -- I'll
> have to go look and see, because I don't know the answer off the top
> of my head.  Is that the question?
>
I believe that IS the question, and , like you, I don't know the answer
either. I suggested how the answer could be found but no one has responded.

Pete.
```

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

> "Thane Hubbell" <thaneh@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
> news:bfdfc3e8.0106250956.5a020e7d@posting.google.com...
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Does this not make it an "Abstract Class"?

Hmmm. See what you are saying and am inclined to agree. If a Class is abstract
then its methods must be also. Now this is in BLUE in the Net Express on-line
help - so it isn't part of the standard, but does tend to support your thinking
:-

[IDENTIFICATION DIVISION .]

                                          {IS ABSTRACT
CLASS-ID . class-name-1 {IS EXTERNAL

Class-ID General Rules :
1. Class-name-1 identifies the class being declared.
2. The ABSTRACT clause indicates that class-name-1 is an abstract class. You
cannot create an instance of an abstract class.
3. ..........

However, and as yet I haven't done it - I'm going to try the following :-

1. Edit Materials Table - inherits from base - so "new" instance from Menu
2. MaterialsDBI - "new" instance object created at Menu Level but inherits from
3,
(primarily this is 'doing' collections as I indicated in that earlier sample
method).
3. MaterialsTable(SQL) - inherits from 4.
4. SqlMethods - inherits from Base (connect/disconnect/SqlState errors etc)

It's only the first two I need to instantiate with "new" - I think ?????? -
going directly to methods in 3 and 4, treating them as 'abstracts'.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A case for multiple inheritence.

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-06-26T12:39:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b3881d4.349039155@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<bfdfc3e8.0106220918.6e54b419@posting.google.com> <VaQY6.124$PE2.41079@e420r-atl2.usenetserver.com> <3b342650_6@Usenet.com> <3b34cbbc.97769814@news1.attglobal.net> <3b355968_4@Usenet.com> <gseZ6.2240$pe.97844@e420r-atl3.usenetserver.com> <bfdfc3e8.0106250956.5a020e7d@posting.google.com> <3b37dc55_6@Usenet.com>`

```
On Tue, 26 Jun 2001 12:37:41 +1200, "Peter E. C. Dashwood"
<dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>>
>> COBOL does not have "Abstract Methods".
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Does this not make it an "Abstract Class"?

The Interface does something like, but not exactly the same as Java
re: Abstract Methods.  In Java I can throw one of these Abstract
Methods in anyplace I want - with COBOL only the interface specifies
these, and I don't want to use the word Abstract to describe them.

>
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Just terminology.

>> COBOL can inherit from an Interface.  If the question is whether or
>> not every method in the interface must have an implementation -- I'll
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>either. I suggested how the answer could be found but no one has responded.

And the answer is....

Any class that inherits from an interface SHALL (that means must)
implement ALL of the methods of that interface including all methods
described in interfaces from which that inteface inherits.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A case for multiple inheritence.

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Grinder" <no.spam@no.spam.spam.spam.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-26T19:32:46-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KW9_6.8950$a4.317104@e420r-atl3.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<bfdfc3e8.0106220918.6e54b419@posting.google.com> <VaQY6.124$PE2.41079@e420r-atl2.usenetserver.com> <3b342650_6@Usenet.com> <3b34cbbc.97769814@news1.attglobal.net> <3b355968_4@Usenet.com> <gseZ6.2240$pe.97844@e420r-atl3.usenetserver.com> <bfdfc3e8.0106250956.5a020e7d@posting.google.com>`

```
"Thane Hubbell" <thaneh@softwaresimple.com> wrote:
> Replies to this got way off track.  So, let me clarify a few things
> and then ask you what your question is again.
>
> COBOL does not have "Abstract Methods".

Some one else has pointed out that inheriting an interface amounts to
abstract methods.  This is true from my perspective--an interface is a
collection of abstract methods.  But I am curious, since it's come up,
if you can explicitly name a method to be abstract...  It sounds like
you can't.

This is unfortunate.  I find interfaces and/or abstract methods to be
invaluable in setting up a hierarchy.

> COBOL does not "Derive" it only Inherits.

I think this is just choice of words--do you see a functional
difference between inheriting a class and deriving a class?  Deriving
sort of implies that you'll be expanding the definition, but this is
the prime justification for inheritance.

> COBOL can inherit from an Interface.  If the question is whether or
> not every method in the interface must have an implementation --
I'll
> have to go look and see, because I don't know the answer off the top
> of my head.  Is that the question?

Yes, the description of interfaces is that it's a "contract" with the
implementor, that it will provide the properties and methods defined
by the interface--implementing them as it sees fit.
```

```
...Snipped the rest - we agree about what an interface is and how
COBOL interfaces work...
```

```
"Grinder" <no.spam@no.spam.spam.spam.net> wrote in message

> > The Interface is your tool.  You are correct that you can't setup
> > abstract methods.  There's another thing missing as well, but these
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

To answer your question, my understanding is as follows:

The proposed standard supports it with the INTERFACE-ID instead of CLASS-ID.

Net Express implements this but it doesn't work properly in release 3.1.

Fujitsu don't implement this in their current offerings, but allow the same
functionality by using prototypes.

Pete.
```

```
not made use of it.  In my code I'm trying to stick as close to
standard syntax as I can so when we do have a standards conforming
compiler (after we have an approved standard) then I won't have to
change much code.  There's no vendor that I am aware of who has
implemented interfaces as defined in the draft -- but they are in the
draft.
```

#### ↳ Re: A case for multiple inheritence.

- **From:** "Schroeder" <jfriedman@nc.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-23T05:00:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qnVY6.8803$Md.2096194@typhoon.southeast.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<bfdfc3e8.0106220918.6e54b419@posting.google.com>`

```
It is Especially useful for COBOL application which are long lived. Most C++
programs (or Java, etc..) tend to be short lived applications (certainly by
comparison);  I have COBOL programs still running with DATE-WRITTEN
paragraphs specifying 1959!   Things change over a long period of time -
especially inheritance heirarchies.  A particular functionality which this
program needs may have been moved to a new class to go along with a
corporate restructing or takeover.  Inheriting it from where it currently
lives is an easy maintenance thing to do.  Redesigning the entire
application to take advantage of a new heirarchy is not.





"Thane Hubbell" <thaneh@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:bfdfc3e8.0106220918.6e54b419@posting.google.com...
> I posted a message indicating I would provide an example of why I like
> Multiple Inheritence in COBOL.  Others have questioned it's utility,
…[81 more quoted lines elided]…
> See?  This is what Multiple Inheritence is good for!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: A case for multiple inheritence.

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-23T22:30:33+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b3523fa_1@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<bfdfc3e8.0106220918.6e54b419@posting.google.com> <qnVY6.8803$Md.2096194@typhoon.southeast.rr.com>`

```
"Schroeder" <jfriedman@nc.rr.com> wrote in message
news:qnVY6.8803$Md.2096194@typhoon.southeast.rr.com...
> It is Especially useful for COBOL application which are long lived. Most
C++
> programs (or Java, etc..) tend to be short lived applications (certainly
by
> comparison);  I have COBOL programs still running with DATE-WRITTEN
> paragraphs specifying 1959!

Excellent! And exactly which OO Compiler was that compiled on in 1959?

Oh, you have converted your structured code to OO,right?

So wrapping it in an OO bandage does not make it OO. Are you familiar with
the "Interface" feature of OO and the use of abstract Classes?

If you are, I'd be interested to understand why you chose an OO COBOL
specific feature (multiple inheritance)  over an OO approach which works in
ANY OO Language...

You could define all your structured modules as classes and set up
prototypes or interfaces to them, then invoke them from ANYWHERE, not just a
Class hierarchy.

Like I said before, multiple inheritance is provided in OO COBOL but it is
UNNECESSARY. Wonder how many OTHER features of the new Standard just serve
to complicate and obfuscate rather, than actually add value to the
Language... and NOT just OO either.

Pete.


Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A case for multiple inheritence.

- **From:** Joseph J Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2001-06-24T23:23:08+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B3605DC.10706@iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<bfdfc3e8.0106220918.6e54b419@posting.google.com> <qnVY6.8803$Md.2096194@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <3b3523fa_1@Usenet.com>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:

> 
> Oh, you have converted your structured code to OO,right?
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I have to agree with Pete here.
Interfaces are a great idea. One that is not unique or even new with OO.
You have all been using interfaces for many years - OS API's.
The C language has provided prototype call definitions for quite some time.

I quite like the new OO style of doing things.
Obviously there are some problems : the inheritance discussion is a prime 
example. I have seen at least 4 plausable generic implementations of class 
inheritance for the airplance scenario. All appear to be equally valid and in 
fact are equally valid. The problem being solved is the wrong problem.

Think of inheritance as being able to COPY copy-books only from a strict 
heirarchy of all possible copy books. What multiple inheritance restores is the 
ability of the programmer to copy any copy book at any point in the 
program/class. Does this in anyway help the construction of a solution for the 
airplane problem? Maybe, maybe not? What does help is the definition of domains 
that are independant of each other - ie. DO NOT INHERIT and then the definition 
of interfaces between these domains to allow them to co-operate in the solution.

What OO has brought to the discussion is the realisation that what we have done 
with API's for the past god knows how many years can also be done to data. That 
is you can define what can be done - methods - to a collection of data - an 
object - with what parameters - more objects.

Note: that API's define a function to be done, whereas OO defines the thing to 
be operated on and what can be done to it.

This ability to be able to define objects and their methods is completely 
independant and does not require either inheritance (multiple or otherwise) or 
polymorphism. What these features bring is a shorthand way of not having to 
enumerate every class that a given class inerhits. You can use the heirarchy to 
ASSUME some of the inheritance for you.

If it's one thing we have learnt, it's that a given program should detail all 
it's assumptions. This is not for elegant design, it's for ease of maintenance!

I would happily see both inheritance and polymorphism assigned to the bustbin of 
history as being academic faux pax's that brought only confusion to the OO debate.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A case for multiple inheritence.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Great post, Joe.

Comments below...

Pete.

"Joseph J Katnic" <josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote in message
news:3B3605DC.10706@iinet.net.au...
> Interfaces are a great idea. One that is not unique or even new with OO.
> You have all been using interfaces for many years - OS API's.
> The C language has provided prototype call definitions for quite some
time.
>
> I quite like the new OO style of doing things.
> Obviously there are some problems : the inheritance discussion is a prime
> example. I have seen at least 4 plausable generic implementations of class
> inheritance for the airplance scenario. All appear to be equally valid and
in
> fact are equally valid. The problem being solved is the wrong problem.
>
 It is also important to realise that Thane did NOT propose it as a really
serious model of inheritance. (At least, I don't think he did...<G>) Various
flaws in it have already been pointed out.  However, it DID serve the
purpose for which it was intended and DID allow us to contrast interfacing
with MI.

> Think of inheritance as being able to COPY copy-books only from a strict
> heirarchy of all possible copy books. What multiple inheritance restores
is the
> ability of the programmer to copy any copy book at any point in the
> program/class. Does this in anyway help the construction of a solution for
the
> airplane problem? Maybe, maybe not? What does help is the definition of
domains
> that are independant of each other - ie. DO NOT INHERIT and then the
definition
> of interfaces between these domains to allow them to co-operate in the
solution.
>
This is a very interesting analogy and a new (to me, at least....) way of
looking at the problem.

> What OO has brought to the discussion is the realisation that what we have
done
> with API's for the past god knows how many years can also be done to data.
That
> is you can define what can be done - methods - to a collection of data -
an
> object - with what parameters - more objects.
>
> Note: that API's define a function to be done, whereas OO defines the
thing to
> be operated on and what can be done to it.
>
> This ability to be able to define objects and their methods is completely
> independant and does not require either inheritance (multiple or
otherwise) or
> polymorphism. What these features bring is a shorthand way of not having
to
> enumerate every class that a given class inerhits. You can use the
heirarchy to
> ASSUME some of the inheritance for you.
>
Yes, that's spot on. Use of SUPER and SELF allow control of the direction of
search up and down the implicit hierarchy

> If it's one thing we have learnt, it's that a given program should detail
all
> it's assumptions. This is not for elegant design, it's for ease of
maintenance!
>
> I would happily see both inheritance and polymorphism assigned to the
bustbin of
> history as being academic faux pax's that brought only confusion to the OO
debate.
>
Wow!! That's pretty strong stuff, Joe! I can't go along with that, but I
fully understand how you arrived at it. I find BOTH of these features so
useful that I believe removing them would be like cutting the legs off OO.

I accept your point about implicit assumptions in programs being counter
productive but with OO it is the incremental extension of Classes which
means not having to re-code code that is common. This in turn leads to the
hierarchy.

(I like  the way Java implements this by using the word "extends". For
example (to quote an earlier example I posted here: Class "legs" EXTENDS
cass "Animals" . (No argument about that, I think...<G>)

Now we can talk about four legged, two legged, or any number of legged
animals including animals with NO legs, and we don't have to code all the
attributes of an "Animal". If I instantiate a "dog" obect it will inherit
attributes of "Legs" and "Animal". While these may be implicit, they are
very useful and I don't have to include or re-code them when I start looking
at methods for my "dog" object. (Note that I have NOT defined a "Dog" Class
in this case. This is another useful feature of OO. If I am dealing
primarily with many different animals then it is enough to instantiate them
at the "legs" level. If I was dealing with the history of Breeds of Dog,
then I might EXTEND the "legs" class  by adding "Dogs" (or cats or snakes or
anything else...). I could also set up a Class for "Training". Personally, I
wouldn't make this part of the hierarchy (but some people would, then
override the methods  when they were not applicable). Instead I would
INTERFACE to this class from objects where it made sense... NOT "Fish" or
"Cockroaches" for example, but certainly "Dogs"...

I believe your objection to implicit assumptions in code is valid, but with
OO you should really consider the WHOLE hierarchy as ONE large logical
domain.

It is a powerful argument for considering very carefully the size, nature,
structure, and "logical progression" of Class hierarchies BEFORE committing
to code.

Using OO does NOT remove the need for careful design; it's just that an OO
approach allows this design to develop naturally,with collections of like
attributes being grouped as Classes.

Anyway, thanks for a thought provoking post, Joe. It certainly got me
thinking.

Pete.


Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A case for multiple inheritence.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Joseph J Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2001-06-25T23:39:34+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B375B36.8010901@iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<bfdfc3e8.0106220918.6e54b419@posting.google.com> <qnVY6.8803$Md.2096194@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <3b3523fa_1@Usenet.com> <3B3605DC.10706@iinet.net.au> <3b368cb0_2@Usenet.com>`

```
Thanks Peter,

See my comments below:

Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:

>

>  It is also important to realise that Thane did NOT propose it as a really
> serious model of inheritance. (At least, I don't think he did...<G>) Various
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Yes, it also pointed out the fundamental problem of heirarchies, where do I put 
my class in the heirarchy? In fact this is not a correct question in this case. 
The domain of things that fly with more or less stiff wings is no way related to 
the domain of things that drop lick a rock and go boom, other than the flying 
things can carry the dropping things.

This does not mean that a correct solution is to put the bombs class under the 
plane class. They are two different but equal domains that need an interface to 
cooperate to achieve the desired results.

> 
> Wow!! That's pretty strong stuff, Joe! I can't go along with that, but I
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> hierarchy.

Yes, that works whist you are within a domain.

> 
<snip> long discussion of animals and legs </snip>

Ok, so where would you put the animal domain in the heirarchy?

Personally, I don't believe it belongs anywhere in the supplied heirarchies. 
These heirarchies are generally related to the programming domain and involve 
computer related classes - collections, OS, ... none of which are related to 
animals.

Animals are a NEW domain, so you start them off the highest hierarchy class - 
possibly OBJECT. Now, how do you inherit the collections class for linked lists, 
or the OS class for file definitions?

You need to be able to import the collections link lists class and it's entire 
heirarchy as well as the OS file classes.

Or better yet - you create interfaces from the new domain - animals - to the 
collections domain and the OS domain.

Now we are back to modular coding - with the modules being represented by 
domains and the interfaces representing the calling API's.

 
> I believe your objection to implicit assumptions in code is valid, but with
> OO you should really consider the WHOLE hierarchy as ONE large logical
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> to code.


But why should I have to. If I create a new domain, I should not have to decide 
where in the heirarchy is best! for this implementation of the domain.
The whole idea of OO is reuse. When I create a new domain, I will inevitably 
need to use classes from the programming domains (collections, file, etc). I 
should be able to declare what I need from the other domains. Then I would not 
need to create interfaces for these domains, and could concentration on 
interfacing to other user domains (accounting - for a pet shop, etc).


> 
> Using OO does NOT remove the need for careful design; it's just that an OO
> approach allows this design to develop naturally,with collections of like
> attributes being grouped as Classes.
> 

But careful design is not required. I'm a fan of extreme programming or lean 
programming or just in time programming. The philosophy behind these techniques 
is to not design for the future for the future may not come. So the only 
thinking that needs to be done is to design the domain we are currently 
concerned with. This means that we should not have to care about any other 
domain. I don't care about collections of linked lists or whether I should 
subordinate my domain to the linked list class. I'm only interested in using the 
programming domain to re-use the logic encapsulated there.

To achieve this, some thought should be given to the way domains relate to each 
other. Which is NEVER heirarchical by definition. Domains may need to import the 
classes of other domains to achieve the desired result. This has not been 
addressed in ANY OO language to my satisfaction at this time.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: A case for multiple inheritence.

- **From:** klatteross@aol.commmm (Ross Klatte)
- **Date:** 2001-06-23T12:50:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010623085025.05276.00000568@ng-ci1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<qnVY6.8803$Md.2096194@typhoon.southeast.rr.com>`

```
>From: "Schroeder" jfriedman@nc.rr.com 
>Date: 2001-06-23 01:00 Eastern Daylight Time
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>application to take advantage of a new heirarchy is not.

It is the concept of Inheritance which most bothers me 
about Object Orientation.  
For example, if I code: 
    class Airplane
        method Fly
    class Fighter extends Airplane
        method Shoot
    class Fighter-Bomber extends Fighter implements Bombs
        method DropBomb (overriding "Bombs" DropBomb method?)
    class F117 extends Fighter-Bomber
        method BeStealthy
then I have created a *sequential* structure.  
If I invoke F117 I am going to get everything that's in 
Airplane, Fighter, and Fighter-Bomber whether I want it or 
not.  
If an engineer designs a plane called the F117Passive which 
is identical to the F117 except that it doesn't Shoot, then 
I am stuck.  My only recourse is to add an empty Shoot 
method to the F117Passive to override the Fighter's 
aggressive Shoot method.  
    class F117 extends Fighter-Bomber
        method BeStealthy
        method Shoot (do nothing)
In order to know to do this, I must know the contents of 
every single inherited class.  It is exactly the same as a 
big ol' Cobol program.  
    copy "Airplane" working-storage code
    copy "Bombs" working-storage code
    copy "Fighter" working-storage code
    copy "Fighter-Bomber" working-storage code
    copy "F117" working-storage code
    if value-airplane
        perform Airplane
    if value-fighter
        perform Airplane thru Fighter
    if value-fighter-bomber
        perform Airplane thru Fighter-Bomber
            (the Fighter-Bomber paragraph calls a DropBomb library routine)
    if value-F117
        perform Airplane thru Be-Stealthy

It seems to me that an "object oriented" structure ought to require:  
Methods library
    method Fly
    method Shoot
    method DropBomb
    method BeStealthy
    method TakePills
Classes library
    class Airplane
        call method Fly
    class Fighter
        call method Fly
        call method Shoot
    class Fighter-Bomber
        call method Fly
        call method Shoot
        call method DropBomb
    class F117
        call method Fly
        call method Shoot
        call method DropBomb
        call method BeStealthy
    class F117Passive
        call method Fly
        call method DropBomb
        call method BeStealthy
If the engineer tells me he has designed a high-tech 
18-wheel killer, I can code:
    class WarTruck
        call method Shoot
        call method DropBomb
        call method BeStealthy
        call method TakePills

To me, inheritance wrecks the value of Object Orientation 
in the same way that PERFORM THRU wrecks the value of 
structured code.  



Ross
http://www.geocities.com/ross_klatte/
```

```
F117P without the extra overhead using an interface.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A case for multiple inheritence.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-24T18:05:02+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B361DBE.6225F82D@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<qnVY6.8803$Md.2096194@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <20010623085025.05276.00000568@ng-ci1.aol.com> <3b34ccac.98010416@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:

> >    class F117 extends Fighter-Bomber
> >        method BeStealthy

Whenever I see an OO example like this I just 'turn off'. I mean _one_
of us has completely missed the point of writing a program.  An 'F117'
is an instance of an aeroplane, or maybe even a class of aeroplane, but
shouldn't be an instance of an object in a program let alone a class.

The class may be 'Stealth-Fighter-Bomber extends Fighter-Bomber' but
'F117' should only be the value of an attribute for those that are
F117s.

One example using Payroll that I saw had code using 'Bob' in the source
code so the example could be:  Bob.salary.  Excuse me, is this program
intended to be rewritten if 'Bob' is fired and 'Dennis' is employed ?  

Will you rewrite your class hierarchy when the F117s are taken out of
service ?

Also 'BeStealthy' is not a _method_ of the actual object, it is an
attibute of its design.
```
