###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A case for multiple inheritence.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-06-25T10:53:47-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0106250953.2d494fb1@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<qnVY6.8803$Md.2096194@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <20010623085025.05276.00000568@ng-ci1.aol.com> <3b34ccac.98010416@news1.attglobal.net> <3B361DBE.6225F82D@Azonic.co.nz> <3b35e1fa_3@Usenet.com>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3b35e1fa_3@Usenet.com>...

> > > >    class F117 extends Fighter-Bomber
> > > >        method BeStealthy
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


Actually to be fair, I was not the one who wrote ANYTHING that says
"EXTENDS".  That is not OO COBOL syntax, and in fact I offered no
syntax.  Some points though:

On the payroll example - Bob and Dennis are different instances of an
object, not separate classes.

F117 - however, *IS* a class.  You can have different INSTANCES of
F117's.  When one crashes you lose the instance, the class maintains
validity.

As far as rewriting the class heirarchy when F117s go out of service: 
No, if some other aircraft is derived from the F117 (F117B, P, T, etc)
then it remains.  If the new aircraft does not derive from the F117
then it's just a separate branch of the tree and no longer falls UNDER
the F117, but rather probably exists at the same level.

PS - my formal apology for murdering the English language in my
original post and subsequent quotations and expansions of said post. 
It's inheritAnce.  Sorry.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A case for multiple inheritence.

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-26T07:21:46+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B3829FA.1514B6A8@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<qnVY6.8803$Md.2096194@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <20010623085025.05276.00000568@ng-ci1.aol.com> <3b34ccac.98010416@news1.attglobal.net> <3B361DBE.6225F82D@Azonic.co.nz> <3b35e1fa_3@Usenet.com> <bfdfc3e8.0106250953.2d494fb1@posting.google.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> On the payroll example - Bob and Dennis are different instances of an
> object, not separate classes.

There may well be instances of employees that contain data for "Bob" and
"Dennis" but these names were used in the source code as the names of
the instances.  The implication is that the program requires rewriting
everytime an employee leaves or joins.  "Bob" and "Dennis" should only
ever appear as the value of an attribute (data item).

I don't rewrite my clients order systems whenever they have a new
product or a new customer just so there can be an instance called
'widget', the use of attribute values as instance names shows some sort
of dissonance between program and data.
 
> F117 - however, *IS* a class.  You can have different INSTANCES of
> F117's.  When one crashes you lose the instance, the class maintains
> validity.

Then I think that one of us has completely missed the point of writing
programs.  If _I_ were to write an aircraft inventory program then I
would make sure that it is not specific to the types of aircraft at a
particular time.  "F117" is the value of an attribute that may be, say,
'Service-Designation' within those instances of aircraft that are
actually F117s.

Stealth is an attribute, the ability to carry bombs is an attribute,
these may be complex attributes that require, say, interfacing.

Other, later, aircraft types may well fit exactly the same functionality
as that of the current F117, will these new McDonnel F132 aircraft be
put into the F117 class ? or will you then rewrite the program to
inheret a new F132 class for them from the F117 class, or from the same
class that the F117 inherets from ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A case for multiple inheritence.

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-06-26T09:41:54-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0106260841.245cb673@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<qnVY6.8803$Md.2096194@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <20010623085025.05276.00000568@ng-ci1.aol.com> <3b34ccac.98010416@news1.attglobal.net> <3B361DBE.6225F82D@Azonic.co.nz> <3b35e1fa_3@Usenet.com> <bfdfc3e8.0106250953.2d494fb1@posting.google.com> <3B3829FA.1514B6A8@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
See Below....

Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3B3829FA.1514B6A8@Azonic.co.nz>...
> Thane Hubbell wrote:
> > 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I must have missed that point.  I agree of course that Bob and Dennis
are NOT classes - they are instances of a class, and DO have object
references, but not NAMED ones in the source code.
 
> I don't rewrite my clients order systems whenever they have a new
> product or a new customer just so there can be an instance called
> 'widget', the use of attribute values as instance names shows some sort
> of dissonance between program and data.
>  

Of course you don't - but your program does create another "instance"
of the "widget" object.

> > F117 - however, *IS* a class.  You can have different INSTANCES of
> > F117's.  When one crashes you lose the instance, the class maintains
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

This is a matter of perspective.  Are you tracking TYPES of airplanes
or ACTUAL Airplanes?  If we are tracking actual airplanes, each
individual serial numbered aircraft is an instance of an object as
described in Class F117.

> Stealth is an attribute, the ability to carry bombs is an attribute,
> these may be complex attributes that require, say, interfacing.
>

Agree - and since they are attributes of the object they should be
described in the objects class.  Stealth and bombs are things that can
be attributes of other objects as well, so why not make them separate
classes (objects) and inherit them where necessary?  Interfaces are a
waste here because you still have to implement them.  Inheriting the
actual class means you don't have to implement it (read code
ADDITIONAL source code).
 
> Other, later, aircraft types may well fit exactly the same functionality
> as that of the current F117, will these new McDonnel F132 aircraft be
> put into the F117 class ? or will you then rewrite the program to
> inheret a new F132 class for them from the F117 class, or from the same
> class that the F117 inherets from ?

No, the F132 might inherit from all the same base classes that the
F117 does, but will have some different properties.  I would only
inherit from the base F117 class for different types of F117's.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A case for multiple inheritence.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-27T19:01:09+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B3A1F65.D134A040@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<qnVY6.8803$Md.2096194@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <20010623085025.05276.00000568@ng-ci1.aol.com> <3b34ccac.98010416@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
> This is a matter of perspective.  Are you tracking TYPES of airplanes
> or ACTUAL Airplanes?  If we are tracking actual airplanes, each
> individual serial numbered aircraft is an instance of an object as
> described in Class F117.

Why would you create an instance for each individual aircraft at all ? 
It seems that you want to create the 'inventory' by running the program,
rather than having it stored in a database.

Will you also have in your hierarchy:

        CLASS Supermarine-Spitfire EXTENDS Propeller-Fighter
        CLASS P51-Mustang          EXTENDS ..
        CLASS F86-Sabre            EXTENDS Jet-Fighter
        CLASS ..

for several thousand different types ?

If not then why is F117 so special as to deserve its own CLASS ?
 

> > Other, later, aircraft types may well fit exactly the same functionality
> > as that of the current F117, will these new McDonnel F132 aircraft be
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> inherit from the base F117 class for different types of F117's.

So you really would rewrite your program just because there is a new
model of aircraft ?

In my order systems I do not have to rewrite the system just because
there is a new customer:

       Acme-sales = NEW Customer

"Acme Sales" is a data value for the customer name.

"F117" and "F132" are the data values for an aircraft designation.

Your ideas of 'abstraction' seem to be quite different from mine.

Also, if the 'different type of F117' were, as is likely, an ECM version
(just as the E6B Prowler Intruder developed an ECM version with no
bombing capability at all) then it would be completely inappropriate to
have this associated with the existing F117 class which is well down the
tree in 'fighter-bombers'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A case for multiple inheritence.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2001-06-27T09:52:03-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0106270852.10659677@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<qnVY6.8803$Md.2096194@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <20010623085025.05276.00000568@ng-ci1.aol.com> <3b34ccac.98010416@news1.attglobal.net> <3B3A1F65.D134A040@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3B3A1F65.D134A040@Azonic.co.nz>...
> > This is a matter of perspective.  Are you tracking TYPES of airplanes
> > or ACTUAL Airplanes?  If we are tracking actual airplanes, each
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Yes - I would, but only if I am tracking an inventory of actual
aircraft.

> > > Other, later, aircraft types may well fit exactly the same functionality
> > > as that of the current F117, will these new McDonnel F132 aircraft be
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

A new model of aircraft is not the same as a new customer.  The same
thing would be another serial number of the same model.   Acme-Sales
is now an instance of the customer class - it is not a new class.

> "F117" and "F132" are the data values for an aircraft designation.
> 
> Your ideas of 'abstraction' seem to be quite different from mine.
> 

It's a matter of perspective.  I think I am going for a lower level
than you are.

> Also, if the 'different type of F117' were, as is likely, an ECM version
> (just as the E6B Prowler Intruder developed an ECM version with no
> bombing capability at all) then it would be completely inappropriate to
> have this associated with the existing F117 class which is well down the
> tree in 'fighter-bombers'.

One might make a case for creating a new class then that inherits the
same things as the F117  - with the exception of the bombing class. 
Which is why the multiple inheritence works so well.

----
We are debating class structure and while the example is not perfect,
there are a couple of different ways to look at this.

Another way is to think of an "item" as a collection of objects RATHER
than a set of inherited objects.  This is where collections come into
the picture.  Maybe we make our F117 by combining an instance of a
bomb object, an instance of a stealth object, an instance of a jet
aircraft military object, etc etc...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A case for multiple inheritence.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Grinder" <no.spam@no.spam.spam.spam.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-27T19:56:28-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5ev_6.30464$f4.1292125@e420r-atl1.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<qnVY6.8803$Md.2096194@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <20010623085025.05276.00000568@ng-ci1.aol.com> <3b34ccac.98010416@news1.attglobal.net> <3B3A1F65.D134A040@Azonic.co.nz> <bfdfc3e8.0106270852.10659677@posting.google.com>`

```
> > If not then why is F117 so special as to deserve its own CLASS ?

> Yes - I would, but only if I am tracking an inventory of actual
> aircraft.

Both scenarios, in differen contexts, are correct.  I've your writing
an aircraft encyclopedia, the details of any specific model can
probably be generalized into a class rather than having an actual P51
class.  On the other hand, if you're writing a flight simulator,
there's probably enough specific information about that model to
warrant it's own class.

> > Why would you create an instance for each individual aircraft at
all ?
> > It seems that you want to create the 'inventory' by running the
program,
> > rather than having it stored in a database.

I hope you won't see this as nit-picking, but the language employed
here is a bit cross-pollenated.  Instancing is typically the act of
using a class to create (into memory) an object.  So from your
position in the debate, you would not design a class for each specific
model, but rather set properties on an instance of a class to identify
that model:

   oAirplane = New Fighter
   oAirplane.Model = "P51 Mustang"

Sorry about the syntax--I'm not familiar with OO Cobol, and have
resorted to using a pseudo OOP language.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A case for multiple inheritence.

- **From:** "Grinder" <no.spam@no.spam.spam.spam.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-23T13:20:43-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c25Z6.2221$f6.99409@e420r-atl2.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<qnVY6.8803$Md.2096194@typhoon.southeast.rr.com> <20010623085025.05276.00000568@ng-ci1.aol.com>`

```

"Ross Klatte" <klatteross@aol.commmm> wrote in message
news:20010623085025.05276.00000568@ng-ci1.aol.com...
> >From: "Schroeder" jfriedman@nc.rr.com
> >Date: 2001-06-23 01:00 Eastern Daylight Time
> >Message-id: <qnVY6.8803$Md.2096194@typhoon.southeast.rr.com>
> >
> >It is Especially useful for COBOL application which are long lived.
Most C++
> >programs (or Java, etc..) tend to be short lived applications
(certainly by
> >comparison);  I have COBOL programs still running with DATE-WRITTEN
> >paragraphs specifying 1959!   Things change over a long period of
time -
> >especially inheritance heirarchies.  A particular functionality
which this
> >program needs may have been moved to a new class to go along with a
> >corporate restructing or takeover.  Inheriting it from where it
currently
> >lives is an easy maintenance thing to do.  Redesigning the entire
> >application to take advantage of a new heirarchy is not.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>         method DropBomb (overriding "Bombs" DropBomb method?)

Technically, DropBomb doesn't override anything, it implements
the interface defined in Bombs.

>     class F117 extends Fighter-Bomber
>         method BeStealthy
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>         method Shoot (do nothing)

How about:
        class Airplane
             method Fly
        class Stealth-Airplane extends Airplane
             method BeStealthy
        class Fighter extends Airplane
             method Shoot
        class Fighter-Bomber extends Fighter implements Bombs
             method DropBomb
        class Stealth-Bomber extends Stealth-Airplane extends Bombs
             method DropBomb

Your F117 would be a Fighter-Bomber, and the F117Passive
would be a Stealth-Bomber.  If it's not a Fighter why inherit
from that base?

> In order to know to do this, I must know the contents of
> every single inherited class.  It is exactly the same as a
> big ol' Cobol program.

Even if you inherit methods that you don't want implemented,
you only have to know the hierarchy's structure--not it's contents.
```

---

<a id="t-7fc8e1b594"></a>
## Thread: Programming Business Applications — 14 msgs

### Programming Business Applications

- **From:** Foodman <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-22T18:45:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GnMY6.5437$yp1.199359@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Hi:

When you look at some of the samples posted by people taking
COBOL courses, it becomes apparent, to me at least, that these
people are not being taught how to program but how to code.

Using the unfortunate Deek as an example (this is NOT to
criticize Deek but the people who are 'teaching' him). If
he had a decent teacher who knew what they were teaching, it
would not be necessary for Deek to resort to seeking help
from the forum. (Does Deek resort to the forum because his 
teacher is hopeless, or what?)

The latest sample posted by Deek appears to be some sort
of report program since it has heading and detail lines
in WORKING-STORAGE. Presumably written in approved 
structured style, the first part of the PROCEDURE DIVISION
says:

MAINLINE.
PERFORM 100-INITIALIZATION.
PERFORM 200-PROCESSING.
PERFORM 300-TERMINATION.
STOP RUN.

Isn't this an awful oversimplification of the logic required by 
Deek's recently-posted 'Project 5'?

At present, Deek's program is far from complete. The likelihood of
him ever getting a thoroughly professional job done on this program
is remote because he hasn't the faintest idea of what the program
NEEDS TO DO and I don't see anybody showing him what he needs to 
know. Again this is not to criticize Deek but his teachers. 

Put yourself in his position. You know NOTHING about computers,
COBOL or business application design. How the heck are you
supposed to know about little things like breaking control,
page headings, checking for invalid conditions, etc. if
you have NO experience. How do you know that minor totals
have to be printed before major totals, etc.? How do you know
how to design records which lend themselves to efficient coding?
You don't.

You can see that the teachers show their students how the 
various divisions are coded and the syntax, etc. They are
probably taught the approved structured method of coding
using PERFORMs, and the extremely important business of
having only one period per paragraph which always goes on
a separate line.

But, do they teach them how to PROGRAM?

In the olden days, there used to be programmers and coders.
The programmer would design the program and the coder would
write the code from the flow chart prepared by the programmer.

Certainly, before you sit down to write a program you have
to decide what it is going to actually do. Does the absence
of a page-counter and line-counter in Deek's W-S mean
he doesn't know he will need them? Does he really know
all the things his program must consider? Unlikely.

Deek's problem is that he has never been taught the things
which he needs to be taught. He will learn the hard way -
like most programmers - either that or he will give up.
The typical cycle of program completion for students 
like Deek is to write something, find out that it is
lacking, stick something in to fix the lack, find another
one, stick in more code, find another one, stick in 
more code and end up with a kludge. If the student 
knew in advance about all these crucial details, they would 
be incorporated in the first version, somehow. But how?

When I used to hire programmers, the first thing I would
ask of them is to draw me a flow chart for a simple report
with two levels of control. If you were to ask Deek or
any other student to do this, what would you get? For sure,
you would get umpteen variations ranging from the ridiculous
but not to the sublime.

Another thing which Deek won't be taught is that most of
the programs he may ever write will be all basically the same
without regard to application or the nature of the enterprise.
All report programs have page counts and line counts and print
headings, break control, and so on but at Deek's stage of 
development he probably does not realize this.

If Deek perseveres with 'Project 5', he will learn about
all the little details as his work progresses. Unfortunately,
again, this will require sticking in code to fix all the shortcomings 
which should have been recognized before he started. 

It seems a shame, but these days people don't seem to consider
flow charting part of being a COBOL programmer. Structured
programming seems to have eliminated flow-charting. As someone
said in some other thread when asked about flow-charting, 
'no, we just sit down and start typing'. That may be fine
for someone who has lots of experience but it is not the way
for Deek or any other student.

Anyway, I have prepared a Web-page which may be of help
to Deek and any one else who wants to be a business COBOL
programmer.  Just remember, this is not about coding, it is
about programming.

To see it, go to: foodman123.com/flow2.htm

Thanks

Tony Dilworth
```

#### ↳ Re: Programming Business Applications

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-22T21:55:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B33BE7F.C5C01C32@home.com>`
- **References:** `<GnMY6.5437$yp1.199359@www.newsranger.com>`

```


Foodman wrote:

> Hi:
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Tony,

I'm not going to follow your reasoning through blow by blow and you make
good points. Reflect, COBOL is taught at schools - how - theoretically it
might look as follows :-

        Year 1 - Introductory COBOL
        Year 2 - File handling
        Year 3 - Advanced Techniques (Yes - maybe even some OO <G>)

That sort of scenario - we expect the guy to come out knowing his 'biz'.
But I betcha for the most part it reads as follows :-

Year 1 - COBOL
Year 2 - OK guys we've got over that mess. Let's get into the real
interesting         stuff, C++, Java or both.........
Year 3 - You pass out with honours knowing all the nifty tricks about
windowing etc. but you haven't got a bloody clue what the difference is
between a debit and a credit. (Less elegantly, but perhaps more germane -
they don't know their arse from their elbow !)

And it is all tied into MONEY. The academics know they are on a winner
pushing the language du jour and the poor sod trying to teach COBOL has a
real fight on his hands - and is in great danger of tenure when the
senior 'bods' decide to drop COBOL all together.

Of course there's the old adage, "Them that can't, teach". Not totally
fair, but I can most CERTAINLY think of one candidate in Calgary that
applied to, before the college gave up on COBOL. My summation of his
expertise - a bloody idiot !

I'd really like to hear the comments of two who we know teach COBOL -
Lloyd and the pussy-cat man in Germany, our good friend Volker.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Business Applications

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-23T10:45:50+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B34654E.3073BCA6@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<GnMY6.5437$yp1.199359@www.newsranger.com> <3B33BE7F.C5C01C32@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
> Of course there's the old adage, "Them that can't, teach". Not totally
> fair, but I can most CERTAINLY think of one candidate in Calgary that
> applied to, before the college gave up on COBOL. My summation of his
> expertise - a bloody idiot !

"Those that can, do, those that can't, teach." is not entirely a
criticism of teachers. I also take it to mean (or indeed use it to mean,
but they may not notice) a criticism of 'doers'.  

Often 'those that do' forget entirely what it was like to not be able to
do and assume that the fundementals are so obvious that they never need
be even mentioned.  When doers are used as teachers the students are
left completely confused and disheartened. It is only much later when
those students that struggle on are finally given the basics that they
make any sense of what the 'doers' were saying.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Business Applications

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-23T12:08:28+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B3478AC.EB303435@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<GnMY6.5437$yp1.199359@www.newsranger.com> <3B33BE7F.C5C01C32@home.com>`

```
Foodman wrote:
>
> When you look at some of the samples posted by people taking
> COBOL courses, it becomes apparent, to me at least, that these
> people are not being taught how to program but how to code.

One may also construct a criticism of, say, English being taught in
primary schools.  They are teaching them to be scribes but not how to be
novelists.  Or in 'Aritmetic' they are being taught to be computors
(people who compute) and not to be mathematicians.

To be coders, or scribes, or computors, they need to understand the
_language_, and that is what they are being taught.  To be programmers
or novelists or mathematicians they need to know completely different
things as well, things that they must learn on other courses or through
experience in working with the languages and in life or work experience.

It is a progression, one in which the fundementals must first be taught
and then built upon.


> Another thing which Deek won't be taught is that most of
> the programs he may ever write will be all basically the same
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> development he probably does not realize this.

Having seen some of your previous discussions on your programming
mechanisms it doesn't surprise me that you think that this is the case. 
Certainly it seems that you mostly rewrite the same programs over and
over, in fact you have said this to be the case.

However, I haven't written any code connected directly with "page counts
and line counts and print headings, break control" for many years. I
would hope that at the appropriate time he is taught that he should
_not_ be writing programs 'all basically the same', that is a complete
waste of expensive resources.

I got very tired of rewriting the same report and maintenance programs
early on in my career in fact when I moved from operations to
programming, 30 years ago, the very first production program that I
wrote from scratch was an invoice print that I developed in two distinct
parts: a reusable framework that handled all the file access and form
handling logic and a set of specific modules for page headings, product
item lines, page totals, invoice totals, etc.

20 years ago I started development of my 'synthetic system' which
includes a 'report writer' to handle the majority of enquiries and
reports for my systems.  This reads a simple report script and uses
dictionaries to determine where the data comes from.  In many cases the
users can construct their own reports, mostly it takes me a few minutes
to write the script to suit their requirements, occationally I add code
into the program to cope with specific cases.  There is a GUI front end
that allows 'click'ing a new new report from selection lists.

The 'synthetic system' also includes a table driven maintenance program
so that the basics of a new system can be built entirely by building
some dictionaries, screen layout tables, report scripts and other system
control text items.  The procesing programs are then added.

While you seem to prefer the rewriting of the same set of programs I
prefer to use one set of programs in each new environment.  This also
has the benefit that old systems get updated when I add in new features
for a new system.  

Another approach is to write a 'program generator', which I have also
done.  This allows repetitive programs to be expressed as text scripts,
such as print-line layouts, dictionaries, screen layouts and such and
then to generate the code from templates.
```

#### ↳ Re: Programming Business Applications

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-23T17:06:38+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b342643_6@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<GnMY6.5437$yp1.199359@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Well, Tony, I went and looked at your Web Page.

It was like a walk down memory lane...

Nothing wrong with what you have done (in fact, I was moved to see the
clearly noted exits from decision boxes which I remember having drummed into
me in 1965, and I still do this on the rare occasions when I draw a
flowchart for a student) but it is a bit like Thomas Edison publishing his
design  for a light bulb on the World Wide Web, without ever knowing that
the lights used to read it will be neon, and the light bulb of today is many
generations removed from his original.

I'm not going to attack your mail (there is no honour in destroying easy
targets) but I have made a few observations below...

Pete.

"Foodman" <nospam@newsranger.com> wrote in message
news:GnMY6.5437$yp1.199359@www.newsranger.com...
> Hi:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> teacher is hopeless, or what?)

Deek, like many students, "resorts" to the forum because he gets support and
help from it (most of the time). It would be quite wrong to assume that any
student posting questions in the NG was suffering from "poor tuition"

>
> The latest sample posted by Deek appears to be some sort
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I don't personally like the style above, but I wouldn't criticise it on the
grounds of being "oversimple".

Surely, any form of simplification is a "Good Thing". At least it is clear.
It is a Top Down statement of the control logic for his program. Can't be
bad.

You recently denigrated some code I posted here and proposed your own
alternative. You then pressed for a vote, and I think not a single one
favoured your approach. Does that not tell you anything?
Could it be that the problem you perceive with the above is in YOUR
perception of it?

> At present, Deek's program is far from complete. The likelihood of
> him ever getting a thoroughly professional job done on this program
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Have you ever asked yourself about the rest of Deek's class? If his
assignment is far from complete there may be factors other than his not
being instructed correctly at work here.


> Put yourself in his position. You know NOTHING about computers,
> COBOL or business application design. How the heck are you
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Learning the Language is the first step toward learning how to program.
There are NO courses to my knowledge that teach people how to program. In
fact there is an argument that says it CANNOT be taught... (It may be an ART
rather than a Science...)

Can you teach someone how to paint Masterpieces? No, but you can give them
the basics on use of paint and canvas, some stylistic techniques like
composition, shade and perspective and try to motivate them to do the best
they can. Gradually, as they acquire experience and confidence, their
paintings will improve and, eventually, they may even produce a Masterpiece.

If and when they do, it is NOT the teacher who will get credit for it. (And
it is right that the teacher shouldn't...)

Now, you could insist that all painting is done by numbers and the only
merit in the work would be how carefully the student has concealed the
underlying guide lines and completely filled in the boxes. A very strict and
rigid approach....oops! I forgot who I'm speaking to... OK 'nuff said.


> In the olden days, there used to be programmers and coders.

And there used to be sailing ships and sealing wax... most of us moved on
and no longer require these commodities.

>
> The programmer would design the program and the coder would
> write the code from the flow chart prepared by the programmer.
>

Sadly, I remember this. It happened in Auckland at NCR in 1966. Funny how
the "programmers" were men, and the "coders" were girls (who aspired to be
programmers...) NCR no longer employ this programming development model and
neither does anyone else. So why are you supporting it as "the right way to
do things"?

<snippage of analysis as to what Deek's problem is...>

> Another thing which Deek won't be taught is that most of
> the programs he may ever write will be all basically the same
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Well, it's certainly true that the programs YOU write are basically the
same. It's like the guitarist who played a single note over and over. His
wife, finding her patience tried beyond endurance, asked him: "Why don't you
move your fingers around like other guitarists?" He replied: "They're
looking for the place; I've found it..."

> If Deek perseveres with 'Project 5', he will learn about
> all the little details as his work progresses. Unfortunately,
> again, this will require sticking in code to fix all the shortcomings
> which should have been recognized before he started.
>
See, there's nothing wrong with what you are saying here... no-one would
reasonably object to sitting down and thinking something through before
starting to program it, and we do. But am I going to defer coding until
every field in working-storage has been identified and defined? I don't
think so...

In the days when it cost huge amounts of money to compile a program and
sites were often remote so that getting a compile could take a day or two,
it made sense to work meticulously through program logic (besides, there
wasn't much else we COULD do while hanging around waiting for the listings)
but now, when a compile takes seconds and costs pennies it is more
productive to "debug on the machine". (Especially if "the machine" happens
to be a PC with a processor dedicated to you and no-one else...)

I am not advocating having no idea of what is required before starting, but
I seriously question the need for 35 year old techniques in today's
programming environment..  Despite your antipathy to structured code (and,
by implication, structure diagrams) I believe these are far more useful for
Students than detailed "flow" charts.

Why? Because a flowchart does exactly that; it charts the sequential flow of
logic. It does NOT encourage a student to think in terms of "functions" and
encapsulation. Structure diagrams do. These days I use a flowchart only to
demonstrate the internals of a given module, where it is not immediately
obvious or it needs to be clarified. If modules and functions are kept small
(and they should be) then personally, I would have no objection to a student
coding them and debugging them on-line. Only if it got into a tangled mess
would I advocate the use of a flowchart and the rewriting of THAT particular
function. Strangely, this doesn't seem to happen with the students I
tutor....

Finally, in this day and age it seems ludicrous to me to be tying COBOL to a
green lineflo page and assuming that it will be 11 inches, printed at 6
lines to the inch, in fixed font characters at one tenth of an inch....

We have Word Processors. Why re-invent the wheel?  Send the output document
(that's "printfile" to you, Tony...) to MS Word or AmiPro or Word Perfect
using OLE Automation (and, yes, you CAN send output from a Mainframe program
in this way across the Corporate Lan) and let the User select his own font,
and while he's at it he can decide whether he wants to print it, e-mail it,
FAX it or save it on disk. Set up the appropriate templates and Bob's your
Uncle, you need never code "IF LINE-COUNT > 55 " again and you don't need to
lose sleep over it not appearing in the working-storage of your (or Deek's)
program.

> It seems a shame, but these days people don't seem to consider
> flow charting part of being a COBOL programmer.

Yeah, it's real sad. In the "good old days" we used to demonstrate how
senior we were by having a bigger and better flowcharting template with more
ISO boxes on it than the other guys. It was kept in a brief case with our
IBM Green (later, yellow) Card, and we wore suits to work, and discussed the
merits of the TRANSFORM verb and CLASS and USAGE clauses instead of that new
fangled PICTURE.

And how we managed to run our application in 8K by reconfiguring the
"unnecessary" parts of the Operating System to get it down to 4K instead of
6.... It's a real shame those days are gone.... (Well, there are still a few
bastions of IBMism where they still have Green Cards and wear suits to
work...but then, there are still some people who cook on a wood stove...).

Nothing against wood stoves or Green Cards or wearing suits to work, just
that NONE of it makes you a COBOL programmer, any more than knowing how to
flowchart does.

> Structured
> programming seems to have eliminated flow-charting.

Well, don't feel bad. It will be superseded by OO...

> As someone
> said in some other thread when asked about flow-charting,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Who says? And on what basis? The opinion of Tony Dilworth, programmer
extraordinaire, and Mentor of the young? I don't think so....

> Anyway, I have prepared a Web-page which may be of help
> to Deek and any one else who wants to be a business COBOL
> programmer.  Just remember, this is not about coding, it is
> about programming.
>

Now do one about starting your car with a crank handle...


> To see it, go to: foodman123.com/flow2.htm
>
Oh, and by the way, I viewed it with Internet Explorer and it looks fine...
Funny how it doesn't to you Tony, even after 11 uploads... some things are
forever a mystery....Maybe this "new technology" is out of reach for a
certain breed of "COBOL programmers"....

Pete.


Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

>clearly noted exits from decision boxes which I remember having drummed into
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

Hi:

No, it isn't. The logical solution to the stated problem was exemplified
in unit-record equipment. That essential logic will be the same until the
end of time and it will work in every application and every enterprise.

>
>You recently denigrated some code I posted here and proposed your own
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>perception of it?

I said "Mr. Dashwood's post gives us all an opportunity to compare
a structured program with one written in a sensible manner. . . "
I think this is mildly sarcastic but hardly denigration. Mr. Dashwood 
called my version an 'eyesore'.

I did not 'press for a vote' but merely said "If anyone can convince me that the
structured way is better in any objective way, I'll eat your source-code in
Macy's window." 

Needless to say, no one did. . .

>
>Learning the Language is the first step toward learning how to program.
>There are NO courses to my knowledge that teach people how to program. In
>fact there is an argument that says it CANNOT be taught... (It may be an ART
>rather than a Science...)

I don't agree that learning a language is the first step toward learning
how to program. Learning a language is the first step toward learning how
to code. I would agree that there are 'NO courses..that teach people how to
program'.  That is my point. I would say that programming is a craft rather
than an art or science, but you still need the aptitude.

Surely, programming can be taught independently of language or platform, no?
If not, why not?


>Now, you could insist that all painting is done by numbers and the only
>merit in the work would be how carefully the student has concealed the
>underlying guide lines and completely filled in the boxes. A very strict and
>rigid approach....oops! I forgot who I'm speaking to... OK 'nuff said.

You got that right - you should try re-reading the thread 'The Big Picture'. It
works. 

>Well, it's certainly true that the programs YOU write are basically the
>same. It's like the guitarist who played a single note over and over. His
>wife, finding her patience tried beyond endurance, asked him: "Why don't you
>move your fingers around like other guitarists?" He replied: "They're
>looking for the place; I've found it..."

If you don't think that programs which print invoices, receipts, statements,
bills of lading, bills of material, paychecks, labels and thousands of other
'reports' don't employ the same fundamental logic, then I wonder what your
systems look like? 

>See, there's nothing wrong with what you are saying here... no-one would
>reasonably object to sitting down and thinking something through before
>starting to program it, and we do. But am I going to defer coding until
>every field in working-storage has been identified and defined? I don't
>think so...


I don't think so either. Do you think I do? Where did I say that I did?

>I am not advocating having no idea of what is required before starting, but
>I seriously question the need for 35 year old techniques in today's
>programming environment..  Despite your antipathy to structured code (and,
>by implication, structure diagrams) I believe these are far more useful for
>Students than detailed "flow" charts.

Why don't you make one of those diagrams for us so we can compare the two?

This '35 year old technique' is as valid today as then and will be valid
until the end of time. 

>
>Why? Because a flowchart does exactly that; it charts the sequential flow of
>logic. 


Isn't that the whole point? My FlowChart shows them when things need to be 
done, why they need to be done at that time, etc.


>It does NOT encourage a student to think in terms of "functions" and
>encapsulation. 


No, that should be done when being taught how to code.


>Finally, in this day and age it seems ludicrous to me to be tying COBOL to a
>green lineflo page and assuming that it will be 11 inches, printed at 6
>lines to the inch, in fixed font characters at one tenth of an inch....

Where did I say anything about 'green lineflo' page?  Where did I say that
all pages are 11" or that all printed-output is 6 lines to the inch? Where?

However, all printed pages have some sort of limitations such as a line-count
which may be from 5 to 500. There is nothing in my post about not using the
Flexus Formprint, for example. Whether one used Formprint or any other
method of producing sophisticated printed output you still have to read the
file(s), format the page somehow, count the lines, etc. This is basic and
elementary and applies in all cases. Doesn't it?

>Now do one about starting your car with a crank handle...

I don't own a car so wouldn't know how.

>
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>certain breed of "COBOL programmers"....

What a fatuous comment. . .

It is somewhat annoying that you constantly accuse me of saying or doing
things which I have not said or did not do. You accuse me of soliciting
votes, advocating 'greenflo' paper, favoring the division of labor between
programmer and coder, etc., when I have not done so. The reason I mentioned
the olden-days practice of using programmer and coder was to make the point
that the two activites are separate and discrete. I did not advocate that
practice for use today. Your comments seem to represent what you think 
rather than what I actually said.

That flowchart shows the essential logic of the stated problem. It considers
EVERYTHING that should be considered. 

As you said above "Because a flowchart does exactly that; it charts the
sequential flow of logic." 

So, I did what you advocate but you don't like it. 

Since this sort of program is so prevalent in the commercial world, do
the students a favor and provide us with a structured diagram which is
functionally identical to the FlowChart so they won't have to use 35-year
old technology (actually, it is more than 35 years old).

In other words 'put-up' or 'shut-up'.

As in the past, I predict that we will not hear from you. . .

Thanks

Tony Dilworth
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Business Applications

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-24T15:20:59+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B35F74B.6013A3BD@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<GnMY6.5437$yp1.199359@www.newsranger.com> <3b342643_6@Usenet.com> <uk1Z6.6164$yp1.222982@www.newsranger.com> <3b35279b_1@Usenet.com>`

```
> No, it isn't. The logical solution to the stated problem was exemplified
> in unit-record equipment. 

The designing and formalising of Business Systems predates even
unit-record equipment, which was merely a mechanism of automating
existing systems, just as a computer hould be.

> That essential logic will be the same until the
> end of time and it will work in every application and every enterprise.

I think that you have a rather limited view of what may exist in an
application or indeed an enterprise.

> I don't agree that learning a language is the first step toward learning
> how to program. 

Learning the language is the first step towards learning _anything_.  To
learn accounting you first learn the language of accountants, to learn
aeronautical engineering you first learn the language of aeronautics.

Otherwise how can you understand what they are yabbering about ?

> Surely, programming can be taught independently of language or platform, no?
> If not, why not?

Certainly programming can can be taught independently of a specific
computer language, 'Business Programming' was done well before computers
or even unit record equipment and was done using accounting terms and
flowcharts.  To learn how to do this you first learn the language of
accountants and the [symbolic] language of flow-charting.

There are books about programming and algorithms which are not tied to a
specific coputer language - they use pseudo-language.  First you learn
this pseudo-language, then you move on to the algorithms.

> If you don't think that programs which print invoices, receipts, statements,
> bills of lading, bills of material, paychecks, labels and thousands of other
> 'reports' don't employ the same fundamental logic, then I wonder what your
> systems look like? 

What my systems look like is that there is _one_ 'print form' program. 
This is invoked with parameters speifying what is required such as the
form name.  The form template (a text file or a postscript file) is
opened and the tags within that are processed to produce to appropriate
document.  The tags specifiy the fields required and the looping and
stepping mechanisms involved in producing lists, such as invoice lines.

There is also one 'print report' program that uses report scripts and
dictionaries to direct it as to which file to use and what fields are to
be printed, accumulated, calculated, selected on and sorted on.

The argument isn't about whether the systems produce invoices,
statements, labels, etc but whether you have to sit down and rewrite yet
another copy of the same program to do a task which is only trivially
different from one already done.

You like writing the program yet again because perhaps, you count your
achievements in numbers of lines of code or numbers of programs.  I like
to develop just one program to suit each variety of tasks because of
many reasons, including it being far more efficient of my time.

It also allows the user to control what the reports are and what the
forms look like.  If they want a different invoice layout then it is
only necessary to change the template to suit.

BTW I have been doing this since I was developing for 8 bit 8085 based
machines and there has _never_ been an issue with machine performance in
doing this (except the compile times were a bit ponderous but then this
multi-tasked while I got on with other things).

> >Despite your antipathy to structured code (and,
> >by implication, structure diagrams) I believe these are far more useful for
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> until the end of time. 

Flow charting is only useful if you have previously learnt that
particular [symbolic] language.  It is actually a much older technique
than '35 years' and was adopted for unit record equipment and early
computers because it was the language that 'Business Systems' people
used when formalising data flow and processing requirements in a
business.  (My father was involved with designing Business Systems in
the 1950s around business forms and ledgers).

Flow Charts are just one language that could be used to express
procedures, and not a particularly good one for computer programs.  When
used to design a Business System the Flow Chart _was_ the system, it was
the mechnism of expressing how the system functioned and how individuals
had to operate on the data.  It was because this language was widely
understood that it was used to express computer mechanisms, however,
that didn't mean that it was or still is appropriate.  

Again, it is necessary to learn the language before it can be applied. 
(A factor that you seem to ignore because, perhaps, you think it is so
'obvious' that people could just start using it - something that would
make you a rather poor teacher IMHO).

I used to prefer to use pseudo-code to sketch out the problem (another
language that must be learnt first, but is rather simple).  However, my
'pseudo-code' happens to look very much like Cobol, and in fact can be
compiled, too.  It gets developed as the problem is refined and turns
into the program code.

> Whether one used Formprint or any other
> method of producing sophisticated printed output you still have to read the
> file(s), format the page somehow, count the lines, etc. This is basic and
> elementary and applies in all cases. Doesn't it?

It only applies in 'all' cases if you insist on rewriting yet another
copy of the program that is trivially different from the last.  'Count
the lines' ?  it gets done everytime, but I haven't had to _program_
that for years, it gets done it some part of the code that is invoked
whenever I 'PERFORM Print-a-Line' that was written many years ago, I
never have to _worry_ about it, and I certainly don't intend rewriting
it.

Haven't you ever heard of 'reusable code' ?
```

"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

> <snip>

> Deek, like many students, "resorts" to the forum because he gets support and
> help from it (most of the time). It would be quite wrong to assume that any
> student posting questions in the NG was suffering from "poor tuition"

Judging by the number of mind-bogglingly elementary queries posted, and the
corresponding "do your own homework" responses, it's either poor tuition or
utterly unsuited posters ...

> <snip>
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

This is not so.  There are a great many standard programming situations, ranging
in complexity from (i.e.,) check-digit calculations to sequential updating of a
sequential master file to ... (insert your own), which could and should be
taught, without reference to language, after a course on logic as used in
programming.    This would routinely include control break logic and such
topics.  THEN the students could be turned loose on programming them.  They
would learn far faster and better by doing the standard situations first and
then getting turned loose on original problems.  Programming may be an art, but
it also has much in common with ordinary engineering, and could use some of the
same training methods.

It would be a very useful project for someone to prepare and encyclopedia of
standard programming problems.  (It would not be at all difficult to make it
language-independent).


> <snip>
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

your own bias.

> <snip>

> Funny how it doesn't to you Tony, even after 11 uploads... some things are
> forever a mystery....Maybe this "new technology" is out of reach for a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Maybe there are several breeds of programmers, each wedded to a particular style
and oultook.  What's the problem?  The person who knows enough to be correct on
all occasions doesn't exist.  Define your criteria for excellence and be guided
by the results.  Who cares how old someone is?

PL

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programming Business Applications

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-24T12:07:44+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b353249_2@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<GnMY6.5437$yp1.199359@www.newsranger.com> <3b342643_6@Usenet.com> <3B34D25E.95639FC8@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```

"Peter Lacey" <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:3B34D25E.95639FC8@mb.sympatico.ca...
> "Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:
>
> Judging by the number of mind-bogglingly elementary queries posted, and
the
> corresponding "do your own homework" responses, it's either poor tuition
or
> utterly unsuited posters ...
>
If a student comes here on the chance of getting their homework done and is,
quite properly, advised to "do your own homework", how is that the Teacher's
fault?


> This is not so.  There are a great many standard programming situations,
ranging
> in complexity from (i.e.,) check-digit calculations to sequential updating
of a
> sequential master file to ... (insert your own), which could and should be
> taught, without reference to language, after a course on logic as used in
> programming.    This would routinely include control break logic and such
> topics.  THEN the students could be turned loose on programming them.
They
> would learn far faster and better by doing the standard situations first
and
> then getting turned loose on original problems.  Programming may be an
art, but
> it also has much in common with ordinary engineering, and could use some
of the
> same training methods.
>
I did not suggest anywhere that standard techniques should not be taught. In
fact the analogy I used with painting mentioned standard techniques like
composition, perspective, etc.

However the teaching of techniques is, arguably, NOT the teaching of
Programming. It is debatable and I don't have the time or inclination to
argue it, as it isn't really important.  What I do know is that, as students
acquire experience and confidence, their approach to problems changes. This
has little to do with the tuition I provide. It is a process of "watching
them grow".


> It would be a very useful project for someone to prepare and encyclopedia
of
> standard programming problems.  (It would not be at all difficult to make
it
> language-independent).
I believe their are various catalogs of techniques on various Web Sites.
Obviously I maintain such a catalog which is not organised and has grown
like Topsy over the 37 years I have been a computer programmer.

The fact is, that there are often many solutions to even "standard
problems". I can recall many years ago going through the logic of a "binary
chop" table search with a student. It was a standard algorithm I had used
for years. The student, never having seen it before, listened attentively
and then said: "But why don't you...<intuitive improvement, achieved in a
flash of insight>". I was gobsmacked. But instead of taking the attitude
"Hey!, this is a standard solution. We DO it this way, so just shut up and
get on with it..." I had the algorithm changed on that particular site to
conform to his suggested improvement. To this day I still use "his" improved
algorithm. The point is that the imagination of individuals should not be
stifled by rigid adherence to "the way it has always been done and the way
it always will be done, based on what we did with unit record equipment."


> >
> > Well, it's certainly true that the programs YOU write are basically the
> > same. It's like the guitarist who played a single note over and over.
His
> > wife, finding her patience tried beyond endurance, asked him: "Why don't
you
> > move your fingers around like other guitarists?" He replied: "They're
> > looking for the place; I've found it..."
> >
>
> Is this sort of comment really going to forward the discussion?  It just
shows
> your own bias.
>
When it comes to Foodman I openly admit to bias. He has called me a liar,
has denigrated the work I do with students, has suggested that my Web Site
is a lie, in fact has attacked my approach on a number of occasions over the
years. I wrote to him privately and sent him evidence supporting the claims
on my Web Site (I could've publicly embarassed him by posting it here, but I
didn't). He never once retracted his statements or expressed any kind of
regret, publicly or privately, so yes, I probably react a bit too much when
it comes to him.

I realise that he is set in his ways and I have moved on. I posted these
very restrained (for me...<G>) comments because I honestly believe that his
approach is wrong. However, I'm not going to debate it further than I have
already.

As for  ">
> Is this sort of comment really going to forward the discussion?  It just
shows
> your own bias."

Well, fuck you, if you can't take a joke....<G>

>
> Maybe there are several breeds of programmers, each wedded to a particular
style
> and oultook.  What's the problem?  The person who knows enough to be
correct on
> all occasions doesn't exist.  Define your criteria for excellence and be
guided
> by the results.  Who cares how old someone is?
>
OK. No problem with that. And I am not biased against the aged (or the
young).

Pete.

<a id="t-ef6bfb6996"></a>
## Thread: How to hide cobol code — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to hide cobol code

- **From:** Ron Bell <ronbell@cais.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-22T16:53:36-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B33B050.78A8609B@cais.com>`

```
William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> What compiler and what operating system?
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> > ronbell@cais.com

I am using IBM mainframe OS/390 cobol.  Years ago I remember using
something like 'printoff' and 'printon' statements to stop/start the
display of code when it compiled, I will try that.  Copy statements are
easy to hide with the 'suppress' command.  My programs include a lot of
++include statements which generate a lot of CICS code (like 25,000
lines of compiled output), so I need to hide the ++include code.  Thanks
for your reply,
```

#### ↳ Re: How to hide cobol code

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-22T16:22:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9h0ctv$3cd$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3B33B050.78A8609B@cais.com>`

```
A few answers for you (for IBM mainframe environments)

A) ++includes are PANVALET, not COBOL syntax.  Therefore, you may want to
check their manuals for the best way to handle those statements

B) If you are using a currently supported COBOL (IBM COBOL for
this-and-that), then you may place a

  *CBL NOLIST
      or
   *CONTROL NOLIST

statement before code that you do not want to show in a listing.  For more
information, see:


http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igylr205/8.1.3?

C) If the code to be "hidden" is in a copybook (not ++include) then you can
(as suggested elsewhere) use the IBM extension of COPY SUPPRESSING.

    ***

All of these are ways to hide the source from the LISTING.  *None* of them
prevent a programmer from actually "looking" at the members via non-compile
methods.  For something like that you would want to use your security
package (e.g. RACF, ACF, etc).  However, you should be aware that the
compile process itself *must* have "read" access to the source code.
Therefore, I can't (easily) think of a way of preventing the programmer (who
really wants to) manually reading this source outside the compile process.
The only way that you could avoid this would be to REQUIRE that the
"average" programmer go thru a "security" person to submit compiles.  This
would be highly unusual and pretty user-unfriendly, but COULD theoretically
be done.

NOTE:
  Another thing you MIGHT want to think about is putting all of your
"secure" source code (to be copied in) in a separate (RACF protected)
library and always use the
   COPY xxx  OF yyy
format of the COPY statement.  In this way, the DDName (pointed to by YYY)
could have a "higher" security requirement than where normal source is
found.

If the thing that you want to "secure" is actually procedural code, then I
would suggest that you put it into a separate "callable" module that simply
has a "documented" interface.  (Or better yet, start thinking about OO COBOL
and "encapsulation").  In this way, all the "others" would need was access
to your "load module" - and would never see the code involved.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How to hide cobol code

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-22T16:25:19-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9h0d3e$ruq$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3B33B050.78A8609B@cais.com> <9h0ctv$3cd$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Correction to my samples, what you are interested in are:

   *CBL NOSOURCE   (not NOLIST)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How to hide cobol code

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-06-22T22:03:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B33C04E.3D98E80D@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3B33B050.78A8609B@cais.com> <9h0ctv$3cd$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:

> If the thing that you want to "secure" is actually procedural code, then I
> would suggest that you put it into a separate "callable" module that simply
> has a "documented" interface.  (Or better yet, start thinking about OO COBOL
> and "encapsulation").  In this way, all the "others" would need was access
> to your "load module" - and would never see the code involved.

Well Bill - that's real neat. You've actually found an excuse for using OO with
a mainframe <G>. I can just see Howard running up one wall, across the ceiling
and down the other wall. "Encapsulation ?", he says. "You've got to be kidding.
How the hell can I test my modules without having access to the source ?".

Over to you Howard <G>

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to hide cobol code

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-06-23T12:40:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<070Z6.77$UQ2.37774@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<3B33B050.78A8609B@cais.com> <9h0ctv$3cd$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3B33C04E.3D98E80D@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:3B33C04E.3D98E80D@home.com...
> "William M. Klein" wrote:
>
> > If the thing that you want to "secure" is actually procedural code, then
I
> > would suggest that you put it into a separate "callable" module that
simply
> > has a "documented" interface.  (Or better yet, start thinking about OO
COBOL
> > and "encapsulation"). In this way, all the "others" would need was
access
> > to your "load module" - and would never see the code involved.
>
> Well Bill - that's real neat. You've actually found an excuse for using OO
with
> a mainframe <G>. I can just see Howard running up one wall, across the
ceiling
> and down the other wall. "Encapsulation ?", he says. "You've got to be
kidding.
> How the hell can I test my modules without having access to the source ?".
>
>
If you want to use this an excuse to introduce OO programming, you'll have
to contend with us proecedural dinosaurs, who will point out you don't need
'OO' to use a separate 'callable' module.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to hide cobol code

- **From:** Joseph J Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2001-06-25T00:36:31+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B36170F.3080506@iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<3B33B050.78A8609B@cais.com> <9h0ctv$3cd$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3B33C04E.3D98E80D@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:

> 
> "William M. Klein" wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Oh Balderdash!


You could do that without OO with just about any language.

OO is just a more formalised way of doing it. AND a callable module is NOT a 
major reason for OO on the mainframe. There are much better reasons as Peter 
Dashwood would point out that have to do with interfaces.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to hide cobol code

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-06-25T14:23:36+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b36a01e_2@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<3B33B050.78A8609B@cais.com> <9h0ctv$3cd$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3B33C04E.3D98E80D@home.com> <3B36170F.3080506@iinet.net.au>`

```
"Joseph J Katnic" <josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote in message
news:3B36170F.3080506@iinet.net.au...

> Oh Balderdash!

I understand your frustration, Joe.

There are a number of misconceptions (on both sides of the fence -
mainframers misinterpreting what OO is and what it is good for, and OO
people who don't really understand the mainframe environment...I'm not
suggesting this is the case for EVERYONE; there are others who have a very
good understanding of both sides.)

This is all pretty normal as a "new technology" emerges...

> You could do that without OO with just about any language.
>
> OO is just a more formalised way of doing it. AND a callable module is NOT
a
> major reason for OO on the mainframe. There are much better reasons as
Peter
> Dashwood would point out that have to do with interfaces.
>
Agreed.

I'm reproducing my response to some OO questions asked in another thread,
which may have been missed, and which deals with the similarities between
callable Mainframe modules and OO.

In response to Clark F. Morris.... I wrote:

"Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote in message
news:3B328EF5.707E3593@istar.ca...
> The COBOL dynamic call on the mainframe has exactly the same drawbacks
> of immediately changing for all invokers of a given called module when
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Don't confuse dynamic mainframe calls with instantiating Objects via a
constructor method. There is no comparison. The OO Factory method is already
loaded in memory and churns out instances of data when it is requested to
(via "NEW"). When Object methods are invoked they are applied to this
instance data, via the Object Reference which was returned from the
instantiation. Logically, the process can be visualised as similar to
dynamically loading a module, but that is where the similarity ends.
Physically the processes are quite dissimilar.

>>>
> 1.  What is the overhead of having separately loaded modules and does it
> matter for the target environment?  There is a measurable overhead but
> it is trivial for the applications that I have seen.
>
<<<
That isn't how it works... see above.

>>>
> 2.  How do we know that we have the sub-routine (object, etc.) and how
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
<<<
When it is instantiated it returns an Object Reference to itself. If you
instantiate again you get another instance and another Object Reference. You
know "what it does" from its documented Methods and Properties. Hence you
access the Properties via the same Object Reference as you access the Object
Methods. It is like a Universal Pointer to ONE PARTICULAR instance of the
Object. There is nothing to stop you having an array of Object References
which all represent the same CLASS of Object. However, each one will
represent a different instance of it. Imagine a litho plate which prints a
picture; each print is numbered, but they are all identical. If you want to
mark one of the pictures you refer to it by its number. It is exactly the
same principle.

>>>
> 3.  Because of perceived greater flexibility in some ways, will there be
> more checking and conversion done at the inter-module boundary?
>
<<<
Yes. The new OO COBOL standard (grimace) calls for conformance checking at
compile time to ensure that the parameters passed to an Object's methods are
valid for it. (It is similar to the use of Header files in C++). OO COBOL
defines a Repository which enables it to do this checking, but (in my
opinion) a better way is to define Method Prototypes which can be used as
"stubs" while you are developing a system. (I don't know what the Standard
says about this, but it is common practice in other languages and Fujitsu OO
COBOL supports it, so it gets my vote...)

>>>
> 4.  From what I have seen, the general rule is to pass the elements
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> the number and weirdness varies by state (and other jurisdictions).
<<<
Well I can't comment on what you've seen, but I can assure you I have
objects which pass "structures" (Group Properties, if you like) when this is
desirable. If you wanted to make such Properties individually available as
Public Properties, you would need to define them as Public Properties and
set them within the Factory Method by moving elements of the passed
structure to the individual fields. (I don't do that, but it is quite
feasible and avoids the need to make every Property a separate parameter.)

You may have seen this process arise because of some misunderstandings
regarding the nature of COM objects. Many people believe that for a COM
object, every property must be defined and requires a GET or SET Method.
This rules out the passing of structures. However, this belief is fallacious
and I have COM components which pass structures as COM type VT_STRING and
work fine.

So to answer your question, what you have seen is not "wrong" but it is
unnecessary. A "large number of elements such as state tax calculations
where the number and weirdness varies by state (and other jurisdictions)"
could be passed as an array of type "String".

From the theoretical point of view it would be better if the Object went and
fetched them off a database or from a Collection; or maybe a "collector"
object could be invoked, depending on the jurisdiction, and then pass what
it collected as an array to the actual calculation module. This has to do
with encapsulation and visibility of data to processes which don't require
it.

Hope this helps,

Pete.





Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```
